low.title: A Pawnee Trading Party
low.description: Late this afternoon, as your wagons crest a gentle rise, you spot a group of roughly twenty Pawnee riders approaching from the northwest. They move unhurried, in loose formation—hunters rather than warriors, you judge. One rider carries a small hide bundle. Your scout reports they've signaled peaceful intent, and they're angling to intercept your line of march. The Pawnee have traded with emigrant trains before, though some parties report hard bargains.

choices.label: Hold position and invite them to trade. You have supplies to spare, and fresh game would improve morale.

consequences.description: The Pawnee hunters trade you fresh buffalo and antelope meat for ammunition and coin. The exchange is fair—their interpreter speaks passable English. The encounter delays you half a day, and the fresh meat lifts spirits noticeably. You learn they've seen no cholera outbreaks on the trail this season, a relief.

choices.label: Quicken pace and maintain distance. Trading halts progress, and unknown intentions make unnecessary risks.

consequences.description: Your party wheels into a faster march. The oxen strain under the urgent pace, and one shows signs of lameness by nightfall—overwork in the spring heat. Thomas and Sarah grow visibly frustrated at what they perceive as fearfulness. The Pawnee watch from a distance, make no hostile move, and eventually veer away. You've gained ground but lost goodwill and an animal's health.

choices.label: Send James forward alone with a rifle and trade goods to negotiate at a distance. Minimize risk while exploring terms.

consequences.description: James rides forward with rifle in hand and a small cache of goods. The Pawnee respect the gesture. He negotiates jerked meat and dried pumpkin for modest amounts of ammunition and coin. Mary is furious that you sent her husband into potential danger for minimal gain; Sarah shares her worry. But James returns unharmed with fresh provisions, and William and Thomas approve of his bold approach. The incident reinforces the tension between caution and trust that defines emigrant life.

low.journal_entry: April 20, 1848. The Great Plains reveal themselves as less empty than we feared. Today we encountered Pawnee hunters—a reminder that this land belongs to nations who know it far better than we ever shall. Every choice feels weighted. Progress, safety, and neighborliness pull in different directions. We are not the first to walk this trail, nor will we be the last. The burden falls to us to move forward with eyes open.

low.title: The Broken Axle
low.description: A sharp crack splits the morning air as the lead wagon lurches. The left rear axle, weakened by weeks of mud and rocky terrain, has fractured near the hub. The wagon lists dangerously, and oxen strain to hold it steady. Without repair, the wagon cannot move forward—but the break is repairable if you act now. Your spare parts inventory includes one hickory axle section and the tools to fit it, though the work will consume most of the day. Alternatively, you could redistribute that wagon's load among the others and leave the wagon behind, pressing on by afternoon.

choices.label: Repair the axle using spare parts—halt for the full day.
Redistribute cargo and abandon the wagon—move on by early afternoon.

low.journal_entry: April 28th — A sickening crack at dawn. The left axle gave way under the weight of winter stores and trade goods. We've come 150 miles on these same wheels, and the prairie shows no mercy. Lost a day to repair or lose a wagon to abandon. Either path costs us dearly.

low.title: Ruined Provisions and a Difficult Choice
low.description: The flour you forded through yesterday has begun to spoil in the humid heat. You discover the sacks have developed a sour smell and visible mold blooms across the surface. With Mary already weakened by cholera and rations already modest, losing this flour means dangerously thin meals ahead. A Pawnee hunting party approaches your camp from the south—perhaps a dozen riders. They appear peaceful, but communication will be difficult. Thomas suggests you might trade some ammunition for fresh corn or dried meat. James worries this sets a precedent. Sarah points out that Mary's condition demands better nutrition than hardtack alone.

choices.label: Dump the ruined flour and attempt to trade ammunition for food with the Pawnee hunters.
Salvage what flour you can by scraping away the mold. Avoid contact with the Pawnee—circle the wagons and appear cautious.
Offer trade goods (some money and a few items) rather than ammunition. Show goodwill but keep your fighting capacity intact.

personality_effects.James: Leader anxious about resource depletion and group cohesion.
personality_effects.Mary: Too ill to weigh in meaningfully on decisions.
personality_effects.Thomas: Pragmatic; favors trades that solve immediate problems.
personality_effects.Sarah: Concerned for the sick; wants nutrition prioritized.

low.journal_entry: June 15, 1848. The creek crossing cost us dearly—our flour soured in the damp and heat. We have perhaps 80 pounds of decent provisions left, and Mary grows weaker each day. A Pawnee hunting party appeared at midday. They made no threatening moves, but we must decide quickly how to respond. Every choice carries weight. The oxen tire, the heat oppresses, and we are still 900 miles from the Willamette Valley.

low.title: The Alkali Sink
low.description: By afternoon, the landscape flattens into a vast depression ringed by white crusts—alkali deposits that mark a poisoned water hole. Your scout reports the nearest reliable water is eight miles north, off the main trail. The oxen are labored and thirsty. Mary spots what might be a shallow well someone dug years ago, partially collapsed. The heat is merciless—104 degrees by your reckoning. You have perhaps four hours before the oxen become too weakened to move safely.

choices.label: Push directly north to the distant water source, leaving the trail.

consequences.narrative: The detour costs time and effort. One ox collapses from heat exhaustion and must be abandoned. The party presses through dust and scrub, rationing water from canteens. You reach a clean spring by dusk, but the deviation costs nearly a full day's progress.

choices.label: Attempt to clear and use the old well, gambling on finding water.

consequences.narrative: Hard labor in the heat. Thomas and James dig for two hours. The well yields brackish water—unsafe. Sarah develops severe stomach illness from tainted water drunk earlier. You're forced to camp here, using medicine to settle her condition. No meaningful progress made.

choices.label: Water the oxen sparingly from canteens and press forward on the main trail toward Fort Laramie.

consequences.narrative: A grim march. Two oxen fall and must be shot to end their suffering. The party's spirits plummet at the waste. You cover only four miles before being forced to halt. Tensions rise; whispers of poor planning surface.

James.relationship: Cautious leadership weighs on him. If well chose fails, he shoulders blame.

Mary.relationship: Pushing north appeals to her pragmatism, but abandoning the trail unsettles her sense of order.

Thomas.relationship: Digging the well would exhaust him further; his health is already fragile.

Sarah.relationship: She fears poisoned water and will voice concern strongly.

low.journal_entry: July 10th, 1848. The plains play cruel tricks. What looked like salvation—that old well—proved a snare. We lost good oxen today, and tempers fray thin. The heat drains not just water but hope. Fort Laramie cannot come soon enough. William would have made a joke of this misery. I miss that.

low.title: A Shortcut Through the Pass
low.description: On the morning of July 20th, your party reaches a fork in the trail near the Medicine Bow range. An old trapper, camped nearby with his furs, offers intelligence: the main trail switchbacks north for 40 miles through well-watered valleys. But he claims there's a direct route over Bridger Pass—steep, rocky, only 18 miles—passable now in summer, though his mules struggled two weeks back. He warns the pass narrows between two peaks where lightning strikes are common in afternoon storms. Your oxen are tired. Your food supplies are meager. Sarah has developed a persistent cough.

choices.label: Take the well-established route north through the valleys. It's longer, but safer.

consequences.note: Extra distance, but good grazing. Sarah's cough improves with rest.

choices.label: Risk Bridger Pass. Save time and supplies, but gamble on weather and terrain.

consequences.note: Afternoon lightning storm traps you on exposed ridge. One ox breaks a leg and must be shot. Steep descent damages wagon wheel and springs. Everyone is shaken. Sarah's cough worsens dangerously.

choices.label: Send James and Thomas ahead to scout the pass with minimal supplies. Party follows the main route.

personality_effects.Mary: anxious about separation
personality_effects.Sarah: worried for father and brother

consequences.note: James and Thomas return in 36 hours with news: pass is treacherous but navigable if approached carefully at dawn before storms form. Party takes longer route but knows the truth. Mary resents being left behind. Morale suffers from separation.

personality_effects.James: pragmatic, weighing risk versus time
personality_effects.Mary: concerned for Sarah's health
personality_effects.Thomas: eager to prove himself, wants the faster route
personality_effects.Sarah: weakened by cough, afraid of another setback

low.journal_entry: July 20, 1848 — Near Medicine Bow Range. Met a weathered trapper this morning who speaks of mountains as though they were old adversaries. He offered us a choice: the safe road or the desperate one. Every mile saved now brings California closer, but Sarah's cough frightens me more than any storm. The oxen need rest, though I dare not voice that fear where the others might hear it. William would have known what to do.

low.title: Mountain Pass Decision
low.description: Your party has reached a fork in the mountain trail. The left path climbs higher toward South Pass—longer, exposed to afternoon storms, but well-traveled and marked. The right path descends into a narrow canyon, supposedly a shortcut used by fur trappers. A faded chalk mark on a rock reads 'Pass—2 days' while someone has scratched 'Canyon—1 day' below it. Your oxen are tired. Thomas is coughing. The weather is turning; those clouds gather fast in afternoon.

choices.label: Take the established pass route—safer, well-known, but adds a day to the journey.
Risk the canyon shortcut—saves time, but narrows escape routes if weather turns dangerous.
Make camp here and wait for clearer weather—rest the animals and party, but burn supplies and time.

low.journal_entry: August 1st, 1848 — The mountains tower above us now, their peaks white with snow though it is summer. We stand at a crossroads, both literal and circumstantial. James studies the chalk marks on the stone with furrowed brow. Mary tends Thomas, who has developed a troubling cough from the thin air and cold nights. Our oxen are lean and weary. The sky darkens by three o'clock every day now, and lightning splits the ridges. We have lost Sarah and William to this journey already—fever and dysentery took them both in the last week. Every choice now carries weight. The shortcut calls to James, as it always does. But I wonder if speed matters when the mountains care nothing for our hurry.

low.title: The Poisoned Spring
low.description: Near midday, your party discovers a natural spring—a rare blessing in this arid stretch. The water is clear and cold. But as James kneels to fill a canteen, Mary notices something: animal bones scattered around the rim, and a faint chemical smell beneath the mineral tang. The spring may be alkaline or contain arsenic leached from the rocks. It's impossible to know without drinking first. Your water barrels are down to half. The next reliable water source is at least two days away if you push hard.

choices.label: Fill the barrels cautiously. Test it on one animal first.

consequences.description: You water one ox with the spring's contents and wait. By evening, the animal shows signs of distress—trembling, refusal of feed. By morning, it is dead. The spring is unsafe. You've lost an ox and a day, but you tested before risking the whole party.

choices.label: Avoid the spring entirely. Push hard for the next water source.

consequences.description: You press forward through dust and heat. Throats dry, tempers shorter. Thomas complains bitterly about James's caution, though he understands the logic. Mary maintains her composure but her sanity frays. Two days of hard marching leave everyone exhausted, but you reach a clean spring tributary by evening. No one dies, but the cost in morale and health is steep.

choices.label: Drink moderately from the spring. Ration it carefully for the next two days.

consequences.description: By evening, stomach cramps grip James and Thomas. By the next morning, both men are feverish and weak. The spring water was indeed contaminated. You use your last medicine to manage their symptoms. Mary and James argue over whose decision this was. Thomas, weakened by illness, falls behind on the march. You lose a full day to their recovery, and morale plummets as the party realizes how close they came to disaster.

personality_effects.James: Questioned for his judgment; leadership authority weakened regardless of choice.
personality_effects.Mary: Demonstrates practical caution; gains respect if spring is avoided.
personality_effects.Thomas: Frustration builds. Needs evidence of progress to restore morale.

low.journal_entry: September 1st. The desert offers nothing freely. Found a spring today—clear water, but bones nearby and a smell we couldn't place. A grave two miles back bore the mark of someone who didn't reach the valley. Every choice on this trail costs something. We chose... and we live with it. 1,300 miles behind us. Maybe 300 ahead if the weather holds. The oxen grow lean. So do we.

low.title: A Pawnee Encampment
low.description: As your party rounds a bend in the canyon, you spot a Pawnee hunting camp perhaps a quarter-mile distant—three lodges positioned near a water source, with smoke rising lazily into the cool morning air. You count roughly a dozen figures moving about. They appear to have not yet noticed you, though that will change within minutes. Mary is still weak from fever, and your food stores are alarmingly low. The Pawnee in this region are generally peaceful traders, though encounters always carry risk when your party is this depleted.

choices.label: Approach openly with a white cloth tied to a walking stick—attempt trade for food

consequences.narrative: The Pawnee hunters are cautious but not hostile. After tense exchanges and barter, you trade 150 cents and five rounds of ammunition for roughly 8 pounds of dried meat and pemmican. The encounter costs you a full day but provides critical provisions. The Pawnee leader nods at your honesty—a small gesture, but it steadies the nerves of your exhausted party.

choices.label: Quietly retreat upstream and attempt to skirt the camp under cover of canyon walls

consequences.narrative: You guide the party away from the Pawnee camp, but the detour forces you into rougher terrain. One ox becomes lame navigating loose canyon stone and must be left behind. The escape is successful, but fear and exhaustion wear heavily on everyone. Mary's fever worsens from the exertion and cold night bivouac. James questions whether avoiding a potential trade was wise.

choices.label: Send James alone to scout and negotiate—Mary stays with the remaining supplies

consequences.narrative: James spends a tense two hours in negotiation, alone and unarmed by agreement. The Pawnee are respectful but the discussion is lengthy and uncertain. He returns with 5 pounds of meat and has bartered away 75 cents, but the delay leaves Mary feverish and increasingly anxious. She resents being left behind when she is already weakened. The party feels fractured by the separation.

personality_effects.James: The weight of leadership is visible in his face—three dead already, supplies dwindling, and difficult choices at every turn.
personality_effects.Mary: Weakened by fever, she alternates between determination to continue and quiet despair about her condition.

low.journal_entry: September 10, 1848. We encountered Pawnee hunters in the canyon today. With Thomas, Sarah, and William gone, and Mary burning with fever, I felt the thinness of our company keenly. We had perhaps 15 pounds of food left—not enough to reach Fort Boise at our pace. Difficult choices ahead. The canyon walls seem to press closer each day.

low.title: An Old Shoshone Encampment
low.description: While searching for water near a dried creek bed, you stumble upon the remains of a Shoshone summer camp—perhaps abandoned weeks ago. There are willow-branch structures still standing, blackened fire rings, and discarded hide scraps. Most importantly, you spot what appears to be a partially buried clay vessel near one of the old hearths. Given the dust storms and your dwindling water situation, this could contain something useful. However, disturbing indigenous sites carries its own weight, and you're uncertain what you'll find or whether leaving it untouched is the respectful choice.

choices.label: Excavate the vessel carefully and check its contents.

consequences.notes: The vessel contains nothing but dry clay dust and a small cache of pemmican—badly spoiled and inedible. The labor leaves James weary and disappointed. Mary's morale suffers as she watches him dig through another people's camp, sick and exhausted.

choices.label: Leave the camp untouched and move on; search downstream for natural water sources.

consequences.notes: You travel hard for two days with minimal water and reduced rations. James and Mary both suffer from the exertion and heat, but you find a seep where the creek still holds moisture. No desecration, and a small sense of dignity preserved.

choices.label: Take shelter in the old structures for the night; it offers protection from the cold. Search only for water, nothing else.

consequences.notes: The willow structures provide windbreak and insulation. A night's proper rest helps James's health considerably. You locate a small underground spring near the camp—enough to refill barrels. Respectful use of shelter without violation.

personality_effects.James: Exhausted. Three graves behind him. Every decision now weighs differently.
personality_effects.Mary: Weakened by dysentery. Her patience thin. She watches James to see what kind of man he still is, out here.

low.journal_entry: Sept. 15 — Found an old Shoshone camp this afternoon. The land out here swallows people whole, Indian and emigrant alike. Our water situation is dire. Every choice feels like it might be the last one that matters. Mary is still feverish. We are now five. The desert doesn't care how many we were when we started.

low.title: The Sluggish Ox and the Muddy Trail
low.description: October has turned the Oregon trail into a mire. One of your oxen, poisoned by the alkali water two days back, now moves with painful slowness. The animal's breathing is labored, and it stumbles frequently in the mud. Your party has covered only eight miles since dawn. James studies the beast with grim practicality: it will either recover or become a dead weight you cannot afford. Thomas suggests pushing harder to make up lost time. Mary points out that the ox might recover with rest and careful management. The forest closes in around you—towering firs and hemlock dripping with rain. Winter is coming.

choices.label: Rest the party and ox for two days. Monitor its condition closely.
Lighten the wagon by discarding the damaged spare parts. Push on at steady pace.
Slaughter the weakened ox for meat. Redistribute its load among the remaining animals.

personality_effects.James: James respects practical decisions but fears losing capacity.
personality_effects.Mary: Mary values mercy toward animals; slaughter wounds her deeply.
personality_effects.Thomas: Thomas wants speed. Delay frustrates him; efficiency appeals to him.

low.journal_entry: October 1st. The poisoned ox is failing. We are still 300 miles from the Willamette Valley, and the rains grow colder each day. Sarah and William are buried behind us. The forest is beautiful and suffocating—we see few emigrants now, mostly just our own struggling line of canvas and wood. James says we must choose: mercy, speed, or pragmatism. Each choice costs us something. The oxen smell the winter coming too.

low.title: The Oxen Stumble
low.description: On a muddy forest track, one of your three remaining oxen steps into a concealed hole and comes up lame. The animal can barely walk, favoring its front left leg. James examines it and finds no break, but the swelling suggests a severe sprain. The ox will not recover quickly—perhaps not at all. You are only a few weeks from the Willamette Valley, but traveling with an injured animal will slow you considerably, and you cannot afford to lose another beast.

choices.label: Leave the injured ox behind. Redistribute the load among the remaining two.
Slaughter the ox for fresh meat and rest the party for a day.
Reduce rations further and nurse the ox along slowly. Trust it will improve.

low.journal_entry: October 10th. We lost an ox to the mud and mire of this cursed forest. The creature is useless now. Three oxen have carried us this far; we cannot afford to waste what remains. The rain shows no sign of stopping. Our flour is damp, our spirits damper still. Two of our party are buried behind us. If the road does not improve soon, I fear we shall not see the valley before winter settles in earnest.

low.title: The Pawnee Hunters
low.description: Late afternoon, your party crests a rise and spots a hunting party of perhaps a dozen Pawnee on horseback, perhaps a mile distant to the north. They have not seen you yet, occupied with tracking buffalo. Your scouts report the hunters are well-armed with rifles and bows. James calls a halt. The trail ahead passes through territory where the Pawnee range frequently. You have three options: press forward at steady pace and risk encounter, make camp here and wait until dark to move past, or ride north to make peaceful contact and trade.

choices.label: Continue at steady pace. Show no fear, stay alert.

consequences.narrative: You press forward, rifles visible but unhoistered. The Pawnee spot your dust and turn their mounts toward you—a tense moment stretching across the prairie. James rides ahead, hand raised. After a long minute, the hunting party veers east, uninterested in confrontation. You exhale. They wanted buffalo, not trouble. But every person in the party felt the weight of that moment.

choices.label: Make camp immediately and move at night. Avoid contact altogether.

consequences.narrative: You circle the wagons and wait. The party is anxious; William mutters about wasted daylight. That night, moving in darkness over unfamiliar ground, one of the oxen steps in a prairie dog burrow and pulls up lame. You press on, but it costs you time and the animal is now of limited use. The avoidance cost you more than the risk would have.

choices.label: Ride north under a white cloth to initiate trade. Offer tobacco and coffee.

consequences.narrative: James, Mary, and one other ride toward the Pawnee with trade goods held high. The hunters are cautious but accept the overture. After thirty tense minutes of sign-language negotiation, an exchange occurs: your tobacco and coffee for fresh antelope meat and information about water sources ahead. The Pawnee hunter gestures west toward better grazing—a gift of genuine knowledge. Trust, however fragile, proved valuable.

personality_effects.James: Leadership tested—his judgment determines party survival
personality_effects.William: Courage and caution in conflict
personality_effects.Mary: Diplomacy and social instinct

low.journal_entry: 20 April, evening. Saw Pawnee hunters today—a moment that clarifies how thin our margin is out here. We are guests in their land, traveling through it by permission of circumstance, not law. The sky is enormous and indifferent. We are smaller than we believe ourselves to be. Still, we pressed on. Two weeks' travel to Fort Laramie if the weather holds.

low.title: The Pawnee Hunters
low.description: On the morning of May 1st, your party spots a group of five Pawnee hunters on horseback approaching from the northwest, moving parallel to your wagon train. They do not appear hostile—their weapons are lowered—but their presence has unsettled everyone. James estimates they are likely hunting buffalo in this region. The Pawnee have shown mixed relations with emigrant trains: some trade peacefully, others demand tribute, and a few have been known to steal livestock or supplies. Your scouts report the hunters have slowed their pace, watching your wagons with obvious interest. A decision must be made quickly about how to proceed.

choices.label: Halt the wagons and attempt peaceful trade. Offer dried meat and tobacco.
Circle the wagons defensively and prepare arms, but do not shoot unless fired upon.
Ignore them and press forward at increased pace. Demonstrate confidence and do not acknowledge their presence.

personality_effects.James: Leader; values pragmatism and protection of the group.
personality_effects.Mary: Cautious by nature; prefers diplomatic solutions; unnerved by armed confrontation.
personality_effects.Thomas: Skeptical and defensive; believes most strangers are threats.
personality_effects.Sarah: Empathetic; curious about other peoples; concerned with fairness.
personality_effects.William: Anxious and protective; worries about livestock and survival.

low.journal_entry: May 1st, 1848 — Prairie, 200 miles from departure. This morning brought our first real test of judgment. Five Pawnee hunters appeared on the horizon, and every person in this wagon train held their breath. These are the people who know this land better than any map or guide. Some say they are reasonable traders. Others whisper darker stories around the fire. We chose our path and must live with the consequences. Whatever happens, we move west. We must move west.

low.title: The Pawnee Trade
low.description: As your wagons rest in the river valley bottom, a party of six Pawnee riders approaches from the north. They are armed with bows and trade goods—buffalo hides, dried meat, and tanned leather. Their leader, a man of middle years with a scarred face, gestures peacefully and calls out in broken English. He offers fresh buffalo meat and high-quality hides in exchange for ammunition, cloth, or tobacco. His hunters have had success on the plains nearby. The mosquitoes swarm relentlessly as you negotiate, and dark clouds gather to the west. Your party is low on fresh meat, but ammunition is precious on the trail ahead.

choices.label: Trade 30 rounds of ammunition and 2 yards of cloth for 40 pounds of dried buffalo meat and two hides.
Politely decline and move camp to higher ground before the storm arrives.
Trade 50 rounds of ammunition for 60 pounds of dried meat, two hides, and information about water sources ahead.

personality_effects.James: The leader weighs the practicality of the trade against the importance of ammunition for potential troubles ahead.
personality_effects.Mary: She advocates for fresh meat to bolster the party's health through the remaining journey.
personality_effects.Thomas: He worries about depleting ammunition too quickly, remembering stories of Indian conflicts.
personality_effects.Sarah: The young woman is eager for trade and sees the Pawnee as allies rather than threats.
personality_effects.William: He is cautious and tired, more concerned with reaching camp before the storm.

low.journal_entry: June 1st, 1848 — The Platte country grows hotter each day. We encountered Pawnee traders this afternoon near our camp in the river valley. Their offer was fair, and the sight of fresh meat stirred the party's appetite. The sky threatened rain, and mosquitoes were relentless. We made our choice and settled the matter with goods and words rather than suspicion. The storm came hard before sundown, but our camp held firm. We are 450 miles from Independence now, and the true plains stretch before us. The oxen are holding steady, though William complains of the heat.

low.title: Injured Oxen and Mountain Passage
low.description: One of your oxen has grown increasingly sluggish since drinking from the alkali pool two days ago. Its breathing is labored, and it limps noticeably. You've made good time through the valley, but ahead lies a steep mountain pass—the most direct route to Fort Laramie. The animal may not survive the climb. A scout reports an alternate path, longer and more exposed to weather, but gentler on livestock. You have enough feed for either route, though meager rations mean every pound of supply matters.

choices.label: Push the sick oxen through the mountain pass. It's faster, and the animal may recover once we're past the worst.
Take the longer route. Slower, but easier on the livestock and less risk of losing the oxen entirely.
Cull the sick oxen now. Butcher it for fresh meat to supplement our meager rations. Trade time for food security.

personality_effects.James: Leader decisions weigh heavily. Choose poorly on livestock, and the party suffers doubly.
personality_effects.Mary: Practical about hard choices, but hates waste and cruelty to animals.
personality_effects.Thomas: Weakened by illness; he worries openly about whether he'll make it to Oregon.
personality_effects.Sarah: Young and anxious. Loss of oxen frightens her—they represent security.

low.journal_entry: July 20th. — Spotted Chimney Rock yesterday, a wonder rising from the plains like a sentinel. But our troubles multiply. One of the oxen is gravely sick from the alkali water. Its hide has turned dull, and it cannot keep pace. We are in a devil's choice: push on through difficult terrain and risk losing it entirely, take the long way round and burn precious stores, or end its misery ourselves for meat. William would have known what to do. The mountains ahead do not care about our difficulties. We must decide by morning.

low.title: The Mountain Sickness Worsens
low.description: Mary has grown noticeably weaker over the past two days. What began as a persistent cough and headache has progressed to fever and difficulty breathing in the thin mountain air. As you climb toward South Pass, the elevation gain is working against her. Thomas suggests pressing harder to reach lower elevations quickly. James considers stopping for a day to let her rest and recover. The weather remains unpredictable—clear now, but those afternoon thunderstorms could turn treacherous on exposed slopes.

choices.label: Push forward at a faster pace to reach lower ground within two days.
Make camp for a full day. Rest Mary, tend to her fever with poultices.
Send James ahead with a fast horse to scout for shelter and medicinal plants. Party continues at steady pace.

personality_effects.Mary: Her condition reflects whether the party prioritizes her welfare or the schedule. Recovery builds trust; deterioration breeds resentment.
personality_effects.Thomas: Growing impatient with delays. Faster pace earns his approval, but callousness toward Mary's suffering tests his conscience.
personality_effects.James: As leader, he bears the weight of the decision. Rest reassures the party but costs time as winter closes in.

low.journal_entry: August 15th, 1848 — Segment 11, Rocky Mountains. We have climbed to where the air itself feels thin and cold. Mary is very ill. The fever comes and goes with the afternoon heat, and she cannot walk without leaning on someone's arm. We are caught between two hard truths: the mountain will not wait for the sick, and neither will winter. One man—or woman—must give way. I pray for wisdom, though I suspect there is none to be had in these peaks.

low.title: Water and Sickness
low.description: By noon, the party reaches a spring in the sagebrush—a reliable landmark from the guidebooks. The water is clear and cold. Within hours, Mary complains of stomach cramps and fever. Thomas develops a dry cough that sounds worse than the desert dust warrants. The fresh venison, stored without salt in the heat, may have turned. Or it could be something in the water itself. Either way, two members are sick, and you have only 2 units of medicine.

choices.label: Use 2 medicine doses immediately on Mary and Thomas. Rest here one full day.
Ration the medicine: give 1 dose to Mary only. Continue at steady pace. Hope Thomas recovers on his own.
Discard the venison entirely and rely on stores. Use no medicine. Push hard for the next settlement (4 days ahead) where a doctor might help.

personality_effects.James: A leader must decide who lives and dies with what he has. The weight settles differently each time.
personality_effects.Mary: If abandoned to sickness without medicine, she grows bitter. If tended, she remembers.
personality_effects.Thomas: Pride won't let him admit weakness easily. Ignored suffering makes him resentful.

low.journal_entry: September 1st. Found good water at the spring, but sickness came with it or with the meat—can't say for certain. Mary's fever came on sudden. Thomas coughs like he's swallowing sand. Two people down, four oxen tired, and winter breathing down our necks. The guidebooks don't tell you what to do when your own body becomes the enemy.

low.title: Ox Goes Lame in the Canyon
low.description: As your wagons descend into the rocky canyon terrain near the Snake River, one of your oxen—a sturdy cream-colored beast that has carried you since Missouri—steps awkwardly on loose shale and begins to favor its front left leg. By evening, it cannot bear weight on it. The animal is still alive but clearly in pain. You have four oxen total; losing one will slow your pace considerably, and the terrain ahead only gets rougher. James estimates you have perhaps 400 miles left to the Willamette Valley. Your food stores are dangerously low at fifteen pounds.

choices.label: Put the ox down humanely and distribute its weight among the remaining three. Press on at a slower pace.

personality_effects.James: grim_practicality
personality_effects.Mary: resigned_acceptance

choices.label: Attempt to treat the injury—rest the ox for two days, apply what medicine you have, and hope it recovers.

personality_effects.James: cautious_optimism
personality_effects.Mary: hopeful

choices.label: Lighten the wagon drastically—abandon non-essential items to reduce the load and let the ox travel without weight.

personality_effects.James: frustrated_determination
personality_effects.Mary: anxiety

personality_effects.James: weary_but_resolute
personality_effects.Mary: growing_concern

low.journal_entry: September 10th, 1848. The cream ox is lame—stepped on loose stone in this godforsaken canyon. We are so close now, yet every setback feels like the trail is determined to break us. Three of us dead already. I look at Mary's face and see the question there: will we make it? The nights are bitter cold. First frost could come any time. We must decide about the ox before tomorrow's march. —J.H.

low.title: Pawnee Trading Party
low.description: As your exhausted party makes camp near the Snake River, a small group of Pawnee hunters—perhaps eight or nine—approaches from the south. They are armed but not hostile in their bearing. Through gestures and broken English from one older man, they offer to trade: fresh antelope meat for ammunition, or horses for your remaining oxen. Your oxen are worn thin, but ammunition is scarce. The Pawnee seem genuine in their interest—this is a known trade route for them—but you have little to spare and Mary's condition requires steady nutrition.

choices.label: Trade 15 rounds of ammunition for fresh antelope meat (20 lbs)
Offer one ox and 10 rounds for two Pawnee horses—better for rough terrain ahead
Politely decline and give them 5 pounds of flour as a gift—preserve your supplies

personality_effects.James: Leader must weigh immediate survival against future resources. Every decision draws from a depleted well.
personality_effects.Mary: Still fevered and weak. Meat means strength; ammunition means defense. She cannot advocate loudly, only suffer the choice.

low.journal_entry: Met Pawnee hunters today—honest traders, it seemed. The sight of fresh game nearly broke my resolve to be cautious. Mary is no better. Three thousand miles behind us now, and the mountains still ahead. We are thin as the oxen. Every choice feels like choosing which part of ourselves to abandon.

low.title: Oxen Mired in Mud
low.description: The forest trail has become treacherous. Overnight rain has transformed the packed earth into deep, sucking mud. This morning, your lead oxen—already thin from weeks on the trail—sank to their bellies while crossing a low stretch near a creek bed. The animals are exhausted and distressed. Your party can hear the sucking sound as they strain against the yokes. You have perhaps two hours before the oxen tire completely and refuse to move, stranding the wagon here.

choices.label: Unload supplies, lighten the wagon, and drive the oxen through.
Wait for the sun and warmer weather to dry the trail somewhat. Camp here today.
Abandon the wagon. Pack essentials on the oxen and proceed on foot.

personality_effects.James: The weight of leadership settles heavier. Every choice costs something.
personality_effects.Mary: She studies the mud, calculating. Patience may serve better than haste.
personality_effects.Thomas: His youth works against him—three weeks of hunger and rain wear differently on the young.

low.journal_entry: October 1st, 1848 — We have traveled 1630 miles since departing Missouri. The forest here is dense with Douglas fir and hemlock, beautiful but merciless when rain comes. The trail turns to mire within hours. Our oxen are struggling; we have lost two good men already and cannot afford to lose the animals that carry us forward. The fog this morning was so thick we could not see ten feet ahead. Winter is coming early to these mountains. Every day now feels borrowed.

low.title: A Shoshone Family at the Crossroads
low.description: Through the rain and fog, your party encounters a Shoshone family—two adults and three children—camped near a damaged travois. Their horse has gone lame, and they're stranded with winter approaching. The man gestures toward your oxen, then toward the mountains ahead. He speaks in broken English learned from traders: 'Snow come soon. We need help reach valley.' His wife holds a child against her chest, watching your reaction carefully. Your supplies are threadbare, and you're uncertain whether you have enough to spare—or whether you can afford to delay.

choices.label: Give them one ox and some jerky. They can reach the valley faster, and your conscience demands it.

consequences.journal_entry: We gave them an ox and eight pounds of jerky. The man's face showed relief, not gratitude—he knew it was borrowed time for them, not salvation. James said little, but I saw him watching them disappear into the mist. Mary said a quiet prayer. We have two oxen left. The mathematics of survival grow harder each day.

choices.label: Offer to guide them to the valley yourself—delay your journey by two days to ensure they don't perish.

consequences.journal_entry: We lost two days escorting the Shoshone family through the passes. James fretted about our supplies and the snow. Mary seemed at peace. The father pressed a small carved token into James's hand at the valley's edge—a gesture that meant something, though neither of us could say what. We press on, leaner and later.

choices.label: Give them nothing. Winter is coming for your people too, and charity is a luxury you cannot afford.

consequences.journal_entry: We rode past them in the rain. The children watched us leave. James did not speak for hours. Mary's jaw was set so tight I feared her teeth might crack. I told myself we were choosing survival. I am still telling myself that. The fog swallowed them behind us, and I wonder if that makes it better or worse.

personality_effects.James: Leader burdened by impossible choices; pragmatism wars with conscience
personality_effects.Mary: Compassion strained to its limit; each hardship chips away at her faith

low.journal_entry: October 10th, 1848. Rain all day. The forest is thick and patient. We buried Thomas, Sarah, and William three weeks back. Sometimes I cannot recall their faces clearly anymore. Today we met people alive, and had to decide whether our survival was worth more than theirs. The Shoshone man's eyes will stay with me longer than I wish.

low.title: Buffalo Herd and the Sluggish Ox
low.description: On the morning of May 1st, the prairie stretches endlessly before you. A massive buffalo herd—easily two thousand strong—grazes a few miles ahead, moving slowly westward across your intended route. Behind the wagons, one of your oxen, still affected by yesterday's alkali water, moves with a pronounced limp and labored breathing. The animal's condition is worsening. You have perhaps two hours before the herd crosses directly into your path, or you could attempt to move around it, adding distance to your journey.

choices.label: Wait for the herd to pass completely. Rest the oxen and let the sluggish animal recover.
Push the wagons northward immediately to circle around the herd's eastern flank before they move further.
Attempt to navigate the wagons slowly through the herd's southern edge, staying alert for danger.

personality_effects.note: William fears losing more animals; Sarah's courage may shine if danger is braved; Thomas respects decisive action; Mary values caution; James must weigh leader's burden.

low.journal_entry: May 1st, 1848 — A sight to humble any man. The buffalo stretched from horizon to horizon like a living sea of brown. One ox taken sick from the alkali yesterday—the creature is failing. We faced a choice that all of us felt keenly: rest and risk delay, or push hard and risk the animals further. The prairie gives little mercy, and every choice costs something we cannot get back.

low.title: The Pawnee Hunters
low.description: At midday, your scout spots a group of Pawnee hunters on horseback perhaps a quarter-mile distant, moving parallel to your wagon train. They appear to be tracking buffalo. Your party halts. The hunters have not approached, but their presence raises immediate questions: Will they view your wagons as a threat to their hunt? Should you attempt communication, or press forward quickly? James recalls stories—some favorable, some dire—of Pawnee interactions with emigrant parties.

choices.label: Halt and attempt peaceful trade. Offer coffee and tobacco in exchange for fresh game.

consequences.narrative: The Pawnee hunters accept your overture. An older hunter who speaks English trades three fresh buffalo hindquarters for your coffee, tobacco, and a few coins. The meat is excellent—the first fresh game in weeks. Your party gains confidence and well-being.

choices.label: Maintain steady pace and keep weapons visible but lowered. Show no fear or aggression.

consequences.narrative: You proceed calmly. The Pawnee hunters observe your train for perhaps an hour, then veer westward toward a distant buffalo herd. No confrontation occurs. The message was clear: you are not prey, and your path does not threaten theirs. Relief and quiet pride settle over the camp.

choices.label: Circle the wagons immediately and prepare for defense. Send riders ahead to scout.

consequences.narrative: You form defensive positions and dispatch two riders to reconnoiter. The hunters ignore you entirely and continue their pursuit westward. By evening, your scouts return—no threat detected. The defensive posture was unnecessary, and your party knows it. Exhaustion, wasted effort, and the loss of one ox to stress-induced lameness underscore the cost of panic.

personality_effects.note: Party members value cautious diplomacy and confidence. Panic undermines morale significantly.

low.journal_entry: May 10th. Prairie stretches endless before us. Sighted Pawnee hunters at midday—perhaps a dozen, moving across the grassland. The soaked flour worries me still, but today brought us face to face with the true inhabitants of these lands. Our choice in that moment will echo through the remainder of our journey. The oxen grow tired. Sarah's cough has not worsened, a mercy. Clear skies hold, though storms gather to the north. We press on.

low.title: Cholera's Grip Worsens
low.description: Mary's condition has deteriorated overnight. What began as stomach complaint five days ago has progressed into the characteristic violent purging and cramping. Her skin is clammy, her eyes sunken. You have quinine and laudanum in your medicine stores, but neither reliably treats cholera — the disease simply runs its course, and many don't survive it. The humid river valley weather seems to worsen her state. James and Sarah have begun showing early signs of stomach upset themselves. Moving her in a jolting wagon will cause her agony; stopping will consume rations while she recovers nothing but days. Thomas suggests bleeding her, a common remedy, though you've heard conflicting opinions on its worth.

choices.label: Make camp here for three days. Dose Mary with quinine and laudanum. Boil all drinking water and restrict the party to well water only.

personality_effects.Mary: grateful but fading
personality_effects.Thomas: anxious about delay
personality_effects.James: burdened by leadership

choices.label: Continue at half pace. Keep Mary in the wagon with frequent water breaks. Proceed to the next settlement (40 miles) where there may be a doctor.

personality_effects.Mary: suffering and resentful
personality_effects.James: grim determination
personality_effects.Thomas: conflicted

choices.label: Have Thomas perform bloodletting as folk remedy. Continue steady pace. Use remaining laudanum sparingly to manage her pain.

personality_effects.Thomas: confident in tradition
personality_effects.Mary: slightly improved (temporarily)
personality_effects.Sarah: doubtful

personality_effects.James: The weight of decision settles heavier each day. A woman under his care is dying, and he has no certain remedy.
personality_effects.Mary: Fever clouds her mind. She asks for her mother, then doesn't recognize her own name.
personality_effects.Thomas: He clings to old remedies because they're the only tools he knows. Helplessness grates on him.
personality_effects.Sarah: She's begun the ritual preparations — cleaning, organizing — the woman's work of death-watch.

low.journal_entry: June 15th, 1848. Mary took fever three nights past the Platte crossing. We've lost William already; I will not lose another. The river air is thick and foul. The men look to me for answers I do not possess. Tonight I shall pray, though I wonder if the Almighty takes requests from fools who led their families into a plague-ground.

low.title: Mountain Pass Narrowing
low.description: The trail ahead funnels into a steep canyon with rock walls on both sides. Your scout reports that recent rockfall has created a treacherous passage—loose stones litter the path, and the cliff face shows fresh breaks. The oxen are already tired from the altitude. Your options: attempt the narrow pass (saves 15 miles but risks injury), or backtrack 8 miles to find an older, longer route around the mountains that will cost time but is safer. A third option: wait here two days for the loose rocks to settle and stabilize, gambling that weather won't worsen.

choices.label: Push through the narrow pass immediately
Backtrack to the longer route around
Make camp and wait for the rocks to settle

personality_effects.James: Leader must decide. Pragmatic concern for the party's safety weighs against the need for speed—supplies are running short.
personality_effects.Mary: Growing fatigue from high altitude. Questions whether the rapid pace is worth the risk.
personality_effects.Thomas: His weakened constitution makes him most vulnerable to injury or illness from either delay or danger.

low.journal_entry: August 1st — We have come 1,000 miles since leaving Missouri. The mountains test us daily. Lost sight of Sarah and William months ago, God rest them. Today we faced a choice that will shape our survival. The Pawnee warned us this pass could be unstable. Jerky from their trade sustains us, but our flour and salt pork dwindle. The oxen favor the rest; we favor the distance. Every decision now carries weight.

low.title: The Pawnee Trading Post
low.description: As your party rounds a bend in the Snake River valley, you spot a small trading post—a weathered structure of adobe and timber. Outside, three Pawnee traders have established a temporary camp with horses and goods. One man waves you over. They have fresh water, dried meat, and vegetables. They're interested in your spare oxen or clothing. Their prices are steep—this is the only resupply for fifty miles—but their goods could mean the difference between reaching the valley before winter sets in.

choices.label: Trade 2 oxen and 1 set of clothing for 40 lbs food, 4 medicine, and fresh water barrels

consequences.description: The traders drive a hard bargain, but the fresh food and medicine ease the party's anxiety. Mary is visibly relieved to see provisions improve. James grimaces at losing oxen—two teams will strain the remaining animals. Thomas accepts the logic of it.

choices.label: Trade only 10 lbs clothing and $50 for 20 lbs food and water

consequences.description: James negotiates firmly, keeping oxen and most supplies intact. The traders accept less, respecting his resolve. Mary worries the meager food won't last. Thomas supports James's caution but wonders if they're pushing luck.

choices.label: Refuse to trade and push on at faster pace toward water sources ahead

consequences.description: The party moves quickly but water becomes scarce. By evening, Thomas collapses from thirst and heat exhaustion, recovering by morning but weakened. Mary openly questions James's judgment. The next water source is real but distant—you're betting on finding it.

personality_effects.James: Pragmatic but weary. His decisions carry the weight of two graves already behind the party.
personality_effects.Mary: Anxious about provisions. The sight of fresh food triggers deep worry about the coming snow.
personality_effects.Thomas: Young and hardy, but showing signs of doubt. He follows James but increasingly asks why.

low.journal_entry: September 1st — The Snake River country is beautiful and pitiless. We met Pawnee traders today at a post that shouldn't exist out here, yet does. They know this land better than any map we carry. We have little left to trade and less time to spare. Winter doesn't wait for the indecisive. Whatever we choose, it must be soon. William's grave is already fifty miles behind us. I won't add more names to this trail if I can help it.

low.title: The Sluggish Ox and the Canyon Pass
low.description: One of your oxen, which drank from an alkali pool two days ago, has grown noticeably weaker. Its gait is unsteady, and it lags behind the others. You've made good time through the Snake River canyon, but the terrain ahead narrows further, with steep walls on both sides. The pass is passable, but difficult. If this ox fails completely, you'll be down to three beasts of burden—barely enough to pull both wagons through rough country. You could rest here for a day and try to nurse the animal back, or push on and risk losing it in the canyon where recovery would be impossible.

choices.label: Rest the oxen for a full day. Prepare a poultice of salt and herbs to treat the sick animal.
Push forward at a steady pace. The ox may recover on the move, or it may not. Either way, you'll gain distance.
Slaughter the weakened ox for meat. Its leather can be used for repairs. One less mouth to feed.

James.note: Leader must decide the ox's fate. No good option exists.

Mary.note: She has grown quieter as the journey wears on. The deaths of Thomas, Sarah, and William weigh on her constantly.

low.journal_entry: September 10th, 1848. We passed Chimney Rock in the distance this morning—a sight that lifted our spirits briefly. But one of the oxen that drank from that cursed alkali pool grows weaker by the hour. The canyon closes in around us now, walls of red rock rising like a trap. James sits with the animal tonight, checking its breathing. I counted our remaining food: fifteen pounds. At this rate, we have perhaps a week's worth if we stay on meager rations. Three good oxen pulling two wagons through the Snake River canyons. It was supposed to be four. Thomas used to tell jokes at the evening fire. I cannot remember what any of them were anymore.

low.title: A Shoshone Hunting Party Approaches
low.description: As your oxen pick through the muddy forest trail, you spot three Shoshone hunters on horseback emerging from the rain-dampened trees ahead. They carry bows and fresh elk meat tied to their saddles. They seem cautious but not immediately hostile. One of them gestures toward your wagon, clearly curious about your goods. Your party is low on food, and fresh meat would be a significant help before the mountain passes. However, your ammunition is limited, and tensions with Native peoples have been high along the trail this season.

choices.label: Attempt to trade: offer cloth and ammunition for fresh meat
Stop and offer only money—preserve ammunition and goods
Politely decline and keep moving—avoid any interaction

personality_effects.James: Leader's judgment tested in the moment.
personality_effects.Mary: Values practical survival and cooperation.
personality_effects.Thomas: Nervous about encounters; prefers caution.

low.journal_entry: October 1st. Rain has turned the forest path to sucking mud. Saw three Shoshone hunters this afternoon with fresh kill. Our meager rations grow thinner each day, and snow is coming to those mountain passes ahead. We have little time and less food. The men were watchful but made no threatening move. A choice had to be made.

low.title: The Axle Splinters
low.description: During a creek crossing in heavy rain, the lead wagon's axle cracks with a sharp snap. The wood is compromised—likely from the earlier panic when thunder spooked the oxen. You can hear the strain in the remaining wheels. The wagon still moves, but it won't last more than fifty miles in this condition. You have one spare axle among your parts, but installing it means half a day lost to repairs in the cold, wet forest. Alternatively, you could redistribute the load to the other wagons, lighten this one drastically, and push forward, hoping the axle holds until you reach a trading post or settlement where repairs are more feasible.

choices.label: Stop and replace the axle. Better safe than stranded.
Redistribute cargo to the other wagons and press on carefully.

personality_effects.James: Faces a command decision under pressure; his leadership will be judged by the outcome.
personality_effects.Mary: Worries about the oxen either way—they're working harder than ever in this mud.

low.journal_entry: October 10th — The rain won't stop. This morning, as we forded the creek near the timber stand, I heard the axle crack like a gunshot. The wagon settled wrong, and my heart sank. Three hundred miles left, maybe more, and now this. The spare part in our stores could fix it, but that costs us time and warmth we can't afford to waste. Mary thinks we should stop; I can see it in her face. But every hour we sit still is an hour the cold settles deeper into our bones. The meat from Thomas's deer is keeping us fed, at least. Small mercies.

low.title: The Shoshone Trade
low.description: On the morning of April 20th, your party breaks camp to find a small group of Shoshone—perhaps a dozen—waiting peacefully at the trail's edge. An older man steps forward, gesturing to four strong oxen they've brought with them. Through pantomime and the few words your party recognizes, you understand: they offer two oxen in exchange for ammunition, or all four oxen for ammunition plus several pounds of coffee and sugar. Your own oxen are holding steady, but the trail ahead is brutal, and spare animals are insurance against death. However, your ammunition is finite, and the Shoshone have clearly traveled far to make this offer.

choices.label: Trade 40 rounds of ammunition and 15 pounds of food for 2 oxen.
Trade 80 rounds of ammunition, 20 pounds of food, and 50 cents for all 4 oxen.
Politely decline and continue without trading. The Shoshone depart peacefully.

personality_effects.James: Pragmatic. Recognizes the oxen's value but worries about ammunition reserves.
personality_effects.Mary: Cautious. Dislikes parting with food stores but sees the security in extra animals.
personality_effects.Thomas: Ambitious. Eager to trade heavily—more oxen means faster pace and less risk.
personality_effects.Sarah: Idealistic. Respects the Shoshone's fairness and wants to build goodwill.
personality_effects.William: Conservative. Suspicious of any trade that significantly reduces ammunition.

low.journal_entry: April 20th, 1848. A small band of Shoshone appeared this morning with oxen to trade. They seemed peaceful and purposeful—not raiding, but conducting commerce. We debated whether to risk ammunition and provisions for animals we might never need, or to keep our stores intact and risk running short later. The choice revealed much about each person's hopes and fears for the road ahead.

low.title: The Pawnee Delegation
low.description: As your wagons crest a low rise on the prairie, you spot a party of approximately fifteen Pawnee riders approaching from the north. They move at a measured pace, not charging. Your scout estimates they're hunters, not a war party. They gesture for parley. Your oxen are strong, your ammunition adequate, but you're in open country with no natural defensive position.

choices.label: Halt the wagons and attempt trade. Offer tobacco and a few pounds of flour in exchange for information about the trail ahead and water sources.

consequences.notes: The Pawnee accept trade peacefully. They warn of swollen creeks two days ahead and recommend a northerly detour. One of your party members grows ill from stress—likely minor, but watch for fever. Morale increases from successful negotiation and useful intelligence.

choices.label: Circle the wagons immediately and prepare for defense. Load rifles. Do not engage.

consequences.notes: The Pawnee interpret the defensive posture as hostility and ride away without incident, but your panicked oxen bolt briefly—one is lost to a twisted leg before you recover control. The incident shakes confidence in leadership and wastes hours. No actual combat occurs.

choices.label: Continue forward at steady pace as if unconcerned. Acknowledge the riders with a wave, but do not stop. Trust in speed and numbers.

consequences.notes: The Pawnee match your pace briefly, observe, then veer away without interaction. You gain ground, but uncertainty lingers—you lost an opportunity for intelligence. Some members question whether you were foolish or wise to avoid engagement. William respects the decisiveness; Mary and Sarah worry about what you missed.

personality_effects.context: Responses depend on each member's temperament. Thomas (steady, practical) and James (leadership-focused) respond well to trade. Mary and Sarah (cautious) fear combat. William (bold) prefers forward momentum.

low.journal_entry: 28th April, 1848 — Prairie of the Platte Region. This morning we encountered a Pawnee hunting party. The decision we made here will shape how word of our passage spreads across these plains. The prairies offer no hiding place; reputation becomes currency when you are far from law and settlement.

low.title: The Stampede
low.description: Late afternoon. William spots a dust cloud to the north—a buffalo herd, hundreds strong, moving south across your path. The animals are still two miles off, but the ground trembles. Your oxen grow restless. The herd will cross the trail within the hour. You must decide: push the wagons hard to cross ahead of the stampede, circle back and camp until the animals pass, or hold position and hope the herd veers around you.

choices.label: Race ahead to cross before the herd arrives

consequences.description: The wagons lurch forward. Drivers crack whips. One ox stumbles on uneven ground and goes lame—you'll have to shoot it tonight. The remaining animals are pushed hard. You cross the trail's low point with perhaps thirty minutes to spare. The herd's thunder fades behind you by sunset. Close. Too close. But you made it, and the party feels the surge of narrowly dodging disaster.

choices.label: Circle back and camp in a protected ravine until the herd passes

consequences.description: You turn the wagons around and find a shallow ravine about a mile back. The herd arrives near sunset—thousands of animals thundering past your position all through the night. The noise is deafening. No one sleeps well. But the wagons and oxen are safe, and the animals eventually move on. By morning, the ground is torn up and the air thick with dust. The delay costs you a full day, and tempers are strained.

choices.label: Hold position and trust the herd will split around you

consequences.description: The herd does not split. The leading animals see the wagons and swerve, but the mass behind pushes forward. William and Thomas fire warning shots to turn the animals' path. Two oxen are trampled and killed before the herd surges past in a chaos of dust and noise. A buffalo bull clips the side of one wagon, cracking a wheel spoke. Everyone is shaken. Sarah is thrown to the ground during the panic and struck in the ribs. She's breathing, but in pain. The oxen and ammunition spent, morale shattered—you've escaped the worst outcome, but barely.

personality_effects.William: William's quick spotting of the herd gains him respect, though his bold nature would favor the race-ahead option.
personality_effects.Sarah: Sarah's low health makes any hard decision risky for her survival.

low.journal_entry: May 10th. The buffalo came. We saw them from miles off—a river of brown backs flowing across the plains. The earth shook. We had to choose: forward, backward, or pray. The weight of that choice sat on all our shoulders. Whatever we decided, none of us will forget the sound of ten thousand hooves.

low.title: The Abandoned Supply Cache
low.description: While navigating a rocky descent into a high valley, Thomas spots the remains of a canvas-wrapped bundle wedged between boulders—clearly left by an earlier emigrant party. The package is weathered but intact. Inside: approximately 30 pounds of hardtack, a tin of preserved beef, a wool blanket, and a small medical kit containing what appears to be laudanum and bandages. The discovery raises an immediate question: these supplies were deliberately cached, likely by travelers who planned to retrieve them on the return journey. Taking them means benefiting from another party's emergency reserve. Leaving them means passing up critical provisions as you climb toward the highest passes.

choices.label: Take the supplies. They're here for the taking, and the mountain passes ahead are dangerous.

consequences.journal_entry: Found an abandoned cache today—hardtack, beef, and medicine. James disapproved of taking what wasn't ours, but we need every advantage for the passes ahead. The others understood necessity.

choices.label: Leave the supplies untouched. Mark the location so the owners can retrieve them on the return.

consequences.journal_entry: Passed by a supply cache today without touching it. James insisted it was the right thing—leaving it for its owners to find. Thomas was angry about it. Our meager rations suddenly feel thinner in these mountains.

choices.label: Take only the medicine and leave the rest. It's what we need most, and the rest might help those who cached it.

consequences.journal_entry: Found supplies today but took only the medicine—enough to patch up Sarah and the others if fever or infection sets in. A compromise that sat uneasily with everyone, but perhaps fairly.

personality_effects.James: Values honesty and fairness; taking cached supplies troubles him
personality_effects.Mary: Practical; understands the necessity of supplies but respects James's judgment
personality_effects.Thomas: Growing frustrated with hardship; wants every advantage
personality_effects.Sarah: Weakened; concerned about reaching the passes safely

low.journal_entry: July 20th — High in the mountains now. Found an old cache today. The question of what to do with another party's supplies divided us. The peaks above are still capped with snow.

low.title: Mountain Fever Spreads
low.description: Mary's condition worsens through the night. By morning, Thomas complains of chills and a persistent cough. The high altitude—you're now above 7,000 feet—seems to be working against you. James notices that two of the oxen are moving slower than yesterday, their breath labored in the thin air. The party is exhausted. You have three choices: push ahead at steady pace to reach lower elevation within two days, hold here for three days to let the sick recover, or send ahead a fast scout to locate water and better grazing while the main party rests.

choices.label: Push ahead at steady pace despite illness. Lower elevation will help.

consequences.note: Mary's fever peaks on the second day. Thomas develops full mountain fever. Both survive, but morale suffers as party members feel pushed too hard. You make good distance downslope.

choices.label: Make camp here for three days. Rest, let bodies acclimate.

consequences.note: One ox dies from the altitude stress despite rest. Mary and Thomas improve measurably. James grows anxious about delays and dwindling supplies. Weather holds; no storms.

choices.label: Send James and one other ahead with minimal supplies to scout. Main party rests.

consequences.note: Party splits leadership. Those left behind worry about abandonment. James returns after two days with news of a valley with fresh water two miles downslope—good grazing there too. But Mary interprets the separation as James prioritizing speed over her welfare.

personality_effects.James: The leader feels the weight of decision. Two members dead already; two more falling sick. His earlier confidence has eroded.
personality_effects.Mary: Feverish and uncertain. Every choice that doesn't prioritize her recovery feels like betrayal.
personality_effects.Thomas: Previously steady, now frightened. He's watched Sarah and William die. Mountain fever is terrifying.

low.journal_entry: August 15, 1848 — Rocky Mountains, near 7,200 feet elevation. Mary is burning with fever. Thomas now complains of the same symptoms. The altitude is merciless. We have lost four people—Sarah to cholera, William to dysentery—and now the mountains test what remains of us. The oxen suffer as much as we do. Every choice feels like gambling with lives. We are 1,150 miles from Independence. California is still 800 miles ahead. I do not know if we have the strength.

low.title: The Shoshone Camp
low.description: As your party crests a ridge above a narrow canyon, you spot a Shoshone encampment perhaps half a mile distant—perhaps thirty lodges arranged near a spring. Smoke rises from cooking fires. You count riders moving between the structures. Your scouts report the camp appears peaceful, focused on preparing for winter. Your food stores are critically low at 25 pounds, enough for maybe four more days at meager rations. The Shoshone have fresh game and dried provisions. However, approaching an armed camp of strangers is always a calculated risk. Your party is exhausted, and two members are dead. James considers three paths: attempt trade for food, bypass the camp entirely and press forward to find your own water and game, or send a small party ahead under a flag to negotiate.

choices.label: Approach the Shoshone camp under a flag of truce to trade for food.

consequences.description: The Shoshone are cautious but willing to trade. You exchange ammunition and cash for dried elk meat and pemmican. The exchange takes most of the day, but morale improves knowing food is secured. A Shoshone elder shares information about the terrain ahead—water and grass become scarcer within the week.

choices.label: Bypass the camp. Push forward hard to reach the next water source shown on your map.

consequences.description: Your party moves fast but the strain is severe. The desert offers no mercy—dust, cold nights, and hunger exact a toll. One ox collapses from exhaustion and must be left behind. Thomas develops signs of dysentery; you use precious medicine. You do reach water, barely, but the cost is high. Morale suffers from the forced march and visible suffering.

choices.label: Send James and one other member to the Shoshone camp to scout intentions before committing the whole party.

consequences.description: James and Mary ride ahead while the rest wait—a tense few hours. The Shoshone greet them neutrally but do not invite trade. They indicate the camp is moving east within two days and suggest the emigrants move on. James returns with no goods but better intelligence: the next reliable water is 35 miles north, difficult terrain. The delay and uncertainty weigh on the party.

personality_effects.James: Leader must decide—his choice defines the party's immediate survival and long-term trust with indigenous peoples.
personality_effects.Mary: Already fragile morale; starvation and forced marches will test her endurance further.
personality_effects.Thomas: Youngest and most vulnerable to illness; his health is a concern in harsh conditions.

low.journal_entry: September 1st, 1848. Found a Shoshone camp in the canyon lands. Food nearly gone. The desert is becoming a furnace by day and an ice house by night. We have lost Sarah and William to this merciless country. Every decision now is a choice between different kinds of suffering. We must eat within days or face ruin.

low.title: Ox Goes Lame
low.description: As your wagon rounds a sharp bend in the canyon, one of your oxen suddenly pulls hard to the side, favoring its rear left leg. Upon inspection, you find a deep gash—likely from a jagged rock hidden in the dust. The wound isn't bleeding profusely, but the animal cannot bear weight properly. You have four oxen; losing one here means your wagon will be significantly slower, and your remaining animals will work harder.

choices.label: Use your medicine to treat the wound and rest the animal for a day.
Tend the wound as best you can with cloth and water, continue moving.
Put the animal down. It's slowing you down and resources are scarce.

James.description: James must decide the fate of the animal and live with the consequences.

Mary.description: Mary watches her husband's choice with quiet concern.

low.journal_entry: September 10th, 1848. A canyon narrows around us, beautiful and unforgiving. One of our oxen came up lame this afternoon—a sharp stone cut deep. We've three members in the ground already. Every decision now feels like choosing who survives and who doesn't. Even the animals are running on borrowed time.

low.title: A Stranded Emigrant Family
low.description: As your wagon wheels sink deeper into the mud, you spot a small canvas shelter ahead—poorly constructed, listing to one side. Outside, a man in a tattered coat waves desperately. Drawing closer, you see a woman and two children huddled near a dying fire. The man explains they abandoned their wagon three days ago when their oxen gave out. They've rationed their remaining hardtack and have almost nothing left. The woman coughs badly—sounds like lung sickness. They're asking if you can spare food or take them with you, at least as far as the next settlement. Your own supplies are lean, and taking additional passengers will strain your oxen further.

choices.label: Share what food you can spare and continue on—they'll have to catch up or find their own way.
Offer the man one ox and basic directions to the nearest settlement; you cannot afford to slow your pace further.
Take the family into your wagons despite the risk. Use medicine on the sick woman and ration carefully.

low.journal_entry: October 1st, 1848. The rain has turned everything to mud. We came upon a family who lost their oxen three days back—the woman is genuinely sick, the children thin as rails. Whatever we decide will cost us something. The trail gives you few choices that don't hurt.

low.title: Ox Goes Lame
low.description: Dawn breaks cold and gray over the forest camp. As you prepare to move out, Mary notices one of the oxen favoring its front left leg badly. Upon closer inspection, you find a deep cut on the hoof—likely from sharp rocks hidden in yesterday's mud. The animal can barely put weight on it. Without all three oxen, your heavily laden wagon will be nearly impossible to manage on these forest roads. You have enough medical supplies for one treatment, but it's uncertain whether it will work on an animal rather than a person. The ox might recover in a few days of rest, or the infection could worsen. You cannot afford to lose another animal.

choices.label: Use the medicine on the ox's hoof and rest here two days. Pray it takes.
Push on slowly with two oxen. Redistribute weight, abandon non-essentials.
Shoot the lame ox for meat. Two oxen will move faster unencumbered.

personality_effects.James: Hardened by loss, but growing weary of difficult choices
personality_effects.Mary: Compassion for the animals wavering under strain

low.journal_entry: October 10th — We are three now, truly three. Thomas, Sarah, William all in the ground behind us. This morning one of our oxen went lame in the mud. We cannot spare it, yet we cannot move fast enough with it either. Every choice leads somewhere darker than the last. The rain falls without mercy, and the forest is closing in. We are still 200 miles from the valley, and I wonder if we shall see it at all.

low.title: The Sluggish Ox and a Stranger's Offer
low.description: One of your oxen drank from an alkali pool two days back and has grown listless, eating little. This morning, a Pawnee trader approaches your camp on horseback, leading a healthy ox. He speaks passable English and offers a trade: your sluggish animal plus 50 cents for his sound beast. He seems genuine, but your oxen are already stretched thin, and you cannot afford to lose leverage in negotiation. Behind him, storm clouds gather on the western horizon.

choices.label: Accept the trade. A healthy ox is worth the gamble.

consequences.note: The Pawnee trader departs satisfied. Your new ox is strong and pulls well. The party's confidence in James's judgment improves slightly.

choices.label: Decline and treat the sluggish ox yourself with medicine and rest.

consequences.note: The trader rides off without comment. You lose a day's travel, but the ox recovers somewhat. Medicine is precious, and the delay stings, but you kept your coin.

choices.label: Counteroffer: 25 cents and your sluggish ox for his healthy one.

consequences.note: The trader takes offense at what he sees as an insult. He leaves abruptly. The party senses tension and worry—you may have missed a fair deal. The sluggish ox continues to slow your pace.

personality_effects.James: Shows leadership under pressure, though the weight of supply decisions is visible.
personality_effects.Mary: Watches the exchange carefully; she knows the value of draft animals better than most.
personality_effects.Thomas: Quiet concern. He worries the sluggish ox signals worse problems ahead.
personality_effects.Sarah: Optimistic that whatever is chosen, the party will adapt and push forward.
personality_effects.William: Frustrated by delay; he wants to make distance while the weather holds.

low.journal_entry: April 20th — Chimney Rock stands plain on the horizon now, a landmark that means we are truly upon the trail proper. But fortune tests us: one ox, poisoned by alkali, drags behind. A Pawnee came to trade this morning, offering sound flesh for weak. We chose our path, and the weight of it settles on us all as dark clouds mass to the west. The plains offer no comfort to those who hesitate.

low.title: The Buffalo Herd
low.description: In the distance, a dark mass moves across the prairie—a buffalo herd, hundreds strong, stretching across the horizon. Your scouts report they're moving south, roughly perpendicular to your path. This is both opportunity and danger. Buffalo meat could significantly improve your stores, but a stampede could cost you oxen, supplies, or lives. Meanwhile, fresh wagon ruts nearby suggest another emigrant party passed this way recently, perhaps six hours ahead.

choices.label: Hunt the herd. Fresh meat is crucial for the mountain passes ahead.

consequences.description: The hunt is successful but dangerous. William takes a minor goring to his leg during the chase—nothing life-threatening, but it slows him. You butcher three buffalo and preserve roughly 85 pounds of meat. The effort costs an entire day, and the close call with the animals leaves everyone unsettled.

choices.label: Avoid the herd and press on. The party ahead mustn't gain too much distance.

consequences.description: You push the oxen hard to maintain pace with the distant wagon party, moving well into the evening. The herd passes north without incident. You cover good ground, but the urgency and the missed opportunity weigh on morale. By dark, you spot the other party's campfire ahead—they're close now.

choices.label: Detour west to shadow the herd at a distance, then cross their path after they pass.

consequences.description: You add ten miles to your route but secure a safer position. You manage to shoot two buffalo from a distance and butcher them quickly. The detour costs time and a little money on repair to one wagon's wheel, but nobody is injured. You cross the herd's path cleanly once they've moved on.

William.hunts: He's eager to prove himself on the hunt; choosing to hunt increases his morale by an additional 2 points. Choosing to avoid it disappoints him slightly (−1 morale).

Thomas.cautious: The weak herd encounter worries him. Any choice involving direct danger to the party reduces his morale less (he expects hardship) but direct hunting worsens his health concern (−3 additional).

James.leader_burden: As leader, James bears the weight of whatever choice is made. If the herd hunt succeeds, his morale rises (+2). If avoided, he questions the decision (−2 morale).

low.journal_entry: May 1st, 1848. The plain alive with thunder-dark clouds and a river of beasts. We spotted a buffalo herd this morning moving across our path—a sight that fills a man with both hunger and fear. The party that passed ahead of us has kept us motivated, though I confess the warning about mountain snow troubles my sleep. We must choose between safety and provisions. The oxen are steady, the rations holding, but winter doesn't wait for caution.

low.title: Fever in the Camp
low.description: By evening, Sarah complains of chills despite the oppressive heat. Her forehead burns to the touch.
