The mosquitoes have left welts across her arms and neck—some already showing signs of infection. Mary suspects marsh fever, common along these river valleys. The nearest trading post is still four days away. Your medical supplies are limited, and pushing hard might worsen her condition. The party looks to you for a decision.

choices.label: Rest here two days. Boil water, make poultices from willow bark, keep Sarah comfortable.
Push forward at a steady pace. The motion and fresh air might help. Stop only at dusk to minimize mosquito exposure.
Send James and William ahead on horseback to the next trading post for quinine and proper medicine. The rest wait here.

personality_effects.James: leader, methodical, cautious about wasting supplies
personality_effects.Mary: pragmatic, experienced with illness, wants to help Sarah
personality_effects.Thomas: anxious about delays, worries about schedule
personality_effects.Sarah: currently incapacitated; fever worsens morale generally
personality_effects.William: impatient, wants to make distance, least concerned with individual hardship

low.journal_entry: June 1st, 1848. The mosquitoes here are biblical. Sarah is taken ill—fever and infection from their bites. I fear marsh sickness. We are two weeks from Fort Laramie and supplies of proper medicine are gone. The river that gave us water and game now seems to mock us. Every choice feels wrong. We cannot afford to lose Sarah, but we also cannot afford to lose time. The others watch me, waiting.

low.title: A Pawnee Trading Party Appears
low.description: As your party prepares camp near a bend in the river, you spot riders approaching from the north—a group of roughly a dozen Pawnee warriors on horseback, moving deliberately but not aggressively. They carry trade goods: furs, dried meat, and what appears to be fresh vegetables. One rider holds up his hands in a peaceful gesture. Your oxen are nervous. Mary's fever has worsened since this morning, and you have limited medicine. The Pawnee gesture toward your camp, then toward their goods.

choices.label: Invite them to trade. Mary needs food that won't strain her digestion, and fresh provisions would strengthen the party.
Politely decline and break camp immediately. You're wary of any contact, and you want to push toward Fort Laramie.
Send James and Thomas to meet them halfway, armed but non-threatening. Keep the wagons ready to move if needed.

low.journal_entry: June 15th, 1848. The Pawnee appeared near sundown, peaceful but unexpected. Their timing troubles me—we are in their lands, after all, moving westward with what little we have. Mary worsened through the day; the heat and her sickness feed each other. William is gone these three days now, and his absence lightens the oxen's load but darkens our spirits. A decision weighs on us: trust, or caution. Both carry risk.

low.title: A Shoshone Encounter at the Pass
low.description: As your wagons climb toward South Pass, you spot a small Shoshone hunting party—perhaps a dozen warriors on horseback—descending the ridge ahead. They move with purpose but not aggression. One rider breaks from the group and approaches under a raised hand. Your scout estimates they're after mountain sheep in these peaks. Your supplies are meager, and the pass ahead is still treacherous with snow. The Shoshone are known to trade, but they're also protective of their hunting grounds.

choices.label: Offer to trade: bacon and ammunition for fresh game and information about the pass ahead.

consequences.notes: The Shoshone share knowledge of a safer route through the pass and provide elk meat. Mary's fever improves with fresh nutrition. Trust is established, reducing future conflict risk.

choices.label: Maintain distance. Move your wagons forward slowly and steadily, showing no threat but no interest in contact.

consequences.notes: The hunters watch but do not interfere. You make good distance but miss the opportunity for supplies and local guidance. Thomas grows anxious; Mary's condition stabilizes but doesn't improve.

choices.label: Send your two strongest men to parley directly while the wagons wait. Negotiate openly for supplies.

consequences.notes: Extended negotiation yields mountain sheep and detailed pass information, but costs time and resources. James and Thomas both return unharmed, though the parley was tense at moments. Sarah becomes irritated at the delay; confidence in James's leadership wavers slightly.

low.journal_entry: 20 July, 1848 — South Pass Region. Spotted Shoshone hunters on the ridge this morning. Mountain peaks still held snow in their shadowed hollows despite the warm valley below. We are now 914 miles from our departure, and the high country lies between us and Oregon. Every choice has weight up here. Mary's fever persists. The trail grows harder still.

low.title: Mountain Pass Sickness
low.description: Thomas wakes with a high fever and severe cough. Mary suspects mountain fever—common at this altitude—though it could worsen into pneumonia. The pass ahead is narrow and treacherous; descending would add five days but offers lower altitude and warmer camps. Staying at this elevation and pushing through means reaching Fort Laramie three days sooner, but Thomas is too weak to walk and will need to ride in the wagon, slowing your pace regardless. You have three doses of medicine remaining.

choices.label: Descend immediately to lower altitude; abandon the direct pass route

personality_effects.Thomas: grateful, tired
personality_effects.James: responsible

choices.label: Use medicine and continue through the pass at slower pace; Thomas rides in wagon

personality_effects.Thomas: anxious, strained
personality_effects.Mary: worried
personality_effects.James: uncertain

choices.label: Stay camped here three days; let Thomas recover before moving; use minimal medicine

personality_effects.Thomas: recovering
personality_effects.James: patient
personality_effects.Mary: cautious

personality_effects.James: leadership tested; weight of two dead already behind him
personality_effects.Mary: nursing instinct; limited supplies weigh heavily
personality_effects.Thomas: vulnerable; survival uncertain

low.journal_entry: August 1st, 1848. Thomas is burning with fever this morning. The altitude sits heavy on all our chests, but his is worse—rattling like a wagon wheel in need of grease. Mary has nursed consumption before in her sister; she recognizes the signs. We are 1,000 miles from Independence, perhaps 200 from Fort Laramie. William lies three days' travel behind us. I cannot lose Thomas too. The mountains offer no mercy, and our medicine stores are thin as summer ice. Every choice narrows our path forward.

low.title: The Oxen Won't Drink
low.description: You've reached a brackish water hole—the only source for miles in this high desert country. Your oxen circle the pool nervously, sniffing but refusing to drink. The water smells of minerals and something faintly sulfurous. Your animals are already thin from weeks of sparse grazing. Without water, they won't make it to the next reliable source, which scouts estimate is three days' hard travel ahead. You have perhaps a day before dehydration begins killing them.

choices.label: Force the oxen to drink. Mix the bad water with what clean water remains in your barrels to make it more palatable.

consequences.notes: One ox dies within hours despite your efforts. Two party members develop mild dysentery from the contaminated water. The remaining oxen drink reluctantly but survive. You lose time caring for the sick.

choices.label: Push on immediately, driving hard for eighteen hours without water to reach better sources. Risk everything on speed.

consequences.notes: Two oxen collapse from exhaustion and must be abandoned. The forced march breaks Thomas's resolve—he speaks openly of turning back. Mary tends to blistered feet and dehydration sickness in the group. You make good distance but at severe cost.

choices.label: Camp here for a full day. Rest the oxen, ration the clean water carefully, and search systematically for better water sources upstream or in nearby rock formations.

consequences.notes: The oxen recover somewhat with rest. James and Thomas find a seep in the rocks that provides fresher water—not perfect, but drinkable. The group's spirits lift slightly. You lose a full day but preserve your animals and health.

personality_effects.James: The oxen are your responsibility as leader. Each choice carries weight.
personality_effects.Mary: She worries constantly about the animals—she's grown attached to them over the long journey.
personality_effects.Thomas: Already flagging. The desert wears on him more than the others. He notices every sacrifice.

low.journal_entry: September 1st, 1848 — The water here is no good. Alkali, the men call it. The oxen know it before we do. We've lost two people already—Sarah and William—and I won't lose the animals that might carry us to Oregon if I can help it. Every decision now feels like gambling with lives. The nights grow colder. Winter is coming, and we're still in the desert.

low.title: The Shoshone Encounter
low.description: As your party navigates a narrow canyon section of the Snake River region, you spot three Shoshone hunters on horseback examining something near the trail ahead. They haven't seen you yet. Your oxen are tired, and your provisions are critically low. The hunters appear to be tracking game—possibly elk or deer—which they've recently killed. This could be an opportunity, but it requires careful judgment. The Shoshone in this region have had varied encounters with emigrant parties, some friendly, others hostile.

choices.label: Approach openly and attempt to trade for fresh meat

consequences.narrative: You wave from a distance and call out peacefully. The hunters are cautious but not hostile. After tense negotiations conducted in a mix of gesture and broken Chinook jargon, they agree to trade a substantial portion of fresh elk meat for ammunition and coins. The transaction takes two hours, but your food situation improves considerably, and the hunters depart without incident. Your party feels relief at the exchange.

choices.label: Quietly lead your party around them using a side ravine

consequences.narrative: You attempt to circle around the hunters by leading your oxen down a steep, rocky side ravine. One ox stumbles and breaks its leg; you must shoot it to end its suffering. The detour is exhausting and demoralizing, consuming an entire day. You lose ground, and the party grows anxious about your leadership. By nightfall, you rejoin the trail several miles behind where you started, with four oxen instead of five and flagging spirits.

choices.label: Wait in the canyon until the hunters leave, then continue after nightfall

consequences.narrative: You order the party to remain hidden in a narrow side canyon. Hours pass. The cold settles in as dusk approaches, and no fires can be lit for fear of detection. Your supplies dwindle as people eat quietly from meager rations to stay warm. The Shoshone eventually move on at sunset. You resume travel in darkness, traveling slowly and carefully by starlight, covering only a mile. The psychological cost of fear and constraint weighs on everyone.

personality_effects.description: James values pragmatism; he approves of trade or cautious maneuvering but dislikes fearful inaction. Mary is worn down by hardship; she hopes for moments of peaceful interaction but fears violence.

low.journal_entry: September 10th — Canyon of the Snake. We encountered Shoshone hunters today in the narrows. The question of how to proceed tested us all. Our stores are insufficient for the mountain passes ahead, and winter creeps closer with each cold night. The warning from those eastbound travelers echoes in my mind. We must reach the Willamette Valley before the snow closes the gates.

low.title: The Shoshone Meet
low.description: As your party camps near the Snake River, a band of Shoshone hunters—perhaps eight or nine riders—approaches from the northeast. They are well-mounted and armed with rifles and bows. Their leader, a man of middle years with a scarred jaw, raises his hand in a peaceful gesture. Through gestures and broken English from one younger rider, they convey that they have been tracking the same elk herds your party has hunted. The Shoshone are friendly but direct: they propose trade—your remaining ammunition in exchange for fresh elk meat, tanned hides, and information about water sources ahead. The younger rider mentions that the trail ahead grows harder, and the Shoshone have traveled it many times. However, your ammunition is already dangerously low, and Mary's dysentery worsens daily. You must decide quickly.

choices.label: Trade 40 rounds of ammunition for elk meat, hides, and route guidance.

consequences.description: The Shoshone prove reliable traders. You gain nearly 35 pounds of freshly killed elk—enough to ease rations for nearly two weeks. The hides, when dried, patch two worn blankets. The younger rider draws maps in the dust, showing where reliable springs lie ahead and where the desert grows harshest. Your party's spirits lift knowing danger and hardship are shared knowledge, not unknown horrors. Mary's strength slowly returns with regular meat.

choices.label: Politely refuse and continue alone; ammunition is too precious.

consequences.description: The Shoshone leader nods without anger and departs. But your caution proves costly. Two days later, your party misses a spring marked on no map you possess. You backtrack 15 miles, exhausted and thirsty. Mary's dysentery worsens from drinking alkaline water from a poisoned source. Morale sinks as James realizes his refusal may have endangered the entire party. The remaining oxen are thin and tired.

choices.label: Trade 20 rounds and offer your remaining tanned hide clothing as well.

consequences.description: The Shoshone are pleased by the extra generosity. They provide 20 pounds of elk meat and dried pemmican, plus 50 cents in trade goods (beads and a small steel knife of Shoshone craftsmanship). One hunter gives James explicit directions in halting English. You lose your warmest clothing to the desert cold, but gain trust. The modest supplies help stabilize Mary's condition slightly, though recovery remains uncertain.

personality_effects.James: Faces a leader's genuine dilemma: security through isolation versus survival through cooperation. His confidence wavers.
personality_effects.Mary: Weakened by dysentery, any food gains matter to her survival odds. She watches James's decision carefully.

low.journal_entry: September 15th, 1848 — Snake River country. Shoshone hunters found us today, honest traders by all appearances. They know this land as we never will. I must choose between ammunition—that small guarantee of safety—and the knowledge and food that might see us through. Three of our party are already gone. Mary grows no stronger. The desert respects no hesitation.

low.title: Oxen Mired in Forest Mud
low.description: The autumn rains have turned the forest floor into a treacherous mire. One of your three oxen has sunk axle-deep in mud near a creek crossing. The animal is panicking, thrashing uselessly. You have perhaps two hours before nightfall makes the situation worse. Your options: attempt to free it with rope and manual labor, or make the difficult choice to leave it behind and redistribute its load.

choices.label: Spend the afternoon attempting to free the oxen using rope and combined effort.
Slaughter the trapped oxen for meat and reduce your burden. Salvage what you can.
Abandon the oxen and push forward with two animals. Accept the loss.

low.journal_entry: October 1st — The rains have made the forest impassable in places. We lost an oxen to the mud near a creek. Every mile now feels purchased with sweat and difficult choices. The Willamette Valley cannot be far, but the animals are weakening and tempers grow short. Sarah and William are gone these many weeks now, and their absence is felt keenly when hardship strikes.

low.title: The Muddy Trail Narrows
low.description: The rain has turned the forest path into a treacherous ribbon of mud. One of your oxen has gone lame—perhaps from the cold, perhaps from a stone bruise. Your remaining three animals are exhausted. Ahead, the trail splits. The left fork climbs higher into denser forest, likely drier but steep. The right fork descends toward what sounds like another river valley, muddy but flatter. You have perhaps two hours of decent light remaining. Neither choice is safe.

choices.label: Take the higher path through the forest. It will be slower, but the elevation might offer better drainage.

consequences.narrative: The steep climb is brutal. Your oxen slip repeatedly in the mud despite the higher ground. By nightfall, one ox collapses from exhaustion and will not rise in the morning. You make a cold camp without adequate shelter, and both you and Mary suffer from the damp cold seeping into your bones. The trail ahead is barely visible through the fog.

choices.label: Risk the valley route. It's muddier, but you can move faster and reach the river crossing before dark.

consequences.narrative: The wagon lurches through the mud like a ship in heavy seas. Your clothes are soaked through. Two miles down, the right wheel sinks into a hidden rut, nearly overturning. You haul it out with great effort, but both you and Mary are shaken and exhausted. By the time you reach the river, visibility is nearly gone. Your clothes are filthy and wet. The river here is swift and deep—no crossing tonight.

choices.label: Make camp here and wait out the rain. Rest the animals and gather firewood while you can still see.

consequences.narrative: You find a relatively sheltered spot beneath tall firs and establish camp. The lame ox begins to recover with rest. You and Mary manage a proper fire and a warm meal, which lifts your spirits slightly. The rain continues through the night, but you are dry and the animals are secure. Morning may bring better visibility.

personality_effects.James: Your leadership weighs heavily. Three members dead, supplies dwindling, and now this test of judgment. You feel the weight of every decision.
personality_effects.Mary: The constant setbacks gnaw at you. You wonder if turning back at Fort Laramie would have been wiser. Still, you endure.

low.journal_entry: October 10th — Rain has dogged us for two days now. Found the lame ox this morning. The forest is beautiful but merciless. We are 1,700 miles from Independence. The trail ahead splits like a forked tongue. I have made mistakes before; I pray I do not make another now. Mary says little, but I see the doubt in her eyes. We must reach the Willamette Valley before winter closes the passes. Time is no longer our friend.

low.title: The Pawnee Trade Encounter
low.description: Late afternoon, a group of five Pawnee riders approaches from the north at an easy pace. They carry furs and appear peaceable. The lead rider signals for parley. Your scouts report the Pawnee have been reported as reliable traders along this stretch of the Platte, though you've also heard cautions from other wagon companies. The Pawnee are gesturing toward your oxen and supplies, then toward their furs and leather goods.

choices.label: Engage in trade. Exchange some grain and ammunition for furs and leather—good for winter clothing.

consequences.narrative: The trade took an hour. The Pawnee trader spoke some English, enough to agree on value. Your party now has sturdy leather goods for the colder months ahead, though you've depleted foodstores and ammunition. The riders departed peacefully as the sun lowered.

choices.label: Decline politely and move on. Your supplies are finite, and trading now risks shortages later.

consequences.narrative: You thank the Pawnee leader and explain your stores must be conserved. He nods without offense, and the group wheels back north. Mary mutters about missed opportunity for better winter gear. You press on another few miles before camp, making good time despite the awkwardness.

choices.label: Attempt to barter using only money, no supplies. Offer $40 for a selection of their furs and leather.

consequences.narrative: The Pawnee trader accepts the cents without hesitation—your coin is worth more to him than grain. You acquire two good hides suitable for patching and repair, keeping your food and ammunition intact. A practical outcome that pleases the pragmatists in your group.

personality_effects.James: Leader values security and self-sufficiency; excess trade may concern him.
personality_effects.Mary: Practical about supplies; concerned about winter provisions.
personality_effects.Thomas: Young and adventurous; more inclined toward trading and cultural exchange.
personality_effects.Sarah: Cautious; prefers to conserve what little stores remain.
personality_effects.William: Steady temperament; motivated by group morale and cohesion.

low.journal_entry: April 20th — We encountered a small party of Pawnee traders near the river bend today. They were armed but peaceful, seeking goods rather than conflict. Such meetings remind us we are not alone on this trail, and that survival may depend as much on fair dealing as on caution. The question of what to trade and what to keep weighs heavy—every pound of flour might be needed before we reach the Rockies.

low.title: The Injured Pawnee Scout
low.description: Late afternoon, as your oxen drink from a wide creek, a Pawnee scout emerges from the cottonwoods on horseback. His left arm hangs useless—the shoulder clearly dislocated, maybe worse. He speaks broken English and hand signs, gesturing to your wagons, then to his injury. His horse is exhausted. He's alone and far from his band. Mary, who has some medical knowledge, recognizes the severity: infection will set in within days if left untreated. Helping him means medicine, time, and risk of complications. Turning him away means leaving a skilled tracker to die of infection or predators.

choices.label: Treat his injury and provide food and rest for two days

personality_effects.Mary: grateful_vindicated

consequences.note: The scout's shoulder is reset with difficulty. Mary stays awake through one night managing infection risk. He leaves at dawn with jerky, stable but still in pain. The delay costs two days of travel but strengthens party cohesion.

choices.label: Give him food and water, but keep moving—you cannot spare the time

personality_effects.Mary: resentful_pragmatism_rejected

consequences.note: You provide jerky and fresh water. The scout nods, understanding. His chances of survival are poor. Mary is visibly disturbed and withdrawn for days. The rest of the party feels the weight of the decision, though James justifies it as necessity.

choices.label: Offer him a horse trade and directions to the nearest Pawnee encampment five miles north

personality_effects.Mary: practical_approval
personality_effects.James: resourceful

consequences.note: A middle path: you trade an extra ox for his knowledge that his band camps nearby. He can reach them in hours with a fresh horse. The sacrifice is real, but so is the relief of not abandoning him. His gratitude is visible before he rides north. The party feels they've done right without derailing the journey.

personality_effects.Mary: moral_weight
personality_effects.James: leadership_test

low.journal_entry: 28 April, 1848. We encountered a Pawnee scout today with a badly injured shoulder. His people are skilled horsemen and hunters—the difference between a safe passage and constant vigilance. Mary wanted to help. James had to decide what the wagon train could afford. Every choice carries weight on this trail. We are learning that survival and conscience do not always align.

low.title: Buffalo Hunt Opportunity
low.description: Early this morning, a scout spotted a substantial buffalo herd grazing less than two miles northwest of your camp. The animals are moving slowly and appear unbothered by human presence—a rare opportunity. Hunting could replenish your meat stores significantly, but it will cost time, ammunition, and carries the inherent risk of injury or failure. The herd shows no signs of moving on quickly, but weather is unpredictable this time of year.

choices.label: Organize a hunting party to pursue the buffalo

personality_effects.Thomas: Thrilled by the hunt; confidence boosted
personality_effects.William: Relieved to contribute meaningfully
personality_effects.Mary: Concerned about the delay, though glad for fresh meat

choices.label: Ration your current supplies and press onward

personality_effects.Thomas: Frustrated at the missed opportunity
personality_effects.Mary: Appreciates the steady pace while recovering from fever
personality_effects.William: Understands the logic but feels the monotony

choices.label: Send two hunters while the rest break camp and move ahead slowly

personality_effects.Thomas: Eager to hunt but anxious about splitting the group
personality_effects.William: Agrees to hunt but worries about catching up
personality_effects.Sarah: Uneasy traveling without full strength nearby

personality_effects.James: As leader, feels weight of the decision; concerned about Mary's recovery affecting judgment
personality_effects.Mary: Still weak from fever; prefers steady rest but doesn't want to hold others back
personality_effects.Thomas: Young and energetic; sees the buffalo as both food and adventure
personality_effects.Sarah: Practical; wants to maximize supplies without unnecessary risk
personality_effects.William: Steady and reliable; content with either choice if group stays unified

low.journal_entry: May 1st, 1848 — Buffalo visible two miles off. The herd is a gift we don't often see twice. Mary's fever broke last night, but she's weak still. We face a choice that will define the next stretch: secure provisions at the cost of time, or trust our stores and keep moving. The oxen are holding, the weather favors us for now. Whatever we choose, we choose together.

low.title: Buffalo Hunt Opportunity
low.description: Late afternoon, your scout reports a substantial buffalo herd grazing two miles north of the trail. The animals are moving slowly, and wind conditions favor an approach. Your party has ammunition and capable hunters, but pursuing game means a half-day detour. The herd could provide 200+ pounds of fresh meat—a significant boost to your dwindling supplies. However, buffalo are unpredictable, and a hunt carries real risk of injury or wasted ammunition. William's recent death weighs on everyone's minds; some may welcome the distraction, while others might view it as tempting fate.

choices.label: Organize a full hunting party. Send James, Thomas, and Mary with rifles.
Send only Thomas—the best shot—to take down one animal quickly.
Decline the hunt. Push on toward water and safer ground before nightfall.

personality_effects.James: Weighing hunger against caution. His leadership is tested.
personality_effects.Thomas: Eager to prove himself after the hardships. Hunting would restore confidence.
personality_effects.Mary: Practical. Sees meat as essential but worries about injuries.
personality_effects.Sarah: Still grieving. Hesitant about unnecessary risk.
personality_effects.William: Not applicable—deceased.

low.journal_entry: May 10th. Buffalo spotted two miles north. The herd moves slow and fat with spring grass. Our stores are lean—150 pounds won't last the week at filling rations. Thomas says he can drop one cleanly at distance. James is torn. We buried a man five days ago, and the women are still quiet at meals. A hunt might lift spirits, or it might invite more sorrow. The sky is clear now, but those storm clouds don't look far off. Every choice costs us time we can't spare.

low.title: The Pawnee Hunters
low.description: Late afternoon. Your wagons crest a rise overlooking a creek valley thick with cottonwoods. Below, a hunting party of roughly a dozen Pawnee riders has killed an elk and are butchering it. They notice your wagons immediately. The hunters are armed but relaxed—not positioned for conflict. Their horses are lean but sound. The creek ahead is your planned water source for tonight.

choices.label: Approach peacefully and trade for fresh meat.
Water at the creek but avoid direct contact. Travel quietly past them.
Detour south to avoid them entirely. Find water elsewhere.

low.journal_entry: June 25th, 1848. The bluffs roll endlessly westward. This morning, those eastbound travelers spoke truly of the mountains ahead—snow in summer is no fancy. We encountered Pawnee hunters at the creek at day's end. Armed men, but not hostile. Sarah held her rifle steady. The question of how we meet these people weighs heavier each day. William's death reminded us we are not as many as we were. James must decide.

low.title: Pawnee traders at the pass
low.description: As your wagons climb toward South Pass, you encounter a small group of Pawnee hunters—perhaps eight men with packhorses laden with furs and dried meat. Their leader, a man of middle years with a scarred cheek, approaches James with clear intent to trade. He points to your oxen, then to his meat stores. The gesture is friendly enough, but your animals are already thin from the climb, and you're three weeks from Fort Bridger at minimum. The Pawnee have fresh provisions—at least 80 pounds of dried buffalo and elk—which would ease the hunger that's been gnawing at your party. But parting with even one ox here, in the mountains, could mean disaster if an animal goes lame or injured before you reach the valley settlements.

choices.label: Trade one ox for the meat and a few furs. Lighter loads mean easier passage through the passes.
Offer trade goods—some cloth and ammunition—instead of livestock. Keep your oxen intact.
Decline politely and press on. Your supplies will suffice; trust the trail ahead.

personality_effects.description: Sarah's spirits often lift when the party shows practical courage. Thomas grows restless when provisions tighten further. Mary values good judgment; James respects both caution and decisive leadership.

low.journal_entry: July 20th — South Pass approach. Met Pawnee traders this morning, their packhorses heavy with game. The mountains here are beautiful and merciless. Snow still clings to the peaks above, and the air grows thin as we climb. Our oxen labor under the weight. Mosquitoes have finally released us, but hunger is a harder master. The Pawnee seemed honest enough in their dealing. Hard to know what choice serves us best when all roads from here lead through thin air and stone.

low.title: The Shoshone Trade
low.description: As your party navigates a narrow mountain pass in the late afternoon, you encounter a small group of Shoshone—perhaps eight or nine, including women and children. They have fresh elk meat and are willing to trade. Their leader indicates interest in your spare clothing and ammunition. The encounter is peaceful, their manner direct and businesslike. Mary shivers with fever beside the wagon. You have food, but it is running low, and fresh meat would sustain the party well. The Shoshone are patient but clearly will move on soon.

choices.label: Trade ammunition and one set of clothing for fresh elk meat (roughly 40 pounds)
Offer only money (50 cents) and one mending kit—preserve ammunition and clothes
Politely decline and move on—game trails are visible and your hunters can provide

personality_effects.James: Leader must weigh present need against future hardship.
personality_effects.Mary: Every calorie matters when fever burns through your body.
personality_effects.Thomas: Hunting takes time and skill; a sure trade is tempting.

low.journal_entry: August 15th, 1848. High in the mountains now, the air thin and cool. We met Shoshone traders this afternoon—honest folk, I judge. Mary grows worse with the mountain fever. Her appetite is poor, yet her body demands strength. We have food enough to reach Fort Hall, but 'enough' and 'well' are different propositions. The Shoshone offered meat, fresh-killed. It would ease the hunger and lift spirits. But ammunition is hard currency in this wilderness, and spare clothes harder still.

low.title: The Shoshone Encampment
low.description: As your party crests a ridge overlooking a broad valley, you spot smoke rising from a Shoshone encampment perhaps a mile distant. Your scouts report roughly thirty people—families, not warriors. They appear to be preparing for winter, processing deer hides and drying meat. One of your oxen has developed a limp, and your food stores are dangerously low. The Shoshone have already noticed your presence.

choices.label: Approach the encampment peacefully to trade. Offer money or ammunition for fresh meat and supplies.
Avoid the encampment entirely. Push hard through the night to pass unseen, even though the oxen are tired and the terrain is rough.
Send James alone as an emissary with a gift of ammunition, to gauge their intentions before the party approaches.

personality_effects.James: The burden of leadership weighs heavily. Each decision costs him sleep.
personality_effects.Mary: Fearful of conflict but aware the party is starving. She trusts James's judgment, though her nerves are frayed.
personality_effects.Thomas: Still recovering from earlier losses. He watches the horizon with dread, but hunger makes him reckless.

low.journal_entry: September 1st, 1848. We are thin as rails. The Shoshone know we are here. Sarah and William lie behind us now, and four oxen remain where we need six. The smell of their cooking fires reaches us on the wind. James must choose, and we must live with it.

low.title: Dust and Difficult Decisions
low.description: The canyon narrows ahead, forcing your party into a bottleneck where the trail switchbacks steeply downward. Your scouts report two possible routes: the main trail descends gradually but adds 8 miles to your journey; a steep shortcut cuts 3 miles but requires careful navigation and risks damage to your remaining wagons. Mary's fever has broken, but she remains weak. Your food supplies are critically low—15 pounds won't sustain two people for more than a few days at meager rations. The first frost could come any night now, and winter is genuinely close.

choices.label: Take the longer main trail. Safety matters more than speed.
Attempt the steep shortcut to save distance and food.
Hunt in the canyon for several days. Rest Mary and stretch your food supply.

personality_effects.James: The loss of three party members weighs on him. He's methodical now, almost grim. He wants to reach Oregon before winter—and he won't lose you too.
personality_effects.Mary: Weakened by fever, she defers to James's judgment but quietly worries that they've waited too long. She's practical about the odds.

low.journal_entry: September 10th, 1848 — Snake River Canyon. The walls close in here like a fist. We've come 1,400 miles and buried three good people. Just Mary and me now, and she's only just sitting upright. The canyon goes two ways: safe and slow, or fast and treacherous. Winter won't wait for us to decide. The nights already bite. I count our food in handfuls. Every mile matters. Every day matters more.

low.title: Snake River Crossing
low.description: You've reached a ford across the Snake River. The water runs swift and cold, swollen with autumn runoff. Your scout reports the crossing is passable but treacherous—three oxen have already shown signs of lameness from the hard desert travel. One of your remaining oxen is noticeably weaker than the others. The riverbed is rocky. A trader camped nearby warns that if even one ox goes lame in the river, you'll lose it to the current or be forced to abandon it on the far bank.

choices.label: Attempt the crossing now with all three oxen. You'll push hard to get across before dark.

consequences.description: The crossing is brutal. The weakest ox loses footing on the rocks midstream. Despite your efforts to steady it, the current sweeps it downstream. You manage to reach the far bank, but the loss of draft power hits morale hard. Everyone is exhausted and bruised from wrestling with the wagon in the icy water. Mary's dysentery worsens from the cold exposure.

choices.label: Wait two days for the river to drop. Ration carefully and hope the water recedes enough to make a safer crossing.

consequences.description: The river stubbornly maintains its level. Two days of waiting test everyone's nerves, and with meager rations stretched thinner, hunger becomes the dominant concern. James manages to gather some willow bark and boil it into a tea that provides Mary slight relief. The delay costs valuable time as winter closes in.

choices.label: Lighten the wagon—abandon the weakest ox now and use the remaining two to pull a lighter load across. Cache supplies in a marked location to retrieve later if possible.

consequences.description: You shoot the weakest ox—a grim necessity—and bury what supplies you cannot carry in a rock cairn marked with a prominent stone. The crossing with two oxen is manageable, though the wagon lurches alarmingly on the rocky bottom. You're now committed to an even leaner march. The cached supplies represent both hope and uncertainty—you may never see them again.

personality_effects.James: Burdened by three deaths and dwindling options. His leadership is tested daily. Any further loss will shake his resolve.
personality_effects.Mary: Weakened by dysentery and exposure. Her survival is increasingly uncertain. She watches the river with quiet fear.

low.journal_entry: September 15th. The Snake River bars our way—swift, cold, and unforgiving as everything else in this country. William is behind us now, along with Thomas and Sarah. Mary grows weaker. We have three oxen left and the will to see this through, but the mathematics of survival grow harder each day. Winter will not wait.

low.title: A Wagon Axle Cracks in the Mud
low.description: The steady rain has turned the forest trail into a quagmire. This morning, as the wagons lurched through a particularly deep rut, you heard the sharp crack of wood. James examines the left axle of the supply wagon—a long split runs along the grain. It will hold for now, but another hard jolt could shatter it entirely. You have one spare part. The alternatives are grim: abandon some cargo and lighten the load to reduce strain on the axle, or press forward carefully and risk a complete failure miles from any shelter or help.

choices.label: Use the spare part to reinforce the axle with binding and a wooden splice.
Abandon 8 pounds of food and non-essential cargo to lighten the wagon.
Continue at a slow walk, distributing weight between wagons. Proceed with caution.

personality_effects.James: The leader bore responsibility for the spare part's use. Using it bought time but exhausted his resolve.
personality_effects.Mary: Watching good food abandoned in mud weighed on her spirit more than hunger itself.
personality_effects.Thomas: The slow pace suited his weakened frame, though caution felt like surrender.

low.journal_entry: October 1st, 1848 — The rains here are relentless, and the forest offers no mercy. We've lost two good people already: Sarah to fever in late August, William to a fall from his horse near Fort Laramie. Now the wagons themselves betray us. One of the oxen from the alkali pool moves like it's already halfway to the grave. We are 1,630 miles from Independence. The Willamette Valley is still three weeks away if fortune smiles. It won't. Not yet.

low.title: The Soaked Flour Problem
low.description: By morning, the flour you salvaged from yesterday's creek crossing has begun to swell and ferment in its damp canvas sack. The smell is unmistakably sour. Mary estimates you have perhaps two days before it becomes entirely inedible—already your provisions are dangerously low at 35 pounds. You face a choice: attempt to dry what you can by spreading it in the cold, wet forest (a slow process that may not work), mix the compromised flour into a thick paste and cook it immediately into hard biscuits (salvaging some nutrition but committing to an unpalatable meal), or abandon the sack entirely and ration your remaining stores even more severely.

choices.label: Spread the flour to dry despite the rain and fog. It's wasteful to throw away food.
Cook the compromised flour into dense, hard biscuits now. Unpleasant, but filling.
Discard the sack and reduce rations further. Better to preserve what's good.

personality_effects.James: Pragmatic but haunted by the deaths behind us. Each supply loss weighs harder.
personality_effects.Mary: Exhausted. She wants food that doesn't taste like rot, but understands necessity.

low.journal_entry: October 10th — The flour is lost to the creek. I can smell the fermentation from ten feet away. Mary and I argued quietly about what to do with it. Three gone now—Thomas, Sarah, William—and the two of us pressing on through mud and fog toward a land we've only heard about in stories. The oxen are tiring. The rain won't stop. We are 1700 miles from home and 200 or more from our destination, with food dwindling and winter coming down the mountains like a hammer.

low.title: The Pawnee Hunters
low.description: Late afternoon. Your scouts report a group of Pawnee hunters—perhaps a dozen strong—moving parallel to your column a half-mile north. They carry fresh buffalo meat and seem focused on their own business, but their presence is unmistakable. James confers with the other adults. Some favor pressing on without interaction. Others suggest approaching peacefully to trade for fresh meat, which would ease your food situation. A third option: circle wide around them to avoid contact entirely, though this costs time and energy.

choices.label: Approach cautiously and attempt trade for buffalo meat

consequences.notes: Trade is successful but tense. You exchange ammunition and coin for 35 pounds of smoked buffalo. The Pawnee hunters are civil but wary. William's nervousness during negotiations costs you a few extra cents, but James's steady demeanor prevents worse. Minor scrape on Thomas's hand during transfer of goods.

choices.label: Circle wide around the hunters, maintain distance

consequences.notes: You lose a full day and a half skirting northward to avoid them. One ox goes lame on rough ground. Sarah approves of caution; William grows irritable and vocal about the detour. James feels the delay gnaw at his leadership.

choices.label: Continue on your current path; ignore the hunters

consequences.notes: You press ahead without acknowledgment. The Pawnee hunters veer away and disappear over a rise. No incident. Some relief at avoiding complication, but Mary worries you've missed a chance for better provisions. Thomas feels tension for hours afterward.

personality_effects.James: Leader carries weight of every choice. Steady nerve in negotiation.
personality_effects.Mary: Pragmatic. Eyes always on food security.
personality_effects.Thomas: Young, eager to prove himself. Unease in tense moments.
personality_effects.Sarah: Values safety and preparation. Trust in James's judgment.
personality_effects.William: Youngest, prone to anxiety near strangers. Loyalty to group strong.

low.journal_entry: April 20th. We encountered a Pawnee hunting party this afternoon—a reminder that we are not alone on these plains, nor are we masters of them. The land belongs to those who have always known it. We made our choice and continued forward. The grass stretches endless, and ahead lies the Platte River valley. Snow in the passes, they said back at the last settlement. We must make good time.

low.title: Sickness Spreads Through Camp
low.description: By evening, Mary complains of a sharp pain in her stomach. By morning, Sarah has joined her with fever and chills. The fresh venison from yesterday sits heavy in the cooler air—Thomas suspects it may have spoiled faster than expected in the damp prairie conditions, or perhaps it was simply unsafe to eat uncooked overnight. William tends to both women, but his supplies are limited. James must decide how to proceed: push forward and hope the illness passes naturally, halt and rest (losing time but potentially preventing worse), or dose them with the limited medicine on hand.

choices.label: Continue at steady pace—illness often passes on its own, and falling behind schedule is costly.
Halt the wagons and make camp for two days. Rest and fluids, with no medicine used yet.
Use 2 doses of medicine immediately on both women to arrest the sickness before it worsens.

personality_effects.James: Leader must weigh urgency against welfare—all three options carry judgment.
personality_effects.William: His medical role is tested; he prefers medicine be spared, not squandered.
personality_effects.Thomas: Feels responsible for the venison; loss of time frustrates him.
personality_effects.Mary: Trusts James but fears falling behind.
personality_effects.Sarah: Youngest; hardest hit; quick recovery matters to morale.

low.journal_entry: April 28th, evening. The venison was a blessing and a curse. Two of our party are laid low with fever and stomach complaint. I have seen this before on other journeys—some recover in a day, others linger. The rains have made everything damp, and meat spoils faster than a man might wish. We are 150 miles from Independence. The season is young yet, but every day lost now is a day stolen from our window before the mountain snows come. William looks to me for direction, as he always does. The oxen are still spooked from yesterday's thunder, and I cannot ask them for speed if the women cannot travel. A hard choice lies ahead.

low.title: The Pawnee Encounter
low.description: Near midday, your scout rides back with urgent news: a band of roughly twenty Pawnee hunters has been spotted two miles ahead, moving parallel to the trail. They appear to be tracking buffalo, not the wagon train, but they've certainly noticed you. Your party must decide how to proceed. Do you approach them openly to trade or request safe passage? Do you maintain distance and hope they move on? Or do you attempt to take a faster route around them, risking rough terrain and lost time?

choices.label: Ride out under a white cloth to parley and trade for fresh meat

consequences.description: James and two others ride out with trade goods and spare ammunition. The Pawnee are wary but willing. A tense negotiation follows—neither side fully trusts the other—but you secure roughly 45 pounds of fresh buffalo meat and safe passage. The encounter takes several hours but boosts morale considerably. Thomas proves surprisingly skilled at sign language.

choices.label: Keep steady pace and maintain distance; do not engage

consequences.description: You hold your line and push forward deliberately. The Pawnee hunters watch but do not approach—they have no interest in slowing for a wagon train. By evening, they've disappeared over the western horizon. The party's nerves settle somewhat, though William grumbles about cowardice and missed opportunity. Mary quietly approves of the cautious choice.

choices.label: Circle north around them using a rougher creek valley route

consequences.description: Hoping to avoid all contact, you veer north into rougher terrain. The creek valley proves narrower and muddier than expected. One axle cracks crossing a rocky wash; you spend hours making repairs. Two oxen go lame from the rough ground. Rations stretch thin as you improvise the longer route. By the time you rejoin the main trail, you've lost two full days and everyone is exhausted. Sarah, already the weakest, falls ill from the stress and cold night air.

personality_effects.James: Calculating risk versus reward
personality_effects.Mary: Prefers peaceful resolution
personality_effects.Thomas: Eager to prove himself
personality_effects.Sarah: Anxious about confrontation
personality_effects.William: Frustrated by caution

low.journal_entry: May 1st, 1848. The plains stretch endlessly, and we are not alone upon them. Before noon, our scout brought word of Pawnee buffalo hunters—twenty strong, moving west-northwest. We are forced to choose: meet them as neighbors, keep apart as strangers, or risk the country beyond the trail itself. Each path carries its own weight. James studies the map and waits for our decision. The weather holds fair, at least.

low.title: Fever Breaks Out
low.description: Sarah wakes with a high fever and severe chills. Her skin is hot to the touch, and she complains of aching joints. Mary suspects it may be the fever that struck down two families in the wagon train ahead of you three days ago. The afternoon heat is oppressive, and mosquitoes swarm near the river. You have four doses of quinine in your medical kit—enough to treat one person for four days, or ration it across multiple people. Stopping here for a day would give Sarah rest, but delay your progress. Continuing at steady pace risks her condition worsening.

choices.label: Make camp for one full day. Administer quinine and keep Sarah cool with damp cloths.
Continue at steady pace. Give Sarah one dose of quinine and keep moving; she may recover as we travel.
Send James and William ahead to scout faster terrain while Mary stays with Sarah. Rejoin the main group in two days.

personality_effects.Mary: Insists on proper nursing; will resent hasty decisions.
personality_effects.James: Torn between pressing on and protecting the party.
personality_effects.William: Impatient with delays; wants to maintain speed.
personality_effects.Thomas: Already struggling; additional stress weakens him further.
personality_effects.Sarah: Vulnerable; her recovery depends on rest and medicine.

low.journal_entry: June 1st, 1848 — River Valley, Great Plains. Sarah took ill this morning with a fever that came on sudden and fierce. The heat and damp of this river valley seem to breed sickness. We have quinine enough for one person's full course, but not enough for all if others fall ill. Every decision now carries weight. Mary tends to her with cool water from the Platte, but there is only so much nursing can do without rest. We are 450 miles from Independence, and the trail ahead grows longer with each day Sarah cannot walk. The mosquitoes are relentless.

low.title: A Pass Divides: Bitter Creek or the High Route
low.description: Your party reaches a fork in the trail near the base of the Rocky Mountains. The left path follows Bitter Creek through a longer but lower-elevation route—safer from early snow, but the creek's water is strongly alkaline and could sicken both livestock and people. The right path climbs directly over a high pass. It's shorter by 40 miles, but the peaks still hold snow patches, and your sluggish ox will struggle. Local Shoshone traders you encountered this morning warned that lightning storms strike the high passes nearly every afternoon this time of year. Your food is running low at 45 pounds, and you've already lost William to dysentery two weeks back. Sarah's health is declining noticeably.

choices.label: Take the Bitter Creek route—safety in numbers, water be damned.

consequences.notes: The alkaline water causes intestinal distress. One ox becomes too weak to continue and must be left behind. Sarah develops mild fever from the brackish water exposure.

choices.label: Risk the high pass—faster, but gamble on weather and the weakened ox.

consequences.notes: A lightning storm catches you on the exposed ridge on day 5. No direct strikes, but proximity causes panic. The sluggish ox and one healthy ox collapse from exhaustion and exposure; both must be shot to prevent suffering. Cold winds tear one person's clothing. Everyone is shaken but grateful to descend into warmer valleys.

choices.label: Send a scout ahead with two oxen to find a middle route while the party rests.

consequences.notes: James scouts with Thomas for two days, moving carefully to spot a game trail. They find a moderately safe switchback route between the two options, though it adds 15 extra miles. The party rests and Mary tends to Sarah's fever with the last of your medicine (uses 1 med). You lose 2 days but avoid the worst hazards of both routes.

personality_effects.James: Leader's judgment weighs heavily. A wrong choice here could cost lives.
personality_effects.Mary: Practical and steady. She supports whatever decision James makes, though she worries aloud about Sarah.
personality_effects.Thomas: His declining health makes the high pass terrifying. He'll follow but resents the risk.
personality_effects.Sarah: Feverish and fearful. She needs warmth, rest, and medicine—not a grueling mountain crossing.

low.journal_entry: July 20th, 1848. We stand at a crossroads in truth and in geography. Bitter Creek winds left through alkali flats. The high pass rises before us, still flecked with snow though it is summer. The Shoshone warned of thunder. William is three weeks buried behind us. I watch Sarah shiver despite the warm valley air, and I wonder if my decisions have strength enough for what lies ahead. The oxen will not carry us much farther. We must choose wisely, or choose again with fewer of us to make the choice.

low.title: Mountain Sickness and Thin Air
low.description: At nearly 8,000 feet elevation, the party struggles with the thinner atmosphere. James complains of a persistent headache. Thomas has developed a dry cough that won't ease. Mary's lips have taken on a slightly bluish tint. The oxen are laboring visibly—their ribs becoming more prominent each day. You have three days of travel ahead before reaching lower ground, but you could attempt to rest here for a full day to acclimate, or push forward with reduced pace.

choices.label: Rest one full day at this elevation. Let the party and oxen recover.
Push forward at steady pace. The exertion will be difficult, but you'll descend sooner.
Reduce rations further and slow to a walk. Conserve strength for the next week.

low.journal_entry: August 1st — We climb higher into these great mountains, where the very air grows thin and mean. The flour spoiled in yesterday's creek crossing, and now we depend on salt pork and what game we can shoot. James's head pounds with an ache he cannot shake. The oxen flag with each mile. Two souls already lost to this journey—Sarah in June, William just weeks past. The survivors must decide: rest and risk delay, or press on and risk worse. The mountains do not forgive hesitation, but neither do they reward foolish haste.

low.title: Mountain Fever Spreads
low.description: Mary's condition worsens overnight. By morning, James notices Thomas has developed the same symptoms—fever, chills, and a persistent cough. Both are too weak to walk more than a few miles. The party made only 8 miles yesterday, and the mountain passes ahead are narrowing. Snow from yesterday's storm lingers in the shaded ravines. The oxen are growing lean; grass is sparse at this elevation. One of the oxen shows signs of lameness in its rear left leg.

choices.label: Push hard toward the next pass. Medical aid might be available on the western side. Keep rations at filling level to maintain strength.

personality_effects.James: desperate
personality_effects.Mary: suffering
personality_effects.Thomas: deteriorating

choices.label: Make camp for three days. Ration food to bare minimum. Use remaining medicine on Mary and Thomas. Hunt and forage to extend supplies.

personality_effects.James: cautious
personality_effects.Mary: hopeful
personality_effects.Thomas: improving

choices.label: Send James ahead with one healthy ox to scout the pass and locate the next settlement. Mary and Thomas stay in camp with reduced rations. High risk of separation.

personality_effects.James: divided
personality_effects.Mary: abandoned
personality_effects.Thomas: fearful

personality_effects.James: responsible and tired
personality_effects.Mary: fevered and fading
personality_effects.Thomas: newly afflicted
personality_effects.William: deceased since seg_8
personality_effects.Sarah: deceased since seg_9

low.journal_entry: August 15th. The mountain fever that took Sarah has risen again, this time in Mary and now Thomas. We are at 8,800 feet. The passes close with snow in October—we have perhaps six weeks. I made a camp count this morning: fifty-five pounds of flour and hardtack, three oxen in serviceable condition, one lame. Mary burns with fever. Thomas complains his lungs feel stuffed with cotton. The snow on the peaks is not beautiful anymore. It is a warning.

low.title: The Dust Storm and the Shoshone Traders
low.description: By mid-afternoon, the sky darkens to a sickly brown. A dust storm rolls across the Snake River plain with surprising speed—visibility drops to mere feet. The party takes shelter behind the wagons, but the wind is relentless, coating everything in fine grit. As the storm begins to clear at dusk, you notice riders approaching from the south: a small band of Shoshone, perhaps six in number, led by an older man on a spotted horse. They carry furs and trade goods. The leader raises an open hand in greeting. He gestures toward your oxen, then holds up several beaver pelts and a bundle of dried salmon. His intent is clear: he wishes to trade.

choices.label: Accept the trade: offer 1 ox and some ammunition for the dried salmon and furs. Fresh protein and warmer hides could help you endure the coming cold.
Politely refuse and prepare to move on. The Shoshone have been friendly, but you cannot afford to lose an ox this late in the season. Press forward before dark.
Attempt to negotiate hard: offer only some ammunition and dried deer meat for the salmon, keeping your oxen. Test whether the Shoshone are genuinely peaceful traders.

personality_effects.James: The leader weighs practicality against the party's trust. His judgment here will color how others see his command.
personality_effects.Mary: She is concerned about food reserves and warmth. Generosity toward strangers sits uneasily with her worry.
personality_effects.Thomas: His morale has been fragile. A good trade energizes him; stingy dealing or harsh refusal reminds him of harder times ahead.

low.journal_entry: 9 September, 1848. The dust storm came sudden and fierce, turning day into twilight. When it cleared, Shoshone riders appeared—traders, it seemed, not raiders. The leader offered furs and dried salmon for an ox. James had to decide whether to strengthen our stores at the cost of hauling power, or hold fast to what little we had left. Either way, winter waits for no one out here.

low.title: Ox Gone Lame
low.description: At dawn, you discover one of your oxen won't put weight on its front left leg. The animal limps badly, favoring the injured limb. It's likely a stone bruise or a hairline fracture in the hoof—common enough on this rocky terrain, but potentially serious. You have four oxen remaining; losing even one reduces your hauling capacity significantly. The canyon walls press close here, and there's no good grazing for miles ahead. You must decide quickly: rest the animal a full day and hope it recovers, attempt to push on slowly with reduced load, or, if the injury seems truly severe, slaughter it for meat before it becomes worthless.

choices.label: Rest camp one day and tend the ox's hoof carefully.
Slaughter the ox now. Fresh meat and fewer animals to manage.
Reduce load by 40 pounds, redistribute weight, push forward at walking pace.

James.note: Leader feels the weight of every decision now. Three members lost already.

Mary.note: Remaining adult woman watches resources dwindle. Anxiety mounting.

low.journal_entry: September 10th, 1848 — Snake River Canyon. Found the spotted ox favoring its left foreleg this morning. The stone here is unforgiving. With three already gone to fever and dysentery, we can ill afford to lose livestock. The canyon narrows ahead and the nights grow bitter. One ox failing now could strand us before winter.

low.title: The Shoshone Camp
low.description: As your party descends into a valley near the Snake River, you spot smoke rising from a camp perhaps a half-mile distant. Through the dust, you make out several lodges and what appears to be a significant number of horses. A Shoshone hunting party, by the look of them. Two riders break away from the camp and approach at a measured pace, neither hostile nor particularly welcoming. They carry bows but have not drawn them. One raises his hand in a gesture that might be greeting.

choices.label: Approach openly with a white cloth tied to your wagon. Attempt to trade for fresh provisions.

consequences.notes: The Shoshone are willing to trade. You exchange ammunition and currency for dried elk meat and roots. James gains trust among the remaining party for his diplomacy. Mary's condition improves slightly with better nutrition. The interaction costs time and resources but bolsters morale significantly.

choices.label: Circle the wagons and prepare defensive positions. Do not engage unless they approach closer.

consequences.notes: The Shoshone riders observe your defensive preparation with apparent indifference, then wheel their horses and return to camp. Your party spends an anxious night in a defensive posture. No attack comes. By morning, the camp has moved on. You have lost time and the opportunity for trade. The tension aggravates Mary's illness and leaves the depleted party feeling more isolated.

choices.label: Send James alone on horseback with a rifle and one bolt of cloth as a gift. Let him negotiate while the wagons wait.

consequences.notes: James rides into the Shoshone camp. There is genuine risk—he is one man among many. He returns after several hours with dried meat, a small bag of camas roots, and an extra horse the Shoshone gifted him as a sign of safe passage. James's courage impresses the party, though Mary worries the entire time and her dysentery worsens from stress. The gift horse will help lighten the load on existing oxen for the remainder of the journey.

personality_effects.James: Leadership decisions. Risk-taking. Diplomacy.
personality_effects.Mary: Stress from separation. Diet and nutrition. Illness progression.

low.journal_entry: September 15, 1848. We are three oxen, five persons—four now that we count the living—and we are running on fumes and stubbornness. Thomas, Sarah, and William lie behind us in unmarked graves. The desert gives nothing freely. Today we saw smoke and knew we were not alone in this vast emptiness. The Shoshone are here, have always been here, and we are the strangers passing through their country. Our next choice will determine whether they see us as traders, threats, or merely ghosts passing in the dust.

low.title: The Injured Teamster
low.description: A man on horseback approaches from the south, moving slowly. He's a freighter—one of the independent traders who work the trail—and his left leg is badly swollen, wrapped in soaked cloth. He explains he was thrown from his horse three days ago near the Umpqua crossing. Infection is setting in; he can smell it himself. He has trade goods (tobacco, flour, salt) on two pack horses, but he cannot ride farther. He asks for shelter, food, and medicine in exchange for what supplies he can part with. He's lucid but feverish. Your party has 1 medicine available.

choices.label: Give him shelter, share food, and use your medicine to treat the infection.
choices.outcome: The medicine—laudanum and quinine—slows the infection's spread, though it doesn't cure him entirely. He stays the night, fevered but stable. In the morning, unable to ride, he pays you 100 cents and gives you directions to water sources ahead. Your party's health suffers slightly from the stress and lost sleep. The teamster remains behind; whether he survives is no longer your concern.

choices.label: Offer to purchase his flour and salt at half value; provide some food but keep your medicine.
choices.outcome: The teamster accepts bitterly. You gain 40 lbs flour and 15 lbs salt at a steep discount, but the transaction is tense. He asks for nothing more and rides on slowly, disappearing into the rain. Mary falls silent afterward. The supplies are welcome, but the moral cost lingers.

choices.label: Politely decline. You have your own people to think of. Point him toward the next trading post (2 miles north).
choices.outcome: He nods, unsurprised. He rides north with obvious effort. You don't know if he makes it. That evening, James and Mary are quieter than usual. The weight of three deaths behind you, and now this refusal, sits heavy in the camp. No supplies change hands, but the decision costs you something harder to quantify.

personality_effects.James: Pragmatic but not callous; the trail teaches hard choices.
personality_effects.Mary: Compassionate, but aware that kindness has limits when survival is at stake.

low.journal_entry: October 10th, 1848. Rain all day. Met a freighter with a festering leg wound. Infection spreading. Difficult to know what mercy means out here. We are six now—James, Mary, and myself remaining. Three in the ground behind us. Every choice feels like it carries weight. The forest is thick and the trail slick. We push on.

low.title: The Pawnee Trade Opportunity
low.description: On the morning of April 20th, a small hunting party of Pawnee—perhaps a dozen men on horseback—approaches your camp from the south. Their leader, a weathered man with distinctive face paint, signals peacefully and indicates he wishes to trade. Through gestures and broken English from one younger tribesman, you understand: they have fresh buffalo meat and want cloth, ammunition, or metal tools. Your party is well-supplied, but Mary's fever has depleted your medicine stores, and fresh meat could strengthen everyone. The Pawnee seem genuinely interested in commerce, not conflict. However, William is visibly nervous around them, and you'll need to decide quickly—they won't wait long.

choices.label: Trade ammunition and cloth for fresh buffalo meat and information about the trail ahead.
Politely decline and break camp immediately. You have enough supplies and don't want to risk conflict.
Trade only metal tools and a small amount of cloth for fresh meat. Keep your ammunition.

personality_effects.William: anxious about contact with Pawnee; trusts James's judgment but prefers caution
personality_effects.Mary: weakened by fever; needs nutritious food to recover her strength
personality_effects.James: pragmatic; sees trade as sensible if the risk is manageable
personality_effects.Thomas: cautious but not fearful; willing to follow the party's consensus
personality_effects.Sarah: practical; focused on what benefits everyone's survival

low.journal_entry: April 20, 1848 — A hunting party of Pawnee came into camp at dawn, peaceful and wanting to trade. Mary's fever still hangs on her like a wet blanket, and fresh meat would do her good. The question is whether we can spare what they're asking for. William's knuckles have gone white gripping his rifle. These men seem honest enough, but the trail teaches you not to assume anything. We'll need to decide quick—they won't linger.

low.title: The Pawnee Hunters
low.description: Late in the afternoon, you spot a hunting party of roughly a dozen Pawnee riders moving parallel to your wagon line across the grassland. They carry bows and spears, and appear to be tracking a buffalo herd. They notice you as well. One rider approaches slowly, hand raised in a gesture that could mean greeting or warning. Your scout reports they seem cautious rather than hostile, but their intentions are unclear. The flour you soaked at the creek is still damp and beginning to spoil—you have perhaps two days before it becomes inedible. This group likely knows the land well and might trade.

choices.label: Offer to trade: give them ammunition and dried meat in exchange for safe passage and information about water sources ahead.

consequences.notes: Pawnee hunters accept the trade. They point you toward a reliable spring two days' ride northeast and confirm buffalo herds are moving north—game will be scarce on your direct route for another week.

choices.label: Maintain distance and quicken pace. Circle wagons if they approach closer, and avoid engagement.

consequences.notes: The Pawnee riders make no hostile move but keep pace with you for several hours. Your hurried march exhausts one ox. The encounter passes without conflict, but tensions remain high. Your water is depleted from the forced march.

choices.label: Send James forward under a white cloth flag to parley directly and gauge their mood.

consequences.notes: James meets with the Pawnee leader. Communication is limited, but the meeting is respectful. They are hunting buffalo and have no interest in your wagons. They depart peacefully. James returns with minor abrasions from rough terrain but good intelligence: a Pawnee village is 25 miles north; they trade regularly with wagon trains.

Thomas.notes: Thomas trusts direct action and respects warriors who face danger openly.

William.notes: William's poor health makes any rushed march or conflict anxiety particularly draining.

low.journal_entry: May 10th — A Pawnee hunting party crossed our path this afternoon on the prairie. We are now 320 miles from our starting point, deep in lands that belong to peoples we are only beginning to understand. The flour spoils from yesterday's creek crossing; we must decide whether to trade, press on, or negotiate. The air smells of grass and distant rain. Thomas grows restless for action. William tires more easily each day.

low.title: Sickness in Camp
low.description: By evening, Thomas develops a high fever and complains of severe headache and body aches. Mary examines him and suspects either cholera morbus or a heavy fever from the marsh air. The mosquitoes have been relentless all day. Thomas is weak enough that he cannot manage the oxen tomorrow. You have four doses of medicine remaining, but using them now means none for serious injury or illness later. The party looks to you for a decision.

choices.label: Administer medicine and rest here one day
Keep moving at reduced pace; conserve medicine
Give Thomas light duty; James takes his oxen work

low.journal_entry: June 1st — The river valley is fertile but treacherous. Thomas fell ill this evening with fever and aching limbs. Mary fears it may be the marsh sickness that has taken others on this trail. We have medicine, but not enough to spare. The snow warnings from the eastbound party weigh on my mind — we cannot afford delays, yet a man cannot walk himself to death.

low.title: The Dust Storm and the Pawnee Camp
low.description: Late afternoon brings an unnatural darkness—a wall of dust rises from the southwest, swallowing the horizon. Your scout reports riders approaching from the north: Pawnee hunters, perhaps twenty strong, moving parallel to your column. They haven't attacked, but they're watching. The dust storm will hit within the hour. Your oxen are already nervous. You must decide quickly: push hard for higher ground to the west and risk exhaustion in this heat, attempt to communicate with the Pawnee and seek shelter near their camp, or make a defensive stand here on the open plains and hope the storm passes.

choices.label: Push west toward higher ground—cover as much distance as possible before the storm hits.

consequences.note: One ox collapses from heat and exhaustion; you are forced to leave it behind. The party reaches a ridge but Mary and Thomas show signs of heat sickness by nightfall.

choices.label: Approach the Pawnee camp under a flag of truce and negotiate shelter from the storm.

consequences.note: The Pawnee are cautious but not hostile. They allow your party to shelter near their encampment during the worst of the storm. You trade 50 cents worth of goods for information about water sources ahead. A tense but peaceful night; no violence occurs.

choices.label: Form a defensive circle with the wagons here—wait out the storm and show no weakness to the Pawnee.

consequences.note: The storm is severe; visibility drops to ten feet. Dust penetrates everything. The Pawnee do not approach during the chaos. Your supplies are coated in grit; cleaning firearms and tools takes hours. No direct conflict, but the day is lost to survival.

personality_effects.James: Leader must decide the party's fate in minutes.
personality_effects.Mary: Her health is fragile; extreme heat worsens her condition.
personality_effects.Thomas: Young and anxious; distrusts the Pawnee, fears ambush.
personality_effects.Sarah: Practical and calm; her steadiness is a stabilizing force.

low.journal_entry: July 10th, 1848 — The plains turned against us today. A dust storm rose like the wrath of God, and with it came riders we could not identify until they were nearly upon us. The heat has drained us all. William is gone these three weeks now, and I wonder if he saw something in this land that told him to quit it. The oxen suffer. We are 800 miles from Independence, and the Rocky Mountains still seem a distant dream.

low.title: Mountain Pass Dilemma
low.description: As your party climbs toward the South Pass, you discover the trail splits. The main route—well-worn and documented—curves north around a series of steep ravines, adding nearly 40 miles. A faint secondary path cuts directly west through a narrow mountain pass. Two traders you met yesterday warned it's treacherous, unmarked, and prone to afternoon rockslides, but could save three days of travel. Your oxen are weary. Thomas has been coughing since yesterday. The afternoon sky darkens with clouds.

choices.label: Take the main trail—longer but proven safe.
Attempt the direct pass—save time, but gamble on safety.
Camp here two days, wait for better weather and let Thomas recover.

personality_effects.James: Torn between caution and the need to reach Oregon before snow. Leadership weighs heavily.
personality_effects.Mary: Practical. Prefers the known route, but worried about Thomas's condition.
personality_effects.Thomas: Feverish and frustrated. Would push forward to prove his strength, though his body disagrees.

low.journal_entry: August 1st, 1848. We stand at a crossroads, literally. The South Pass yawns before us, and we must choose our path carefully. Snow caps these great mountains—beautiful and terrible. Yesterday I saw where J. Henderson was laid to rest last year. No man wants to become a grave marker. Thomas coughs through the night. The Pawnee jerky sustains us, but time and exhaustion wear harder than hunger.

low.title: The Mountain Pass Narrows
low.description:
As your party climbs into denser pine forest, the trail ahead splits. The main route curves north through a wider pass—longer by perhaps ten miles, but well-established with visible wagon ruts. A steep, narrow alternative cuts directly west through rocky terrain, gaining elevation rapidly. Local traders mentioned this shortcut yesterday, claiming it saves two days if the weather holds.

Mary shivers despite the afternoon warmth, her mountain fever making her sensitive to cold. The thermometer suggests snow has fallen recently at higher elevations. Your oxen are tired; three show signs of lameness from the rocky ground you've covered since morning.

choices.label: Take the established northern pass. It's longer but safer for the oxen and Mary's recovery.
Risk the shortcut west. Save two days, but push hard over rough terrain in uncertain weather.
Camp here an extra day to let Mary rest and the oxen recover before choosing a route.

personality_effects.James: Feeling the weight of command. Two members already lost, and now hard choices every day.
personality_effects.Mary: Fever clouds her thinking, but she knows the shortcut could be dangerous for her condition.
personality_effects.Thomas: Impatient. Every day behind schedule gnaws at him.

low.journal_entry: August 15, 1848 — The Rockies do not forgive haste, yet they do not reward it caution alone. The trail splits like a fork in a branch, and we must choose. Mary burns with fever while the oxen stumble. Snow visible above us. Two weeks to Fort Hall, if fortune allows. If we move fast enough, we might clear the high passes before the season turns. If we do not, we will not clear them at all.

low.title: Shoshone traders at the crossing
low.description: As you approach a narrow ford where the Snake River bends south, you spot a small camp of Shoshone traders with horses and goods displayed on blankets. They gesture welcomingly, offering to trade. You count perhaps eight people, armed but not hostile. Your oxen are gaunt from the desert crossing, and your provisions are critically low. The Shoshone have fresh meat, furs, and appear to have grain. They seem interested in your remaining clothing and ammunition.

choices.label: Trade ammunition and one set of clothes for meat and grain
Decline and push on; you can hunt or find water farther south
Attempt to trade for horses; offer money and remaining parts

low.journal_entry: September 1st. The desert has worn us down to bone and grit. Met a band of Shoshone today at the ford—honest traders, not raiders. The sight of their horses and meat stores tested every one of us. Sarah and William gone these past weeks. The living must decide how to remain so. Cold nights coming. The mountains loom ahead.

low.title: The Sluggish Ox
low.description: One of your oxen, which drank from an alkali pool two days ago, has grown noticeably weaker. Its gait is labored, and it refuses much of the dried grass you offer. The animal is still functional but clearly declining. You have four oxen total—losing even one would stretch your remaining beasts dangerously thin across the remaining 400 miles to the Willamette Valley. Mary suggests resting here a full day to see if the ox recovers. James worries that delay invites colder nights; frost could damage what little clothing you have left. William is gone, along with Thomas and Sarah—their oxen were sold or traded weeks ago to other parties.

choices.label: Rest the party and oxen for one full day. Give the sick ox extra water and what medicinal care you can manage.

consequences.narrative: You make camp at midday. Mary tends the ailing ox with the last of your quinine, cooling its head with river water. James keeps watch. By evening, the ox stands and drinks on its own—a good sign. The rest does morale good; people are exhausted. But you've burned precious daylight and medicine, and the first frost may come sooner than expected.

choices.label: Push on at steady pace. Use a walking stick to help support the weak ox if necessary. Conserve medicine.

consequences.narrative: You press forward. The weak ox falls further behind by midday. By evening, it collapses a mile short of camp. There is nothing to be done. James shoots it mercifully at dawn—three oxen remain. The party is silent and grim. You've gained ground, but the cost is written plainly across everyone's faces.

choices.label: Trade the weakened ox to a passing freight company heading the opposite direction. Use the money to buy grain and dried meat from their stores.

consequences.narrative: A freight outfit heading east appears around midday. The drivers are skeptical of the ox's condition but offer fifty cents and basic provisions. It's a loss—the ox was worth more—but you trade. The grain and meat improve rations noticeably. Three oxen now must pull your wagon the rest of the way. It will be tight.

personality_effects.James: Leader burden deepens. Each animal lost is a command decision with lives attached.
