personality_effects.Mary: Practical nurse; she has seen too many die already. Every attempt to save something—even an ox—matters to her.

low.journal_entry: September 10th. The alkali pool claimed one of our beasts in slow fashion. James and I debated what to do with an afternoon slipping away. The canyon walls hem us in. Winter is not far behind. Three more oxen to reach Oregon. Some days I do not recognize the mathematics of this journey—what must be spent to reach what remains to be gained.

low.title: The Poisoned Water
low.description: At midday, you discover that one of your water barrels has been contaminated—likely by alkali seepage through a crack in the wooden staves. The water is cloudy and tastes bitter. Your oxen refuse to drink it. You have perhaps two hours before you must make camp, and the nearest reliable water source is uncertain in this desert terrain. Mary, already weakened by dysentery, is in no condition for a difficult search.

choices.label: Push forward hard toward the Snake River, hoping to reach it by evening.

consequences.description: You drive the oxen harder than they can sustain in this heat. One ox collapses by dusk—exhaustion and dehydration finish it. You do reach the river at twilight, but Mary's condition worsens from the rough pace and insufficient water. James's hands are blistered from managing the animals.

choices.label: Make camp now and send two people to scout for fresh water within walking distance.

consequences.description: You ration the good water carefully and rest the oxen. James scouts north with rifle and finds a small spring two miles away—enough to refill barrels. The delay costs you time and four rounds of ammunition (fired to signal your location), but the party avoids serious hardship. Mary takes the time to recover slightly.

choices.label: Boil the contaminated water thoroughly and ration it carefully alongside your good water.

consequences.description: Boiling kills some pathogens but not the mineral content. By evening, both you and Mary develop stomach cramps. You use your last medicine to treat Mary's worsening dysentery. James's stomach troubles slow him down. You press on anyway, desperate to make distance before winter, and cover fourteen miles despite the discomfort.

personality_effects.James: pragmatic but worn—each death on the trail has hardened him, and the arithmetic of survival allows no room for sentiment
personality_effects.Mary: resilient despite illness, but her strength is visibly failing; she follows James's lead without complaint, which troubles him more than protest would

low.journal_entry: Sept 15, Snake River Basin. Discovered the water barrel fouled this afternoon. Lost three good people behind us already. Three oxen left. Mary can barely walk some mornings. The desert has no mercy for the weak, and I find I have less and less to offer her but forward motion. The nights grow cold enough now that I wonder if we'll see snow before we reach the valley. God willing, we've got time yet.

low.title: The Muddy Crossing
low.description: The Willamette River crossing lies ahead, but two days of rain have swollen it considerably. Your scout reports the ford is passable but treacherous—the current runs fast and the riverbed is slick with mud. An older Karok man camped nearby, preparing to cross himself, offers to guide your party through for payment. He knows the safe route but his fee is steep: $8. Alternatively, you can attempt the crossing with your own knowledge, risking oxen and supplies, or wait two more days for the water to recede—though your meager rations won't stretch far, and snow in the passes waits for no one.

choices.label: Pay the Karok guide $8 and cross safely
Attempt the ford yourself with careful rope work
Wait for the river to drop; hunt nearby to stretch rations

personality_effects.James: If guide chosen: nods approvingly at pragmatism. If ford chosen: jaw tightens—he feels the weight of risk. If wait chosen: silent disapproval of lost time.
personality_effects.Mary: If guide chosen: relieved exhale. If ford chosen: grips the wagon wheel, anxiety mounting. If wait chosen: supports the decision quietly.
personality_effects.Thomas: If guide chosen: sullen at the expense. If ford chosen: volunteers immediately to help. If wait chosen: brightens at the prospect of hunting.

low.journal_entry: October 1st, 1848. The Willamette swells before us, dark and insistent. A Karok ferryman appeared like Providence itself, or temptation—his price is dear when every cent counts toward Oregon City. We've lost Sarah and William already; the passes await no dawdler, yet haste kills as surely as delay. The men are worn thin, and the rations won't forgive poor judgment. Whatever we choose, we choose now.

low.title: The Pawnee Trade
low.description: On the morning of April 20th, a small band of Pawnee hunters—perhaps a dozen strong, well-mounted and armed—approaches your camp from the south. Their leader, a man of perhaps forty with a scarred left cheek, signals peaceful intent by raising an open hand. Through gestures and a few words of English picked up from previous traders, he makes clear his purpose: his people are willing to trade fresh game and information about water sources ahead. In exchange, they want ammunition, cloth, or metal tools. The hunters have two elk carcasses lashed to packhorses—enough meat to supplement your stores significantly. Your ammunition is moderate, your cloth limited, and your metal tools few. James must decide whether to trade, and if so, what to offer.

choices.label: Trade ammunition for the elk meat and water information. You have plenty of rounds and will need food.
Trade one bolt of cloth and offer to purchase information only—keep your ammunition intact for hunting and defense.
Decline politely and move on. You've managed well so far; no need to diminish supplies or risk entanglement.

personality_effects.description: Thomas, the youngest and most hopeful, favors engagement and trade. Mary and Sarah value security and sustenance above all. William respects practical decisions. James must weigh survival against caution.

low.journal_entry: April 20, 1848 — Pawnee hunters appeared at dawn. Their leader offered fresh elk and knowledge of water ahead. A decision hung in the air: trust and trade, or self-reliance and suspicion. We have come far enough to know that the trail respects neither caution nor recklessness equally. The choice belonged to James.

low.title: The Pawnee Hunters
low.description: Late in the afternoon, your scout reports a group of roughly twenty Pawnee hunters approaching from the north, moving parallel to your trail. They appear to be tracking a buffalo herd. The hunters notice your wagon train and slow their pace, observing you carefully. Your oxen are visibly tired after today's march. One of your party members, William, is nervously checking his rifle. The Pawnee have not made any hostile gestures, but the encounter creates obvious tension.

choices.label: Halt the wagons and attempt peaceful trade. Offer tobacco and a small portion of flour in exchange for fresh game.
Maintain steady pace and keep weapons visible but lowered. Do not initiate contact; let them pass.
Circle the wagons immediately and prepare for potential conflict. Load weapons and prepare the defensive position.

personality_effects.James: Leader must decide between diplomacy and caution
personality_effects.Mary: Prefers peaceful solutions; fears unnecessary conflict
personality_effects.Thomas: Pragmatic; neither aggressive nor overly trusting
personality_effects.Sarah: Anxious about safety; relies on group consensus
personality_effects.William: Nervous temperament; prone to suspicion of outsiders

low.journal_entry: May 1st, 1848 — Clear day on the prairie. At approximately 3 o'clock in the afternoon, a hunting party of Pawnee came into view from the north. Perhaps twenty men in pursuit of buffalo. The sight stirred unease among our number, particularly William, though the hunters showed no signs of aggression. We are three hundred miles from Independence now, deep in country we must share with those who know it far better than we do. How we respond to such encounters may well determine whether this journey ends in success or tragedy.

low.title: The Lame Ox
low.description: As the wagons cross a shallow creek bed, one of your oxen stumbles on a hidden rock and emerges limping badly. The animal can still walk, but it favors its front left leg heavily. Thomas, who tends the stock, believes the ox has a cracked hoof—painful but possibly survivable with rest. However, you're making good time, and every day of delay risks summer storms and mountain crossings. You have two days' travel to the next trading post where you might find a replacement ox, or you could rest here for three days and hope the animal recovers enough to pull its weight.

choices.label: Push on to the trading post. Rest the lame ox in the wagon bed and redistribute its load.

consequences.note: The ox's condition worsens during travel. At the post, you sell it for meat (value deducted). Thomas resents the decision; William worries about remaining oxen capacity. You locate a replacement—not young, but serviceable.

choices.label: Make camp here for three days. Rest the ox and let it heal.

consequences.note: Thomas tends the hoof with poultice and binding. The ox improves noticeably. However, James grows impatient; Mary worries about lost time. You consume extra rations and one dose of medicine during the layover.

choices.label: Cull the ox now and redistribute its load among the healthy ones.

consequences.note: You butcher the ox for meat and continue immediately. The fresh provisions help, but the act weighs heavily on the party. Thomas is deeply upset; Sarah and William are disturbed. The remaining oxen strain under heavier loads.

personality_effects.Thomas: The stock tender's skill is evident—he notices details others miss. His care for the animals runs deep.
personality_effects.James: Leadership means making hard choices, and not everyone will understand them.
personality_effects.Mary: Practical and cautious, she calculates the cost of delay.
personality_effects.Sarah: Sensitive to suffering, both animal and human.
personality_effects.William: Young and uncertain, he mirrors the mood of those around him.

low.journal_entry: May 10, 1848. The creek crossing cost us an ox. Thomas says the hoof is cracked—we must decide now whether to rest and hope, or press on and replace it at Fort Kearny. The grave we passed yesterday reminds me that time is the greatest luxury we have, and the least we can spare. The sky threatens rain again tonight. —J.

low.title: Cholera and Storm Warning
low.description: Mary's condition worsens through the afternoon—fever, severe dysentery. The sky darkens to an unnatural greenish-gray. A Pawnee scout you met yesterday warned of violent storms moving from the northwest, and the barometer in your wagon confirms falling pressure. You must decide: push toward higher ground five miles north to reach the ridge before the storm hits, or make camp here by the river where water is plentiful but you risk being trapped in low ground if the storm is severe. Mary needs rest and access to fresh water either way.

choices.label: Push hard for the ridge. Mary can rest once we're above the floodplain.

consequences.notes: Mary's condition deteriorates from the hard pace. One ox goes lame pushing the animals on rough ground. You reach the ridge just as the storm breaks—heavy rain, no flooding risk, but Mary requires immediate attention at camp.

choices.label: Make camp here. Mary needs stability. We'll secure the wagons and ride out the storm.

consequences.notes: The storm strikes hard. Wind tears one wagon cover; you lose 8 pounds of flour to rain. River rises three feet but doesn't overflow camp. Mary stabilizes slightly with rest and medicine. Thomas develops a cough from wet conditions.

choices.label: Send James ahead with two oxen to scout a better camp site. Rest the party here until he returns.

consequences.notes: Splitting the party unnerves everyone. James finds a defensible camp two miles upstream on higher ground, but you've consumed extra rations waiting. The delay costs time, and uncertainty about James's safety weighs on morale—especially Sarah, who worries constantly.

personality_effects.James: Leader feels the weight of William's death and Mary's illness. His decisions now are calculated, less confident.
personality_effects.Mary: Weakened, dependent. Becomes quieter, less engaged with camp life.
personality_effects.Thomas: Growing anxious about the trail's hazards. Questions leadership more openly.
personality_effects.Sarah: Protective of Mary; frustrated by circumstances beyond her control. Tired.

low.journal_entry: June 15th — Five hundred miles behind us. Buried William three days ago. The river valley is beautiful and terrible in equal measure. Mary is burning with fever. The sky warns of violence. We are down to five oxen and four medicines. I must choose between risk and stability, and there is no good answer anymore. The cholera doesn't care which way we go.

low.title: The Alkali Lake
low.description: By mid-afternoon, your oxen begin to lag despite the steady pace. The animals' tongues loll from their mouths. You crest a rise and spot what appears to be a substantial lake—a rare blessing on these plains. As you draw closer, however, the water's surface shows an unnatural white crust along its edges. One of your oxen, maddened by thirst, lurches toward it before you can stop the wagon. The water is alkaline, bitter with mineral salts. The animal will drink it if given the chance, and such water causes severe sickness in livestock. You have perhaps an hour before the herd becomes desperate enough to ignore your commands. Your water barrels are down to manageable levels after the ford yesterday, but not abundant.

choices.label: Push the oxen hard through the night to reach the Platte River—you estimate it's 15 miles northeast, reachable by dawn if conditions hold.
Make camp here. Ration the fresh water carefully among the party and animals. Rest the oxen and proceed at first light, knowing the Platte is still a full day's march away.
Send James and Thomas on horseback with the two fastest mounts to scout for freshwater springs or seepage in the nearby ravines. The rest wait with the wagons.

personality_effects.James: As leader, James feels the weight of the decision. The oxen are his responsibility; losing another would be a serious blow to the party's chances.
personality_effects.Mary: Mary, practical and experienced, knows water sickness kills livestock slowly and painfully. She favors caution.
personality_effects.Thomas: Thomas is young and restless; he chafes at delay but fears the unknown terrain ahead in darkness.
personality_effects.Sarah: Sarah, already weakened, worries aloud about her strength for a night march. She prefers rest.

low.journal_entry: July 10, 1848. We found what looked to be salvation—a vast lake spreading across the plain like God's own provision. It was a cruel deception. The water is poisoned with alkali salts, fit only for the basins of the earth. We cannot let the oxen drink. Three choices, none of them good. We have crossed 800 miles now. William is gone these six weeks. The heat shows no mercy.

low.title: The Shoshone Hunter
low.description: As your oxen drink from a shallow creek bed, a Shoshone man on horseback approaches from the north, leading a pack animal laden with fresh antelope meat. He's alone, well-armed, and regards your party with cautious curiosity. His horse is superior to anything you've seen in weeks. He gestures toward your meager camp, then toward the meat, then toward your oxen. The message is clear: he's offering trade.

choices.label: Trade two oxen for the antelope meat and information about the trail ahead.
Offer $30 in coin for the meat only. Keep your oxen—you'll need them for mountain passes ahead.
Decline politely and continue with current rations. Ask him to point you toward the safest crossing of the Snake River.

personality_effects.James: Pragmatic. Weighs immediate survival against long-term resources.
personality_effects.Mary: Desperate for her family's welfare. Hunger weighs on her.
personality_effects.Thomas: Weakest member. Fresh meat would strengthen him considerably.

low.journal_entry: September 1st, 1848 — Near the Snake River, Idaho Territory. We have lost Sarah and William to fever and dysentery. Four of us remain. A Shoshone hunter appeared at dusk with meat to trade. We are thin-ribbed and tired, and the mountains wait for no one. James must decide quickly: oxen are irreplaceable, but so is strength when climbing begins.

low.title: Oxen Gone Lame
low.description: One of your four oxen has developed a pronounced limp, favoring its front left leg. The animal is still mobile but clearly in pain. You notice swelling above the hoof—likely an abscess or stone bruise from the rocky canyon terrain. Your remaining three oxen are tired after weeks on the trail. You have roughly 300 miles left to reach the Willamette Valley, and winter weather is approaching fast.

choices.label: Rest the oxen for two days and treat the injury with poultice and warm water.
Push on at steady pace. The oxen will have to manage.
Slaughter the lame oxen for meat and redistribute the load among three.

personality_effects.James: Leader feels the weight of thinning resources. Three dead already. The math is brutal.
personality_effects.Mary: The loss of human life has hardened her practicality, but she still flinches at necessary cruelty to animals.

low.journal_entry: September 10th. We are 1,400 miles from our starting point. One of the oxen has gone lame in the rocky canyons—abscess or stone bruise, difficult to say. The animal cannot be spared. We have no veterinary knowledge, no blacksmith within fifty miles. James and I disagreed on what should be done. He is thinking like a commander counting losses. I am thinking of winter, and whether three oxen can pull a wagon through mountain snow. We have very little medicine left. Thomas, Sarah, and William are buried in graves that will be forgotten within a season. The living must decide what living costs.

low.title: The Abandoned Wagon
low.description: As your party crests a ridge above the Snake River valley, you spot the skeletal remains of a wagon—canvas rotted away, axles splintered, personal effects scattered across the dust. A child's wooden doll lies near the wreckage. The oxen bones suggest the animals died here, possibly months ago. There are no graves nearby. You have perhaps two hours of daylight left to decide whether to investigate thoroughly, salvage what remains, or press on toward the river crossing before nightfall.

choices.label: Search the wreckage for supplies and clues about what happened.

consequences.description: You find scattered hardtack, a pouch of rifle balls, a worn jacket, and a broken axle piece. The human remains—partially preserved by the dry climate—suggest death occurred within the past year. James is sobered by the reality; Mary's dysentery worsens from the exertion and psychological strain. You lose daylight and fall short of your intended camp.

choices.label: Take only what you can grab quickly—the jerky trade has bought you time—and make the river crossing before dark.

consequences.description: You press hard toward the Snake River crossing. The night is bitterly cold, but you reach water and make camp with fuel for a fire. Mary recovers slightly from the momentum of forward progress. James feels the pull of discipline overcoming morbid curiosity.

choices.label: Bury whatever human remains you can find and leave a marker—it's the right thing, regardless of time lost.

consequences.description: You spend the remainder of daylight gathering stones and digging a shallow grave in the hard ground. Mary's hands tremble as she helps; the ritual steadies something in her spirit despite her fever. You fashion a simple wooden cross from wagon boards. The effort exhausts your food reserves and consumes medical supplies for blisters, but your party's resolve hardens. You camp without fire, pressed for time, but with conscience intact.

personality_effects.James: Each choice carries weight. The pragmatist in him wars with the man who buried Sarah and William weeks ago.
personality_effects.Mary: Her illness renders her vulnerable to both hardship and moral clarity. Compassion might bolster her will to survive; haste might break it further.

low.journal_entry: Sept 15 — Found a wagon on the ridge above the Snake. Someone's grave was never marked. The doll lay in the dust like an accusation. How many more are scattered across this desert? The river is close. We must choose between honoring the dead and saving ourselves. There is no good answer here, only the one we can live with.

low.title: The Abandoned Homestead
low.description: While picking through dense forest for firewood, Thomas discovers the skeletal remains of a cabin—fresh enough that blackened timbers still stand. Inside, scattered across rotting floorboards: a cast-iron pot, a leather-bound ledger swollen with moisture, and three burlap sacks. Two contain moldy grain. The third holds salted meat, largely preserved. The cabin's previous inhabitants left no bodies, no graves nearby. Signs suggest departure was hasty—tools abandoned, supplies left behind. Mary's fever has worsened; she shivers despite the heavy blanket. The rain intensifies.

choices.label: Take the salted meat and grain. Use the cabin as shelter for the night. Let Mary rest indoors.

consequences.reasoning: The salted meat and grain ease immediate hunger. Mary's condition stabilizes with rest and warmth, though medicine is used. The group loses no ground but gains physical recovery. The damp cabin offers marginal shelter.

choices.label: Take only what you can carry—the salted meat, nothing else. Press on through the rain to make better time.

consequences.reasoning: The salted meat provides sustenance, but pushing through mud and rain while Mary worsens rapidly tests everyone's endurance. Her fever climbs. The party gains distance at significant physical cost. Morale suffers from the decision to abandon what could have been respite.

choices.label: Salvage the cast-iron pot and ledger, make camp here for two days. Try to cure more of the meat over a slow fire.

consequences.reasoning: Deliberate curing requires cloth strips to smoke the meat and careful fire management in wet conditions. Clothes suffer minor damage from smoke and weather. The ledger—if readable—might offer information about water sources or Shoshone territories ahead. Mary improves substantially. The party loses two days but gains food security and the practical value of an iron pot.

personality_effects.James: The leader weighs survival against progress. Two comrades are already dead; the calculus of rest versus distance grows sharper each day.
personality_effects.Mary: Fever clouds her thinking. She defers to the group but clearly needs shelter. Her consent to push forward would mask desperation.
personality_effects.Thomas: The discovery excites him—proof that people have traveled this way before, lived here. He sees opportunity where others see haunting emptiness.

low.journal_entry: October 1st, 1848 — Found the ruins of a cabin in the forest. Whoever built it abandoned it in haste. The place smells of rot and old ash. Mary's fever worsens in this damp cold. We found food—salted meat, some grain gone bad. Thomas wants to know who lived here and why they left. I don't know if staying here costs us time we cannot afford, or if moving forward will cost us Mary. The ledger is waterlogged but legible in parts. One date visible: 1846. Two years. This land does not give easily.

low.title: The Oxen Are Failing
low.description: One of your three remaining oxen has gone lame—likely from the rocky terrain of the past two days. It cannot continue. The animal is suffering, moving with visible pain. You have two choices: shoot the ox mercifully and lighten the load, or attempt to nurse it through the remaining 400 miles to the Willamette Valley. The other two oxen are tired but sound. Rain falls steadily. Your supplies are already dangerously low.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Preserve ammunition and time for the journey ahead.

consequences.mood_entry: Necessary mercy. We cannot afford to slow for a crippled animal.

choices.label: Rest the ox for three days. Use the time to repair gear and let everyone recover.

consequences.mood_entry: Gambled on rest. The ox showed signs of improvement. Our stores diminish, but so does our exhaustion.

choices.label: Leave the ox behind. Redistribute its load to the other two and push on.

consequences.mood_entry: Abandoned the creature to the forest. Two oxen now carry what three once did. The guilt settles like the fog.

James.description: As leader, the decision falls to you. Mary watches for your choice.

Mary.description: Mary's composure depends on whether mercy or pragmatism wins the day.

low.journal_entry: October 10th, 1848. The rain will not stop. An ox has gone lame—we can see it in every step. We have come 1,700 miles and buried three souls. Two remain besides myself. We cannot carry the animal, and it cannot carry itself. The forest is dark with fog. Every choice now feels like it costs us something we cannot spare.

low.title: The Flour Catastrophe
low.description: During last night's creek crossing, water seeped into two of your flour sacks despite careful wrapping. This morning, you discover the damage: roughly 40 pounds of flour have turned to paste, and another 30 pounds are damp enough to mold within days if not dried immediately. The coyotes you heard were likely drawn by the scent of spilled grain. You have three options: spend a full day drying what can be salvaged, discard the ruined flour and press on, or trade with a Pawnee hunting party you spotted at dawn—they're willing to exchange 60 pounds of dried corn for some of your metal tools.

choices.label: Halt the wagon train for a full day to dry the damp flour by fire and sun.
Discard all ruined and compromised flour. Accept the loss and continue at steady pace.
Trade 2 spare parts to the Pawnee for 60 pounds of dried corn.

low.journal_entry: April 28, 1848. A careless ford has cost us dearly. The creek was shallow, but the current stronger than expected—water found its way past canvas and rope. Now we face hard choices on the prairie with supplies already stretched thin. The coyotes circled us all night, drawn by grain and desperation. Whatever we decide, we cannot afford another mistake like this one.

low.title: Fever in the Camp
low.description: William wakes with a high fever and severe chills. Mary suspects it's the fever that has plagued travelers along this stretch—likely brought on by the mosquitoes and damp river air. His cough is productive and his skin burns hot to the touch. The condition is serious enough that he cannot travel today, and the group must decide how to proceed. Remaining here costs time and supplies; pushing on risks his life.

choices.label: Remain at camp for two days. Use the medicine and rest to treat William aggressively.
Continue at a slow pace. Use minimal medicine and hope William improves on his own.
Move William to the rear wagon where he can rest fully while the group travels at normal pace. Use what medicine you have.

personality_effects.Mary: As the group's de facto caregiver, Mary is deeply invested in William's survival. She will resent any choice that seems to gamble with his life.
personality_effects.William: His fever clouds his judgment, but he is conscious enough to know whether the party is making sacrifices for him. Feeling abandoned will worsen his condition beyond the physical illness.
personality_effects.James: As leader, James bears responsibility. A decision to abandon hope—or to waste precious supplies—will weigh on him either way.

low.journal_entry: June 1st, 1848. William fell ill this morning with a burning fever and a cough that rattles his whole frame. Mary believes it is the river fever that has taken others before us. The mosquitoes here are relentless—they have plagued us for days, and now we see the cost. We have perhaps three ounces of quinine bark left and some willow-bark tea. The question is whether to stop, to push on, or to find a middle path. We are 450 miles from the start and still have a hard journey ahead. Every day matters, but a man's life matters more. God grant us wisdom.

low.title: The Pawnee Scout
low.description: As your party makes camp near a bend in the river, a Pawnee scout approaches on horseback from the west. He is alone, unarmed, and signals peaceful intent. Through gestures and broken English, he indicates that a safe ford exists two miles upstream—but warns that a Lakota hunting party was spotted five miles north yesterday, moving east. He offers to guide you to the crossing for trade goods. James notices Mary shivering despite the heat, and Thomas is favoring his left leg from the wagon salvage work this morning.

choices.label: Accept the guide's offer. Trade 15 lb of food and 20 rounds of ammunition for safe passage to the ford.

consequences.description: The scout leads you efficiently upstream. The ford is solid, well-marked by previous travelers. You cross without loss. However, Mary's condition worsens in the humid afternoon heat—cholera advances. The others feel safer with the guide's local knowledge.

choices.label: Thank the scout politely but decline. Search for the ford yourselves; avoid any entanglement with local tribes.

consequences.description: You spend two frustrating days probing upstream. Sarah cuts her foot badly on submerged rocks while wading to test depth. The party loses faith in James's decision-making. You eventually find a passable crossing four miles upstream, but time and resources are wasted. No contact with Lakota, but the delay costs you.

choices.label: Invite the scout to camp for supper and gather intelligence. Offer 5 lb of food, no ammunition.

consequences.description: The scout shares a meal and describes the ford in detail—and warns more pointedly: the Lakota party numbers eight men, well-armed, and unpredictable. He speaks of earlier conflicts with wagon trains. You learn he belongs to a Pawnee band camped ten miles south. He departs at dusk without guiding you further. Mary must use precious medicine to manage fever. The intelligence is valuable but incomplete.

personality_effects.James: Recognizes the scout's honesty but faces the weight of decision: trust a stranger or self-reliance? His judgment is under strain.
personality_effects.Mary: Too ill to offer counsel. Her survival depends on swift, safe travel.
personality_effects.Thomas: Injured and skeptical of strangers. Prefers the proven path.
personality_effects.Sarah: Frightened by talk of Lakota raiders. Trusts James to keep her safe.

low.journal_entry: June 15, 1848 — River Valley, Great Plains. A Pawnee scout came to us this evening, alone and calm. He knows the country better than any map. Mary grows worse by the hour. The heat here is merciless, and the mosquitoes bite through cloth. There is talk of Lakota to the north. We must reach that ford tomorrow, one way or another. William's absence is felt acutely now—an extra hand, an extra gun. Every choice feels heavier when one is down to five oxen and four in meds.

low.title: The Dust Storm Warning
low.description: Late afternoon, a Pawnee trader you met this morning rides back toward your wagons, urgency in his posture. He gestures toward the western horizon, where a massive dust storm is building—the kind that can swallow a wagon train whole. He offers to guide you to a creek bed three miles north, off the main trail, where you can shelter until it passes. The detour costs time and the creek may be dry. Pushing forward means gambling that you'll reach the next reliable water source before the storm hits.

choices.label: Accept the Pawnee guide's offer and head north to the creek bed.

consequences.narrative: The Pawnee guide leads you off the trail. The storm hits within two hours—visibility drops to thirty feet, dust coating everything. The creek bed is damp but flowing. You shelter there for the night, losing time and paying the guide, but the wagons survive intact. Sarah coughs badly from the dust; her health suffers. The party's morale lifts knowing you made the right call.

choices.label: Thank the guide politely and maintain course toward Fort Kearny.

consequences.narrative: You press on. The storm hits hard an hour after sunset. For six hours, the party drives blind through choking dust, visibility near zero. One ox stumbles and breaks a leg—you must shoot it. Ammunition, rations, and water are consumed in panic. Thomas and Sarah are badly sickened. Mary's eyes are seriously irritated. James keeps everyone moving but questions form in every face. You cover eight miles, but at terrible cost.

choices.label: Circle the wagons now and wait for the storm to pass here.

consequences.narrative: You corral the wagons and distribute wet cloth for faces. The storm rages overhead for eight hours—sand and dust penetrate everything despite precautions. Water and food are consumed; you use medicine to treat respiratory irritation. No one is seriously hurt, but tempers fray from confinement. The oxen are spooked and restless. You lose a day but avoid catastrophe. Thomas grumbles about lost time; James's steady leadership prevents panic.

personality_effects.James: calm, pragmatic. Dislikes unnecessary risk but respects good information.
personality_effects.Mary: practical and observant. Trusts James's judgment but worries about Sarah.
personality_effects.Thomas: impatient, eager to reach Fort Kearny. Skeptical of Native advice.
personality_effects.Sarah: anxious in extreme conditions. Her health is already fragile.

low.journal_entry: July 10th — High Plains, near the Platte. A Pawnee man warned us of a storm coming from the west. The clouds build like dark mountains. We chose our path this afternoon: trust or press on. Either way, this land tests us. William would have complained about the heat. His grave is somewhere behind us now, like Henderson's.

low.title: Mountain Fever Spreads
low.description: Mary's mountain fever worsens overnight. Her cough deepens, and by dawn she's delirious with fever. Thomas now complains of chills and a sore throat—the same symptoms Mary showed three days ago. Your medicine supply is limited, and the nearest trading post is still forty miles away. The sluggish ox from yesterday is also showing signs of illness, moving slowly and refusing to eat.

choices.label: Use two of your three remaining medicines on Mary and Thomas immediately. Reduce rations to conserve food for a longer journey if needed.
Ration the medicine—give Mary half a dose, hold the rest in reserve. Push the pace to reach Fort Laramie faster, hoping the change in elevation helps.
Make camp here for two days. Rest Mary and Thomas, nurse the sick ox, and prepare herbal remedies from foraged plants. Accept the delay.

personality_effects.James: Leader's burden: watching members fall ill tests resolve
personality_effects.Mary: Fever clouds judgment; survival depends on others' choices
personality_effects.Thomas: Fear of contagion and uncertainty about his own fate

low.journal_entry: August 15th. The mountains are beautiful and merciless. Mary's fever has taken deeper hold, and now Thomas shows the same sickness. One ox is worthless—barely moves. We've lost two already; I will not lose more to this fever if medicine and rest can prevent it. Every choice narrows our path forward. We are 1,150 miles from Independence, but tonight it feels like we're only measuring distance in heartbeats.

low.title: Ruined Provisions
low.description: As you make camp this evening, Mary discovers that yesterday's creek crossing did more damage than initially thought. The flour sack, despite your efforts to dry it, has begun to mold. A foul smell emanates from the burlap. You estimate roughly 8 pounds of your precious flour is now inedible. With winter approaching and the Willamette Valley still weeks away, every pound of food matters. The party gathers around the fire, exhausted and worried.

choices.label: Discard the moldy flour entirely and stretch remaining rations further.
Mix the moldy flour with fresh rations—mold won't kill you, just makes it unpleasant. Use it slowly.
Hunt game tomorrow morning instead of traveling. Delay departure to secure fresh meat.

James.description: Leader bears responsibility for supply management. Any choice weighs on him.

Mary.description: The discovery of spoilage troubles her. She fears the journey's toll.

Thomas.description: Pragmatic, but hunger gnaws at him as rations dwindle.

low.journal_entry: September 1st, 1848 — Snake River region. The creek that tested our oxen yesterday has proved more costly than its crossing. Mary found the flour sack this evening, thick with mold and smelling of rot. Eight pounds—perhaps more—now fit only for the fire. We are 1,300 miles from Independence and perhaps 300 from the Willamette Valley, but the distance feels immense when the cupboard empties. The nights grow cold. I fear what November will bring if we cannot make better time. The coyotes called again last night. At least they do not need flour.

low.title: Dust Storm in the Canyon
low.description: As your party navigates through a narrow canyon section of the Snake River basin, the wind picks up dramatically. Within minutes, a thick dust storm rolls through, reducing visibility to mere feet. Your oxen grow anxious and difficult to control. The canyon walls offer some shelter, but you must decide whether to press forward cautiously or make camp and wait it out. You have perhaps three hours of daylight remaining.

choices.label: Push forward slowly through the storm, feeling along the canyon wall.

consequences.narrative: The party proceeds cautiously, leading the oxen by hand. One animal stumbles on loose rock and refuses to move further; you must leave it behind. By evening, the storm begins to clear, but your clothes are coated in grit and everyone's eyes are raw. You made progress, but at cost.

choices.label: Make camp immediately and wait for the storm to pass.

consequences.narrative: You find a sheltered alcove and hunker down with the animals. The storm rages through the night and into the next day, obscuring the canyon completely. Your meager rations stretch thinner as you wait. By the afternoon of the second day, the dust settles and visibility returns. You lost time, but your oxen are rested and your party intact.

choices.label: Send one person ahead on foot to scout while the others wait.

consequences.narrative: James volunteers to scout ahead through the dust. He returns two hours later, coughing and with a deep cut on his leg from a hidden rock outcrop—he used the last of your medicine to treat the infection risk. He reports the canyon narrows further ahead and a rockfall blocks the main trail. The party must find a way around at first light. Everyone is shaken by how close James came to being lost entirely.

low.journal_entry: September 10th — Canyon dust storm today. The air turned brown as silt, and we could barely see the oxen in front of us. The canyon walls trapped the wind and made it worse. We lost one good animal and at least two days, but we're still moving. Snow in the passes, they said. We don't have much time left, and we're running light on food. Mary hasn't complained once, though I see her rationing her portion to the animals. We'll make it through, if the mountains don't stop us first.

low.title: The Shoshone Encampment
low.description: As your party crests a ridge above a broad valley, you spot an encampment of Shoshone people—perhaps thirty individuals—near a spring two miles distant. Thin smoke rises from cooking fires. Your oxen are exhausted, your water barrels nearly empty, and Mary's dysentery has worsened. The Shoshone have not made aggressive moves, but they are watching your party. A few riders have moved closer to observe. You must decide how to proceed.

choices.label: Approach the encampment peacefully to trade for fresh water and supplies

consequences.note: Trade yields 10 lbs flour, 5 lbs jerky. James negotiates carefully; Shoshone are wary but willing to trade. Mary rests and drinks fresh water. One rider warns you to keep moving—U.S. Army patrols are behind you.

choices.label: Avoid the encampment entirely and push hard for the next water source downriver

consequences.note: Mary's condition deteriorates from thirst and strain. One ox collapses at dusk; you must leave it behind. The party pushes through the night in cold, anxious silence. You reach a seep near Snake River at dawn, but morale is brittle.

choices.label: Send James alone to negotiate while the party camps at a safe distance

consequences.note: James spends three tense hours at the Shoshone camp. Trade is modest (5 lbs jerky, some roots). Mary worries he won't return. The delay allows illness to deepen. Shoshone are cordial but unimpressed; they have little surplus to trade.

personality_effects.James: Pragmatic. Knows water is life in this country. Trusts his instincts about strangers.
personality_effects.Mary: Weakened by sickness. Desperate for rest and fresh food. Trust in James remains strong despite fear.

low.journal_entry: September 15th. We topped a ridge at midday and spotted Shoshone in the valley below—a large camp, fires burning. My throat is dust. Mary can barely ride. The oxen smell the water and strain against the yoke. We are three thousand miles from Independence, and my party has shrunk to two. I must think clearly. The Shoshone are not our enemy, but neither are we safe. What we do in the next hour may determine whether we see the Willamette Valley alive.

low.title: Oxen Bogged in Forest Mud
low.description: A cold October rain has turned the forest trail into a treacherous mire. One of your three oxen has sunk to its belly in mud near a creek crossing. The animal is panicking, thrashing uselessly. James estimates you have perhaps an hour before the ox exhausts itself or the mud claims it entirely. The creek itself is swollen from rainfall—fording it will be difficult regardless. Your supplies are damp but still manageable. The question is whether to spend hours attempting a rescue, cut the animal loose and press on with two oxen, or seek higher ground and wait for the mud to firm.

choices.label: Organize a rescue. Fell branches, build a makeshift platform, and haul the ox out.
Cut the ox loose and leave it. Use the remaining two oxen to haul supplies across the creek.
Backtrack a half-mile to higher ground, camp early, and wait for conditions to improve tomorrow.

low.journal_entry: October 1st — Forest terrain, Oregon region. Rain without mercy. An ox mired in the worst mud I've seen in weeks. The animal was worth perhaps $40 back in Missouri. We have two left, and they're growing thin. Thomas suggested we try to pull it out, but I wonder if we're throwing good effort after bad. Mary is frightened about making it through the mountains before November snow. We're 1,630 miles from Independence, Missouri. Still 250 miles at least to the Willamette Valley. The meager rations aren't helping anyone's spirits.

low.title: The Oxen Are Failing
low.description: One of your three remaining oxen has developed a severe limp. Its left front hoof is cracked and infected—you can smell the rot. The animal is losing condition rapidly in this cold, wet forest. With only three oxen left and the Willamette Valley still fifty miles ahead, losing another beast now could strand you entirely. Mary believes the animal should be put down and butchered for meat. James thinks you might nurse it along for a few more days, at reduced pace, hoping it recovers. You have laudanum and cloth—enough for basic treatment—but no guarantee it will help.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Butcher it for meat and distribute the hide for patches.
Treat the hoof with laudanum and cloth. Move slowly—one mile per day.
Leave the animal behind. Redistribute its load among the two healthy oxen and press on at steady pace.

low.journal_entry: October 10th. The off-side ox has gone lame. Three souls left, and now two oxen to bear us to the valley. James and I stood in the rain this morning, watching it hobble. The mud here is thick as tar. We are fifty miles from the Willamette, and our supplies are thin. Every choice now feels like choosing which of us gets to California and which does not.

low.title: Buffalo Herd Crossing
low.description: In the early morning, a massive buffalo herd moves across the prairie ahead of your wagons—thousands of animals stretching from horizon to horizon. The ground trembles with their passage. Your oxen are nervous, and the herd will take several hours to pass completely. You can wait it out, attempt to drive through a gap in the herd if you spot one, or take a detour around the northern edge that will add miles to your day's journey.

choices.label: Wait for the herd to pass completely. It's safer and gives Mary more time to rest.
Drive carefully through a gap in the herd. Risk stampede, but save time.
Take the northern detour around the herd. Adds distance but avoids danger entirely.

personality_effects.James: Pragmatic. Notes that buffalo herds are unpredictable but waiting costs time we can't spare.
personality_effects.Mary: Weakened by fever, she admits she'd prefer not to endure the stress of a stampede, but won't slow the group.
personality_effects.Thomas: Suggests the detour as the most prudent option. 'We came to see the frontier, not test our luck.'
personality_effects.Sarah: Excited by the spectacle. 'Look at them! How many do you suppose there are?' Her enthusiasm masks underlying anxiety.
personality_effects.William: Quiet, but his jaw is set. He's watching the oxen carefully and seems ready for whatever comes.

low.journal_entry: May 1st — We encountered a buffalo herd of truly staggering proportions this morning. The animals stretched across the prairie as far as we could see, moving south and east in a living river. The sound was unlike anything I have heard—a low rumble beneath the earth itself. The Pawnee traders we spoke with in Council Bluffs warned us of such herds. Now we understand why. Our oxen were terrified, and any miscalculation could have ended catastrophically. We made our choice and pressed on. The buffalo, indifferent to our existence, continued their ancient migration.

low.title: Buffalo Hunt Opportunity
low.description: Near midday, your scout Thomas spots a small herd of buffalo grazing two miles north of the trail—perhaps thirty animals moving slowly across open grassland. The herd is upwind, and the terrain offers decent cover. Your party has adequate ammunition and the fresh meat would substantially improve morale and provisions. However, pursuing game means losing half a day's travel, and buffalo hunting is unpredictable. A wounded animal could prove dangerous. Additionally, you've noticed William's health declining noticeably since you left him in that grave two days past—the weight of it sits heavy on the party.

choices.label: Organize a hunting party. Send Thomas and James with rifles; rest of party maintains camp.
Bypass the herd. Press forward at steady pace. Preserve ammunition and time.
Send only Thomas to scout the herd closer. Decide hunting approach based on what he reports.

personality_effects.Thomas: The young scout's eyes light up at the prospect of action and proving his worth to the party.
personality_effects.James: The weight of recent loss makes quick decisions difficult. The leader feels the responsibility keenly.
personality_effects.William: Already weakened, watching others pursue vigor rankles him—he fears slowing the party further.
personality_effects.Mary: Practical consideration: fresh meat means reduced dependence on stores and better nutrition for miles ahead.
personality_effects.Sarah: She watches the oxen with concern; they too need rest, and pushing harder without gain troubles her.

low.journal_entry: May 10th, 1848 — Prairie near Plum Creek. Thomas spotted buffalo this morning. The men are restless. Two days in camp recovering from the dust storm has frayed nerves, and we're all thinking about William—though no one speaks his name much anymore. The buffalo represent fresh provisions and a chance for the younger men to feel useful rather than haunted. But time is the currency we cannot replace on this journey, and every hour spent away from the trail is an hour closer to winter in the mountains.

low.title: Storm Gathering & Sarah's Fever
low.description: Dark clouds mass over the river valley by late afternoon. Before the party can react, Sarah collapses with fever—likely from the brackish water drunk yesterday. Mary suspects cholera or dysentery. The storm will hit within the hour, and the nearest shelter is a rocky outcropping a mile ahead, but pushing a weakened girl through rain risks pneumonia. Thomas suggests making camp here and riding out the weather, letting Sarah rest.

choices.label: Push for the outcropping shelter before the storm hits

consequences.description: The party breaks camp hastily. Sarah is strapped to a wagon as wind picks up. The rain begins hard and cold. You reach the rocks with minutes to spare, but Sarah's fever spikes. She develops a hacking cough. Mary tends her through the night with what little medicine remains.

choices.label: Make camp here and weather the storm in place

consequences.description: The party circles wagons and digs in. Rain floods the camp. William's shoes are ruined chasing a spooked ox in the downpour, and his feet are soaked and cold. But Sarah rests in the dry wagon bed, and her fever drops slightly by morning. The storm passes by dawn.

choices.label: Send James and Thomas ahead with Sarah on horseback to find better shelter; rest of party follows slowly

consequences.description: James and Thomas ride hard ahead with Sarah between them, wrapped in a blanket. They find a settler's abandoned shelter two miles on and get her inside. The main party follows in the storm and loses an ox to exhaustion and lightning strike. When they reunite at the shelter, Sarah is stable but the group is shaken. Trust fractures over the split.

personality_effects.Mary: Frustrated by lack of proper medicine. Questions James's leadership if Sarah worsens.
personality_effects.William: Anxious about Sarah. Blames himself for not catching her illness earlier.

low.journal_entry: 1 June 1848. Sarah took ill this morning—fever and chills. The Platte runs thick and murky here; doubt the water is wholesome. A terrible storm built in the west. Had to choose between shelter and rest. Made our decision and pressed on. Chimney Rock stands tall behind us now. Still 350 miles to Fort Laramie. Sarah must recover.

low.title: The Pawnee Camp Crossing
low.description: As your wagons descend into a wide valley near the Platte River, your scouts spot a Pawnee hunting camp ahead—perhaps fifty people, including women and children. They're butchering buffalo. The camp is not directly in your path, but the most direct route to water and good grazing would take you within a quarter-mile of their fires. Your oxen are thirsty. The Pawnee have already seen your dust.

choices.label: Approach openly and peacefully, offering to trade for fresh meat and information about the trail ahead.

consequences.description: The Pawnee are cautious but accept your approach. There is no violence. Through gestures and a few traded words, you obtain roughly 45 pounds of fresh buffalo meat and learn that the Platte crossing ahead is manageable but that a Lakota war party was spotted two days north. The encounter costs you 8 rounds of ammunition and 150 cents in trade goods, but your people gain confidence from successful diplomacy. Thomas in particular is heartened—he has advocated for respectful dealings all along.

choices.label: Avoid the camp entirely. Swing wide to the north, losing time and distance but keeping clear of any complication.

consequences.description: The detour adds 4 miles of hard travel through rocky, broken ground. One ox founders on a hidden stone and must be left behind—it will not recover. The party grows tense and exhausted. You reach water by evening, but the delay and the loss of an animal weigh heavily on morale. Sarah remarks bitterly that 'fear has made us wasteful.'

choices.label: Send two riders ahead under a white cloth to parley and scout their intentions before committing the wagons.

consequences.description: You dispatch James and Mary with a cloth tied to a rifle. They approach the Pawnee camp at a walk. The Pawnee are initially tense but do not move to attack. After twenty minutes of cautious exchange, they signal the wagons forward. The Pawnee are hunters, not raiders—they are moving south themselves and have no interest in conflict. The parley costs time but confirms the camp is safe to pass. Your people's nerves settle somewhat, though the tension of the moment has worn on everyone.

low.journal_entry: June 25th — We came upon a Pawnee hunting camp in the valley this afternoon. The air was thick with heat and the smell of smoke and fresh kill. The men were skinning buffalo, and the sight of fresh meat stirred our hunger. We were faced with a choice: trust or fear. The decision rested on how we read the intentions of people we could not speak to, save in the crude language of gesture. William would have counseled caution. He always did. But William is gone, and the oxen are thirsty.

low.title: The Alkali Sink
low.description: By midday, the thermometer reads 103 degrees. The landscape has turned hostile—white mineral deposits crust the earth where a creek once ran. Your scout reports that the next reliable water source lies 18 miles ahead, beyond a broad alkali flat. The oxen are already laboring in the heat, their tongues hanging. Mary points out that you passed a smaller water hole two miles back, but the scout warned it tasted foul—likely poisoned by mineral salts. Thomas, weakened from recent illness, suggests turning back to that hole rather than pushing forward.

choices.label: Push forward to the distant water source. Trust the scout's knowledge and keep the oxen moving at steady pace.

consequences.journal_entry_append: Forced march across the alkali flats. Lost an ox to heat exhaustion. The land here is dead—white salt stretches to the horizon. But we found water, if barely potable. Thomas suffered most.

choices.label: Halt and wait for the coolest hours of evening, then travel by moonlight to conserve oxen strength.

consequences.journal_entry_append: Wise to rest during the worst heat. The oxen recovered somewhat. We traveled through the night—strange and haunting, the white earth glowing pale under stars. Made slower progress but preserved our animals.

choices.label: Backtrack to the alkali hole and boil the water repeatedly. It may be drinkable if filtered through cloth and boiled long enough.

consequences.conditional_risk: dysentery possible if mineral content too high
consequences.journal_entry_append: Took a gamble on the foul water. Boiling helped, though the taste was still abominable. The oxen benefited from the rest and extra grazing. Sarah's stomach turned sour by evening—we'll need to watch her closely.

Thomas.note: Thomas's weakened state makes him especially susceptible to heat exhaustion. Choices that preserve rest or reduce distance will ease his suffering more noticeably than others.

Mary.note: Mary's observation about the alkali hole suggests she's thinking practically, though her concern is valid. She remains steady under stress.

low.journal_entry: July 10th. The plains have become an oven. Water is life now—more precious than ammunition or coin. We stand at a crossroads, and every choice carries weight. The oxen are failing. Thomas is fading. We must decide whether to trust distance and the scout's word, or take our chances with the poisoned hole behind us. There is no good answer here, only the least terrible one.

low.title: Chimney Rock and the Shoshone Hunters
low.description: As your party rounds a bend in the trail through the rocky foothills, you spot three Shoshone hunters on horseback examining fresh elk tracks near the path ahead. They notice your wagons simultaneously. One of them—a man in his forties wearing a trade blanket—raises his hand in a peaceful gesture. His companions remain alert but do not reach for weapons. The terrain here is narrow; passing around them would require leaving the trail and risking your wagons on loose scree.

choices.label: Approach slowly with James at the front, showing open hands. Attempt to trade for fresh meat.

consequences.description: The lead hunter nods cautiously. After tense minutes of gesturing and pointing at your dwindling supplies, an exchange occurs—you trade $99.71 and six rounds of ammunition for roughly 48 pounds of freshly butchered elk meat, still warm. The hunters depart northward without incident. Your party eats well that evening for the first time in weeks, though the meat must be consumed or preserved quickly in this heat.

choices.label: Stop the wagons at a distance and wait. Let the hunters pass first.

consequences.description: Your party halts nervously. The hunters, uncertain of your intentions, watch for several minutes before riding off toward the mountains. The delay costs you a full day—your oxen grow restless in the heat, and water must be rationed. More troubling, Mary and Thomas voice frustration: you had fresh meat within reach and chose caution over survival. Two days of travel are consumed by this standoff and the slower pace afterward.

choices.label: Attempt to drive quickly past them on the trail, wagons at a faster pace.

consequences.description: Your wagons lurch forward at an unsafe pace on rocky ground. One Shoshone hunter rides briefly alongside, clearly signaling you to slow down—a warning, not a threat. But your rear wagon strikes a boulder hidden by dust; the left rear axle cracks severely. You lose one oxen to injury and must stop immediately. The hunters ride off, uninterested in a desperate caravan. Repairs take hours. James's commanding decision—born of fear rather than reason—has damaged morale and cost you a critical beast of burden. The spare part inventory cannot fully compensate.

low.journal_entry: August 1st, 1848. We encountered three Shoshone hunters near the rocks that some call Chimney Rock, though none of us have seen it yet. The mountains press in from all sides now. Sarah and William are gone these six weeks past—buried far behind us. We are four, and our food runs to nothing. The elk meat those hunters carried would have saved us days of slow hunger. Every decision now is survival or pride.

low.title: Mountain Fever Spreads
low.description: Mary's condition worsens overnight. Her fever peaks at 103 degrees. By dawn, Thomas complains of chills and body aches—the same symptoms Mary exhibited two days ago. You have three doses of medicine remaining. James appears exhausted from tending the sick. The party has made good time, but the Rocky Mountains demand steady progress. Delay risks early snow; pushing on risks losing more people.

choices.label: Halt for two days. Use two doses of medicine on Mary and Thomas. Let James rest.
Continue at steady pace. Ration one dose of medicine to Mary only. Thomas will have to manage.
Push to fast pace. Skip medicine doses entirely—save them for true emergencies. The altitude might clear their fevers naturally.

personality_effects.James: Torn between caution and urgency. The weight of leadership shows.
personality_effects.Mary: Fever-addled but conscious. Worried about slowing the party.
personality_effects.Thomas: Angry at the injustice of illness at the worst moment.

low.journal_entry: August 15, 1848. We stand at 7,400 feet elevation. Mary burns with fever. Now Thomas shows signs. Four buried behind us already. The medicine chest is nearly empty, and the passes will not wait. Snow waits for no sick person. Every decision now feels like gambling with lives.

low.title: The Shoshone Camp at the Water Hole
low.description: As your party approaches a reliable water source in this desolate stretch, you spot a Shoshone encampment of perhaps thirty people—mostly women, children, and elders. A few hunters are visible. They notice your wagons. An older man with a scarred face approaches cautiously, hand raised in a peaceful gesture. He speaks through hand signs and a few words of broken English: they have fresh antelope meat and will trade for manufactured goods. Your oxen are gaunt, your food critically low, and winter is coming fast.

choices.label: Trade wisely: offer a bolt of cloth and one tin of preserved peaches for thirty pounds of smoked antelope meat.
Drive hard bargain: offer only $1.50 and a needle-and-thread kit. You need to preserve your goods.
Decline and push on. You have enough food and cannot risk conflict or delay.

personality_effects.James: Leader's pragmatism tested—feed the party or save resources?
personality_effects.Mary: Exhausted and hungry. Resents rationing. Any trade for food lifts her spirits.
personality_effects.Thomas: Young and resilient but growing weaker. Needs protein to maintain strength for labor.

low.journal_entry: September 1st, 1848. Saw smoke rising from a water hole and found a Shoshone encampment. The old man with the scarred face was neither hostile nor overly friendly—just practical. We are thin as rails and the oxen worse. Sarah and William lie buried behind us now. Every decision feels like we are negotiating with death itself. The scarred man's antelope smelled like survival.

low.title: The Shoshone Encampment
low.description: As your party moves through the sagebrush near the Snake River, you spot smoke rising from a canyon ahead. Within an hour, you encounter a Shoshone encampment of perhaps thirty people — hunters and families moving south for winter. A man in his fifties, wearing a trade blanket and carrying a rifle, approaches on horseback. He is cautious but not hostile. Through gestures and the few words your party understands, he makes clear that the Shoshone have fresh antelope meat and dried fish. He points to Mary, clearly ill, then to your meager stores. The message is plain: his people will trade food for something of value — ammunition, clothing, or money — though they seem most interested in your spare oxen.

choices.label: Trade ammunition for food. Your oxen are irreplaceable this late in the season.
Trade one ox for a large quantity of preserved meat and fish.
Trade $40 in money and negotiate hard for whatever you can get.
Politely decline and move on. Mary's condition may improve on its own.

personality_effects.James: Leader must weigh survival against resources. Each choice reveals priorities.
personality_effects.Mary: Desperately needs nutrition and rest. Her morale directly tied to whether party invests in her recovery.

low.journal_entry: September 15, 1848 — Snake River Country. Met with Shoshone hunters this afternoon. Their camp was orderly, their weapons well-maintained. The headman's eyes were sharp; he knew we were struggling. Mary has weakened considerably. The dust and meager rations are taking their toll. We have barely enough to reach the Willamette Valley. Every pound of food, every round of ammunition must now be weighed against what lies ahead. Winter will not wait.

low.title: The Sluggish Ox and the Muddy Pass
low.description: October rain has turned the forest floor to mire. Your third ox—the one that drank from the alkali pool three days back—moves with obvious lameness. Its left rear leg buckles periodically. The other two oxen are sound, but they cannot pull the full weight of the wagon through this mud alone. You've reached a fork: the main trail climbs steeply through dense timber for another six miles before descending into clearer country. A Pawnee guide you met yesterday mentioned a flatter route along a creek bed to the north—longer by perhaps two miles, but easier going. However, that route is less traveled and you have only one day of clear memory of its landmarks.

choices.label: Push the ox harder through the main trail. It may recover with rest once we reach dry ground.

consequences.message: The ox collapses in the mud at mile four. Rather than watch it suffer further, you make the difficult decision to put it down. You press on with two oxen, the wagon riding heavier. The remaining animals strain visibly.

choices.label: Take the creek-bed route. It costs distance but spares the weakened ox.

consequences.message: The northward route follows a creek through older-growth forest. Travel is slower but the soft ground near the water is more forgiving. The ox recovers somewhat with the easier pace. You lose a day and shoot two game birds for broth, but everyone arrives at the next camp in better spirits.

choices.label: Lighten the wagon. Leave non-essential supplies here and cache them for the return journey.

consequences.message: You bury a spare coat, extra rope, and a worn cooking pan beneath a marked stone. The wagon is notably lighter. All three oxen pull more easily, though the psychological weight of abandonment settles over camp. If the return route is attempted next spring, supplies may not be here.

personality_effects.James: The decision falls to you. Your judgment will be remembered.
personality_effects.Mary: She studies the weak ox with resignation. 'Whatever you decide, James, we move forward.'
personality_effects.Thomas: He favors the lighter load. 'Some things cannot continue with us. Best to know it now.'

low.journal_entry: October 1st, 1848. The rains came hard last night. One ox is lame from the alkali—a costly mistake. We stand at a true crossroads. The trail ahead is rough but known. The creek route buys time. And there is always the harder choice: travel lighter, leave something behind. The Oregon country does not reward sentiment. We will decide at first light.

low.title: Soggy Supplies and Dwindling Stores
low.description: The creek crossing yesterday has done its damage. Your flour—already rationed thin—has absorbed water and begun to sour. You estimate perhaps 8 pounds remain usable; the rest will mold within days in this damp Oregon forest. With only 35 pounds of food total and winter approaching the Willamette Valley, this loss cuts deep. Mary suggests drying what you can over a smoky fire, though fuel is wet and smoke will be poor. James, your leader, notes grimly that the three surviving oxen are gaunt and tiring. One shows signs of lameness from yesterday's ford.

choices.label: Attempt to dry the flour over a smoky fire. It will consume time and fuel, but might salvage another 5–6 pounds.
Discard the ruined flour entirely and ration harder. Push forward at steady pace to reach settlements before deeper winter.
Hunt for game to offset the flour loss. Send out James and Mary for two hours while you rest the oxen.

low.journal_entry: 10 October, 1848. — The flour is lost to the creek. We stand at 1,700 miles with three souls gone and three oxen barely holding. The forest presses close, dripping cold. I can see my breath. Mary's face is thin. We must decide what hunger we can bear, and what ground we must cover. The Willamette is perhaps two weeks' hard travel. The coyotes know we are here. So does the season.

low.title: Fever in the Valley
low.description: Sarah wakes with a high fever and severe headache. The heat and humidity of this river valley, combined with the thick mosquito swarms near camp, have likely brought on a bout of fever—possibly ague or another river-valley ailment common to this region. She is coherent but too weak to ride. The day is already hot, and afternoon storms are building to the west. You must decide how to proceed.

choices.label: Halt camp for two days. Use medicine to treat her fever and let her recover.

consequences.description: You dose Sarah with your remaining medicine—willow bark tea and a poultice for the fever. By the second day, her temperature drops and her strength begins to return. The party appreciates the decision to care for one of their own rather than press onward. The delay costs you time, but Sarah recovers enough to travel slowly.

choices.label: Continue at a slow pace. Sarah rides in the wagon while the party moves only 8 miles today.

consequences.description: You give Sarah one dose of medicine and place her in the wagon, hoping rest during travel will help. The constant jolting of the wagon over rough ground makes her condition worse. She alternates between chills and burning heat. The slow pace frustrates William, who worries aloud that delays mean missing good mountain crossings later. By evening, Sarah's fever has worsened despite the medicine.

choices.label: Push hard today—travel 20 miles before dark, then camp and treat her tonight.

consequences.description: You decide speed matters more than comfort. Sarah is bundled into the wagon, feverish and miserable. The party drives hard through the humid afternoon, racing ahead of the storm clouds. Everyone is exhausted by the time you make camp. Sarah's fever spikes dangerously high that night. Mary tends to her with medicine, but the day's ordeal has shaken the group. Thomas questions whether rushing a sick person serves anyone.

personality_effects.James: The leader's judgment will be tested by how he balances duty to an individual against the needs of the group.
personality_effects.Mary: As the most level-headed member, she watches to see if the decision reflects wisdom or desperation.
personality_effects.Sarah: Her suffering is immediate and visible; she will remember who prioritized her care.
personality_effects.William: Impatient by nature, he is concerned about delays but also worried about Sarah's survival.
personality_effects.Thomas: He values fairness and will judge harshly if the group is pushed for selfish reasons.

low.journal_entry: June 1st, 1848. Sarah fell ill this morning with a high fever and chills. The river valleys are beautiful but treacherous—the mosquitoes thick as clouds, and the air itself seems to carry sickness. We are 450 miles from Independence, well into dangerous territory. Every decision from here forward will matter. We have medicine enough for a few more illnesses, but not many. The mountains still await, and a sick traveler on the trail is a risk we cannot ignore forever.

low.title: The Buffalo Herd
low.description: Late afternoon. The lookout spots dust on the horizon—a massive buffalo herd moving northeast across the plains, perhaps two miles distant. The animals number in the hundreds, maybe more. Your oxen grow restless at the vibration and smell. The herd's path will intersect the trail within the hour. Storm clouds darken the western sky, and the temperature is dropping fast. You must decide whether to push through before the herd arrives, circle wide to avoid them entirely, or hold camp and wait for them to pass.

choices.label: Push the oxen hard and cross the trail ahead of the herd.

consequences.reasoning: Hard driving exhausts the oxen; one stumbles in prairie dog burrows and must be shot. The herd's dust and noise panic the animals. You cross with minutes to spare before the lead buffalo reach the trail, but everyone is shaken and Thomas suffers heat exposure.

choices.label: Wheel northwest and circle wide around the herd's flank.

consequences.reasoning: The detour adds two days and eighteen pounds of food consumed navigating unfamiliar terrain. No water sources appear where you expected. The storm passes you by—a blessing—and the oxen rest. Sarah's morale improves; she appreciates the caution. The delay is costly but safe.

choices.label: Make camp and observe the herd. Trade for fresh meat if possible.

consequences.reasoning: Pawnee hunters are traveling with the herd. You trade eight rounds of ammunition and seventy-five cents for thirty-five pounds of fresh-killed buffalo meat. The storm hits hard that night—heavy rain, lightning—but your camp holds. The hunters share stories and tobacco. Spirits lift considerably, and fresh meat does wonders for health and resolve.

James.reasoning: The leader's caution frustrates James; he sees delay as weakness.

Mary.reasoning: Still weak from fever, the hard driving worsens her condition. She resents being pushed.

Thomas.reasoning: Thomas finds the encounter with the Pawnee hunters fascinating and is grateful for the rest.

Sarah.reasoning: The fresh meat and sense of safety reinvigorate her. She helps prepare the buffalo hide.

low.journal_entry: June 25th. A sight to humble any mortal—the buffalo came like a dark tide across the plains, thousands strong, shaking the earth beneath us. We made our choice and held camp. Pawnee hunters traveling with the herd proved friendly enough. Traded ammunition and coin for fresh meat; it was worth every cent. The storm that followed was fearsome but we weathered it. Tomorrow we push on. The oxen are stronger for the rest, and our bellies are full.

low.title: Mountain Pass Decision
low.description: August 1st, 1848. Your party has climbed into genuine high country. The trail splits: the established route winds north through a broader pass, adding perhaps three days of travel but well-marked by previous wagons. A Shoshone trader you met at the last settlement mentioned a southern shortcut—steeper, crossing exposed ridges, but saving five days. Snow caps the peaks above. Your flour is still damp from yesterday's creek crossing, and Thomas has developed a persistent cough. The afternoon sky darkens with storm clouds.

choices.label: Take the established northern route—safer, slower, certain.

personality_effects.James: steady

choices.label: Attempt the southern shortcut—faster, risky in approaching storm and high altitude.

personality_effects.James: reckless

choices.label: Camp here for two days, dry the flour properly, let Thomas recover before deciding.

personality_effects.James: cautious
facing hard choice in unfamiliar terrain

low.journal_entry: August 1st, 1848. We stand at a crossroads in the mountains—literally and by circumstance. The flour we salvaged yesterday is still damp; mold will ruin it within days if we don't address it. Thomas coughs constantly now, whether from the damp night air or something deeper, I cannot say. The Shoshone were helpful enough, but their 'shortcut' is no guarantee. Mary looks exhausted. Our meager rations are stretching thin, and we are still a month from the Willamette Valley if fortune favors us. Snow above. Storm coming. Every choice costs something.

low.title: The Shoshone Trade Offer
low.description: As your party navigates a rocky canyon at elevation, a small group of Shoshone hunters appears on the ridge above—perhaps eight or nine men on horseback. They don't approach aggressively, but gesture downward. After tense moments, an older man rides down with a younger translator. They've observed your wagons and offer trade: fresh elk meat and deer hides in exchange for ammunition or cloth. The hides would help insulate against mountain cold; the meat could extend your supplies significantly. The translator mentions snow coming hard in two weeks. He seems genuine, but ammunition is scarce, and cloth is already stretched thin.

choices.label: Trade 25 rounds of ammunition and 1 bolt of cloth for 40 pounds of dried elk meat and 4 deer hides.
Politely refuse and press forward at a faster pace to clear the high passes before winter arrives.
Trade only 10 rounds for 15 pounds of dried meat and negotiate for information about the safest route through the passes.

personality_effects.James: pragmatic—weighs immediate needs against winter risk
personality_effects.Mary: cautious despite fever—fears hostile encounters
personality_effects.Thomas: wary of strangers—prefers self-sufficiency

low.journal_entry: August 15, 1848. The mountains close in. Snow gleams on peaks we must cross. Shoshone hunters found us in the canyon today—civil enough, offering trade. Mary's fever hasn't broken, and we've felt the bite of altitude mornings. The choice before us: secure provisions and hire guides, push hard through exhaustion, or find middle ground. Two weeks, the translator said, before the passes ice over. That's not much time.

low.title: The Broken Axle
low.description: At dawn, while preparing to move through the canyon, you discover the wagon's rear axle has cracked—likely from the constant jolting over rocky terrain. The wagon is still mobile but fragile. Repair requires one spare part and roughly six hours of work. Alternatively, you could lighten the wagon significantly by discarding items, redistribute weight across the remaining oxen, and push forward carefully at a slower pace without repairs. A third option: abandon non-essential supplies here and continue with only what the oxen can carry without the wagon.

choices.label: Repair the axle properly using your spare part.
Lighten the wagon by discarding 5 pounds of food and moving slowly.
Abandon the wagon entirely and redistribute supplies among oxen.

low.journal_entry: September 10th, 1848 — The canyon walls tower above us, red and rust-colored. This morning we found the axle split clean through. Three good people gone already, and now the wagon threatens to give way beneath us. The canyon narrows ahead; there will be no turning back once we commit to a course. The nights grow bitter cold. Winter will not wait.

low.title: A Lame Ox in the Mud
low.description: The morning's fog has lifted, but the forest floor remains treacherous—a maze of roots and hidden stones beneath inches of mud. As the oxen strain forward, the rear left animal suddenly pulls up lame. You can see it favoring its front right leg heavily. The creature is still capable of movement, but any sustained load will worsen the injury. You have three oxen remaining, and the wagon is laden with what little you have left. The nearest settlement is still sixty miles away through this difficult country.

choices.label: Lighten the wagon by discarding non-essential items and push the lame ox forward at reduced pace.

consequences.narrative: You reduce the wagon's weight, discarding a worn blanket and some rope. The lame ox struggles but moves. Progress is slow, but steady.

choices.label: Rest the lame ox here for two days, allowing it to recover while the party waits.

consequences.narrative: The ox remains at rest while cold rain persists. Food supplies dwindle during the layover. The animal's condition improves marginally, but the delay weighs heavily on morale.

choices.label: Slaughter the lame ox for meat and redistribute its work among the remaining three.

consequences.narrative: The ox is dispatched and butchered. The meat provides substantial provisions, but losing a second animal deepens the grim reality of your situation. The remaining oxen must now bear greater strain.

personality_effects.James: The leader bears the burden of this choice. His health suffers from the weight of difficult decisions. {choice_index}
personality_effects.Mary: She has seen enough loss. Each option troubles her deeply. {choice_index}
personality_effects.Thomas: The younger man's morale fluctuates with the group's fortunes. {choice_index}
