low.journal_entry: October 1st — The road turns cruel in ways I did not anticipate. An ox has gone lame in this forsaken mud. We have lost two already—Sarah to fever in the summer heat, William to dysentery near Fort Kearny—and now we face losing a third beast, which amounts to losing our livelihood. The choice before us is between suffering now or suffering later, and I confess I cannot see a path that does not end in more suffering. The rain shows no sign of stopping. We are one thousand six hundred and thirty miles from our starting point, and yet I sometimes feel as though we have gone nowhere at all.

low.title: The Oxen Founder in Mud
low.description: On a particularly sodden morning, one of your three remaining oxen stumbles badly in the deep forest mud. Its front left leg folds awkwardly beneath it. The animal bellows—a sound of genuine distress. James examines the leg and suspects serious injury, though the ox can still bear weight. The rain continues. You are 1,700 miles from Independence, perhaps 400 miles from the Willamette Valley. Winter weather is coming earlier each day.

choices.label: Rest the ox for two days. Give it the best grazing you can find and hope the swelling reduces.
Shoot the ox now and process it for meat. You need food more than a lame animal.
Redistribute loads to the other two oxen and continue slowly. The injured ox may recover on the trail.

low.journal_entry: October 10th. The rains have turned this forest into a mire. One of the oxen went lame this morning—struck its leg badly in the mud. We are running thin on animals and thinner still on options. Winter will find us whether we move or wait. James has been quiet since the injury. Mary tends the fire with less vigor each evening. We are only five now, and the road stretches on.

low.title: The Pawnee Horse Traders
low.description: On the morning of April 28th, your party encounters a band of Pawnee traders on horseback—perhaps twenty strong, moving northeast across the prairie. Their leader, a man with three parallel scars across his left cheek, rides forward under a cloth flag. He signals peaceful intent. Through gestures and broken English from one younger rider, they indicate they have fresh game to trade: elk meat, dried and ready. They also want to trade for metal goods—specifically your spare wagon parts and any iron tools you can spare. The Pawnee appear well-supplied and experienced on these plains. Your group has lost weight since the dust storm delays; food stores are adequate but not abundant. The trade could strengthen your party, but parting with repair parts leaves you vulnerable to future wagon failures.

choices.label: Trade generously: offer 2 spare parts and 3 iron tools in exchange for 40 pounds of dried elk meat.
Decline the trade politely and move on; your current supplies are sufficient, and spare parts may prove critical later.
Counter-offer: trade only 1 spare part and ask for 25 pounds of meat plus information about water sources and Lakota movements ahead.

personality_effects.James: Pragmatic—prefers balanced risk.
personality_effects.Mary: Cautious about trade but values group nutrition.
personality_effects.Thomas: Eager to interact with plains peoples; frustrated by delays.
personality_effects.Sarah: Anxious about mechanical failures; protective of supplies.
personality_effects.William: Recently grieving; quieter than usual, but still invested in survival.

low.journal_entry: April 28, 1848 — Pawnee traders appeared this morning on the open prairie. A negotiation tested our judgment: do we trade durability for immediate sustenance, or preserve what little security our spare parts provide? The choice feels emblematic of this entire undertaking—every decision surrenders something to gain something else. We have buried kin. We have lost water and time. The trail ahead remains long, and we remain alive. That is both blessing and burden.

low.title: The Sluggish Ox and the River Crossing
low.description: One of your oxen, having drunk from that alkali pool two days back, has grown noticeably weak. Its pace has slowed the whole team. Now, as you approach the North Platte River crossing—swollen with spring runoff—you must decide how to proceed. A Pawnee hunter you encountered at the last camp mentioned that the ford is passable but treacherous, and that a Lakota band has been seen moving north along the river. You can rest here for a day to let the ox recover, push ahead immediately, or attempt to trade the weakened animal at a nearby trading post (though supplies are limited and prices steep).

choices.label: Rest here one full day. Give the sick ox time to recover before the crossing.
Push forward immediately. The ox will either keep pace or fall behind—cull it if necessary.
Detour to the trading post. Trade the weakened ox for supplies and a fresh animal.

personality_effects.William: The weakened ox troubles him deeply—he's grown attached to these animals. Pushing forward without rest feels cruel.
personality_effects.Mary: Ever practical, she calculates which choice wastes the least time and resources.
personality_effects.Sarah: Her waning strength makes any extra day of travel a trial; rest appeals to her.

low.journal_entry: May 1st, 1848 — Saw Chimney Rock clear against the sky this morning. Felt like a milestone, a sign we're truly committed to this journey now. But our oxen troubles weigh on us. The alkali pool was a hard lesson—one we cannot afford many more of. The North Platte lies ahead, high with snowmelt, and we must cross it soon. William is quiet tonight, tending to the sick animal. Mary studies the maps by firelight. Sarah rests her aching back. The land is beautiful and merciless in equal measure.

low.title: A Shoshone Hunting Party
low.description: Late afternoon brings riders across the prairie—six Shoshone hunters on horseback, moving parallel to your trail at a distance. They appear to be tracking buffalo herds that have been sighted to the north. One rider breaks formation and approaches slowly, hand raised in a gesture that could mean greeting or caution. Your scouts report the group is well-armed with bows and rifles. The hunter stops about fifty yards off and calls out in broken English: 'Trade? Horses good. Fresh meat good.' He gestures toward your oxen, then toward several freshly killed antelope strapped to his horse's flank.

choices.label: Approach with James and trade carefully—offer money or goods for the antelope meat. Learn what you can about the trail ahead.

consequences.description: The hunter accepts $2.50 and two pounds of sugar for four antelope quarters—fresh meat a rarity on the trail. He shares that buffalo are moving north due to heat, water is reliable at Chimney Rock (still two weeks ahead), but a Lakota hunting party has been seen further west. The encounter is civil; your party's morale lifts knowing reliable provisions lie ahead.

choices.label: Politely decline and maintain distance. Keep the wagons moving at steady pace without stopping.

consequences.description: The rider watches your refusal with an unreadable expression, then wheels his horse and rejoins his group. They drift northward and disappear into the grassland within the hour. Sarah finds the caution unnecessary and mutters about missed opportunity; William agrees quietly. Mary notes you avoided potential complications. No information gained, but no risk taken.

choices.label: Attempt to acquire one of their horses through negotiation—offer $15 and some goods. Your oxen are tiring.

consequences.description: The hunter's expression hardens at the offer. He interprets the request as questioning his animals' value and views the ammunition offer as an insult. He rejoins his group quickly; all six riders now watch from a distance with visible displeasure. Tension settles over the camp. No trade occurs. Your party argues about judgment; Thomas and James blame each other for the miscalculation. The group camps uneasily, expecting trouble that never comes.

low.journal_entry: May 10th — Prairie, 320 miles from Independence. Clear sky turning gold at dusk. Shoshone hunters crossed our path today, antelope kills fresh across their mounts. The land here belongs to them as much as to us, perhaps more. We are merely passing through. The great herds they hunt must number in the thousands, though we saw only broken groups. A reminder that the wilderness operates by its own laws, and our wagons are small things upon it. The flour is still damp from yesterday's creek crossing. Rationing must remain careful.

low.title: Sickness Spreads
low.description: Mary's cholera has worsened overnight. She runs a fever and cannot keep food down. This morning, Sarah complained of stomach cramps and diarrhea. Thomas suggests moving to higher ground away from the river—he's heard stagnant water breeds miasmas. James wants to stay put for a day, let Mary rest, and boil all drinking water. The oxen are tired from yesterday's storm and could use the rest. But the calendar doesn't favor delay; you've made good time, and the mountain passes won't wait.

choices.label: Move to high ground upstream. Breaking camp will be hard on Mary, but fresh air and distance from the river might help.
Stay in camp. Boil all water, keep Mary comfortable, and hope rest breaks the fever before it takes hold.
Push on at a slow pace. The deer meat is fresh—eat well and maintain movement. Sometimes motion aids recovery better than lying idle.

low.journal_entry: June 15th. The river valley is beautiful but cursed. Mary worsened in the night—cholera shows no mercy. Sarah now sick as well. The oxen are spent, the weather unpredictable. We stand at a crossroads: move and risk the ill, rest and waste time, or push forward and gamble on fate. William's death three weeks back taught us that hesitation can be as deadly as haste. I must choose, and soon.

low.title: A Shoshone Trade Opportunity
low.description: As your party navigates a high valley at 8,400 feet, you encounter a small band of Shoshone hunters—perhaps a dozen warriors with packhorses laden with fresh elk meat and hides. Their leader, a middle-aged man with a distinctive scar, approaches with open palms. Through gestures and the few words your party understands, he indicates willingness to trade: fresh meat and hide clothing for ammunition, metal tools, or cash. The elevation here is punishing; your oxen are breathing hard, and Sarah has begun coughing. The meat would restore strength to your depleted provisions. But ammunition is scarce, and every round counts for hunting ahead. The Shoshone are not hostile—this is genuine commerce—yet the encounter carries risk either way.

choices.label: Trade ammunition and a metal axe for 40 lbs of elk meat and two hide jackets.
Offer $40 (4000 cents) in coin instead. Keep ammunition and tools.
Politely decline and press onward. Ration your supplies more strictly.

personality_effects.James: Leader feels the weight of decision—ammunition is precious, yet so is his party's strength.
personality_effects.Mary: Practical woman; sees value in trade but worries about future hunting.
personality_effects.Thomas: Restless and skeptical; questions whether the Shoshone can be trusted.
personality_effects.Sarah: Weakened by altitude and cough; hungers for meat and warmth of proper clothing.

low.journal_entry: July 20, 1848. We stand at nearly nine thousand feet, where the air tastes thin and cold. Snow still crowns the peaks above us. We encountered Shoshone hunters this afternoon—honest traders, by all appearances. Their meat smells good. Sarah especially needs strength. We must choose: give up what little ammunition remains, spend our dwindling coin, or trust that the meager berries and salt pork ahead will sustain us. These mountains exact their price either way.

low.title: Mountain Crossing Decision
low.description: August 1st, 1848. Your party has climbed into the Rocky Mountains proper. The trail splits ahead: the northern route winds through higher passes but is shorter—perhaps 80 miles to the Shoshone territories. The southern route drops into a valley, adding 40 miles but stays lower and warmer. Your scouts report fresh snow on the northern peaks. The afternoon sky darkens with storm clouds gathering over both paths. Mary's cough has worsened. You have four oxen, limited feed for them, and meager rations for the people. Winter will come early at this altitude.

choices.label: Take the northern pass—shorter, but risk early snow and steep terrain.

consequences.description: The pass is brutal. One ox breaks a leg on loose rock and must be shot. Mary develops a fever in the thin air; her cough worsens daily. The final descent is treacherous—you lose a spare axle to a stumble, but you save ten days of travel. You emerge onto the western slope exhausted but ahead of schedule.

choices.label: Follow the southern valley—safer, longer, but uses more food and delays you.

consequences.description: The valley route is gentler on bodies and animals. Your oxen regain some condition grazing the lower meadows. The afternoon storms pass to the north. Mary's cough stabilizes with rest. The trade-off: you consume more food and lose nine days. Winter's window narrows.

choices.label: Camp here two days and hunt. Rest the oxen and resupply before choosing.

consequences.description: You make camp near a high meadow where elk graze. Thomas and James hunt; you take two elk and preserve the meat. The rest does wonders for morale and health. You move forward refreshed but stationary for two days—dangerous when winter approaches.

personality_effects.Mary: Her fever and cough dominate her thoughts. She is fearful of the high passes but terrified of running out of food. Delay frightens her.
personality_effects.Thomas: The hunter in him wants to pursue the southern route where game is more likely. He trusts the mountains but worries about Mary.
personality_effects.James: As leader, the weight of decision is yours alone. Every choice costs lives or time.

low.journal_entry: August 1st — We stand at the threshold of the high country. The peaks are beautiful and terrible. I saw Henderson's grave two days back, dated 1847. This mountain does not forgive hesitation. Mary is coughing badly. We have food enough for meager rations for perhaps twenty days more. The Pawnee jerky helps, but it will not last. We have lost Sarah and William already to dysentery and fever in the lower plains. I will not lose more if I can help it. The sun is setting. We must decide by morning.

low.title: The Pawnee Hunters' Proposition
low.description: As your party breaks camp on the morning of August 15th, three Pawnee hunters approach on horseback from the east. Their leader, a man of perhaps forty winters, gestures peacefully and offers to trade: fresh elk meat and knowledge of a passable route through the mountains ahead in exchange for ammunition and one of your oxen. His companions eye your herd with clear interest. Behind them, the peaks loom grey and indifferent. Mary shivers in the wagon, her fever unbroken. The hunters are patient but clearly expect an answer before midday.

choices.label: Accept the trade: 15 rounds of ammunition and one oxen for fresh meat and route guidance
Politely decline and continue on the marked trail; you have a map and supplies
Attempt to negotiate: offer 8 rounds and dried goods instead of an ox; keep your animals intact

personality_effects.James: The leader must weigh the party's survival against the risk of weakening your oxen team. You feel the weight of command.
personality_effects.Mary: Though fevered and weak, you understand that fresh meat and shorter routes might save lives—including your own.
personality_effects.Thomas: You wonder if the hunters mean well or if this is a trap. Four oxen already seems too few for the mountains ahead.

low.journal_entry: August 15th, 1848 — Rocky Mountains, elev. approx. 8,000 ft. — Pawnee hunters came to trade this morning. They offered elk meat and passage wisdom. Mary's fever shows no sign of breaking. The mountains stretch endless before us, and every decision feels like choosing between two kinds of risk. We are 1,150 miles from Independence. We have three oxen remaining, and these mountains are not forgiving to those short on beasts of burden. The hunters waited patient. I had to choose.

low.title: The Flour is Ruined
low.description: You discover the creek crossing yesterday has done worse damage than you thought. The flour sack, despite your efforts to keep it dry, absorbed water and has begun to mold. The smell is unmistakable—sour and rank. Mary, still weak from dysentery, stares at the ruined supplies with hollow eyes. You have perhaps 10 pounds of flour left, maybe less once you cut away the affected portions. The desert stretches ahead. Your oxen are lean. Winter is coming.

choices.label: Salvage what you can and ration even more strictly.
Hunt game to supplement before rations drop further—it will cost time and ammunition.
Push harder toward Fort Boise, gambling on reaching supply and aid sooner rather than stretching meager rations.

personality_effects.Mary: The sight of wasted food—food that might have kept her stronger—deepens her despair. She is frighteningly thin.
personality_effects.James: You curse yourself for not wrapping the flour better. Three dead already. Now this.

low.journal_entry: September 15th. The flour is gone—molded beyond use. What we salvaged tastes of creek water and rot. Mary cannot eat much of anything without it tormenting her belly. I stood there this morning with the ruined sack in my hands, wondering if I've made every wrong choice since we left Missouri. The oxen are ribs and sinew. We've two months of trail left, maybe more if weather turns. Fort Boise is still weeks away. I must decide whether to spend ammunition and time hunting, or risk the oxen driving hard toward help. Either way, something is being lost.

low.title: A Shoshone Family at the Crossroads
low.description: As your wagon train pushes through the wet forest, you come upon a small Shoshone encampment—a mother, two children, and an elderly man. They appear to be moving camp in the rain. The mother approaches cautiously, holding a freshly killed deer. She gestures toward your oxen, then to the deer, then to the children. The message is clear: she's offering to trade meat for an ox. Your stores are meager, and fresh game would sustain you well through the mountain passes ahead. But losing an ox this late in the journey—with snow warnings fresh in your ears—carries real risk.

choices.label: Accept the trade: one ox for the deer and what dried meat they can spare.
Decline politely and offer 50 cents and a blanket instead.
Attempt to barter for the deer using only ammunition—conserve oxen and coin.

personality_effects.James: Pragmatic about difficult choices; any trade weighs survival against sentiment.
personality_effects.Mary: Moved by the children's presence; prefers generosity but respects hard decisions.
personality_effects.Thomas: Growing hardened by the journey; questions whether kindness costs what they cannot afford.

low.journal_entry: October 1st, 1848. Rain pounds the forest around us. We encountered a Shoshone family this afternoon—a mother and her young ones, moving camp in weather that should keep anyone still. She had fresh venison and wanted an ox. The children looked thin. We are thin ourselves. Every animal, every pound of food, every mile represents the margin between reaching Oregon and wintering in these mountains. The warnings of snow pressed heavy on my mind as we made our choice.

low.title: The Wagon Axle Cracks
low.description: This morning, while crossing a swollen creek bed, the left rear axle of the supply wagon splits with an audible crack. The wagon lists badly, and further travel risks losing the entire load—food, medicine, and essential tools. James and Mary inspect the damage by pale October light. The single spare axle you carry could replace it, but the work will take most of the day in the rain and mud. Alternatively, you could redistribute the heaviest items across the remaining wagons and attempt to limp forward with a damaged axle, accepting the risk of total failure at any rough section of road.

choices.label: Stop and replace the axle properly. It will cost us a full day, but the wagon will be sound.
Redistribute the load and push on. Save time and supplies, but gamble with the wagon's survival.

James.description: James worries about the remaining oxen. One animal is already thin; pushing them harder in this mud may kill another.

Mary.description: Mary knows how to repair wagons—her father was a wheelwright. She could oversee the work, but the physical labor in cold rain will tax her.

low.journal_entry: October 10th. Rain has not stopped since dawn. The creek crossing cracked the supply wagon's axle clean through. James and I stood in the muck this morning, water running down our faces, deciding whether to lose a day or lose the wagon entirely. Two oxen remain in good condition; one is failing. The meat from Thomas's deer helps, but we are eating meager portions. Winter is coming hard on our heels now. Sarah and William are gone. It is only James and me pushing forward with three oxen and a damaged wagon, and we are not yet out of Oregon.

low.title: The Pawnee Hunters
low.description: On the morning of April 28th, your party encounters a group of six Pawnee hunters on horseback crossing the prairie ahead. They are armed with bows and lances, moving purposefully toward a distant buffalo herd. Their leader rides forward slowly, hand raised in a peaceful gesture. Your scouts report the hunters seem curious rather than hostile—but your supplies are limited, and trading always carries risk. You have three options: approach them diplomatically to trade for fresh meat, give them a wide berth and continue onward, or post guards and refuse contact entirely.

choices.label: Approach peacefully and attempt to trade for buffalo meat.
Circle around them at a distance and press onward.
Prepare a defensive position and signal that you are well-armed.

personality_effects.James: Leader's judgment is being tested. Peaceful contact builds reputation; defensiveness raises tension.
personality_effects.Mary: Prefers trade and diplomacy. Hostility weighs on her conscience.
personality_effects.Thomas: Cautious by nature. Respects careful preparation but sees unnecessary defensiveness as cowardice.
personality_effects.Sarah: Practical. Wants fresh meat but fears delay.
personality_effects.William: The youngest and boldest. Chafes at slow travel and wants to demonstrate capability.

low.journal_entry: April 28, 1848. We came upon six Pawnee hunters this morning, moving swift across the grass toward the buffalo grounds. The land here belongs to them—we are the visitors. James held a council. The choice before us was not simple: trade fairly, slip past like ghosts, or show we are ready for war. Whatever we decided would tell us something about ourselves and change how the road ahead feels.

low.title: The Pawnee Hunters
low.description: Late afternoon brings dust on the horizon. Your scouts report a band of perhaps a dozen Pawnee hunters on horseback, moving parallel to your wagons about a mile north. They appear to be tracking buffalo, not your party, but the situation demands caution. Your options: press forward at steady pace and hope they pass without incident, detour south toward the river to put distance between you, or attempt to establish peaceful contact by displaying trade goods.

choices.label: Continue steady pace. Do not deviate.

consequences.description: Your party maintains course. The Pawnee hunters continue northward, absorbed in their hunt. By evening they are gone. Relief settles over camp, though the encounter leaves everyone's nerves frayed. Mary appreciates the decisive leadership; James second-guesses whether caution might have been wiser.

choices.label: Detour south toward the river. Add 8 miles to today's journey.

consequences.description: You swing south toward the Platte, pushing the oxen harder. One animal begins to limp—a stone bruise forming on its hoof. The detour costs time and animal condition, but your party feels safer. Thomas respects the prudent evasion. William grumbles about the wasted effort and exhausted stock.

choices.label: Approach under a white cloth flag. Offer trade goods to demonstrate peaceful intent.

consequences.description: James rides forward with cloth tied to a rifle, carrying tobacco and a tin mirror from your trade stock. The Pawnee hunters halt. A tense negotiation—conducted in crude sign language and fragments of English—results in them accepting your gifts. They share information about buffalo herds ahead and water sources. The encounter passes peacefully, bolstering confidence in the party's diplomacy. Sarah sees humanity in exchange; William views it as cowardice and wasteful generosity. The party camps early, buoyed by successful contact.

personality_effects.James: Leader uncertain whether caution or boldness serves the party best.
personality_effects.Mary: Prefers careful judgment over risk.
personality_effects.Thomas: Values practical self-preservation.
personality_effects.Sarah: Believes peaceful relations are possible and desirable.
personality_effects.William: Suspicious of strangers; resents what he sees as weakness.

low.journal_entry: May 1st. Pawnee hunters sighted on the northern prairie, perhaps twelve strong, pursuing buffalo herds. No hostile intent observed, though James convened counsel on how best to proceed. We are now five weeks from Independence, the wagons holding steady and provisions adequate. The land grows wilder each day, and men's dispositions begin to show the strain of constant vigilance. Tonight we rest easier, though the encounter reminds us we are guests in country not our own.

low.title: The Dust Storm Approaches
low.description: By midafternoon, the sky darkens to a sickly brown. A wall of dust rises from the southwest, moving toward your wagons with visible speed. One of your outriders reports it will reach you within the hour. You have time to make a decision: push the oxen hard to outrun it, circle the wagons and weather it in place, or make for a creek bottom two miles north where you might find shelter and water.

choices.label: Race ahead to avoid the storm entirely

consequences.description: You drive the team hard. The dust catches you anyway—a mile of choking, blinding chaos. One ox stumbles badly and must be abandoned at the next stop; another is severely winded. Everyone inhales grit; your throats burn for two days. The storm passes, but the cost was high. You made extra distance, at least.

choices.label: Circle the wagons and hunker down

consequences.description: You form the wagons in a tight circle and cover the gaps with canvas. The storm hits hard—wind shrieks, dust infiltrates everything despite your efforts. It passes in four hours. One wagon wheel sustains a hairline crack, but nothing breaks. You're stationary one full day, but the oxen rest and the worst is behind you by evening.

choices.label: Head north to the creek for shelter

consequences.description: You veer north and reach the creek bottom just as the leading edge of dust arrives. The ravine offers excellent protection. While sheltering, William spots old cottonwood branches—good for repairs. The storm rages for two hours above you, but you're shielded. You lose some time doubling back southwest afterward, but morale stays solid and you discover useful materials.

personality_effects.James: The leader's decision weighs on him. If you race: he feels the urgency but questions the cost. If you shelter: steady, reliable choice reinforces his composure. If you scout north: he respects the resourcefulness.
personality_effects.William: Already weak at 60 hp. Racing or sheltering both trouble him. The northern route's discovery of materials heartens him slightly.
personality_effects.Thomas: Morale is high; he's ready for hard choices. Racing appeals to his confidence, but he sees wisdom in caution too.

low.journal_entry: May 10th — The Plains show us no mercy and no preference. A dust storm rolled in from the southwest like a living thing. We chose our path and paid or prospered for it. The grave we passed yesterday reminds us that the trail forgets no one. Tonight the sky clears. Tomorrow we push on toward Fort Laramie.

low.title: The Pawnee Trade Opportunity
low.description: As your party moves through the river valley near the Platte, a small group of Pawnee hunters—perhaps eight men on horseback—approaches your camp. They carry fresh deer meat and trade goods. The leader, a middle-aged man with deliberate English, indicates they will trade venison and dried meat for ammunition, cloth, or cash. Your group's food stores are adequate but not generous, and Mary's cholera has made her weak; fresh provisions could help. However, you have limited ammunition for the journey ahead, and cash will be needed for river crossings and other necessities. The Pawnee seem peaceful and businesslike, but this is unfamiliar territory.

choices.label: Trade 40 rounds of ammunition for 60 pounds of fresh and dried meat.
Trade $2.50 in coin for 50 pounds of dried meat and some salt.
Trade 2 bolts of cloth for 45 pounds of meat and information about water sources ahead.
Decline politely and move on. Do not trade.

low.journal_entry: June 15. Pawnee hunters met us in the valley today. The heat has been oppressive, and Mary grows weaker. William's death still weighs on us all. The dust storm cost us two days and half our water. We face a choice: spend what little we have to strengthen our provisions, or preserve our reserves and trust the land ahead. The mosquitoes here are maddening. Evening brings relief, but the storm clouds gather again to the west.

low.title: The Sluggish Ox and the Storm Coming In
low.description: By midday, the heat is oppressive. Your sluggish ox—the one that drank from the alkali pool yesterday—is moving slowly and favoring its left rear leg. The animal's breathing is labored. Meanwhile, dark clouds mass on the western horizon. Your scout reports a shallow ravine about three miles ahead that could offer shelter, but reaching it means pushing the animals hard in this heat. The alternative is to make camp here on open bluff, exposed but with water from the creek that runs alongside the trail.

choices.label: Push on to the ravine. The ox can manage three more miles, and storm shelter is worth the effort.

consequences.narrative: The sluggish ox collapses two miles from the ravine. You mercy-kill it rather than leave it to suffer. The remaining oxen strain under the extra load, and the party pushes through rising wind to reach the ravine just as rain begins. You're sheltered but exhausted.

choices.label: Camp here. Tend the sick ox, conserve strength, and weather the storm on the bluff.

consequences.narrative: The storm hits hard. Wind whips the camp, and rain soaks everything. You use a blanket stitching repair to weatherproof a tent seam. The ox recovers somewhat with rest and a poultice of willow bark. Progress halted, but the party is whole.

choices.label: Send riders ahead to the ravine with supplies. Leave the slow ox behind with minimal guard. Meet up in the morning.

consequences.narrative: The split party is vulnerable. The riders reach the ravine safely, but the rear group—including the sick ox—camps alone on the bluff. During the night, neither storm nor predators strike, but anxiety runs high. The ox is still weak come morning. The groups rejoin, but trust in your leadership wavers.

personality_effects.James: As leader, any choice reflects on you. Push hard, and they call you reckless. Camp here, and they wonder if you lack resolve.
personality_effects.Mary: She favors caution. The prudent camp will steady her nerves; reckless choices trouble her.
personality_effects.Thomas: Practical. He respects efficiency and hates wasted effort. Splitting the party frustrates him.
personality_effects.Sarah: Anxious by nature. Comfort and routine calm her. Chaos and risk unsettle her deeply.

low.journal_entry: June 25th — Bluffs. Heat like the devil's own breath. The alkali pool claimed one ox's strength. Storm clouds pile up black as sin to the west. Chimney Rock stands pale in the distance, a marker of the miles we've come and all that remain. Tonight we decide: press on, hold fast, or divide ourselves. The ox that's sick may not see Laramie. That's the plainness of this journey—some will not finish.

low.title: Sarah's Fever Breaks—But the Water Problem Worsens
low.description: Sarah's fever from yesterday has finally subsided, though she remains weak. More pressing: your scout reports that the next known water source—a shallow pond marked on your map—has evaporated entirely. The heat has been relentless. You have two days' travel before reaching the Platte River crossing. Your oxen are flagging, and dust coats everything. A Pawnee hunting party passes at a distance—no confrontation, but it reminds you this land belongs to others who know where water hides.

choices.label: Push hard toward the Platte, rationing water strictly to stretch what you have.

consequences.risk: If you fail to reach water by nightfall on day two, you will lose another party member to dehydration.

choices.label: Detour north to ask the Pawnee band where water can be found, trading supplies if needed.

consequences.outcome: The Pawnee hunters are cautious but not hostile. They point you to a spring two miles west of the main trail. Safe passage through their lands guaranteed for three days only.

choices.label: Send James ahead on horseback with minimal supplies to scout for water and return with news.

consequences.risk: James travels alone in scorching heat. High chance of success if he's lucky, but the party will move slowly and worry increases tension.

personality_effects.James: Leader role carries weight here—his decision could save or endanger everyone.
personality_effects.Mary: Watches Sarah carefully; any harm to the weakest member troubles her deeply.
personality_effects.Thomas: Practical and tough; wants efficiency over sentiment.
personality_effects.Sarah: Still recovering; any further hardship could be fatal.

low.journal_entry: July 10th—Sarah's fever passed, thank the Lord, but nature offers no mercy in these plains. The heat is a living thing, pressing down. Our flour turned to paste from the creek crossing, and now the water we counted on is gone. The Pawnee we saw today—maybe a sign of danger, maybe a chance. James must decide, and quickly. We cannot afford another mistake. William's grave is still too fresh in my mind.

low.title: A Shoshone Hunting Party
low.description: As your party makes camp in a narrow valley, a group of six Shoshone hunters approaches on horseback. Their leader, a middle-aged man with a scarred face, signals peaceful intent but points to the deer carcass Thomas butchered this morning. The meat hangs in your camp—enough for several days' meals. The Shoshone have traveled far from their main camp and appear to be running low on supplies themselves. The leader speaks some English, learned from fur trappers. He asks to trade for half the venison, offering a pair of well-made rabbit-skin blankets and information about the best mountain passes ahead. Your group is fatigued, and the meat is a precious resource. Refusing might invite trouble; accepting means less food, but you'll gain clothing and valuable route knowledge.

choices.label: Trade half the venison for the blankets and directions.

consequences.note: The Shoshone leader sketches a map in the dust showing a lower pass that will save 2–3 days of difficult terrain. The blankets reduce exposure during the cold mountain nights ahead. The gesture of goodwill stabilizes your party's spirits.

choices.label: Politely refuse but offer to buy additional game if the Shoshone hunt nearby.

consequences.note: The Shoshone agree without offense. They return with two elk haunches three days later, which you buy for $1.50. The delay costs miles, but your food security improves. Thomas approves of your self-reliance; Mary worries the extra days in the mountains invite worse weather.

choices.label: Decline firmly and move camp immediately to avoid further contact.

consequences.note: Your hasty departure panics the oxen again. One stumbles on rocky ground and breaks a leg; you must shoot it. The Shoshone leave peacefully, but your party is now down to three oxen and traveling at a crawl. Mary is furious at the decision. The incident deepens the paranoia that has already claimed two members.

personality_effects.James: Leadership tested—the right choice builds trust; the wrong one invites second-guessing.
personality_effects.Mary: Most affected by group morale and safety. Peaceful trade appeals to her; confrontation frightens her further.
personality_effects.Thomas: Proud of his hunting skill. Resists giving away meat but respects honest negotiation.
personality_effects.Sarah: Dead (cholera, seg_07).
personality_effects.William: Dead (dysentery, seg_08).

low.journal_entry: August 1st, 1848 — Rocky Mountains, elevation near 8,000 feet. The thunder yesterday still has the oxen on edge. This morning Thomas's deer was spotted by Shoshone hunters—six of them, well-mounted and armed, though their manner was not hostile. They asked for half our venison. We are already thin on provisions, and the meat is a godsend. But we are also deep in country where help is scarce. James must decide whether these mountains are safer traveled in alliance or in isolation. Two of our number are gone. The rest of us feel it keenly.

low.title: Mountain Pass Narrowing
low.description: The trail ahead funnels into a steep, rocky canyon with high walls on both sides. Fresh Pawnee tracks crisscross the trail—perhaps a hunting party. The narrowness means your wagons will be vulnerable if anyone means trouble. An alternative route, marked on your map by a previous traveler, swings north around the mountains but adds three days and looks unmapped territory. Your lead oxen are nervous in the confined space.

choices.label: Push straight through the canyon. It's the marked trail and likely safest.

consequences.anxiety_note: Passage is tight and tense, but uneventful. Oxen strain on rocky footing.

choices.label: Take the northern alternate route. It's longer but avoids the bottleneck.

consequences.note: One ox goes lame navigating unmapped terrain. You make slower progress but avoid the canyon entirely.

choices.label: Send James ahead on foot to scout the canyon and read the Pawnee tracks.

consequences.note: James is gone 16 hours. He returns exhausted, having found the canyon clear but feeling exposed and vulnerable. The party waits anxiously.

Mary.note: Mountain fever persists in the thin air. She remains weak but insists on riding rather than walking.

Thomas.note: Morale continues to suffer. He questions whether the Rocky Mountains were worth the crossing.

low.journal_entry: August 15th, 1848. We have come 1150 miles from Missouri and the mountains show no mercy. This morning the trail narrows into a dark canyon—Pawnee sign everywhere, though whether they hunted here yesterday or days ago, we cannot say. Mary's fever burns hotter in the altitude. We must choose: trust the marked path or spend four days in rough country. Every choice costs us.

low.title: The Shoshone Camp
low.description: As your party rounds a rocky outcrop near the Snake River, you spot smoke rising from a sheltered valley below. Through your spyglass, you count roughly twenty Shoshone people—hunters, women, and children—processing elk meat. They've clearly seen you; a few riders are moving toward your position, but slowly, without weapons drawn. One carries a hide bundle. The Shoshone have generally been peaceful with emigrants, but your supplies are critically low, and winter is approaching fast. This could be a chance to trade, but your meager rations mean you have little to offer.

choices.label: Ride down to the camp and attempt to trade. Offer $20 and some ammunition for fresh meat and information about the route ahead.
Avoid the camp entirely. Circle wide around the valley and lose a day of travel, but reduce risk of conflict or theft.
Send James ahead alone under a white cloth to parley. Minimize the party's perceived threat while keeping the group ready to move quickly if needed.

personality_effects.James: Leader's judgment will shape how the party views risk and cooperation.
personality_effects.Mary: Her sanity remains fragile; violent conflict or severe hardship could tip her toward breakdown.
personality_effects.Thomas: Growing resentment over Sarah and William's deaths; he questions James's decisions.

low.journal_entry: September 1st, 1848 — Snake River Region. Found a Shoshone hunting camp in the valley. Their riders approached peacefully, but we are in no position of strength. Food will run out in days if we do not find game or make trade. James must decide quickly. We are 1,300 miles from Independence and winter waits for no one. Two graves behind us already.

low.title: Ox Goes Lame
low.description: As your oxen team pulls the wagon through a rocky canyon pass, the lead ox suddenly stumbles and begins favoring its front left leg badly. Upon inspection, you find a deep cut along its hoof, likely from the sharp volcanic rock scattered across the trail. The animal can barely walk. You have four oxen total—losing one would strain your remaining team considerably, especially as the terrain grows rougher ahead.

choices.label: Rest the ox here for two days and treat the wound with your remaining medicine
Shoot the ox and redistribute its load across the remaining three oxen to keep moving
Leave the injured ox behind and continue with three oxen. Fashion a splint from spare wood and supplies, hope it recovers enough to catch up

personality_effects.James: Pragmatic leadership required—each choice reflects difficult frontier mathematics
personality_effects.Mary: Her compassion conflicts sharply with survival logic; she grieves animals as she grieves lost travel companions

low.journal_entry: September 10th. The lead ox came up lame in the canyon this morning—a jagged rock opened its hoof something terrible. We are down to four oxen as it stands. The mountains grow steeper ahead, and winter will find us in these high passes if we do not move smartly. James faces the choice all wagon masters dread: mercy or momentum. Either way, we lose. Mary wept when she saw the blood.

low.title: The Shoshone Encampment
low.description: As your exhausted party descends into a river valley at dusk, you spot smoke rising from a cluster of wickiups a quarter-mile ahead. A Shoshone encampment—perhaps twenty people, mostly women, children, and elders. Two hunters approach on foot, hands visible and empty. They gesture toward your oxen, then toward their camp, then make eating motions. Their sign language is clear: they're offering to trade food for passage through their territory, or they're asking for aid. The nights are growing dangerously cold, and Mary's dysentery has worsened. Your food stores are critically low.

choices.label: Accept the trade. Negotiate for additional dried meat or vegetables. Your oxen are exhausted anyway.
Decline and push on toward the river crossing before dark. You've made it this far without begging.
Approach cautiously under a white cloth flag. Request only medical advice for Mary's condition, nothing more.

personality_effects.James: Leader's judgment will shape party survival and morale in the final push to Oregon Territory.
personality_effects.Mary: Her deteriorating health makes every decision about rest versus travel increasingly consequential.

low.journal_entry: 15 September, 1848 — Snake River region. The desert has turned harsh. We passed poor Henderson's grave yesterday, a grim reminder of what winter does to the unprepared. Today we encountered Shoshone people, peacefully. Mary worsens by the hour. Our stores dwindle. Winter approaches like a predator. Every choice now feels like choosing between different kinds of suffering. Three of our party are already gone—Thomas, Sarah, William. The living must decide: pride or pragmatism. I have never felt the weight of command so acutely.

low.title: Ox Stumbles in the Mire
low.description: One of your three remaining oxen has gone lame after stepping into a hidden mud hole this morning. Its left foreleg swells noticeably, and it cannot bear full weight. The animal is still breathing, still alive—but useless for pulling the wagon. You have three options: destroy it here and lighten the load, attempt to nurse it back to health over several days, or press on and hope it recovers on its own while the other two oxen carry the burden.

choices.label: Put the ox down. Butcher it for meat to supplement your meager rations.
Make camp for two days. Rest the ox, apply cold compresses from the forest stream, and hope the swelling reduces.
Continue onward. The two sound oxen must pull harder, but you cannot afford to fall further behind. The lame ox may recover as you travel.

personality_effects.James: leader_decision_weight
personality_effects.Mary: will_follow_decision

low.journal_entry: October 10th. Lost another ox to the cursed mud of this Oregon country. The rains have turned every low spot into a trap. We are four days from any settlement we know of, and our animals fail one by one. Mary says nothing now when we bury things. I wonder if that silence is better or worse than her crying was.

low.title: The Sluggish Ox and a Stranger's Offer
low.description: One of your oxen, which drank from an alkali pool two days back, has grown noticeably lethargic. It can still walk, but slowly—the poison in that water has weakened it considerably. This morning, a Pawnee trader on horseback approached your camp. He speaks passable English and offers a lean but healthy ox in exchange for fifteen pounds of flour, three rounds of ammunition, and fifty cents. He claims the sick animal will recover in a week or so, but you know alkali poisoning is unpredictable. Pushing hard with a weakened ox risks breaking it down entirely before Fort Laramie. Losing an ox now means slower travel and heavier loads on the remaining animals.

choices.label: Accept the trader's offer. A healthy ox is worth the supplies.

consequences.note: You trade with the Pawnee. The new ox is sturdy; your pace remains steady.

choices.label: Refuse and rest the sick ox for three days. Give it time to recover naturally.

consequences.note: You dose the animal with your limited medicinal supplies and rest. It slowly improves but does not fully recover.

choices.label: Push on at steady pace. The ox will either recover or fail—no time to spare.

consequences.note: Within two days, the weakened ox collapses at midday. You must leave it behind. The remaining five oxen strain under increased load.

low.journal_entry: April 20th — Our oxen troubles mount. One creature poisoned by alkali water two days past now moves like a crippled thing. A Pawnee trader offered us a sound replacement this morning. We are at a crossroads: trade precious supplies for certainty, waste time nursing the animal, or gamble that it will survive the trail. Every choice costs us something. This land tests not just our bodies but our judgment.

low.title: A Pawnee Hunting Party Approaches
low.description: Three riders on horseback crest a distant hill, moving parallel to your wagon line. They carry bows and spears, and one wears a distinctive roach headdress. Your scouts identify them as Pawnee, likely returning from a hunt. They haven't approached aggressively, but their presence demands a decision: your party can attempt peaceful contact and trade, press forward quickly to avoid any complications, or circle the wagons and prepare a defensive posture.

choices.label: Offer trade goods for fresh game. Signal peacefully and halt the wagons.

consequences.description: The Pawnee hunters are cautious but willing. An exchange occurs—they trade a freshly killed antelope for powder, percussion caps, and a few coins. The interaction is tense but civil. No violence. Your supplies improve, though James worries you've shown too much wealth.

choices.label: Increase pace and move toward the river crossing ahead. Keep formation tight.

consequences.description: The party pushes hard for 8 miles in one day. One ox strains and goes lame—it will not recover and must be abandoned tomorrow. The Pawnee riders do not pursue. Everyone is exhausted and frustrated by the forced march. James's caution prevented trouble; the cost was paid in sweat and animal suffering.

choices.label: Circle wagons and stand armed watch. Make your position defensible and wait.

consequences.description: You spend the day in a defensive circle. The Pawnee riders observe from a distance, then move on. No contact, no conflict, but a full day of vigilance and idleness. Morale suffers—people are afraid and tired of being afraid. Food rations dwindle from the stress.

personality_effects.James: Leader instinct: values caution and control.
personality_effects.Mary: Prefers diplomacy; fears violence.
personality_effects.Thomas: Pragmatic; dislikes waste or delay.
personality_effects.Sarah: Compassionate; open to others.
personality_effects.William: Young and adaptable; less experienced with frontier politics.

low.journal_entry: April 28th. The prairie is alive today—not with coyotes, but with Pawnee. We spotted three hunters this morning, well-armed and purposeful. The flour we soaked at the creek sits heavy in our minds, and now we face a choice that will test our judgment. These lands are theirs, not ours. How we conduct ourselves matters more than I care to admit. The oxen sense something in the air. One stumbled this morning as if spooked. Tonight, whatever we have chosen, we will know whether fear or faith was the wiser counselor.

low.title: Pawnee Hunters and the Buffalo
low.description: Late afternoon. Your scouts spot a massive buffalo herd—hundreds of them—grazing two miles north. Minutes later, you encounter a Pawnee hunting party of roughly twenty riders moving toward the same herd. They haven't shown hostility, but they've clearly seen your wagons. Their leader, a man in his fifties wearing a trade-cloth shirt, rides forward with two companions. He gestures toward the buffalo and then toward your oxen, speaking in sign language. One of his men holds up a freshly killed antelope. The message is clear: they're willing to trade meat for the right to pass through your group's proximity without interference—or they can hunt the herd unmolested while your party moves on separately. The buffalo are closer to your current trajectory, but detouring would cost time and oxen strength.

choices.label: Trade with the Pawnee hunters—offer 20 pounds of flour and salt pork for fresh buffalo meat and safe passage.
Politely decline and detour north, giving the Pawnee full access to the herd. Takes extra time but avoids any tension.
Attempt to hunt the buffalo yourself while the Pawnee watch. Risky, but fresh meat would greatly help supply.

low.personality_effects: James values pragmatism and honor. Mary prefers caution. Thomas enjoys the challenge of hunting. Sarah respects wisdom over speed. William grows impatient with delays.
low.journal_entry: May 1st, 1848. The plains stretch endlessly, and today we learned they are not empty. A Pawnee hunting party crossed our path near a great buffalo herd—a sight worth seeing, thousands of beasts moving like a dark ocean. Their leader was civil enough, his meaning clear without words. We are now strangers in their hunting grounds, and the question of respect versus opportunity weighs on every decision. The snow warnings from the eastbound travelers echo in my mind. We cannot afford to dawdle, yet foolish haste invites disaster.

low.title: The Sick Ox and Divided Opinion
low.description: At dawn, you discover one of your oxen—a sturdy red animal you've relied on since Missouri—is severely lame in the hind leg, swollen and hot to the touch. The creature can barely walk. Thomas, your best judge of livestock, suspects an infected wound from yesterday's rocky crossing. You have perhaps two days before it becomes completely useless. The party divides on what to do: James and Sarah argue you must push on and leave the animal behind—you cannot afford delays, and another ox might sicken if you camp. Mary and William want to stay put, rest the ox, and treat it with your limited medicinal knowledge. Thomas remains quiet, awaiting your decision as leader.

choices.label: Leave the ox behind and press onward. Losing one day means losing miles we cannot reclaim.
Camp for two days. Use medicine and rest to save the ox if possible.
Slaughter the ox for meat. You'll gain immediate food but lose hauling capacity.

personality_effects.James: Practical and forward-looking; values progress over sentiment.
personality_effects.Sarah: Shares James's urgency; fears delays will cost them favorable mountain passes.
personality_effects.Mary: Compassionate; believes all animals deserve care, especially those that have carried you this far.
personality_effects.William: Cautious; worries that pushing a sick ox will create cascading problems.
personality_effects.Thomas: Observant and skilled; will respect whatever decision you make, though his medical opinion leans toward rest.

low.journal_entry: June 1st, 1848. Red died in his traces this morning—or near enough. His leg swelled like a melon overnight. The river crossing yesterday must have done it. Everyone has an opinion. Sarah says we cannot afford charity. Mary says we cannot afford cruelty. Thomas just looks at the beast and shakes his head. A man at Fort Laramie told me that every delay now compounds. The mountains wait for no one. But a sound team of oxen also waits for no one to catch up once they fail. The choice feels like choosing between two kinds of ruin.

low.title: The Pawnee Traders
low.description: As your party camps near a tributary of the Platte River, three Pawnee hunters approach on horseback, leading a pack animal. They carry fresh buffalo meat and several clay jars. Through gestures and a few words of English, they offer trade: meat and what appears to be a medicinal preparation for ammunition and cloth. The buffalo meat is freshly killed—a valuable source of protein. Mary's cholera has left her dangerously weakened, and the medicinal jars might contain a remedy, though you cannot be certain of their contents or efficacy. Your ammunition reserves are adequate but not abundant for the remaining journey. The cloth you carry is worn; you have only four units. The Pawnee seem peaceful and patient, but the offer will not remain open long.

choices.label: Accept the trade: 40 rounds of ammunition and 2 units of cloth for the meat and medicine jars.
Decline politely but offer to trade only medicine jars for 20 rounds and 1 unit of cloth—preserve ammunition.
Refuse the trade. Hunt for your own buffalo instead; trust your skills and rations.

personality_effects.James: Leader must weigh Mary's critical condition against reserves.
personality_effects.Mary: Weakened by cholera; any aid offers hope, but uncertainty breeds fear.
personality_effects.Thomas: Practical trader; skeptical of unfamiliar medicines.
personality_effects.Sarah: Concerned for the group; trade feels like progress.

low.journal_entry: June 15, 1848 — Noon camp near the Platte. Three Pawnee hunters rode into our clearing with fresh buffalo and clay jars they claim contain healing herbs. Mary lies in the wagon, burning with fever. We have not seen another soul in days. The meat alone would strengthen us all. But the medicine—we cannot know if it will help or harm. Ammunition is precious. Every round may matter before we reach Oregon. The choice weighs heavy.

low.title: The Wounded Pawnee Scout
low.description: As your wagons crest a rise in the bluffs, you spot a Pawnee scout on horseback, holding his left shoulder. His horse moves slowly—lamely. He sees your party and stops, neither advancing nor retreating, clearly wounded. His presence here, this close to your trail, suggests his band hunts nearby. He makes no aggressive gesture, but he's armed. You have perhaps two minutes before a decision becomes urgent.

choices.label: Approach cautiously with James and one other; offer medical aid if he'll accept it.

consequences.description: James and Mary ride forward slowly, hands visible. The scout watches, tense. Through hand signs and broken Pawnee words learned from traders, James communicates peaceful intent. The wound is deep—an arrow still lodged in muscle. Mary cleans it with whiskey and binds it with cloth. The scout accepts the medicine without speaking. Before he departs, he gestures north, then makes a downward slashing motion: a warning of Lakota raiders in the region. Your party feels the weight of this knowledge but also the small dignity of having helped a wounded man. The scout's band does not appear.

choices.label: Circle the wagons and keep weapons ready; do not engage.

consequences.description: The party forms a tight defensive perimeter. The scout waits for ten minutes, then turns his horse and rides slowly eastward, disappearing into the bluffs. No other Pawnee appear. But Sarah and Mary exchange hard looks; the fear felt premature and ashamed. Thomas approves of the caution. James is uncertain—he wanted to know what the scout was trying to tell you. That night, camp is tense and quiet.

choices.label: Give the scout provisions and signal him to leave; avoid entanglement.

consequences.description: James tosses a bundled cloth containing eight pounds of jerky and hardtack toward the scout. The Pawnee man catches it, nods once, and rides away without looking back. No further contact occurs. The gesture costs you almost nothing and costs him considerably more in pride, perhaps. Whether he interprets it as generosity or dismissal remains unclear. Sarah appreciates the practicality. Thomas mutters that feeding hostile tribes is foolish. The exchange takes minutes.

personality_effects.James: Values measured judgment and communication; fears hasty violence but also fears naïveté.
personality_effects.Mary: Inclined toward mercy; medically minded; trusts her own assessment of threat.
personality_effects.Thomas: Cautious; suspicious of strangers; respects martial readiness.
personality_effects.Sarah: Pragmatic; uncomfortable with aggression; favors efficient solutions that preserve life.

low.journal_entry: June 25th. Bluffs region, 640 miles from Missouri. Hot, humid day. Found a wounded Pawnee scout on the trail—arrow wound, left shoulder. A decision we did not anticipate. The West does not ask permission before presenting its choices. Whatever we chose, we were marked by it. The scout knew something; his warning gesture spoke of danger ahead. We press on, but with open eyes now.

low.title: Shoshone Hunters at the Pass
low.description: At dawn, three Shoshone hunters on horseback appear on the ridge above your camp. They watch your party for several minutes before riding down slowly. One man carries a elk quarter wrapped in hide. They gesture toward your thin oxen, then toward the meat. The message is clear: they offer fresh game in exchange for a trade—likely one of your animals or goods. Your party is low on food, and the altitude here is making everyone weaker. Mary is still fevered. The hunters seem neither hostile nor particularly patient.

choices.label: Trade one ox for the elk quarter and establish goodwill
Decline politely and insist you have sufficient supplies
Offer $10 and ammunition instead of livestock

personality_effects.James: Weighing practical needs against the oxen needed to haul supplies
personality_effects.Mary: Her fever makes the prospect of fresh meat especially appealing
personality_effects.Thomas: Distrustful of negotiation; prefers self-sufficiency
personality_effects.Sarah: Pragmatic; concerned about the weakening condition of the animals

low.journal_entry: July 20, 1848 — Rocky Mountains, 914 miles from Independence. Three Shoshone appeared at dawn with fresh elk meat. The question of trade has divided our thinking. We are thin on provisions and thinner on strength. The altitude punishes us daily. Mary's fever persists. Every choice carries a cost we cannot fully predict.

low.title: Mountain Pass Narrowing
low.description: As your party climbs toward the crest of the Rocky Mountains, the trail ahead funnels into a narrow defile between two cliff faces. The passage is perhaps twenty feet wide at its widest point. Your scout reports fresh evidence of a Shoshone hunting party passing through within the last few days—travois drag marks, cold fire pits, scattered bones from a recent kill. The passage offers the only practical route forward; the alternatives add three days of difficult scrambling through high country where snow still clings to shadowed ravines. Your oxen are already showing strain from the altitude. A midday thunderstorm is building to the south.

choices.label: Press through the defile immediately, before the storm arrives. Move quickly and quietly.

consequences.notes: The narrow passage is treacherous. One ox stumbles badly crossing loose shale and must be shot—it's too injured to continue. You make good time but the physical exertion and emotional toll of losing another animal weighs on everyone. No encounter with the Shoshone party, but you're all on edge.

choices.label: Make camp at the defile entrance and wait. Send James and Thomas to scout the pass on foot first.

consequences.notes: A cautious approach. Your scouts confirm the pass is safe to traverse—the Shoshone party moved through days ago and is long gone. The storm passes to the south without reaching your position. By morning, the way is clear. You lose a day but gain confidence and relative safety.

choices.label: Attempt the high-country detour. Avoid the defile entirely and climb around it.

consequences.notes: The detour is brutal. Thin air, loose footing, and exposure to afternoon thunder and hail. James develops acute mountain sickness—severe headache and nausea. One more ox gives out from exhaustion and must be abandoned. You use significantly more rations. By the third day, all four of you are bone-weary, but you clear the high passes and rejoin the main trail below.

James.cautious_choice: Recognizes the value of reconnaissance; his leadership steadies the group.
James.reckless_choice: Pushes through with determination, but the loss of another animal haunts him.

Mary.cautious_choice: Relieved by the measured approach; her anxiety eases.
Mary.reckless_choice: The death of the ox distresses her deeply; she withdraws into silence.

Thomas.cautious_choice: Feels useful scouting the pass; morale improves with purpose.
Thomas.detour_choice: Struggles badly with altitude; feels he's slowing everyone down.

low.journal_entry: August 1st. The mountains are both beautiful and merciless. We stand now at the threshold of a narrow passage that will carry us over the crest toward the valley beyond. William lies somewhere behind us, and now another ox may follow before this day ends. The peaks above are bright with eternal snow, indifferent to our struggle. Every choice here carries weight. We cannot afford many more losses.

low.title: Mountain Pass Fever
low.description: Mary's mountain fever has worsened overnight. Her skin burns with heat despite the cool mountain air, and she drifts in and out of lucidity. Thomas suggests pushing through the high pass today to reach lower elevation by nightfall, where the air might help her recover. James could instead make camp here and let her rest, though the sluggish oxen are already slowing progress, and the weather forecast suggests afternoon thunderstorms. A third option: send someone ahead to scout the pass while you decide.

choices.label: Push through the pass today. Mary needs lower elevation and warmer nights.

consequences.note: One ox collapses from the climb. Mary's fever spikes midday; medicine brings temporary relief but she remains critically ill.

choices.label: Make camp and tend to Mary. Rest and medicine are what she needs most.

consequences.note: The sluggish ox recovers somewhat. Mary stabilizes but remains weak. Thomas grows impatient; afternoon thunderstorm drenches camp but passes quickly.

choices.label: Send Thomas ahead to scout the pass. Keep moving slowly while Mary rests in the wagon.

consequences.note: Thomas returns by evening with news of a safe route and lower elevation within reach by tomorrow. Mary improves slightly with continued motion and medicine. James worries about splitting the party, even briefly.

personality_effects.James: Weighing the lives of his remaining party against forward momentum—the weight of leadership in hostile terrain.
personality_effects.Mary: Fever clouds her judgment; she defers to others, which frustrates her independent nature.
personality_effects.Thomas: Eager to reach Oregon before winter. Losing patience with delay, especially caused by illness beyond his control.

low.journal_entry: August 15th. Mary burning with fever this morning. The mountains close in around us—towering rock and snow in the high places. One ox is nearly useless now after drinking from that poisoned water days back. We are 1,150 miles from Independence and still have 400 or more to go before the Willamette Valley. Sarah and William are gone. If Mary slips away too... I do not allow myself to finish that thought. The thunderclouds gather every afternoon. Winter will come whether we are ready or not.

low.title: Dust Storm and a Difficult Choice
low.description: By mid-afternoon, the sky darkens to an unsettling brown. A dust storm rolls across the desert from the northwest, visibility dropping to mere yards. The wind is fierce enough to make the oxen nervous. Thomas spots what might be a shallow ravine ahead—shelter of sorts—but reaching it means leaving the trail. James knows that once storms like this pass, they can be disorienting to navigate out of. The alternative is to hunker down where you are, lash everything down, and wait it out. You have maybe twenty minutes to decide before the storm hits.

choices.label: Head for the ravine. Shelter is worth the risk of losing the trail.

consequences.description: The ravine provides genuine shelter from the worst of the wind. The party huddles there for six hours while the storm rages overhead. Sand still gets into everything—food, eyes, clothing tears on sharp rocks—but no one is separated or seriously hurt. By evening, the storm passes. The problem: the landmarks you knew are obscured. It takes most of the next day to reorient yourselves and find the trail again. Thomas's quick eyes saved you from panic, and Mary appreciates decisive action. James worries you've lost more ground than you can afford.

choices.label: Circle the wagons and stay put. Trust the trail will be there when the dust settles.

consequences.description: You lash down the supplies and position the wagons to break the wind. For hours, the world is nothing but churning dust and howling air. The oxen are distressed; you burn four rounds of ammunition firing warning shots when they threaten to bolt. No one is badly hurt, but the confinement and helplessness wear hard on morale. When dawn comes, the trail is exactly where you left it. James is vindicated in his caution. Mary chafes at the lost time and inaction. By now, October is breathing down your neck, and every day matters.

personality_effects.James: Cautious leader, fears falling behind schedule
personality_effects.Mary: Pragmatic, values efficiency, dislikes sitting idle
personality_effects.Thomas: Alert and quick-thinking, steady under stress

low.journal_entry: September 1st. A brown wall of dust came from nowhere this afternoon. We are only 150 miles or so from the Snake River crossings now, and the thought of being stranded out here haunts me. The flour from yesterday's creek crossing is still damp and starting to sour. We cannot afford to waste more food. Sarah and William are in the ground behind us. The four of us are thinner than we were in May. I pray the October snows hold off long enough.

low.title: Dust Storm in the Canyon
low.description: A wall of dust sweeps through the canyon without warning, driven by a cold wind from the north. Visibility drops to mere feet. Your oxen grow panicked. One of the animals has gone lame—whether from a hidden stone or the stress of the storm, you cannot tell. The dust is everywhere: in your eyes, your mouth, coating your remaining food supplies. You'll need to decide whether to push through or make camp and wait it out.

choices.label: Push forward through the storm. You're making good time and can't afford delays.

consequences.description: The lame ox cannot keep pace and falls behind entirely; you have no choice but to leave it. Dust coats everything. By the time you camp, James's throat is raw and both of you are exhausted. Mary is furious at the decision—three oxen are not enough for this load.

choices.label: Make camp immediately and shelter in place until the storm passes.

consequences.description: You lose two days hunkered down in a makeshift shelter. The lame ox recovers somewhat with rest. Rations are consumed while you wait. You use some ammunition to repair a cart axle that was damaged in the initial chaos. By the time the dust settles, you're behind schedule but in better health.

choices.label: Send one person ahead with two oxen to scout the terrain and establish a camp. The other follows with supplies when the storm clears.

consequences.description: The separation is risky and demoralizing. Mary insists on being the scout—she doesn't trust James's judgment anymore. You see her disappear into the dust with the oxen and fear grips you both. When you reunite at a sheltered bend in the canyon, she made it safely, but the psychological toll of the separation weighs heavily. Neither of you speaks much that evening.

James.morale: Already fractured after three deaths in the party. Further setbacks compound his sense of failure as leader.
James.decision_weight: James will favor caution now, having learned hard lessons.

Mary.morale: Grieving and stressed. She's become more vocal about her doubts. She can endure physical hardship but emotional strain is eroding her resolve.
Mary.decision_weight: Mary will support bold action if it seems necessary, but resents feeling powerless.

low.journal_entry: 9 September, 1848 — Canyon Country. The land is beautiful in its harshness, all red rock and deep shadows. But today it tried to kill us. A dust storm came up sudden from the north, and with it a cold that speaks of winter not far behind. One of the oxen is favoring its rear leg badly. We are down to four, and four may not be enough to reach Oregon before the snow comes. Mary and I argued about which way to go. There is no good choice anymore, only different kinds of loss. I am tired in ways that rest cannot fix.

low.title: The Shoshone Camp
low.description: As you water the oxen at a small spring, you spot smoke rising from a canyon a mile distant. Through a borrowed spyglass, you make out a Shoshone encampment—perhaps thirty people, with horses and travois. A hunting party of five Shoshone riders approaches your group at an unhurried pace. Their leader, a gray-haired man with a scarred forearm, raises an open hand. His companion speaks some English: they have dried salmon and fresh vegetables to trade. They also mention that snow already blocks the higher passes to the west. The passage will be difficult soon.

choices.label: Trade with them. Exchange $40 and 2 lbs of your remaining meat for 30 lbs dried salmon and root vegetables.
Politely decline and move on quickly. The Shoshone are peaceful, but you're wary of any delay.
Ask them for directions and advice about the passes ahead. Offer only 50 cents and goodwill.

personality_effects.James: The leader must weigh trust against caution. These people know the land; their warning about snow is worth considering.
personality_effects.Mary: Sick and weak, she needs nutrition. Fresh vegetables and salmon would help her recover, though trade means less money for emergencies ahead.

low.journal_entry: September 15th — Met a Shoshone hunting party near a spring in the Snake River country. Their leader spoke of snow in the passes already. Thomas has been dead four days now, buried under rocks where we found him. I think of his family in Independence, how they'll learn of his death months from now, if at all. Mary's dysentery worsens. We have perhaps three weeks of travel left before winter closes the mountains completely. Every decision now carries weight.

low.title: The Oxen in the Mire
low.description: October rain has turned the forest trail into a treacherous bog. One of your three oxen has sunk to its belly in mud near a stand of Douglas fir. The animal bellows in distress. Freeing it will require time and effort—time you can ill afford with winter approaching and only 250 miles to the Willamette Valley. James estimates you have perhaps four weeks of clear weather left before snow makes the passes impassable.

choices.label: Spend the day extracting the ox. Use rope and leverage to pull it free.
Leave the ox behind. Redistribute its load among the remaining two and push forward.
Slaughter the trapped ox for meat. The carcass will feed the party and lighten what you carry.

personality_effects.James: Pragmatic. Weighs time against animal loss.
personality_effects.Mary: Compassionate. Dislikes waste and cruelty.
personality_effects.Thomas: Realistic. Understands necessity but questions leadership decisions.

low.journal_entry: October 1st. The rain came hard this morning, and with it, the mud. One of the oxen went down near mile 1630, sunk deep in the forest mire. We stood there, watching it struggle, knowing every hour costs us ground toward the valley. Winter waits for no man. James called a halt and we gathered to decide its fate. The animal's breathing came ragged. Mary looked away. I could not.

low.title: Axle Fracture in Difficult Terrain
low.description: As your wagon lurches through muddy forest ruts near a tributary of the Willamette, you hear a sharp crack. The left axle—already weakened by weeks of rough terrain—has fractured partway through. The wagon tilts slightly but remains mobile for now. James estimates you have perhaps ten miles of careful travel left before the axle fails entirely. The nearest settlement is two days' journey ahead, but the rain is worsening and the forest path ahead is deeply rutted. You have one spare part, which could reinforce the axle temporarily, but it was meant for the wagon frame. Abandoning the wagon here means caching supplies in the mud—some will certainly spoil or be lost to scavengers.

choices.label: Use your spare part to reinforce the axle and push carefully toward the settlement
Abandon the wagon, cache non-perishable supplies in a waterproof bundle, and proceed on foot with essentials
Set up camp, repair the axle properly using your remaining spare part and fallen timber—delay departure but ensure the wagon survives

James.note: The loss of three companions already weighs on him. Another setback tests his resolve to reach Oregon.

Mary.note: Steady. She has endured worse and will do what must be done.

low.journal_entry: October 10th. The axle gave way this afternoon in wretched mud. We are now five souls where once we were eight. Thomas, Sarah, and William lie behind us on this trail—fever, dysentery, an accident with the oxen. I sometimes wonder if we press forward out of hope or merely habit. The forest closes in. The rain does not stop. We must decide whether to save the wagon or save ourselves.

low.title: The Broken Axle
low.description: As the wagons creak across a particularly boggy stretch of prairie after last night's rain, the left rear axle of the supply wagon suddenly splinters with a sharp crack. The wagon lurches, and three sacks of flour tumble into the mud. James calls a halt. The axle is beyond simple repair—you'll need to fashion a replacement from the spare wood you're carrying, a job that will consume most of the day. Alternatively, you could lighten the load dramatically, abandon the damaged wagon entirely, and redistribute its contents among the other five wagons, though this means leaving behind some equipment and supplies.

choices.label: Repair the axle using spare parts (takes 8 hours, requires 2 spare parts)
Abandon the supply wagon and redistribute everything (takes 4 hours, lose significant cargo)
Jury-rig the axle with rope and rawhide, move slowly (risky, may fail later)

personality_effects.description: Thomas takes pride in solving practical problems and welcomes a day's hard work. William fears further delays and lost progress. Mary worries about provisions; abandoning supplies troubles her deeply. Sarah's tolerance wears thin with setbacks. James must balance safety against schedule.

low.journal_entry: April 28, 1848. The prairie mud claimed our supply wagon's axle today. We found the grave of J. Henderson yesterday—last year's emigrant, somewhere along this same trail. A reminder that decisions made here matter. We still have nearly 1,500 miles ahead. The three choices before us each carry weight. We cannot afford many more delays, yet we cannot afford to abandon supplies we may desperately need.

low.title: The Buffalo Hunt
low.description: Late this morning, the lookout spotted a massive buffalo herd grazing three miles north of the trail—perhaps two hundred animals moving slowly eastward. The herd represents both opportunity and risk. Fresh meat would restore morale and stretch your dwindling food stores considerably, but hunting means abandoning the trail for a full day, and buffalo are unpredictable. Moreover, rifle shots might attract attention from any Pawnee hunters in the region, who use these same plains. Mary, still weak from her fever, has opinions about the delay.

choices.label: Organize a hunting party of three riflemen to pursue the herd. Leave the wagons moving at a slow pace with guards.
Push on without hunting. Buffalo herds move unpredictably and time is precious. Ration the remaining salt pork carefully.
Send one skilled hunter with two days' provisions to shadow the herd and take what shots he can. The rest continue on.

Mary.note: Mary, still feverish and weak, argues vehemently against any delay. She fears falling further behind schedule and suspects the herd is a distraction from what matters: reaching the valley before August.

William.note: William has handled a rifle since boyhood and sees the buffalo as Providence. Fresh meat means strength for the mountains ahead.

James.note: As leader, James weighs the practical calculus: oxen are tired, food is running low, but time lost cannot be recovered.

low.journal_entry: May 1st, 1848. Clear morning on the plains. A scout rode back with news of buffalo—a sight that stirs both hunger and caution in equal measure. Mary is still abed, her fever lingering. The herd moves north of our path, and we must decide whether the risk of pursuit is worth the promise of meat. We have covered two hundred miles since leaving Independence. The mountains are still weeks ahead if weather holds. —J.

low.title: Buffalo Hunt Opportunity
