low.description: Late afternoon, your scout rides back with news: a substantial buffalo herd grazes two miles north of the trail. The animals are moving slowly, and the wind is favorable for approach. Your party has ammunition and hunting experience, but pursuing game means losing several hours of daylight travel. The herd could provide 200+ pounds of fresh meat—a significant boost to your dwindling stores—but buffalo are unpredictable and dangerous. A failed hunt wastes ammunition and time. Success means fresh provisions and improved morale.

choices.label: Organize a hunting party. Send James, Thomas, and William with rifles.
Decline the hunt. Push ahead while daylight holds. Preserve ammunition and time.
Send only Thomas and William to scout and attempt a kill. James stays with wagons.

personality_effects.description: James values decisive leadership. Thomas craves action and purpose. William grows restless with mere travel. Mary prefers caution and steady progress. Sarah watches the group's dynamics carefully.

low.journal_entry: May 10th. Clear weather at last. Scout spotted buffalo to the north—real food, not hardtack and salt pork. The question is whether we chase it or keep moving. We've lost days already to that cursed storm. William looks gaunt. The children ask when we'll eat fresh meat again. Every choice on this trail costs something.

low.title: Storm and Fever
low.description: The afternoon sky darkens to bruised purple. Thunder rolls across the plains as your party scrambles to secure the wagons. The storm hits hard—rain, hail, wind that nearly topples a cart. When it passes as suddenly as it came, you discover William burning with fever. His skin is hot and dry. Mary suspects it's the fever sickness that sometimes follows mosquito bites in these humid river valleys. You have four doses of quinine in your medicine stores, enough to treat him properly, but quinine is precious and hard to replace. You could also rely on rest, cold compresses from the river, and hope his body fights it off—but fever can turn grave quickly on the trail.

choices.label: Administer quinine and keep William in the wagon for two days of rest
Use cold river water and rest, ration the quinine for emergencies
Push on at steady pace; William can rest while traveling in the wagon

personality_effects.James: The leader must weigh the group's progress against one man's suffering.
personality_effects.Mary: As the most knowledgeable about ailments, her opinion carries weight.
personality_effects.William: His survival and recovery depend entirely on the choice made.
personality_effects.Thomas: Steady and practical—he'll support whatever keeps the group moving.
personality_effects.Sarah: She worries quietly but follows the leader's judgment.

low.journal_entry: June 1st, 1848 — A terrible storm swept across the valley this afternoon, and when it passed, William lay burning with fever. The mosquitoes here are a curse I did not anticipate. We sit now at a crossroads: use our quinine and lose time, stretch it thin and risk losing William, or press ahead and pray. The alkali pool poisoned one ox, and now this. The trail shows no mercy.

low.title: The Fever Spreads
low.description: Mary's condition worsens through the night—her fever climbs and she grows delirious. By morning, Thomas complains of stomach cramps and loose bowels. The cholera that took hold of Mary days ago may now be claiming a second victim. The damp flour from yesterday's creek crossing sits in the supply wagon, and you suspect it harbors the culprit. Sarah tends to both the sick while the others break camp. You have limited medicine—four doses remain—and must decide how to proceed with two potentially dying members and 400 miles still ahead.

choices.label: Administer medicine to both Mary and Thomas immediately. Stop here for a full day of rest and recovery.

consequences.note: Sarah's nursing skills improve morale. Mary stabilizes slightly; Thomas may turn a corner. Lost day costs momentum and provisions.

choices.label: Ration the medicine—one dose each to Mary and Thomas. Continue at a reduced pace to spare the oxen strain.

consequences.note: Pushing forward risks both patients deteriorating faster. The party grows anxious. You make distance but at a cost.

choices.label: Discard the damp flour entirely. Reduce rations and preserve the medicine for critical cases only.

consequences.note: Eliminates contamination risk but drastic food loss looms ahead. Thomas resents the waste. James approves the decisive action. Mary and Thomas worsen without medicine, but reserves remain for true emergencies.

personality_effects.note: Effects applied per choice selected. Sarah shows strength in crisis; Thomas fears abandonment; James weighs hard choices.

low.journal_entry: June 15th. The river valley that seemed a blessing yesterday is now a curse. The damp from the ford has sickened us. Mary burns with fever; now Thomas shows signs of the same affliction. I have four doses of laudanum and calomel—not enough for prolonged illness. The mosquitoes rise in clouds at dusk. The oxen are fatigued. Every decision now feels like gambling with lives. 400 miles to Fort Laramie. We cannot afford to lose another.

low.title: The Drying Well
low.description: By mid-afternoon, your scout finds the well marked on your map—the one reliable water source for the next forty miles. But it's nearly dry. The bucket descends thirty feet and scrapes bottom with only six inches of brackish water visible. The oxen are already showing signs of distress in this merciless heat, and tonight's camp will offer no shade. You have perhaps two gallons to draw before the well gives nothing. Your water barrels are down to cooking reserves.

choices.label: Ration water strictly and push hard for the next well, sixty miles distant. Travel through the night to cover ground while cooler.

personality_effects.Sarah: thirst_desperation
personality_effects.Thomas: questioning_leadership

choices.label: Camp here and wait for cooler evening. Send riders ahead at dawn to scout for any seeps or hidden water sources in the ravines nearby.

personality_effects.James: cautious_deliberation
personality_effects.Mary: pragmatic_support

choices.label: Have Thomas lead a hunting party to search the surrounding grassland for game. Fresh meat can substitute for water stores; animals retain moisture in their tissues.

personality_effects.Thomas: hunter_confidence
personality_effects.Mary: concern_for_rest

personality_effects.James: weight_of_decisions
personality_effects.Mary: maternal_worry
personality_effects.Thomas: growing_strain
personality_effects.Sarah: visible_exhaustion

low.journal_entry: July 10th. Found the well bone-dry as a grave. The heat has turned this land into an oven. We've lost a team member already to cholera, and now the very earth withholds water. The oxen bellow in distress. James stands at the well mouth, staring down into darkness, and I can see the weight of every mile and every decision pressing down upon him. This trail shows no mercy.

low.title: Mountain Pass Crossing – Storm Warning
low.description: You've reached South Pass, the broad saddle through the Rocky Mountains that leads toward Oregon. The terrain is high desert here—sagebrush and rock—but ahead, the peaks darken. A Shoshone trader you encountered this morning warned of electrical storms that sweep across the passes in late afternoon. He suggested two routes: the direct path through the high pass (saves 8 miles, reaches treeline by evening) or a longer route skirting lower ground (adds 14 miles, stays below the storm zone). Your oxen are tired. Thomas is running a fever. The sky is still clear, but the wind carries the smell of rain.

choices.label: Take the direct pass—push through before the storm hits.

consequences.journal_note: Lightning struck near our party as we climbed. One ox panicked and broke a leg—we had to slaughter it. Thomas took shelter poorly and suffered badly from the cold rain at altitude.

choices.label: Take the longer route through lower valleys—safer but slower.

consequences.journal_note: Detour cost us time and ground, but the oxen recovered and Thomas's fever broke with proper rest. We saw a small Shoshone camp in the distance but kept our distance.

choices.label: Scout ahead on horseback—send James to assess conditions and decide from there.

consequences.journal_note: James rode hard for two hours and returned exhausted. The pass is clear for now, but the storm is moving faster than expected. No time to waste either way.

personality_effects.James: Your leadership is questioned—Thomas is sick and the party is tired. Whatever you choose, own it.
personality_effects.Mary: Steady as always. She worries about Thomas and wants rest.
personality_effects.Thomas: Feverish and weak. High altitude will make things worse; rest would help.
personality_effects.Sarah: Anxious about the storm. She's lost family to lightning before.

low.journal_entry: July 20, 1848. We stand at the threshold of the mountains. William's absence weighs on us still. The Shoshone spoke true about the weather. Every choice trades distance for safety, time for health. These mountains feel less like passages and more like tests. Oregon is still far. We are not as strong as we were in May.

low.title: The Mountain Pass Decision
low.description: Your scout returns from reconnoitering ahead. There are two viable routes through the Rocky Mountains to reach Fort Hall. The northern pass is shorter but narrow—recent rockslides have made it treacherous, and your oxen are already fatigued. The southern route adds thirty miles but follows a more established trail with better grazing. Storm clouds gather to the west. You'll need to decide soon; neither route can be crossed safely after dark.

choices.label: Take the northern pass. Risk the narrow route to save time and supplies.

consequences.description: The narrow pass tests both animals and nerves. One ox stumbles badly on loose shale, injuring its leg beyond useful work. A rockslide forces you to abandon a damaged wagon bed for material to shore up the trail. James takes a nasty fall while scouting ahead but recovers. The party emerges exhausted but through.

choices.label: Take the southern route. Trade distance for safety and rest.

consequences.description: The longer route proves gentler. Grazing is adequate; the oxen recover somewhat. The established trail shows signs of other wagon parties—reassuring. An afternoon thunderstorm soaks the camp but no damage occurs. You reach the pass with supplies more intact, though time marches steadily toward autumn.

personality_effects.James: As leader, the weight of this choice falls squarely on your shoulders. Either way, someone suffers for it.
personality_effects.Mary: Watches the storm clouds with deep worry. She's already lost much on this trail.
personality_effects.Thomas: Favors the safer route, but will follow orders without complaint.

low.journal_entry: August 1st, 1848. We stand at a crossroads in the Rockies—literally. The northern pass is tempting: shorter, faster, fewer mouths to feed over time. But I've seen what rockslides do to wagons. The southern route is longer but the oxen desperately need good grazing. Two of our party lie buried behind us. I won't gamble with the lives of those who remain without cause. The choice weighs heavy. Tonight we camp and decide at first light. Mary couldn't sleep.

low.title: Mountain Fever Spreads
low.description: Mary's condition has worsened overnight. The fever that struck her three days ago now comes in waves, leaving her delirious during the hottest part of the afternoon. Thomas complains of chills despite the warm day. You fear mountain fever—a sickness that has claimed lives on this trail—may be settling into your party. Your medicine supply is limited, and you must decide how to proceed through these mountains where the nearest settlement is still weeks away.

choices.label: Reduce rations and allocate extra medicine to Mary and Thomas. Push the pace to reach lower elevation as quickly as possible.
Halt for two days to rest and nurse Mary and Thomas. Conserve medicine and monitor them closely before continuing.
Send James ahead on horseback to scout for a settler's cabin or trading post where you might obtain quinine or better medical knowledge. Party continues at steady pace.

personality_effects.James: Feels the weight of leadership as sickness spreads through the diminished party.
personality_effects.Mary: Wracked by fever and worried she is becoming a burden to the group.
personality_effects.Thomas: The first chills of mountain fever frighten him; he remembers the graves they have passed.

low.journal_entry: August 15th, 1848 — We have climbed into the high country where the air grows thin and cold. Mary took ill on the 12th; Thomas now shows signs of the same fever. The mountains are beautiful but merciless. We have lost Sarah and William already. I cannot lose more. The medicine chest grows light. We press on, but I confess my uncertainty weighs heavier than the oxen's burden.

low.title: The Lame Ox
low.description: As the sun dips low over the basin, Thomas notices one of your oxen favoring its rear leg heavily. Upon closer inspection, you find a deep gash on its hock—likely from sharp volcanic rock scattered across this desolate terrain. The animal is still functional but clearly in pain. You have perhaps three days before it becomes unable to pull its weight, maybe less if the wound festers. Your remaining oxen are already lean from the desert crossing.

choices.label: Treat the wound with your limited medicine and rest the ox for a day
Slaughter the ox for meat—fresh provisions and lighten the load
Leave the ox behind and press on with three animals

personality_effects.Mary: Wants to conserve resources for the final push to Oregon City
personality_effects.James: Pragmatic about losses but mindful of the group's condition
personality_effects.Thomas: Still recovering and worried about further hardship

low.journal_entry: September 1st.—A volcanic rock opened the hock of one of our beasts this afternoon. The animal is suffering greatly. We are at a crossroads: do we spend precious medicine and time, take the meat and lose an ox, or abandon it? The desert tests not just our bodies but our mercy. Three oxen would be tight for the remaining 350 miles, but we have made harder choices. The thermometer read 28°F last night. Winter will not wait.

low.title: The Shoshone Trade
low.description: On the morning of September 10th, a small band of Shoshone appears at your camp—perhaps a dozen mounted riders with trade goods. Their leader, a man of perhaps fifty winters, gestures to your oxen with clear interest. He offers two of his horses in exchange for one ox, plus dried salmon and pine nuts. His signs are plain enough. The horses are lean but appear sound. Your own oxen are gaunt from the hard travel and meager rations. Winter is coming, and the canyon terrain ahead is treacherous for heavy animals.

choices.label: Accept the trade. Two fresh horses might handle the rough terrain better, and the food will stretch your supplies.

consequences.notes: You gain dried provisions but lose hauling capacity. Horses tire faster than oxen on long journeys.

choices.label: Decline politely and offer tobacco from your stores instead. Keep your oxen.

consequences.notes: Tobacco and a small payment preserve the animals. The Shoshone depart peacefully.

choices.label: Refuse and move camp immediately. You don't trust the situation.

consequences.notes: Breaking camp in haste is exhausting. Mary's fever worsens with the stress and cold night ride. No trade occurs.

personality_effects.James: Pragmatic. Recognizes fresh stock as valuable but worries about dependence on unfamiliar animals.
personality_effects.Mary: Cautious despite her illness. Suspects trade may seem fair but carries hidden cost. Prefers to keep what you have.

low.journal_entry: The Shoshone came this morning with honest faces and good horses. We are worn thin—the animals, the food, ourselves. Mary says my hands shake when I think of the canyon ahead. Two oxen have gone lame. A trade might save us, or it might put us on animals we don't understand when we can least afford it. The salmon smells good. The horses' ribs show. Winter will not wait for us to decide.

low.title: Oxen Lameness at Dusk
low.description: As your party makes camp near a rocky outcrop, you notice one of your three oxen favoring its front leg. Upon examination, the animal has a deep cut on its hoof—likely from the sharp volcanic rock scattered across this desert. The cut is not immediately life-threatening, but infection is a serious risk. You have limited medicine and no veterinary knowledge. Night is falling, and tomorrow you face another day of hard travel through this unforgiving terrain.

choices.label: Treat the wound with what medicine you have. It's a risk, but infection could kill the ox.
Leave the ox to rest an extra day. Rest the animal and hope it heals naturally. You'll lose a day of progress.
Cull the ox now before infection spreads. Butcher it for meat. You'll have food, but lose hauling capacity.

low.journal_entry: September 15, 1848. Snake River country. This desert tests us daily. One ox is lame—volcanic rock cuts deeper than any blade. We are down to three beasts, and each one is worth more than gold out here. Mary's dysentery has not improved; the meager rations and bad water have worn her down. We buried William four days back. Sarah and Thomas before that. I wonder how many of us will see Oregon. The nights grow cold now. First frost could come any week.

low.title: The Oxen Are Failing
low.description: October 1st, 1848. The rain has been relentless since dawn. Your sluggish ox—the one that drank from that alkali pool three days back—now pulls with visible difficulty. Its ribs show more prominently than they did a week ago. Thomas notices its left hind leg seems swollen. The forest is thick here in Oregon territory, the mud deep. You're making barely eight miles a day. The ox may pull through with rest and good fodder, but you haven't seen decent grazing in two days, only pine needles and deadfall. Push it, and you risk it collapsing entirely. Stop, and you lose time you can't afford to lose—winter comes early in these mountains, and you're still some 200 miles from the Willamette Valley.

choices.label: Rest the party for two days. Let the ox recover. Forage aggressively for feed.
Lighten the wagon. Leave behind non-essential items to reduce the ox's load.
Slaughter the weakened ox for meat. Redistribute its load to the other two.

personality_effects.Thomas: Thomas, already worn thin, voices strong opposition to slaughter. He cared for these oxen since Missouri.

low.journal_entry: October 1st. Rain all morning. The oxen are suffering—one especially. The forest presses in. Mary says the Willamette can't be more than two weeks off if we hold pace. But holding pace may kill what's left of our stock. We lost Sarah and William to fever back at Fort Laramie. I won't lose more to haste.

low.title: The Oxen Fall Lame
low.description: By midday, the rain has turned the forest floor into a treacherous bog. Your lead ox stumbles badly crossing a ravine, and when it rights itself, it favors its front left leg severely. The animal is still capable of moving, but slowly and with visible pain. Upon examination, you find no obvious injury—no cut, no stone lodged in the hoof. It may be a deep bruise, a strain, or something worse. The ox is losing its usefulness. With only three oxen remaining and Oregon City still 300 miles distant, you face a difficult choice.

choices.label: Rest the injured ox for two days. Let it recover while the party waits.
Slaughter the ox for meat. Process it today and press on tomorrow.
Continue on with the injured ox pulling light load. Trade speed for survival of the team.

personality_effects.James: The leader feels the weight of the decision. Every animal loss compounds the risk of reaching Oregon before winter.
personality_effects.Mary: She knows the ox's suffering. She has seen too much death already on this journey and wants mercy—whether rest or the merciful end.

low.journal_entry: October 10th. Rain without cease. The lead ox went lame crossing rough country this morning. We cannot afford to lose animals, yet we cannot afford to push a suffering beast. The flour is ruined from the creek crossing three days past. Our food dwindles. James and I debated late into the cold evening. Two are lost. Three remain, and now one is compromised. Oregon feels very far away.

low.title: The Shoshone Camp
low.description: Late afternoon, your scouts spot smoke rising from a creek valley ahead. As you approach cautiously, you see a Shoshone encampment—perhaps thirty people, mostly women, children, and elders. The men appear to be away hunting. A woman steps forward, gesturing peacefully. Your interpreter, Thomas, recognizes the sign language: they are asking if you have goods to trade. Their own supplies look thin; the winter was hard on them. They have furs, and they're offering a young horse in exchange for food, tools, or ammunition. Thomas notes the Shoshone control this territory and their goodwill could smooth your passage considerably. But your own provisions are not abundant, and you're still weeks from Fort Laramie.

choices.label: Trade generously—40 lbs of meat, 20 rounds of ammunition, and one spare part for the horse and safe passage.

consequences.note: The Shoshone woman nods respectfully. Word of fair dealings spreads on the plains.

choices.label: Offer minimal trade—10 lbs of meat and 10 rounds for the horse only. Polite but firm.

consequences.note: The exchange happens, but without warmth. The Shoshone watch your wagons depart with unreadable expressions.

choices.label: Decline and move on. Don't stop; don't trade. The trail ahead is long and your supplies are your own.

consequences.note: You press forward without stopping. The Shoshone make no hostile move, but Thomas is visibly troubled by the dismissal. William alone seems to approve.

personality_effects.Thomas: Values fair dealing and respect between peoples. Discouraged by meanness; encouraged by generosity.
personality_effects.Mary: Practical and compassionate. Approves of sustainable trades and consideration for others' hardship.
personality_effects.James: Focused on the mission. Respects measured decisions but won't judge reasonable caution.
personality_effects.Sarah: Cautious by nature. Concerns for supplies weigh on her.
personality_effects.William: Suspicious of delays and afraid of unfamiliar peoples. Prefers to avoid contact entirely.

low.journal_entry: April 28th, 1848. We encountered a Shoshone encampment in the creek valley this afternoon. Their hunters were out, leaving only the vulnerable among them. They sought trade, and we faced a choice about the kind of travelers—and people—we wish to be. The prairies are vast, and word travels faster than wagons on these grasslands.

low.title: The Pawnee Trade Offer
low.description: A small hunting party of Pawnee, perhaps a dozen strong, approaches your encampment in the late afternoon. They are well-mounted and armed with bows and a few firearms. The lead rider, a man of perhaps forty with a distinctive scar across his left cheek, signals peaceful intent by raising his open palm. Through gestures and broken English from one younger rider, they offer trade: fresh buffalo meat (substantial quantity) in exchange for ammunition, cloth, or tools. The buffalo they've killed are only an hour's ride north. The offer is genuine—you can see meat bundled on their horses—but accepting means parting with resources. Refusing might invite resentment, though there's no immediate threat.

choices.label: Trade 40 rounds of ammunition and 2 yards of cloth for approximately 120 pounds of fresh buffalo meat
Trade one of your three spare wagon parts for 80 pounds of meat—a harder bargain that tests their patience
Politely decline and offer only $3 in trade goods for whatever they're willing to spare—a risk of offense

low.personality_effects: William, who has shown the most pragmatism about frontier life, favors the first option strongly. Sarah worries about depleting ammunition needed for hunting and defense. Mary sees fresh meat as essential to morale and health. Thomas remains steadily neutral. James, as leader, weighs the risk of appearing weak or stingy.
low.journal_entry: May 1st. — A hunting party of Pawnee came to our camp this afternoon. Their leader had a terrible scar and spoke through a younger man. They offered fresh buffalo meat for trade. We had to decide quickly whether to part with ammunition, cloth, tools, or coin. The decision was not easy. Fresh meat would improve spirits and health considerably, but ammunition is precious on this journey. We are still weeks from Fort Laramie and hunting country where we might obtain our own. I wonder if we made the right choice.

low.title: Buffalo Hunt Opportunity
low.description: A massive herd of buffalo stretches across the prairie to the north—thousands of animals darkening the grassland for miles. Your scouts estimate the herd will pass within rifle range of your camp by tomorrow morning. Fresh meat is abundant, but hunting buffalo is dangerous work. The herd's movements are unpredictable, and a spooked animal can trample a man or overturn a wagon. Your ammunition supply is adequate but not limitless. Some party members are eager; others are wary.

choices.label: Organize a full hunting party at first light. Take volunteers and bring down as many animals as possible.
Send out a small, cautious hunting party of two experienced hunters. Minimize risk to the group.
Avoid the hunt entirely. The herd will pass by tonight; keep the camp secure and move on in the morning.

personality_effects.James: Leader faces pressure—hesitating looks cowardly, but recklessness costs lives.
personality_effects.Mary: Practical nature weighs risks carefully; prefers steady progress.
personality_effects.Thomas: Hunting appeals to his self-reliance; large hunts feel foolish.
personality_effects.Sarah: Growing weakness makes physical labor frightening; prefers caution.
personality_effects.William: Struggling health makes him fearful of danger; safer routines suit him.

low.journal_entry: May 10, 1848. The prairie darkens with buffalo—more animals than I have ever seen in one place. The thunder of their movement carries on the wind. A fortune in meat stands before us, yet fortune often demands a price. We are 320 miles from the starting point, and the mountains still lie months ahead. A full belly strengthens the body, but a broken leg or trampled man weakens us all. The choice is ours, and the herd will not wait long.

low.title: The Sick Oxen
low.description: You've made good time through the river valley, but as the afternoon heat peaks, two of your five oxen begin moving sluggishly. Their eyes are dull, and one refuses water entirely. Thomas, your most experienced teamster, suspects poisoning—they may have grazed on water hemlock near the riverbank this morning. He's seen it before. The oxen won't be usable for at least two days if they survive at all. Worse, Mary's cholera has worsened; the humid heat and poor conditions are working against her. You have four doses of quinine left, and the nearest reliable water source is uncertain.

choices.label: Rest here two days. Tend the sick oxen carefully and give Mary a full dose of quinine. Hope they both recover.
Push on at a reduced pace, rationing the oxen. Save the medicine for emergencies. Move slowly eastward to find better grazing.
Slaughter one healthy ox now for fresh meat. Rest one day. The protein might help Mary fight the cholera naturally.

low.journal_entry: June 15th, 1848 — River Valley, Great Plains. Two oxen are gravely ill, possibly from poisoned grazing. Mary grows worse by the hour; the heat is merciless to the afflicted. We've come five hundred miles and lost William already. Every decision now weighs like a stone. The Pawnee traders warned us about the hemlock stands near water. Should have listened closer.

low.title: The Pawnee Hunters' Proposition
low.description: Three Pawnee hunters approach your camp at midday, leading two packhorses laden with fresh buffalo meat. Their leader speaks passable English—learned from traders at Fort Kearny. He offers to trade the meat (enough to feed your party for a week) in exchange for ammunition and a bolt of cloth. His eyes linger on your oxen, but he doesn't ask for them. The meat would solve your food shortage and boost morale. However, ammunition is precious on the trail ahead, and cloth is your only trade good left. The afternoon storm is building fast; they won't wait long for an answer.

choices.label: Accept the trade: 40 pounds of food + 1 bolt of cloth for 60 rounds of ammunition.
Offer only ammunition (40 rounds) and refuse the cloth. Negotiate hard.
Decline politely. You can hunt buffalo yourselves and need every round for survival ahead.

personality_effects.James: Steady and cautious, mindful of ammunition reserves but aware the party's morale is fragile.
personality_effects.Mary: Still weak from her recent fever. A supply boost would restore her confidence.
personality_effects.Thomas: Pragmatic. Sees the value in fresh meat but respects careful resource management.
personality_effects.Sarah: Her health is the poorest. She eyes the trade hopefully.

low.journal_entry: June 25th — We encountered three Pawnee hunters near the bluff camp this morning. The lead man, who gave his name as Spotted Bear, offered us fresh buffalo meat at a fair price. The meat could see us through the next stretch, though it would cost us ammunition or cloth. James convened a quick council. The storm clouds gathered overhead as we debated. Every choice felt like wagering with our survival.

low.title: Mountain Pass Fever
low.description: On the morning of July 20th, Sarah wakes with a high fever and severe chills. Her skin is hot to the touch, and she complains of a terrible headache. Mary suspects mountain fever—a common ailment at altitude—though the symptoms could also indicate something more serious. You have three days of medicine remaining. The party is at 9,200 feet in a narrow pass with storm clouds gathering overhead. Pushing ahead risks exposure; stopping here means losing time and consuming supplies while Sarah recovers.

choices.label: Administer medicine and push through the pass before the storm hits. We cannot afford delays.

consequences.description: You dose Sarah with quinine and willow bark tea. The medicine eases her fever slightly, but the rigorous mountain crossing exhausts her. By evening, she's delirious and barely able to sit her horse. The group makes it through the pass ahead of the lightning storm, but Sarah's condition worsens. Mary is visibly angry at James's decision. Sarah will require complete rest tomorrow or risk pneumonia.

choices.label: Make camp in the pass and nurse Sarah back to health. Safety first.

consequences.description: You order the party to halt and establish a sheltered camp between two ridges. Mary tends Sarah closely—cool compresses, broth, and rest. By the third day, Sarah's fever breaks and her appetite returns. The lightning storms pass without incident. The delay costs three days of travel, but the party's morale improves knowing wounded members aren't abandoned. Food stores dwindle slightly from preparing healing broths.

choices.label: Send Thomas ahead to scout a lower pass while Mary stays with Sarah. The rest press on at a slower pace.

consequences.description: Thomas reluctantly splits from the group and descends to find a gentler route. You push forward slowly with James, Mary, and Sarah, but the separation unnerves everyone. Mary is furious at being left behind and refuses to speak to James. Sarah remains feverish and cannot travel fast. Thomas finds a lower pass by evening—gaining 8 miles—but the group's trust fractures. Storm clouds still threaten, and you've now lost cohesion just when you need it most.

personality_effects.James: Decisions regarding safety and haste reveal his leadership priorities.
personality_effects.Mary: Her medical knowledge makes her protective; she judges ruthlessly when health is ignored.
personality_effects.Thomas: Separation tests his patience; he prefers clear orders and steady movement.
personality_effects.Sarah: Illness makes her vulnerable; how she's treated determines her trust.

low.journal_entry: July 20, 1848 — High in the Rocky Mountains. Sarah struck down with fever this morning. The alkali water poisoned our oxen days ago; now it seems to have taken her as well. We are trapped between storm and stone, with supplies running thin and 400 miles yet to Fort Laramie. Every choice cuts deeper than the last. William's grave is already three weeks behind us. I will not lose another.

low.title: Mountain Pass Decision
low.description: By mid-afternoon, your party reaches a fork in the trail. The northern pass appears well-worn and gentle, climbing gradually through pine forest. A Shoshone hunting party you spotted earlier seems to have taken this route. The southern route is steeper, rockier, and less traveled, but cuts through higher elevation terrain that your guide warned avoids contact with any tribal groups in the area. Storm clouds are gathering rapidly to the west. Both routes converge on the far side by tomorrow evening, but one offers shelter in the treeline while the other exposes you on open ridge.

choices.label: Take the northern pass, follow the Shoshone route. Risk encounter, but better terrain and shelter.

consequences.narrative: The party encounters three Shoshone hunters at the pass mid-morning. After tense moments, trade occurs: you exchange 4 rounds of ammunition and 2 pounds of flour for dried elk meat and information about water sources ahead. The Shoshone indicate the southern route is dangerous—early snow hazards. You camp in sheltered pine forest as the storm passes to the north.

choices.label: Take the southern route. Higher risk, but avoid potential conflict entirely.

consequences.narrative: The exposed ridge becomes treacherous as afternoon storms arrive with full force. Hail and wind force the party to hunker down behind rocks for hours. One ox slips on wet stone and breaks a foreleg; you must shoot it. Thomas develops a harsh cough from the cold exposure. The party huddles miserably, burning precious medicine to manage his fever as night falls.

choices.label: Wait here for the storm to pass. Make camp and rest until dawn.

consequences.narrative: Your decision to wait proves wise. The storm howls through the pass for hours but barely touches your sheltered camp in a rock hollow. The party rests, recovers some strength. In the morning, the skies clear and both routes are passable, though you've lost time. A day's delay, but morale improves from having made a prudent choice.

low.journal_entry: August 1st. We stand at a crossroads in these towering mountains, with storm coming and the trail split before us. Sarah and William are gone these many days now, and the living grow weary. The mountains show no mercy to the indecisive. Whatever choice we make must account for the welfare of those who remain. The air grows thin and cold—we cannot afford more loss.

low.title: Pawnee Hunters at the Mountain Pass
low.description: As your party navigates a narrow mountain pass in the late afternoon, you encounter a group of five Pawnee hunters moving through the same route. They are well-armed and appear to be tracking elk higher into the peaks. The lead hunter signals a peaceful greeting, but his companions regard your wagon and oxen with obvious interest. The language barrier is complete—no one in your party speaks Pawnee. The hunters seem to be deciding whether to continue on their way or engage further. Your oxen are restless, and Mary shivers despite the warm afternoon, her mountain fever clearly worsening.

choices.label: Offer trade goods in exchange for safe passage and information about the trail ahead.

consequences.notes: You offer some smoked meat, ammunition, and coins. The lead hunter accepts with a nod. Before departing, he points to higher peaks to the north and makes downward gestures—warning of snow coming soon. Mary's condition does not improve, but the party's morale lifts knowing you gained intelligence about dangerous terrain ahead.

choices.label: Attempt to pass quickly and quietly, pushing your oxen hard to avoid prolonged contact.

consequences.notes: Your rapid retreat is interpreted as hostility. The hunters do not follow, but they watch until you disappear. One ox goes lame from the hard pace on rocky ground. The party's nerves are frayed, fearing pursuit that never comes. You gain some distance but lose time managing the injured animal, and everyone feels the sting of needless conflict.

choices.label: Stop and make camp nearby, signaling peaceful intent by building a visible fire and preparing a meal.

consequences.notes: The Pawnee hunters accept your implicit invitation and share your campfire. Through gesture and mime, they convey that this pass becomes treacherous within two days as snow moves down from higher elevations. You use medicine to treat Mary's fever slightly. The hunters depart at dawn, and your party has concrete warning to increase pace before the weather turns truly dangerous.

personality_effects.James: As leader, you must weigh risk against information. Either choice reveals your priorities—caution, speed, or community.
personality_effects.Mary: Sick and vulnerable, her survival may depend on decisions made in the next 48 hours. She observes how you handle this encounter with strangers.
personality_effects.Thomas: His low morale makes him anxious around armed strangers. He watches James for signs of confidence or fear.

low.journal_entry: August 15th, 1848 — Rocky Mountains, approximately 1,150 miles from Missouri. Met five Pawnee hunters this afternoon in the pass. Mary worsening. The mountains are beautiful and terrible. Snow on the peaks looks close enough to touch. We have perhaps two days before the passes become impassable. Every choice now carries weight.

low.title: The Shoshone Camp
low.description: As your party makes camp near a small creek, you spot smoke rising from a canyon a mile south. Within the hour, three Shoshone riders approach on horseback. They are well-armed and appear to be part of a larger hunting party. The lead rider, a man of perhaps forty with a scarred left arm, signals peacefully but his expression is businesslike. He gestures to your oxen, then to bundles of furs across his horse's back. It is clear he wishes to trade. Your fresh venison is visible in your camp—likely why he stopped.

choices.label: Offer fair trade: some venison and $20 for information about the terrain ahead and a map scratched in the dust

consequences.note: The Shoshone are familiar with the Snake River country. Their directions save you two days of wandering. They also warn of poisoned water sources ahead and offer a poultice for wounds. Relations remain peaceful.

choices.label: Decline politely but offer 10 lbs of venison as a gift, no trade

consequences.note: The rider nods, accepts the meat, and departs peacefully. Good will established, but no intelligence gained. The encounter costs little.

choices.label: Refuse the trade and tell them to move on—do not let them linger near the camp

consequences.note: The Shoshone leave without incident, but the tension is felt in camp. Mary, who favors caution over hostility, is especially troubled by the confrontation. Morale drops sharply.

low.journal_entry: September 1st, 1848. The desert presses on us, and the nights grow cold enough now that we feel winter's breath. Today we were visited by Shoshone hunters—a reminder that we are not alone in this vast country. They came with trade in mind, not trouble. How we answer such moments will shape the remainder of our journey. We are 1,300 miles from Independence. The Sierra Nevada mountains await us, and snow will not wait much longer.

low.title: Ox Gone Lame
low.description: This morning, one of your four oxen won't put weight on its front left leg. The animal limps badly, favoring the injured limb. Your blacksmith—William—is long dead, and you have no experienced farrier in the party. The ox might recover with rest, but you're already behind schedule. Winter will arrive in the high country within weeks. You can attempt to treat it with poultices, push on with three oxen and redistribute the load, or make the hard choice to slaughter it for meat before it becomes completely useless.

choices.label: Rest the ox for two days. Make poultices from willow bark and keep the leg clean.
Slaughter the ox now. Butcher what you can carry; the rest will spoil in this heat.
Continue with three oxen. Lighten the wagon by abandoning the spare parts and one box of supplies.

personality_effects.James: Practical leader, weighs speed against resources
personality_effects.Mary: Cautious about animal suffering, prefers careful decisions

low.journal_entry: September 10th — Snake River Canyon. Spotted the lame ox at dawn. Its leg is swollen. We have lost so many already—Thomas, Sarah, William all in the ground behind us. Every animal we have left matters. The canyon walls press close, and the nights grow sharper with cold. James looked at the ox a long time before speaking. I could see the weight of every decision pressing down on him. We must choose, and choose soon.

low.title: The Shoshone Encampment
low.description: As your party rounds a bend in the Snake River valley, you spot a Shoshone encampment—perhaps thirty people, including women and children. Their horses are lean but present. Smoke rises from cook fires. A scout on horseback has already seen you. The Shoshone have been trading with emigrants this season, but the relationship is strained. Your food supplies are critically low, and winter is coming. Mary is still recovering from dysentery. James feels the weight of leadership with only three oxen and three able-bodied people left.

choices.label: Approach peacefully and attempt to trade. Offer $40 in coin and 2 pounds of your medicine for dried salmon and information about the winter passes ahead.

consequences.journal: We traded with the Shoshone today. They were cautious but not hostile. Their elder told us the winter passes will be treacherous—we have perhaps three weeks before heavy snow. They sold us dried salmon in exchange for medicine and coin. Mary took some of the food. We must move faster.

choices.label: Skirt the encampment. Travel at night and push hard. Avoid contact and conserve resources; you have enough supplies to reach Fort Hall if you ration strictly.

consequences.journal: We skirted the Shoshone camp under cover of dusk and traveled through the night. One ox collapsed from the forced pace. James made the hard choice to leave it behind. Mary's dysentery worsened from the exertion and lack of rest. No one spoke much today. The fear of being trapped by snow is worse than any encounter.

choices.label: Send James alone to negotiate. Offer 3 rounds of ammunition and request fresh game meat, avoiding the appearance of a full trading party.

consequences.journal: James went to the Shoshone camp alone while we waited with the wagons. His nerve steadied us. He returned with two hindquarters of antelope meat and news—the Shoshone said a major storm is expected in four weeks. They seemed respectful of his directness. We spent the evening smoking meat. It is a small mercy, but the first in many weeks.

personality_effects.James: The death of three party members has left him hollow. Leadership now means deciding who eats and who goes hungry. He is capable but marked by grief.

low.journal_entry: September 15th. We are in the Snake River country now, and it is a hard place. The land is stone and dust. Mary grows weaker each day, though the dysentery seems to have loosened its grip somewhat. We saw a Shoshone encampment this evening. Three of our party are gone—Thomas, Sarah, and William are buried behind us. The oxen are failing. James stares at the maps each night and says nothing about what he sees there. We are 1500 miles from Missouri. The mountains are coming.

low.title: The Burnt-Out Homestead
low.description:
As your wagon train pushes through the Oregon forest, the rain intensifies. Near midday, you discover the charred remains of a cabin set back from the trail. The structure is perhaps two years old—recent enough that scorched timbers still stand, and the chimney remains largely intact. Outside lie scattered items: a broken plow, fragments of pottery, and what appears to be a child's wooden toy half-buried in ash and mud.

James examines the site with a grim expression. There are no bodies visible, but the destruction suggests violence or catastrophic accident. The hearth contains no recent ash. A Shoshone hunting party passes by on the ridge above, moving parallel to your column for several minutes before disappearing into the mist. They make no hostile gestures, but their presence and the ruins together paint an unsettling picture.

Your party is already weary. Food is low. The rain shows no sign of stopping. You must decide whether to investigate further or press onward.

choices.label: Search the ruins thoroughly for supplies or signs of what happened.

consequences.description: James and Mary spend two hours combing through the wreckage in cold rain. They recover a tin of salt, a cast-iron pan, and fragments of cloth. The emotional weight of the discovery—the toy suggests children were here—dampens the party's spirits. Mary's sanity dips slightly at the implications. You lose precious daylight but gain modest supplies.

choices.label: Leave the site undisturbed and push harder to reach the next settlement before dark.

consequences.description: You press the oxen harder than usual. The animals strain through mud and undergrowth. You cover extra ground and reach better terrain before the worst of the rain arrives. One ox stumbles badly crossing a stream and develops a pronounced limp; you fear it may not recover fully. Morale improves from swift progress, but James feels the weight of leaving a mystery unsolved.

choices.label: Approach the Shoshone party on the ridge and inquire what they know of the cabin.

consequences.description: James signals the Shoshone hunters and they reluctantly descend. Communication is difficult but patient. They confirm the cabin burned during a dispute with settlers over hunting rights two winters ago. No one was killed—the family fled before the fire. The hunters accept payment (50 cents) and dried meat (3 lbs) as compensation for talking openly. The knowledge brings some ease, though it cost provisions and time.

personality_effects.James: Methodical and cautious by nature, James feels the weight of leadership decisions at this moment. His sanity remains stable, but low morale reflects the strain of losing three party members already.
personality_effects.Mary: Observant and resilient, Mary's morale remains slightly higher than James's, but the constant hardship and rain wear on her resolve.

low.journal_entry: October 10th, 1848. A burnt homestead stands as a grim marker along the trail. The rain is relentless. We have perhaps 2,000 miles yet to cover, and only five oxen to pull our wagons. The graves we pass and the ruins we discover remind us that the frontier claims many who attempt to settle it. Sarah, William, and Thomas are gone. Mary and I must endure.

low.title: The Pawnee Encounter
low.description: As your wagons crest a rise on the prairie, you spot a Pawnee hunting party—perhaps thirty riders—moving parallel to your trail about a mile distant. They haven't approached, but they've clearly seen you. Your scout reports they're well-armed with rifles and bows. The party is nervous. Mary, still weak from fever, looks genuinely frightened. Thomas suggests pushing the oxen hard to reach the next trading post. Sarah proposes stopping to trade—the Pawnee are known to exchange goods peacefully with emigrants, though the outcome is never certain.

choices.label: Push the oxen hard and increase pace to 'fast.' Get past them before nightfall.
Hold steady pace and maintain camp discipline. Show no fear and proceed normally.
Stop and offer to trade. Exchange some ammunition and cloth for safe passage information.

personality_effects.Sarah: Sarah's confidence in peaceful negotiation with tribes contrasts sharply with Thomas's wariness. Her idealism may cloud practical judgment.
personality_effects.Thomas: Thomas fears what he doesn't understand and distrusts any approach that slows the wagon train. His caution has value, but his prejudice limits his thinking.
personality_effects.Mary: Still fevered and weak, Mary's fear is visceral. She looks to James for protection and wants distance from any threat.

low.journal_entry: April 20th — We have had our first real test of the trail. A Pawnee hunting party appeared on the horizon this morning, riding parallel to our line. The fear in camp was thick enough to cut with a knife. Mary took ill again with worry, and I could see the men gripping their rifles tighter than usual. We are in their country now, and the old rules of the settlements no longer apply. Whatever we choose, there is no going back to yesterday.

low.title: A Shoshone camp requests trade
low.description: As your party navigates a narrow canyon pass near a rushing creek, you spot smoke rising from a sheltered valley ahead. A small Shoshone encampment—perhaps twenty people—is visible below. A man on horseback approaches your wagons slowly, hand raised in greeting. He gestures toward your oxen, then toward several fine pelts and dried meat bundled on his horse. His expression is calm and businesslike. Your oxen are visibly tiring from the mountain terrain, but they remain serviceable. The Shoshone have clearly done well hunting this season.

choices.label: Trade two oxen for their furs and meat (roughly 40 lbs dried meat, quality hides worth $60). Your remaining oxen will manage the descent, but you'll be vulnerable if another dies.
Trade 30 pounds of your meager food stores and $40 for the meat and furs instead. It preserves your oxen but leaves you dangerously low on supplies.
Decline politely and press onward. The Shoshone accept this with a nod and turn back to their camp. You conserve resources but miss an opportunity for better provisions.

personality_effects.James: pragmatic
personality_effects.Mary: concerned
personality_effects.Thomas: cautious
personality_effects.William: N/A

low.journal_entry: August 1st, 1848 — Rocky Mountains. Encountered a Shoshone camp in the valleys below the high passes. Their hunters have been more fortunate than we have. The mountains grow colder each day, and the wind carries a bite that reminds us winter will wait for no one. We are now 1,000 miles from Missouri. Sarah and William lie behind us. The living must move forward.

low.title: The Pawnee Hunters
low.description: As your party breaks camp on the morning of August 15th, you spot three Pawnee hunters on horseback approaching from the south, driving a small herd of antelope before them. They notice your wagons and slow their pace, clearly assessing your group. One rider carries a rifle; the others have bows. They do not appear immediately hostile, but they are watching. Mary is feverish and barely able to sit upright. Your food stores are modest—55 pounds spread across four surviving members—and the antelope represent genuine sustenance, though negotiating for meat will require caution and goods you can ill afford to lose.

choices.label: Attempt to trade for antelope meat. Offer $2 and a blanket from your spare clothing.

consequences.note: The hunters accept the trade cautiously. Fresh meat helps morale and provides critical nourishment for Mary's recovery, though your cash and clothing reserves shrink further.

choices.label: Wave them past peacefully and maintain distance. Continue on your way without interaction.

consequences.note: The hunters move on without incident. You make good time and avoid any complications, but the party's spirits dip slightly—hunger gnaws, and the knowledge that fresh game passed you by lingers.

choices.label: Ask James to hunt the antelope herd himself while the Pawnee riders continue past.

consequences.note: James pursues the herd aggressively. The hunters interpret this as a threat and ride hard to intercept, creating a tense confrontation. James fires twice; the herd scatters. No meat secured. Ammunition wasted. The hunters depart angry. The party loses a day managing the fallout and tensions run dangerously high.

James.choice_2: Frustration with restraint, regret over escalation.

Mary.choice_0: Fresh meat may help her fever; grateful for the effort.
Mary.choice_2: The conflict and wasted day take a toll; fever spikes.

Thomas.choice_2: Shocked by the sudden hostility; loses confidence.

low.journal_entry: August 15, 1848. Rocky Mountains, morning. Three Pawnee hunters passed our camp with antelope. Mary is still fevered. James made a decision about how to proceed. The mountains stretch endlessly ahead. Snow caps the high peaks. We are 1,150 miles from Independence. Every choice matters now.

low.title: The Shoshone Hunters
low.description: As your party breaks camp on a cool September morning, three Shoshone hunters on horseback approach from the northeast. They ride slowly, hands visible, carrying a freshly killed antelope across one horse's back. One hunter gestures toward your oxen, then toward the antelope, then toward the southwest—the direction of a river valley you cannot yet see. The message is clear: trade for meat, and they will guide you to water and better grazing. Your food stores are critically low, and the loss of your water barrel has left you rationing. But you have little to offer—your ammunition is barely adequate for hunting, and your money is running short.

choices.label: Trade 50 cents and 10 rounds of ammunition for the antelope and directions to the river valley
Politely decline and push on toward the river on your own; you have a map and can find water
Offer to trade one of your two remaining oxen for the antelope, fresh horses, and their promise to guide you two days' journey

personality_effects.James: The leader feels the weight of recent losses. Every decision now affects survival.
personality_effects.Mary: She watches the hunters carefully, noting their restraint and civility. Trust comes slowly.
personality_effects.Thomas: Hunger makes him reckless. He wants the meat, regardless of cost.

low.journal_entry: September 1st — We encountered three Shoshone hunters this morning with fresh game. William and Sarah are gone now, and our stores dwindle daily. The dust storm took two days we cannot afford to lose. These men do not seem hostile, but every trade weakens us in some way. We are 1,300 miles from Independence and perhaps 400 from the Willamette Valley. The desert tests us hourly.

low.title: The Ruined Flour
low.description: The flour sacks from yesterday's creek crossing have begun to mold. What was already a meager food supply is now compromised. You have perhaps 8 pounds of usable grain remaining—enough for maybe five more days if you maintain the current meager rations, less if anyone falls ill again. Mary is still weak from dysentery. The oxen are growing lean. You are still 300 miles from the Willamette Valley, and winter is breathing down your neck.

choices.label: Stretch the remaining flour with foraged plants and hunt game to supplement. It will slow your pace but preserve what food you have.
Push hard toward Fort Boise, 120 miles ahead. You'll ration even tighter and risk exhaustion, but you might reach resupply before the first snow.
Trade two oxen to a passing freight wagon for 40 pounds of hardtack and dried beef. You'll have fewer animals but guaranteed food.

personality_effects.Mary: The sick woman's strength hangs on food and hope. Choices that deplete either will weaken her further.

low.journal_entry: September 15th — The creek took more than our comfort yesterday. The flour is black with mold, a bitter loss. We buried Sarah and William three weeks behind us; Thomas followed five days ago. Now it is only Mary, James, and myself pressing forward with three oxen and dwindling provisions. The nights are cold enough that frost forms on the canvas. We cannot afford another delay, yet we cannot afford to starve either. The Willamette Valley feels less like a destination and more like a wager against winter.

low.title: The Oxen Refuse
low.description: The morning fog clings to the forest floor so thick you can barely see ten feet ahead. Your three remaining oxen—spooked by yesterday's thunder and exhausted from mud—refuse to budge. They stand in harness, heads down, unmoved by whip or voice. Mary notices one ox favoring its front left leg. The rain shows no sign of stopping. You have perhaps three hours of useful daylight remaining, and the nearest shelter is uncertain.

choices.label: Rest the oxen today and tend to the injured leg with your remaining medicine.
Push forward carefully through the fog, using the deer meat as motivation to drive hard.
Lighten the load: abandon one of the trunks of personal items to ease the oxen's burden.

personality_effects.James: Leader must decide. The fog and exhausted animals strip away any illusion of control.
personality_effects.Mary: Watches the struggling oxen with quiet concern. She has seen animals work themselves to death before.

low.journal_entry: October 10, 1848. The rain has become a constant companion, and with it, despair works its way into camp like water through canvas. The oxen are failing. Thomas is gone, Sarah is gone, William too. It is just Mary and myself now, pushing this handful of beasts through an endless forest toward a promise of land we may never see. The deer meat spoils faster than we can eat it. I wonder if the oxen sense that we are running on prayer and stubbornness.

low.title: The Lame Ox
low.description: This morning, one of your oxen—a steady red-brown animal that has pulled faithfully since Missouri—is favoring its left rear leg badly. Upon inspection, you find a deep stone bruise on the hoof, swollen and warm to the touch. The creature can still walk, but not without pain. You have roughly 800 miles ahead before the mountains. Pushing the animal risks permanent lameness or death. Leaving it behind means your remaining oxen must shoulder heavier loads, or you must abandon cargo.

choices.label: Rest the ox here for three days, apply poultices, and hope it recovers.
Shoot the ox humanely and redistribute its load among the others. Press on today.
Leave the ox with a Pawnee hunting party camped a mile south. Trade it for fresh game and directions.

personality_effects.description: Mary and Sarah, both younger, recoil from cruelty to animals. William is pragmatic about hard choices. Thomas is neither moved nor greatly offended by necessity.

low.journal_entry: April 20th. The oxen have carried us well across this endless grass. Today we lost one to misfortune—the kind of small calamity that defines this journey. Every animal we brought from Missouri grows more precious as the miles accumulate. We are still learning what the wilderness demands of us.

low.title: The Pawnee Trading Opportunity
low.description: As your wagons crest a gentle rise, you spot a Pawnee hunting party—perhaps twenty riders—moving parallel to your route. They carry freshly killed buffalo. One rider breaks away and approaches under a white cloth tied to his lance. Through gestures and broken English from a younger member of their group, they offer to trade fresh meat for ammunition, tools, or cloth. The meat would supplement your stores considerably. However, ammunition is scarce on the trail, and your cloth reserves are modest. The young translator seems genuinely interested in fair dealing, but negotiations with any group require caution and clear judgment.

choices.label: Trade 40 rounds of ammunition for 60 pounds of fresh buffalo meat

consequences.description: The Pawnee accept readily. Within an hour, your party has fresh meat—a welcome change from salt pork and hardtack. Several members note the quality and the hunters' evident respect for fair trade. However, your ammunition reserve drops to 162 rounds, a concern as you move deeper into unfamiliar territory.

choices.label: Politely decline and move on quickly

consequences.description: You explain through gestures that you cannot spare supplies. The Pawnee nod without hostility and turn back to their hunt. William mutters about missed opportunity and fresh meat, though James notes the wisdom of preserving ammunition. The hunters pose no threat, but some in the party feel the sting of scarcity.

choices.label: Trade 2 bolts of cloth for 80 pounds of meat

consequences.description: The Pawnee value cloth highly—it's useful and carries prestige. You trade two of your five bolts and receive a substantial quantity of meat, well-butchered and ready to cook. Your clothing reserves drop to three bolts, leaving little margin for emergency repairs or trade later, but morale lifts with the prospect of hearty meals. Sarah and Mary both seem relieved by the fresh provisions.

personality_effects.James: Leader role evident in negotiation—seeks balance between caution and opportunity
personality_effects.William: Frustrated by scarcity; values practical gains; restless on the trail
personality_effects.Thomas: Pragmatic; respects fair dealing and trustworthy strangers
personality_effects.Mary: Concerned with party welfare and resource security; values provisions
personality_effects.Sarah: In declining health; hopeful that better food might restore her strength

low.journal_entry: 28 April, 1848 — Met a Pawnee hunting party this morning on the plain. Fresh buffalo kill. Their young man spoke some English—polite dealings, no trouble. The question was whether to trade. Our stores are adequate but not generous. A man could eat salt pork for months, but fresh meat steadies the constitution and the spirit both. We made our choice and moved on. The grass is greening. Two hundred miles to Fort Kearny now, if we hold pace.

low.title: Buffalo Hunt Opportunity
low.description: A massive herd of buffalo stretches across the prairie ahead—perhaps two thousand strong, moving slowly northwest. The animals are close enough that a skilled hunter could approach within effective rifle range. Your party has adequate ammunition, and fresh meat would substantially improve morale and food stores. However, the herd is unpredictable. A poor approach could result in a stampede, and hunting will cost time and energy. The oxen are already tired from steady travel.

choices.label: Organize a careful hunt with your best marksmen
Avoid the herd and maintain steady pace toward Fort Kearny
Send scouts to drive a few animals toward the party for easier hunting

low.journal_entry: May 1st, 1848 — The great plains roll on in every direction, and today they brought us face to face with a sight few men witness: a buffalo herd so vast it darkened the grass like a moving shadow. The question before us now is whether hunger and the promise of sustenance outweigh caution and forward progress. We are 200 miles from home, and Fort Kearny lies ahead. Every decision carries weight.

low.title: The Oxen Strain
low.description: By midday, the lead oxen begin to labor noticeably. One has developed a pronounced limp, and the others show signs of exhaustion despite adequate rest. Thomas, who tends the animals, reports that the beast's hoof is infected—likely from a small cut acquired days ago that has worsened. The infection is not yet life-threatening, but it will worsen quickly if untreated. You have medicine suitable for the wound, but using it now means less for human ailments ahead. Alternatively, you could rest here for two days to let the animal recover naturally, or attempt to trade for a replacement animal at the nearest trading post, roughly 30 miles north of your current route—a significant detour.

choices.label: Use medicine to treat the oxen's hoof infection immediately and continue at steady pace.
Rest here for two days to allow the oxen natural recovery.
Detour north 30 miles to the trading post to trade for a fresh oxen.

low.journal_entry: May 10th — The oxen trouble has forced a hard choice. One of our beasts has turned lame, its hoof festering. Thomas caught it early, but nature moves fast on the trail. We cannot afford to lose a working animal, yet every remedy carries its cost: medicine we may need when sickness strikes the party, time we cannot spare, or coin spent on a detour. The sky remains clear, at least, and the prairie stretches before us endless and indifferent to our difficulties.

low.title: Storm Approach & Sluggish Ox
low.description: The afternoon sky darkens to an angry purple. Thunder rumbles across the bluffs as your party approaches a shallow ravine. One of your oxen—the one that drank from the alkali pool this morning—moves noticeably slower, its gait labored. Mary points ahead: a grove of cottonwoods near the ravine bottom offers shelter, but reaching it means descending into a potential flash-flood channel. The mosquitoes are almost unbearable near the low ground. Your scout estimates you have perhaps two hours before the storm hits hard.

choices.label: Make camp in the cottonwoods. Better shelter from the storm.

consequences.narrative: The cottonwoods provide good cover. The storm arrives with violence—wind, hail, and a downpour that turns the ravine into a churning stream for two hours. Your sick ox deteriorates rapidly in the damp. By morning, it is dead. The mosquitoes feast on the party all night despite the smoke from your fires. Sarah develops a fever; you use one of your precious medicines. The storm passes by dawn.

choices.label: Push through to higher ground west. Risk the open bluffs.

consequences.narrative: You crack the whip and push on. The sick ox keeps pace, barely. The storm catches you on the open bluffs—hail the size of marbles hammers down. One panel of canvas in your wagon is torn. Thomas takes a grapefruit-sized hailstone to the shoulder and sustains a deep bruise. You make six miles before the storm passes, but the party is shaken and exhausted. The sick ox survives the ordeal, though it remains weak.

choices.label: Rest here on the slope. Kill the sick ox for meat before the storm.

consequences.narrative: You make the hard choice. The ox is lethargic and will not recover. Two rifle shots end it. The meat, while not prime, provides eighteen pounds of fresh provisions. You butcher it quickly and store what you can. The party eats well that evening, but the mood is grim. James and Thomas handle the work without complaint, but Sarah and Mary are visibly upset by the necessity. The storm rages through the night. You shelter in the wagon with extra meat rations, but supplies remain stretched thin.

personality_effects.James: leader_resolute
personality_effects.Mary: concerned_for_weakest
personality_effects.Thomas: practical_weathering
personality_effects.Sarah: physically_struggling

low.journal_entry: 26 June, 1848 — Bluffs, Nebraska Territory. Passed Chimney Rock yesterday; it stands like a stone sentinel over the plains. One of the oxen took sick from the alkali water. Storm building. The country grows wilder. William's death twelve days behind us now, but supplies grow tighter. The sky is angry. Decision ahead.

low.title: The Dust Storm Rises
low.description: By mid-afternoon, the sky darkens to an unnatural brown. A wall of dust sweeps across the plains toward your wagon train, moving faster than your oxen can travel. The temperature drops suddenly. You must decide quickly: push the oxen hard to outrun it, find shelter and wait it out, or attempt to weather it where you stand.

choices.label: Drive the oxen hard—race ahead to find shelter before the storm hits

consequences.note: The oxen tire badly in the heat. You find a low ravine with some cottonwoods 12 miles on, but one ox founders and must be abandoned. Dust coats everything.

choices.label: Stop and dig in—create windbreaks with wagon canvas and wait for the storm to pass

consequences.note: The storm rages for several hours. Fine dust penetrates everything—food, wounds, eyes. One medicine bottle breaks under the canvas during the worst of it. Oxen remain calm, tethered close.

choices.label: Continue at normal pace—the storm may miss you if you keep steady and watch the sky

consequences.note: The storm catches you in the open. Two oxen panic and bolt into the dust. You lose medicine and clothing to the chaos. Everyone is coated in grit. Sarah coughs violently for hours afterward.

personality_effects.James: As leader, faces criticism for whatever choice is made. Steady pace proponents blame him if the storm hits hard; cautious folk blame him for lost time.
personality_effects.Mary: Her nursing skills are tested—dust aggravates respiratory health across the party.
personality_effects.Thomas: Remains practical, suggests water rationing during the delay.
personality_effects.Sarah: Her weakened state makes her most vulnerable to the dust storm's effects.

low.journal_entry: July 10th, 1848. The plains tried to bury us today. Not a whisper of wind this morning, then the sky turned copper and the earth rose up like judgment. We made our choice and paid for it in dust and sweat. One more river to cross before Fort Laramie, if the oxen hold. The flour from the creek is long gone—we're living on cornmeal and jerky now. William would have hated this part of the trail.

low.title: Mountain Fever Spreads
low.description: Mary's condition worsens overnight. Her fever climbs despite the cool mountain air, and by dawn she's delirious, calling out names of people long dead. Thomas wakes complaining of chills and a splitting headache. The fever that has haunted the trail is claiming another victim. You must decide how to proceed through these mountains with two sick members and dwindling medicine.

choices.label: Push on at steady pace. Rest only at night.
Reduce pace to easy. Let the sick recover properly.
Send James ahead with one ox to find a settlement. Party stays behind.

personality_effects.Mary: Fevered and dependent. Her earlier resilience ebbs as infection takes hold.
personality_effects.Thomas: Frightened. Watches Mary's deterioration and sees his own future.
personality_effects.James: Burdened. Two dead already this season. The weight shows.

low.journal_entry: August 15th — Independence Rock lies behind us now, our names carved there with hundreds of others. Mary took the fever three days past the Sweetwater River. Now Thomas shows signs. These mountains are beautiful and merciless. The altitude does something to people here. Medicine is nearly gone. We are 1,150 miles from Independence, Missouri, and it feels both impossibly far and impossibly close.

low.title: Shoshone Traders at the Crossing
low.description: As your party reaches a shallow ford on the Snake River, you spot a small Shoshone encampment—perhaps eight people with horses and trade goods. An older man approaches cautiously, gesturing toward your oxen, then pointing to a bundle of dried salmon and root vegetables. His signs suggest he'll trade fresh food for an ox. Your scouts report the next reliable water source is three days away, and your meager rations won't sustain the party that long. The Shoshone appear peaceful, but you have little left to bargain with.

choices.label: Trade one ox for the food and supplies.
Attempt to ford the river immediately and press on—preserve your oxen.
Negotiate hard: offer money instead of livestock.

personality_effects.James: Leader must decide the party's survival strategy with limited leverage.
personality_effects.Mary: Advocates for the trade; her practical nature sees the food as necessary.
personality_effects.Thomas: Weakened by illness, supports any choice that brings rest and food.
personality_effects.Sarah: deceased
personality_effects.William: deceased

low.journal_entry: September 1st. Met Shoshone traders at the Snake River ford. Their salmon and camas roots smell of autumn and plenty—a cruel contrast to our dwindling larder. James must choose: lose an ox we need for the final stretches, squander cash we may need for supplies ahead, or risk the crossing and chance our weakened bodies against the cold current. The desert shows no mercy to indecision. Thomas coughs through the night.

low.title: Ox Lameness in the Canyon
low.description: As your party winds through the narrow canyon terrain near the Snake River, one of your remaining oxen begins favoring its front left leg heavily. By midday, it's barely able to pull. The animal shows no obvious wound, but it's clearly in pain. You have two options: rest here for a day to see if the ox recovers, or push forward and risk losing the animal entirely to the rough terrain ahead. Your food stores are critically low, and winter is coming.

choices.label: Rest the ox for one full day and search for water and forage in the canyon.
Lighten the wagon by discarding a spare part, then push forward at a slow pace.

personality_effects.James: The leader feels the weight of dwindling resources and difficult choices.
personality_effects.Mary: Watching another animal suffer tests her resolve.

low.journal_entry: September 10th — The canyon walls press close on either side as we make our way through this desolate country. One of our oxen has gone lame. With only four animals left and winter approaching, every creature is precious. The dust hangs thick in the air, and the cold at night bites harder than it did a month ago. Our meager rations sit heavy in my stomach — or perhaps that's just the weight of knowing how many we've already lost. Thomas, Sarah, William. Three graves behind us now. Mary and I must be enough to see this through.

low.title: The Shoshone Trade Opportunity
low.description: As your party camps near a bend in the Snake River, a small group of Shoshone hunters approaches on horseback. Their leader, a weathered man named Tahkoo, speaks through gestures and broken English. He has observed your struggling party and offers a proposition: fresh antelope meat and three good blankets in exchange for your remaining oxen. He makes clear the offer expires at dawn. Your oxen are exhausted but still mobile. Winter clothing is desperately thin. Mary's dysentery worsens in the cold nights.

choices.label: Accept the trade. Blankets and fresh meat matter more than oxen right now.

consequences.note: You lose oxen but gain substantial food and winter clothing. Without oxen, remaining journey must be completed on foot. Distance to Oregon City increases by ~50 miles due to slower pace.

choices.label: Decline and push on. Keep the oxen; ration remaining food more carefully.

consequences.note: Tahkoo and his hunters leave at daybreak, disappointed. Your oxen give out within two days of hard travel. Mary's condition deteriorates. You'll walk the final 400+ miles.

choices.label: Counter-offer: trade one ox, two blankets, and some jerky for the meat and blankets.

consequences.note: Negotiation requires offering 50 cents additional to secure acceptance. You retain one ox for final stretch. Moderate gain in provisions.

personality_effects.James: practical_desperation
personality_effects.Mary: weakened_judgment

low.journal_entry: September 15th, 1848 — Snake River country. A Shoshone band appeared at dusk. Tahkoo offered trade: oxen for meat and blankets. The nights grow teeth now. Mary shivers despite two blankets. I must decide whether these animals can carry us another 400 miles, or whether they're worth nothing if we freeze before we reach the valley. Thomas would have made this decision months ago. I do not have that luxury.

low.title: The Oxen and the Mire
low.description: The forest road has become treacherous. Overnight rain transformed the trail into a sucking morass of mud and rotting leaves. This morning, one of your three oxen—already lean from weeks of hard travel—has sunk to its belly in a particularly deep stretch. The animal bellows in distress. James estimates you're still 120 miles from the Willamette Valley settlements. The rain continues. Mary's fever has not broken, and Thomas's cough has worsened. Every hour spent here means colder nights ahead and dwindling supplies.

choices.label: Unload supplies and work the oxen free. It will take hours and risk further injury to the animal.

personality_effects.James: determined but exhausted
personality_effects.Mary: grateful for the halt despite fever
personality_effects.Thomas: frustrated by slow progress

choices.label: Slaughter the stuck oxen for meat. It's already compromised, and fresh protein is desperately needed.

personality_effects.James: pragmatic and grim
personality_effects.Mary: conflicted but understanding
personality_effects.Thomas: troubled by necessity

choices.label: Leave the oxen. Redistribute its load among the two remaining oxen and push forward on foot. You'll move slower but avoid the delay.

personality_effects.James: resolute but haunted
personality_effects.Mary: silent and withdrawn
personality_effects.Thomas: angry at the waste

personality_effects.James: The decision falls to you. Your jaw is tight. Two people dead already. An oxen in agony. October rain. The settlements feel impossibly distant.
personality_effects.Mary: Your fever clouds everything. You watch from beneath blankets, useless. You wonder how much further you can go.
personality_effects.Thomas: Your chest hurts when you breathe. You want to rage at something—the mud, the oxen, fate itself—but you're too tired.

low.journal_entry: October 1st. The forest is closing in. Rain will not stop. One oxen stuck fast in mud this morning. We are 120 miles out, perhaps six weeks from Willamette if fortune favors us. Mary burned hot through the night. We cannot afford delay, but we cannot afford to lose stock either. Every choice now feels like losing something we need. I remember green fields. Sometimes I think I dreamed them.

low.title: The Axle Crack
low.description: Morning inspection reveals a hairline fracture running through the right front axle of the lead wagon. The wood is old, weakened by months of jolting over rough terrain and the constant wet from recent rains. It hasn't broken completely—yet. The crack is perhaps six inches long. James estimates you have maybe fifty miles before it fails entirely, possibly sooner if you hit a deep rut or a boulder. Repair would require the spare wooden axle you've been saving, but that's your only replacement. Alternatively, you could nurse the wagon forward carefully, reduce its load, and hope the wood holds until you reach the Willamette Valley settlements, where repair would be someone else's problem. Or you could abandon the wagon now, redistributing its contents to the other two wagons and pushing forward lighter and faster.

choices.label: Replace the axle now, using your spare. Travel carefully until the replacement settles.

consequences.note: Replacing the axle takes most of a day in the damp cold. The work is hard and dispiriting, but you have a reliable wagon again. Morale lifts slightly knowing you've fixed a real problem.
