choices.label: Reduce the wagon's load by half, abandon the heaviest non-essential items, and proceed carefully.

consequences.note: You must discard food, ammunition, and spare clothing to lighten the load. The wagon limps forward, but you move faster and stay mobile. Everyone feels the loss of supplies keenly. Gambling you can make it.

choices.label: Abandon the wagon entirely. Distribute everything to the two remaining wagons and consolidate the team.

consequences.note: Abandoning a wagon is a hard choice. One ox goes lame during the redistribution. The remaining oxen are heavily loaded. You move faster but the loss of independence and the strain on animals and people cuts morale severely. You're committed now.

James.note: The crack in the axle sits heavy with James. Three people dead, supplies dwindling, and now the wagons failing. He carries the weight of every decision.

Mary.note: Mary is quiet this morning, watching James inspect the wagon. She knows what he's thinking.

low.journal_entry: October 10th. Found the axle cracked this morning—right front of the lead wagon. The rain has done its work on the wood. We have maybe fifty miles in her before she goes, maybe less. Lost three good people already. Can't afford to lose a wagon too, but every option costs us something. James stood there a long time just looking at it. Sometimes I think this trail takes pieces of us whether we move or stay still.

medium.title: The Calf
medium.description: A Pawnee girl, maybe eight, stands alone on the rise beside the trail. She's leading an ox calf — your brand, faded but readable on its hip. The animal is thin, ribs showing. The girl watches your wagons approach with an expression that suggests she's been waiting. Her dress is torn at the shoulder. No other Pawnee visible. No sign of how she came to have the calf, or why she hasn't run.

choices.label: Take the calf back. The girl will have to manage on her own.

consequences.journal_entry: Recovered a stray ox today. The girl disappeared into the grass before we could determine her circumstances. James made the practical choice. Mary would not meet his eyes at supper.

choices.label: Give the girl food and let her keep the calf. She clearly needs it more.

consequences.journal_entry: A Pawnee child had our ox. We gave her hardtack and dried meat. She did not smile. As we pulled away, I wondered if this kindness would buy us anything but trouble down the line.

choices.label: Offer to trade. The calf for information about the trail ahead and water sources.

consequences.journal_entry: The girl knew the country. She pointed us to a creek two miles north of the marked trail and warned us away from a ravine where cholera had claimed a wagon party last summer. Whether her information is sound remains to be seen. We kept the ox. She kept her dignity.

choices.label: Approach slowly. Find out who she is and where her people are.

consequences.journal_entry: We lost a day. The girl spoke no English. Her fingers pointed west, then south, then made a shape like grief. We left her the calf and twenty dollars in coin. James thinks we were swindled. I think we were the first white people to treat her as human. The distinction may not matter.

medium.journal_entry: Spring rains held off. The grass is high and the oxen are strengthening. But today we were tested by a question that has no good answer. The girl is still out there somewhere, with or without the calf.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description: One of your oxen—the red one, the strong one—drank from an alkali pool yesterday. Today it moves slower, its breath coming hard. The other five oxen pace around it nervously. By noon, the animal is limping. Thomas says it'll die if you push it. Mary suggests shooting it now and salvaging the meat. William, who's been quiet since his fever broke, just stares at it. The Platte River curves ahead through cottonwoods. You have maybe four hours of good light left. The sluggish ox can't make more than five miles if you're lucky. Behind you, storm clouds are gathering like bruises.

choices.label: Push hard through the afternoon. Leave the ox to catch up or die.

consequences.journal_entry: Left the red ox behind. It couldn't keep pace. By the time we made camp, the storm hit hard—lightning close enough to make the skin sing. No one spoke much. Sarah was the only one who didn't look ashamed.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Butcher it for meat. Lose a day.

consequences.journal_entry: Killed the ox. The smell of blood and storm made everything worse. We'll eat better for a week, but we're down to five beasts. William wouldn't come near the butchering. Sarah cried while we worked. Thomas said nothing.

choices.label: Camp here. Rest the ox through tomorrow. Hope it recovers.

consequences.journal_entry: Stopped to let it rest. The storm came at dusk—hard rain, thunder that made you feel small. The ox lay in the mud most of the night. By morning it was standing. Still slow, but standing. Mary thinks we wasted a day. Maybe. But it's still alive.

choices.label: Give the ox your medicine. Dilute quinine in water, see if it helps.

consequences.journal_entry: Used medicine on an animal. We moved slower but the ox seemed steadier by evening. Thomas said it was wasteful—that quinine is for people. Maybe he's right. We'll need that medicine before Oregon. But the ox didn't die, and that's something.

medium.journal_entry: The red ox won't make it if we do nothing. The choice isn't between good and bad—it's between different kinds of loss. Every mile costs something. Every halt costs something else. The storm's coming and we're running out of daylight to decide.

medium.title: The Sick Woman's Fever
medium.description: Mary's cholera worsens in the heat. Her skin burns despite the river nearby. She's delirious—talking to William as if he still drives the wagon ahead. The others are watching you, James. Thomas suggests moving fast to reach Fort Kearny, where there might be a surgeon. Sarah wants to stop here by the water where Mary can drink and cool herself. You have enough medicine for two or three days of treatment, maybe four if you ration it thin. The afternoon storm is coming in hard from the west.

choices.label: Push hard for Fort Kearny—70 miles, two days of hard riding. Leave Mary in the wagon and rotate riders.

consequences.note: Mary's fever spikes on the road. She may not survive the ride. Thomas respects the decision to move. Sarah grows resentful.

choices.label: Camp here three days. Use all four medicines. Boil water, keep her cool, pray the cholera breaks.

consequences.note: Mary stabilizes but may carry weakness the rest of the journey. You'll be short of medicine for whatever comes next. Thomas grows anxious about lost time. Sarah feels heard.

choices.label: Give her the laudanum—two full doses mixed in water. Let her sleep while you move camp upriver, away from the mosquitoes and bad air.

consequences.note: Mary sleeps fourteen hours. Wakes confused. Might have dreamed of William again—or something worse. Sarah thinks you're sedating her to abandon her. Thomas sees it as pragmatism. You're out of good choices.

personality_effects.James: You're responsible for a dying woman and a party losing faith. Every choice diminishes something.
personality_effects.Mary: Fever dreams blur the real world. William keeps calling from the darkness ahead.
personality_effects.Thomas: Survival math. He understands the cost of delay.
personality_effects.Sarah: Mercy is all she has to offer. She's watching to see if you have any.

medium.journal_entry: June 15th. Mary won't make it to Oregon. I know this the way you know a animal is lame before it fails completely. The heat is a living thing out here. It doesn't kill fast—it teaches you how to die slowly. The others look at me like I'm supposed to know what comes next. I don't. William would've known. He always did.

medium.title: The Fever in the Ox
medium.description:
One of your oxen — the spotted one you've depended on since Fort Kearny — stands apart from the others, head down. Its breathing is labored. The hide around its eyes has gone dull. Thomas says he saw it stumbling this morning. You have five oxen pulling a loaded wagon across open plain with storm clouds building. Lose one now, and you're stretched thin. A Pawnee hunter who appeared briefly at the bluff's edge an hour ago — watching, not approaching — made a gesture that might have meant sickness or might have meant nothing.

Mary suggests pushing hard through the night to reach the Platte crossing, where you could trade for fresh stock if any outfits are there. Thomas wants to stop, bleed the ox, and dose it with what medicine you have left. Sarah is quiet, but you catch her looking back toward where the Pawnee disappeared. James (you) feel the weight of the choice: time is running, supplies are finite, and one decision cascades into others.

choices.label: Stop camp. Treat the ox with medicine and rest. Lose a day.
Cull the ox now. Butcher it for meat. Push on by evening.
Leave the ox behind. Redistribute its load among the others. Keep moving.

personality_effects.Thomas: The ox is suffering. He cannot abide waste or cruelty — treating it matters more than schedule.
personality_effects.Mary: Time is the only resource you cannot replace. The passes wait for no one.
personality_effects.Sarah: She trusts the land's rhythms more than your panic. Whatever comes, comes.

medium.journal_entry: June 25. The spotted ox is dying. We are not equipped to save things that cannot save themselves. The Pawnee hunter watched us choose. I wonder if he knew this was coming — if the land tells some men what is about to break.

medium.title: The Fever
medium.description: Sarah wakes with a burning forehead and won't leave the wagon. Her skin is hot and dry. Mary thinks it's the heat and dust. Thomas says they should bleed her. You have three days of decent water left, and the next reliable source is still two days away if the oxen hold. Sarah's been lagging since William died—whether that matters now is unclear.

choices.label: Rest here one full day. Use precious water to cool her down. Damp cloths, whatever medicine we have.
Give her laudanum, keep moving. She'll sleep in the wagon. We can't afford to lose time before water runs out.
Let Thomas bleed her. Old remedy. Maybe it works. Costs nothing but risk.

consequences.note: If Sarah survives this, she'll be weakened for a week. If she doesn't, you'll know it was the fever, or you'll never know at all.

personality_effects.James: Leadership questioned. Any choice feels like gambling with her life.
personality_effects.Mary: Maternal instinct collides with logistics. She'll resent whatever you choose.
personality_effects.Thomas: Sees himself as practical. Old medicine is honest medicine.
personality_effects.Sarah: Conscious enough to hear the debate about her own body. That matters.

medium.journal_entry: July 10th. Sarah burning up. The deer meat turned out to be a mercy or a curse depending on whether it was already spoilt. Thomas wanted to cut her, to let the bad blood out. Mary wanted to waste water we don't have. I chose [CHOICE]. By evening her skin was still hot. She said something in the dark that might have been a name—not ours. Morning will tell us if I'm a decent man or just lucky.

medium.title: The Sick Pass
medium.description: Sarah's fever spiked in the night. Now it's dawn, and the only way forward crosses Teton Pass—a brutal climb through thin air where snow still chokes the highest elevations. The pass will take two days minimum. Going around adds five days and risks running low on food before the next settlement. Thomas says Sarah shouldn't climb at all in her condition. Mary suggests leaving her at the trading post they passed yesterday, ten miles back. James checks the sky. Storm building.

choices.label: Push through the pass. Sarah rides; we take shifts walking beside the oxen.

consequences.journal_entry: We climbed through the narrows where wind tried to tear us from the earth. Sarah's cough echoed off the rocks like a thing alive. At the highest point, the lightning came so close I smelled scorched stone. Made it through. Sarah's breathing is worse.

choices.label: Go around. Five extra days. Safer for Sarah, but food gets tighter.

consequences.journal_entry: The long way. Sarah rested on the wagon bed while we skirted the high country. Each mile felt like a choice we made twice. The supplies dwindle faster than the fever breaks.

choices.label: Leave Sarah at the trading post. She'll be warm, fed, safer. We'll send for her from California.

consequences.journal_entry: Left her at Fort Laramie with a Frenchman and his half-Shoshone wife. Sarah didn't cry. That was worse. Mary won't speak. Thomas keeps checking the oxen like they might run back. The pass felt shorter on the other side.

choices.label: Give Sarah the laudanum—all of it. Let her sleep through the pass.

consequences.journal_entry: Sarah slept like the dead and woke like something else. Her cough is gone but her eyes don't track right. She smiled at empty air twice. Thomas says the altitude was the problem. I don't know what I saw.

personality_effects.note: Mary has stronger bonds to Sarah. Thomas is practical about suffering. James carries the weight of command.

medium.journal_entry: July 20th. Sarah's fever won't break and the mountains don't care. Every choice feels like it kills something.

medium.title: The Bones
medium.description: You find them scattered in the sage near a dried wash—human bones, mostly intact, arranged in a deliberate circle. Weathered. Maybe six months old, maybe longer. No wagon, no graves, no sign of violence. Just a circle. Mary sees them first and won't stop looking at them. She's been quiet since the dysentery started eating her from inside. Now she's quieter. One of the oxen refuses to pass the circle. It plants its hooves and will not move forward.

choices.label: Investigate the bones. Maybe there's something useful—medicine, information, gear.

personality_effects.Mary: sick_worsened

choices.description: You find a broken journal wedged under the largest bones—pages mostly illegible, water-ruined. One entry is clear enough: 'Day 41. The water tastes wrong. Sarah tastes wrong.' Mary reads it over your shoulder and her whole body goes rigid. She won't eat tonight. The oxen are still spooked. You're three oxen down to two effective ones now.

choices.label: Say a few words, keep moving. Leave them undisturbed.
choices.description: You gather Mary and the oxen, speak something brief about rest and peace—words that feel insufficient. The stubborn ox moves after you press harder. By midday the circle is behind you. Mary eats a small portion of salt pork without being asked. The trail feels cleaner here, or maybe you're just moving through the doubt faster.

choices.label: Leave Mary at the bones with water and a gun. You'll scout ahead for water and return in two days.

personality_effects.Mary: abandoned

choices.description: She doesn't argue. That's the worst part. She just sits beside the circle like she's chosen it. You take one ox and push hard toward the river you hope is there. You find water on day three, not two. When you return, Mary is still there. She's moved the bones into a new arrangement—the shape of a cross now. She says she was keeping them company. Her fever has broken but something else has broken too.

personality_effects.James: haunted
personality_effects.Mary: deteriorating

medium.journal_entry: Found a circle of bones in the sage. Could've been us. Maybe will be. Mary looked at them like they were a map of something she already knew. The oxen knew too. Pushed on. The desert doesn't bury things clean. It just arranges them different.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox and the Shortened Day
medium.description: By midday the rain has turned the forest floor to gruel. The ox that drank from the alkali pool moves like it's wading through tar—its head hangs, ribs visible even under the wet hide. The other two oxen strain to compensate, and the wagon lurches and settles with each rotation of the wheels. Thomas notices the animal's breathing has changed: shallow, labored. Mary suggests pushing on to make up time. James knows they're making two miles an hour at best. The forest closes in. You can hear the creek swelling somewhere unseen, and the fog is thick enough that you've lost sight of the trail markers.

choices.label: Stop and rest the sick ox. Spend the day here, let it recover.

consequences.notes: The ox may recover or it may not. No guarantee. But pushing it in this state could kill it outright. Mary grows frustrated with the delay.

choices.label: Shoot the sick ox and butcher it. Use the meat to extend rations and spare the others.

Thomas.notes: He names things. Even oxen. Watched you do it.

consequences.notes: Meat will attract animals at night. Two oxen left to pull four tons of wagon. The math gets worse every mile.

choices.label: Leave the wagon. Cache the heaviest supplies and pack what you can carry. Move faster on foot.

consequences.notes: You'll move faster but nothing on your back weighs nothing. Oregon is still 200 miles. Winter is already here. The fear of abandoning the only shelter you own will cost you more than sleep.

James.notes: Every choice here is a kind of failure. He knows it.

medium.journal_entry: October 1st. The oxen are betraying us one by one. The land gives nothing. We were told the Oregon Territory would be green and waiting. It is green and indifferent. Thomas asked me this morning if we should have stayed in Missouri. I didn't answer because I don't know anymore what the right answer was. Maybe there isn't one. Maybe there's only the next mile and the choice that lets you take it.

medium.title: The Soaked Flour
medium.description: The flour you forded through yesterday is growing gray-green at the edges. Mary holds up a handful, lets it fall. It's the consistency of wet paste. Three weeks' worth of bread, rotting. The rain doesn't help. Thomas used to say wet flour was still flour if you dried it slow. Thomas is under a cairn near Fort Laramie now. You have thirty-five pounds of food left and another four hundred miles of trail, most of it high country where game is scarce. The oxen are thin. Winter is already tightening its fist.

choices.label: Ration it out anyway. Mold won't kill you if you pick around it.

consequences.notes: The mold spreads faster than you pick. Three days in, James develops a persistent cough. Mary's jaw tightens every time she chews.

choices.label: Dump it. Hunt for meat instead, push slower if needed.

consequences.notes: You kill a black-tailed deer. Mary's hands stop shaking when she has fresh meat to butcher. The delay costs distance but buys time to properly smoke the venison.

choices.label: Trade the ruined flour to the Shoshone camp you spotted downstream. Get dried roots, maybe pemmican.

consequences.notes: An old woman laughs when she sees the mold, then nods. The trade is fair—they use spoiled flour for dye. You return with forty pounds of pemmican and something harder to measure: the knowledge that some people here don't want you dead, just gone.

personality_effects.Mary: Her pragmatism is wearing thin. She's started asking James what happens if they don't make it. He has no answer.

medium.journal_entry: October 10th. The flour went bad in the creek. We lost three that we started with. Now we're losing food to the rain itself. Mary said nothing when I told her, just looked at the gray paste in her hands like it was something she recognized. Like failure tastes a particular way.

medium.title: The Fever Comes to Sarah
medium.description: Sarah woke before dawn burning. Not the slow warmth of exertion—something else. Her skin is hot and papery. Mary says it might be the camp fever that took two families in the last wagon train to pass through. Thomas thinks it's nothing, that everyone gets sick on the trail. William won't look at her. James, you have to decide what Sarah needs and what the party can afford to give her.

choices.label: Stop for three days. Nurse her with medicine and rest. Let the fever break or break her.

consequences.journal_entry: Stopped. Sarah's eyes roll back when the fever peaks. Mary stays with her through the worst of it. The oxen graze. William paces the perimeter like a caged animal. By the third dawn, Sarah's skin is cool again, but something in her face has changed—a thinness that wasn't there before.

choices.label: Keep moving at a reduced pace. Ration the medicine. Sarah rides in the wagon. No time lost, but she suffers.

consequences.journal_entry: Sarah moans in the wagon bed. Mary tends her between the miles, but there's rage in her movements now. William seems relieved we're moving. The fever doesn't break—it just simmers. By evening, Sarah's delirious, speaking names of people who never were.

choices.label: She's slowing us down. Leave her with supplies at the next settlement—real medicine, a roof, a chance. Hard choice. Legal choice.

consequences.journal_entry: The settlement is a collection of sod houses and a trader's post. We left Sarah there with enough coin to buy a month's keep and a promise we don't intend to keep about circling back. Mary didn't speak for two days after. Thomas punches at the wagon wheel each morning. William watches the horizon. You tell yourself it was practical. The fever might have killed her anyway. The fever might have spread.

personality_effects.Mary: Her loyalty is maternal. Choose poorly and you've lost her trust entirely.
personality_effects.William: He's pragmatic to the point of cruelty. He'll respect hard choices but despise sentimentality.
personality_effects.Thomas: He's young and idealistic still. Leaving people behind will hollow something in him.
personality_effects.Sarah: If she lives, she'll remember who chose her. If she doesn't make it, the guilt belongs to whoever made the call.

medium.journal_entry: Spring rain came through the night. The ground is mud. Sarah's fever started before dawn, and now there's a silence in the camp that wasn't there before. Everyone's watching you, waiting. There are no good answers out here—only the ones that let you sleep at night, and even those are getting harder.

medium.title: The Pawnee Scout
medium.description: A young Pawnee man appears on the ridge at dusk, alone. He watches your camp for ten minutes without approaching. When James stands and raises a hand, the scout doesn't flee—he rides down slowly, one hand visible, the other resting on his thigh. He speaks broken English. His people hunt buffalo two days north. "Many soldiers coming from the east," he says. "Bad soldiers. They take horses. They take women." He points at your oxen. "You go north with me. Safe path. Or you stay on trail—soldiers find you before Fort Laramie." The scout's horse is lean and nervous. Behind him, the western sky darkens with incoming weather.

choices.label: Trust the scout. Hire him as a guide north—deviation adds days but avoids soldiers.

consequences.narrative: The scout leads you northwest across broken country. Your oxen struggle with the uneven ground. On the second night, a fever sweeps through—likely the creek water. But no soldiers appear. On the fourth day, you rejoin the main trail north of where cavalry patrols operate. The scout disappears at dawn with payment in silver.

choices.label: Give the scout supplies and send him on his way—stay the course.

consequences.narrative: You offer him dried meat and flour. He nods, takes it, rides east without another word. That night, the camp is uneasy. Mary says you turned away a man trying to help. Thomas mentions soldiers are myth—used to scare emigrants. The next day, you see dust on the eastern horizon. It never comes closer, but no one sleeps well.

choices.label: Accuse him of scouting for horse thieves—force him to leave.

consequences.narrative: The scout's jaw tightens. He says nothing, but his eyes move to William—young, frightened. "You choose wrong," he says quietly. He leaves at full gallop. Two days later, three oxen sicken. A week on, you hear gunshots in the night—distant, but real. No bodies found. No answer to calls. Sarah believes you sent away the only man who could have helped.

personality_effects.James: fears bad judgment will haunt this crossing
personality_effects.Mary: sees in the scout a test of mercy she's uncertain they'll pass
personality_effects.Thomas: doubts everything now—the trail, the safety of numbers
personality_effects.Sarah: wonders if kindness to strangers can survive the prairie
personality_effects.William: is watching to see if they'd trade a Pawnee's life for their own

medium.journal_entry: May 1st. A scout came at dusk. Said soldiers. Said north. Said safe. Said nothing else we could trust. The prairie doesn't give you time to think clearly, and that's how it kills you—not all at once, but through a thousand small choices made half-blind. We chose. Whatever comes now, we chose it.

medium.title: The Pawnee Camp
medium.description: You crest a rise and see them: three lodges in a draw a quarter-mile distant. Smoke rises thin and gray. A Pawnee hunting party, maybe eight warriors, moving north across the grassland—they've seen you. One rider breaks off, approaches slow. His horse is lean. His face isn't hostile, but it isn't welcoming either. He stops fifty yards out and waits. Your oxen are tired. Your food will last maybe three weeks if you ration hard. He hasn't reached for a weapon, but you have ammunition and he has brothers.

choices.label: Approach with James and William, leave guns behind. Offer trade.

consequences.outcome: The Pawnee are heading north to summer hunting grounds. The warrior—Coot, or close enough—accepts dried meat and tobacco. His people have little to trade, but he warns you: the Platte crossing ahead is bad. A Lakota war party came through ten days back, running from cavalry. They left bodies. Coot's expression doesn't change when he says this. You leave with information and a strange sense that you were tested and passed, barely.

choices.label: Circle wide around them, move fast. Do not engage.

consequences.outcome: You push the oxen hard and they suffer for it. One goes lame—favoring the front left leg. Mary tends it with what medicine you have, but it won't be pulling its weight for days. The Pawnee don't follow. That night William has a fever. High. He talks about shapes moving in the dark beyond the fire. Nothing is there. The oxen graze nervously. By morning his fever breaks, but he remembers none of it, and won't meet anyone's eyes.

choices.label: Stand ready, weapons visible but lowered. Send Sarah forward to talk—she speaks some trader's sign.

consequences.outcome: Sarah walks forward. The warrior watches her. What follows is tense negotiation conducted in hand signs and broken English. He wants ammunition. You give him what you can spare. In return, his people provide fresh game—antelope meat—and detailed directions to a safe crossing five miles north. But Sarah comes back shaken. The warrior said something to her near the end, in English clear enough she understood: 'Your people take our land. We take your lives. This is fair.' He said it without anger. Like simple arithmetic. She hasn't spoken much since.

personality_effects.James: leadership_tested_patience_required
personality_effects.William: fever_lingering_distrust
personality_effects.Sarah: encounter_weighing_conscience

medium.journal_entry: May 10th. Saw the Pawnee today. They could have killed us and didn't. I don't know if that was mercy or if we simply weren't worth the trouble. The Great Plains reveal a man's choices too clearly—out here there is nowhere to hide from what you decide to do, or what you choose not to do. We will sleep poorly tonight, whatever we chose.

medium.title: The Fever Cart
medium.description: A wagon sits abandoned beside the trail, oxen dead in their traces. Inside, a man lies on a straw mattress, breathing shallow. His wife stands nearby, hollow-eyed, holding a water bucket. She says he took sick three days ago—fever, delirium, the sweats. She's been waiting for someone to come. The man's skin is mottled. Mosquitoes swarm the wagon bed. She doesn't ask for help. She just looks at you.

choices.label: Stop. Give her medicine and food, help nurse him through the fever.

Mary.reason: nursing work aligned with her instincts

James.reason: made the difficult choice to stop

choices.label: Leave them supplies—flour, a bottle of whiskey for fever. Move on.
Keep moving. You cannot afford to stop. The trail waits for no one.

Mary.reason: sees you as a coward or a killer

Sarah.reason: the woman's face will not leave her

personality_effects.Mary: Your wife watches the wagon recede. She hasn't spoken since we passed.
personality_effects.William: William spits. 'Desert claims its own,' he says. He might be right.

medium.journal_entry: June 1st, 1848. Found a dying man in a wagon. His wife waiting. The fever smell was thick, sweet and wrong. Whatever we decided, we could feel the weight of it. The mosquitoes didn't care. The oxen were already skeleton. I wonder if she's still standing there, or if she's moved on to something harder.

medium.title: The Sickness Spreads
medium.description: Mary's fever broke this morning, but she's weak as a newborn calf. Now Thomas complains of cramps and loose bowels. The Pawnee we traded with yesterday — one of them was sick too, you realize now. The jerky sits heavy in your stomach. The river ahead looks brown and slow, choked with animal waste upstream. You need water, but the well-water cholera kills faster than bullets. Sarah's already eyeing the remaining medical stores like a hawk.

choices.label: Boil all water for three hours before drinking. Move camp upriver, away from the worst of it.

consequences.journal_entry: Burned precious fuel boiling water. Delayed a full day. Mary's breathing easier. Can't shake the thought that Henderson's grave wasn't far from here.

choices.label: Push hard for the next settlement — 30 miles. Dr. there might have proper medicine. Skip boiling, drink from the river.

consequences.journal_entry: Rode through the heat like our lives depended on it. Thomas and Sarah both fevered by nightfall. Mary barely conscious. Saw the settlement lights at dusk — pray there's a doctor inside.

choices.label: Give the remaining medicine to Mary and Thomas. Prepare to leave someone behind if they can't travel.

consequences.journal_entry: Used nearly everything we had. Mary and Thomas might live now. Sarah won't look at me. The unspoken thing — that one of them might have to stay if they worsen — hangs like a noose in the tent. No one sleeps.

choices.label: The Pawnee hunter who traded with us — track him down. Maybe his people know a cure.

consequences.journal_entry: Found their camp five miles south. The sick hunter was already dead — fever took him in the night. His sister gave us a poultice of willow bark and something else — spoke no English. Whether it works or kills us, we'll know soon enough.

medium.journal_entry: Cholera doesn't care about your intentions or how far you've come. It just takes. The Henderson grave marker seems less like a landmark now and more like a prophecy we're following.

medium.title: The Drowned Man
medium.description: You find a body caught in the rocks where your party stopped to water the oxen. Male, maybe forty, bloated and pale. His clothes are eastern—wool coat, good boots. No visible wounds. Thomas thinks he's been there days. Mary won't look. The spring water runs cold from upstream, but you're camped here and your oxen are drinking. There's a leather pouch lashed to his belt, still closed.

choices.label: Cut the pouch free and move camp downriver. Leave the body.

consequences.journal_note: Found coins and a letter. The letter was addressed to someone in California. Never sent. Never will be.

choices.label: Bury him properly. Say something. It'll cost you most of the day.

consequences.journal_note: Gave him what dignity we could. James spoke words from no particular faith. We're all just trying to reach the other side.

choices.label: Check his pockets, take what's useful, and leave him for the wolves. You've lost too much time already.

consequences.journal_note: The oxen wouldn't drink from the spring after. We had to push on thirsty. That night James saw something moving in the dark that looked like a man. Or thought he did. He's not sleeping well.

choices.label: Load the body and take it to the next settlement. Report it to the authorities.

consequences.journal_note: The body rotted fast in this heat. One of the oxen went lame from the weight and uneven yoke. The settlement was abandoned anyway. We buried him there and burned his letter.

personality_effects.Mary: Disturbed. Her fever has made her imaginative about death.
personality_effects.Thomas: Pragmatic. He's seen frontier violence. He wants to move on.
personality_effects.Sarah: Increasingly withdrawn. She's thinking about William.
personality_effects.James: Haunted. He keeps wondering how long the man lay there. Whether anyone knew he was gone.

medium.journal_entry: The spring water tasted wrong after. I don't know if it actually tasted different or if we just knew. Either way, none of us drank deep. The clouds came in thick by evening and it rained hard—not the quick storms, but steady, mean rain that soaked through the tent seams. In the dark, someone said they heard singing. No one else admitted hearing it. We were all awake.

medium.title: The Pass Narrows
medium.description: The trail funnels into a granite corridor barely wide enough for the wagons. Walls of stone rise two hundred feet. Sarah spots something wedged in a crevice thirty feet up—a leather satchel, weathered but intact. Below it, scattered bones. Human. The pass ahead darkens with cloud cover. The sluggish ox has begun to lag noticeably. Thomas wants to attempt the climb. Mary suggests making camp and waiting out the weather. James, you feel the pressure of miles behind you and winter ahead.

choices.label: Send Thomas up for the satchel. Make good time through the pass before the storm hits.

consequences.description: Thomas climbs. Forty feet up, the handholds crumble. He falls the last twenty, landing on his left leg. It bends wrong. The satchel tumbles down intact—contains a journal from a '46 emigrant, half-finished map of the pass, and $50 in Spanish silver. Thomas cannot walk without support. You make the pass before the storm, but he'll slow you considerably tomorrow. The journal entries describe a Pawnee encampment two days north. The writer never reached California.

choices.label: Leave the satchel. Camp in the pass. Rest the animals and wait for clear weather.

consequences.description: The storm arrives like a fist. Lightning cracks so close the oxen panic. One animal—the sluggish one—bolts into the darkness. You lose it to the canyon. The remaining four are badly spooked. By morning the pass is clear but treacherous with loose stone. You find fresh tracks: moccasin prints, heading north. Three people, maybe four. Made in the last hour before dawn. No hostility indicated, but they were watching your camp.

choices.label: Shoot the sluggish ox now. Save the feed. Press through the pass regardless of weather.

consequences.description: The shot echoes off stone. Sarah turns away. You butcher it quickly—twenty-five pounds of lean meat, no fat worth keeping. The storm catches you halfway through the pass. Rain becomes sleet. The remaining oxen move faster when frightened. You push through in three hours, losing the trail once but recovering it by feel. By nightfall you've cleared the mountains. But something has shifted in Sarah's expression. She stares at the meat you're cooking without hunger.

personality_effects.Mary: She noted your choice. She expected mercy or prudence. What you chose will inform what she expects next time.
personality_effects.Sarah: Whatever you decided about the ox, about the satchel, about the storm—she was watching. She remembers.
personality_effects.Thomas: If you sent him up, he knows now what desperation looks like. If you didn't, he knows what you think his life is worth.

medium.journal_entry: The pass is a throat the mountains are swallowing us through. We are small here. We are temporary. The bones in the stone are a reminder that wanting to reach California isn't the same as reaching it. I am beginning to understand the difference. The remaining oxen are nervous. Sarah won't speak. We lost William three weeks ago to cholera. Now we lose other things. Sometimes I wonder if the land itself is deciding who gets through.

medium.title: The Ruined Flour
medium.description: The flour sacks from yesterday's creek crossing have begun to ferment. Mary discovered it this morning—a sour, yeasty smell seeping from the burlap. She's laid it out to dry in the sun, but Thomas says it's already gone. Thirty pounds of flour kept you alive through the grasslands. Now it's paste and rot. You have meager rations. You're in mountains where game is thin and scattered. The nearest trading post is four days' ride north, if it still exists.

choices.label: Salvage what you can. Scrape the good flour into clean cloth and ration it carefully.
Abandon the ruined flour and hunt. Send Thomas and Mary into the foothills at first light.
Trade with a Shoshone band if you can find them. They camp near the high passes this time of year. Offer them oxen for dried meat and pemmican.

personality_effects.James: A leader choosing waste and hunger. The arithmetic of survival leaves no room for sentiment.
personality_effects.Mary: Practical. She already knew the flour was lost before she told you. She was deciding what to do with the knowledge.
personality_effects.Thomas: The weakest. Hunting exhausts him. Hunger breaks him. But he won't say which.

medium.journal_entry: Flour's gone. Mary found the rot this morning. Thirty pounds turned to vinegar and air. We've lost good people and now we lose the food they died carrying. The mountains don't care what you lost. They only care what you have left. Tonight James will not sleep well, and the coyotes will sing while he doesn't.

medium.title: The Ox That Won't Rise
medium.description: Morning. One of your oxen—the red one, the strongest—lies on its side near the fire. Its breathing is shallow and labored. The other three cluster nervously upwind. Mary kneels beside it, checking for injury. There is none. The animal simply will not stand. James, your water stores are low, your food lower. You have four days' travel minimum to Fort Hall. You cannot carry what you need with three oxen.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Use the meat to supplement rations and lighten the load.

consequences.journal_entry: We put down the red ox this morning. It took two shots. The smell of blood spooked the others. We cut what meat we could carry, salted what we couldn't eat today. The rest we left for the coyotes. Mary didn't speak to me until we moved out.

choices.label: Leave the ox behind and push on with three. Pray it recovers or dies quickly.

consequences.journal_entry: Abandoning the red ox was harder than shooting it. We heard it bellowing behind us for an hour. Mary thinks we're cowards. Maybe we are. The three remaining animals pull harder, as if they know what was at stake. We made poor time today.

choices.label: Make camp here an extra day. Ration your remaining food and see if the ox recovers.

consequences.journal_entry: We stayed. The ox did not rise. By evening its breathing had stopped. We cut it up anyway—the meat was still good—and made a thin soup. Mary says we did right by trying. I'm not sure trying and failing is better than choosing quick. But she's speaking to me again, at least.

personality_effects.Mary: Responds poorly to pragmatic cruelty; values mercy and persistence even in the face of futility.
personality_effects.James: Leader guilt compounds any decision. Isolation from group judgment weighs heavier than the act itself.

medium.journal_entry: Snake River canyon, September 10th. The red ox won't stand. We have three choices and none of them good. I keep thinking about Independence Rock, about the names carved there. How many of those people made it to Oregon? How many turned back? How many died between the stone and Fort Hall? We are running on fumes—food, animals, hope. Every decision now draws from a well that's almost dry.

medium.title: The Bones in the Shallows
medium.description: At a bend in the Snake River where it narrows, you find skeletal remains caught between rocks—human, recent enough that scraps of cloth still cling to the bones. A child's shoe, leather worn soft. Downstream, wedged against a cottonwood snag, is a small trunk, water-logged but intact. Mary is fevered and doesn't notice. The oxen are restless. You have maybe two hours of good light left.

choices.label: Open the trunk. Whatever's inside belongs to someone dead—might be useful.

consequences.journal: Found letters addressed to a place called Salem. A woman's wedding ring. Laudanum in a glass bottle, nearly full. Did not bury the bones. Could not bring myself to stay longer.

choices.label: Leave it. Bury what you can of the bones respectfully and move on before dark.

consequences.journal: Gave the child a grave, at least. Said words that meant nothing. Mary woke asking for water. The river took what it wanted. We took what dignity we could offer.

choices.label: Haul the trunk back to camp without opening it. Sell it unopened at the next settlement—some questions shouldn't be answered.

consequences.journal: An ox went lame hauling that damned box. Sold it to a trader at Burnt Ranch for three dollars. He didn't open it either, just nodded and paid. Some men know better than to ask.

medium.journal_entry: The river gives and takes in equal measure. What it takes, it keeps. What it gives, we don't deserve.

medium.title: The Burned-Out Cabin
medium.description: Through the rain and fog, you find a cabin standing alone in a small clearing. The door hangs open. Inside, the remains of a fire—recent enough that ash still holds warmth. A cast-iron pot sits on the hearth, half-filled with something that might have been stew weeks ago. Outside, you find a grave marker, the wood too weathered to read. The cabin's stores appear picked over, but there's a leather-bound journal on a shelf, its pages swollen with damp. No sign of who lived here or when they left. Your oxen are nervous. Thomas thinks there might be canned goods in the cellar. Mary won't go inside.

choices.label: Search the cabin thoroughly. Check the cellar and the journal.

consequences.description: You find three tins of salted meat, a bottle of laudanum, and the journal—entries from a man named Corbett, dead six months now of fever. His last words describe his wife leaving him here, unable to bear it anymore. The cabin smells of death and desertion. Thomas seems energized by the find. Mary's face has gone pale and hard. As you leave, you feel watched from the trees, though nothing is there when you turn.

choices.label: Take only what's visible and move on before dark.

consequences.description: You grab the pot and a sack of dried corn from the shelf, then push the oxen hard through the remaining daylight. No one speaks much. By the time camp is made, the rain has turned to sleet. Thomas mutters about wasted opportunity. Mary helps James tend the animals without being asked. The cabin recedes into the fog behind you, though once you hear what sounds like a door slamming in the wind—carried from miles back, surely.

choices.label: Bury the grave marker's remains and say something over it. Leave the cabin undisturbed.

consequences.description: You spend the afternoon digging in the wet ground, finding bones wrapped in rotted cloth. You read words from memory over the grave—the Twenty-Third Psalm, though you aren't sure why. By the time you're done, the forest has gone quiet. That night, around the fire, Mary speaks for the first time in days. Thomas refuses to eat, saying it's a waste of time to respect the dead when you're barely alive yourselves. James finds his hands shaking, though the fire is warm.

personality_effects.James: Leadership tested by the question of what the dead are owed
personality_effects.Mary: Haunted by the wife who abandoned her husband. Recognizes herself in that choice
personality_effects.Thomas: Impatient with sentiment. Supplies matter more than ghosts

medium.journal_entry: Found a cabin in the woods—someone died here, months ago maybe. The fog was so thick I couldn't see the trees. Mary said the air smelled wrong. She's right about most things now. William and Sarah would have known what to do. Or maybe they wouldn't have either. The rain tastes like iron.

medium.title: The Cough
medium.description: Mary wakes before dawn with a cough that sounds like she's trying to expel a stone from her chest. It's wet and deliberate. She won't meet your eyes. The fog is so thick you can barely see the oxen. She says she's fine. She's not fine. Three days ago William had a cough like that. Two days ago he was delirious. Yesterday you buried him in the mud without a marker because the ground was too soft to dig proper.

choices.label: Insist Mary stay in the wagon today. Rest and medicine.

consequences.description: She argues but complies. You waste the one dose of medicine—quinine that might've done nothing anyway. The wagon moves slower without her helping with the animals. By evening the cough is worse. She stares at the rain and doesn't speak.

choices.label: Keep moving at steady pace. Push through.

consequences.description: The rain hammers down. Mary coughs all day from the back of the wagon, each spasm visible through the canvas. By nightfall she's burning with fever. She asks for water three times and falls unconscious before you can give it to her. You sit beside her in the dark, listening to her breathe like a broken bellows.

choices.label: Stop here. Make a clean camp and wait for improvement.

consequences.description: Wasting time. Every day you camp is a day the snow draws closer. Mary's cough eases slightly by the second morning, but you both know it might return. The oxen are restless in the rain. You use up stores and miles neither of you can afford to lose. James feels the weight of her gratitude and his own selfishness sitting in his chest like a stone.

personality_effects.Mary: Her trust in James wavers. She knows he's calculating—how many miles versus how many lives. She doesn't blame him. That's what makes it worse.

medium.journal_entry: The fog rolled in like we'd driven into a living thing. Mary's cough sounds like William's cough. I haven't told her that. I think she knows anyway. There's a math to this trail that has nothing to do with goodness. The rain keeps falling. The Oregon country doesn't care if we make it or not.

medium.title: The Fever Comes
medium.description: Sarah wakes before dawn burning hot, skin mottled with welts from mosquito bites that have begun to weep. She's delirious—talks about her daughter in Cincinnati, dead these five years. Mary thinks it's the fever that kills people on the trail. Thomas says it's just the bites. James knows they need to move but Sarah can barely sit a horse. The storm from yesterday left standing water everywhere, breeding ground for whatever this is. You have four doses of quinine left.

choices.label: Push on at steady pace. Sarah rides in the wagon. Ration the medicine—one dose now, save three for worse.

personality_effects.Sarah: resentment—she knows you're gambling with her life
personality_effects.Thomas: quiet approval—he understands necessity

choices.label: Stop here two days. Use a full dose of quinine, keep Sarah cool, rest. Let the fever break or kill her.

personality_effects.Mary: gratitude mixed with fear
personality_effects.Thomas: bitter—every day lost is harder on the oxen and harder on him

choices.label: Leave Sarah with two days' rations and the medicine. She knows the trail back. You'll send help from the next trading post.

personality_effects.Mary: won't speak to you
personality_effects.Thomas: contempt—calls you a coward when he thinks you can't hear

personality_effects.note: See choice consequences for nuanced effects

medium.journal_entry: Sarah's burning up. The mosquitoes here are a curse sent by something that doesn't want us to reach Oregon. We carved our names on a rock two days back like we might not make it. Maybe we won't. Thomas watches me like he's waiting to see what kind of man I am. So am I.

medium.title: The Mirage That Wasn't
medium.description: The heat warps the plains into something alive. Around noon, Thomas points ahead—a settlement, smoke rising from chimneys. Mary squints. It's there. James sees it too. But as you push the oxen forward, the shapes don't resolve. No sound. No smell of cooking fires. Sarah, fevered since yesterday, insists people are waving. The horses sense nothing. One mile closer and the buildings begin to dissolve like watercolor in rain. By the time you reach the spot, there's only alkali flat and a single dead cottonwood. Thomas finds something beneath it: a leather journal, half-buried, pages brittle as locusts. The last entry is dated 1846. 'Our daughter won't stop crying. We're turning back.' No bodies. No explanation.

choices.label: Examine the journal closely. Read the entire account of what happened to them.

consequences.journal_entry: Found a dead man's diary under that tree. He was turning back in '46. His daughter cried the whole way. Reading his words made Thomas pale. Didn't help Sarah's fever any.

choices.label: Burn the journal and press on. Don't let the others dwell on it.

personality_effects.Thomas: distrusts James's leadership

consequences.journal_entry: Threw that cursed book in the fire. Some things aren't meant to be known. Thomas watched it burn like I'd murdered something. Maybe I did.

choices.label: Bury the journal properly. Say a few words. Let people grieve what they saw.

personality_effects.Mary: respects James more
personality_effects.Thomas: respects James more

consequences.journal_entry: We gave that unknown family what ceremony we could. Felt like the right thing, though it cost us time we don't have. The silence after was worse than any fever dream.

medium.journal_entry: The heat does strange things. We all saw the settlement. All of it. Then it wasn't there. Found a man's diary instead—a man who saw something too, two years back, and turned his wagon around. His daughter was crying. I wonder if she made it home.

medium.title: The Fever Trail
medium.description:
Thomas collapses at midday, burning hot. His skin is gray-yellow. Mary thinks it's mountain fever—common enough in these passes. The thermometer reads 103 if you trust it, which nobody does anymore. He's conscious but talking in fragments about his daughter's wedding, which happened three years ago in Connecticut.

You're eight miles from the nearest pass descent. The trail here is narrow, hemmed by granite. Pushing hard might get him down to lower altitude and warmer nights by tomorrow. Stopping means exposure tonight—it'll freeze above thirty degrees in these mountains.

Thomas says nothing. Just stares at the sky with his jaw working.

choices.label: Push hard downslope. Get him to lower altitude tonight.
Make camp. Ration carefully. Let him rest.
Send James and Mary ahead with supplies. Thomas and Sarah wait for recovery.

personality_effects.Sarah: fractured
personality_effects.Mary: guilt
doubt
personality_effects.James: hardness

medium.journal_entry: Thomas won't wake properly. The mountains don't care. William's dead two weeks behind us and now this—fever in the passes where you can't go back and won't reach anywhere forward fast enough. Mary keeps asking what she should have done different. She gave him quinine yesterday but his body rejected it. Some things you can't fix with supplies or will. Just weather them or don't.

medium.title: The Sick Ox
medium.description: One of your oxen—the lead animal, steady for a thousand miles—has gone lame. Its left front leg swells to twice the size by midday. The limp worsens with each hour. Thomas notices the animal was favoring that leg yesterday but said nothing. Mary suggests it might be poison from eating locoweed in the high meadow two days back. The ox is still valuable. A sound animal fetches $40 to $60 at the trading posts ahead. A lame one fetches nothing. It cannot pull the wagon much farther, but it can still walk, barely. That evening, it refuses to eat.

choices.label: Shoot the ox now. Use the meat for food. You need it more than the animal needs mercy.

consequences.journal_entry: Put a bullet in the lead ox today. It went hard. The meat is good but stringy. No one talks much at supper. The mountains are darker at night without four oxen.

choices.label: Leave the ox behind to heal or die. Redistribute cargo among the remaining animals. Push on.

consequences.journal_entry: We left the ox standing in the high grass, watching us go. Thomas says we're cowards. Mary says it's mercy. I don't know what it is. The wagon is heavier on three animals now. Something will break before Fort Laramie.

choices.label: Spend time and resources nursing the leg. Splint it, apply poultice, rest here two days. Gamble it recovers.

consequences.journal_entry: Spent the better part of two days wrapping that ox's leg in cloth and poultice. Mary made a treatment from willow bark and something she wouldn't name. This morning it took weight on the leg without favoring it. Maybe. We'll know in ten miles.

personality_effects.Mary: Tends toward mercy, distrusts waste. Quick to diagnose illness.
personality_effects.Thomas: Practical. Sees sentiment as liability. Resents inaction.
personality_effects.James: These choices are yours to make. He observes. He endures.

medium.journal_entry: August 1st. 1000 miles behind us now. The ox goes lame at the worst moment—always the worst moment. Snow on the peaks means we're climbing into thinner air. The animal's suffering is also our suffering. That's the arithmetic of the trail.

medium.title: The Smoke
medium.description:
At dusk, Thomas spots smoke rising from a canyon two miles north—too much smoke for a cooking fire. Mary thinks it's a Shoshone camp. James sees it as a chance for trade, maybe fresh meat. But the oxen are restless. One of them keeps pulling toward water you can't see. The smoke could mean water. It could mean trouble. It could mean nothing at all.

You have maybe an hour of useful light left.

choices.label: Ride north toward the smoke. Risk it.

consequences.description: You find a Shoshone hunting camp—seven people, five horses, fresh elk hung in the rocks. The lead man is older, scarred across one shoulder. He doesn't smile. You trade 8 rounds of ammunition and $0.50 for forty pounds of dried elk meat and information: the next reliable water is at Thousand Springs, four days on if you push hard. He also warns that Paiute have been moving through the canyons. When you ride back to camp, Mary doesn't speak to you for hours. Thomas seems relieved. The meat will keep you alive another week, but you've spent ammunition you might need later.

choices.label: Trust the oxen. Head west to find water without the smoke.

consequences.description: The oxen lead you down a dry wash toward a seep—not much, maybe twenty gallons if you dig. It takes two hours to extract enough to fill your remaining barrel. Your hands bleed from the rocks. Mary is quiet but steady. Thomas mutters about instinct and luck. James wonders if he made the wrong choice not investigating the smoke. You'll never know what that fire meant. The uncertainty will sit with him.

choices.label: Stay put. Light your own fire. Let the smoke be someone else's problem.

consequences.description: You camp where you are. Burn sage and deadwood. The night drops below freezing. One of the oxen goes lame—something in its left foreleg. By morning, the smoke is gone. Thomas doesn't comment. Mary checks the lame ox carefully and says it might recover in a few days if you don't push. James lies awake wondering who made that fire and whether they needed help. You've lost nothing material, but something in the group's confidence has thinned. Caution sometimes feels like cowardice when you live with the consequences.

personality_effects.James: Doubt. He feels the weight of decisions that cannot be unmade.
personality_effects.Mary: Her competence in crisis is noted by no one. She watches James choose.
personality_effects.Thomas: Instinct matters more to him than reason. He's learning to trust it.

medium.journal_entry: September 1st. Saw smoke in the canyon. Didn't investigate. We are all tired. The oxen know something we don't. William would have ridden straight into it. I am not William. Cold tonight. First real cold since Sarah.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description: One of your oxen is moving slower than the others, head hanging. The alkali water from two days back has done damage. The animal sweats despite the cool morning. You've still got the Snake River to cross before winter closes in, and you're down to four beasts. This one might not make it to Oregon. The question is whether it makes it to tomorrow.

choices.label: Push on. The ox either keeps up or it doesn't.
Slaughter the ox while it still has meat. Salt what you can.
Leave it behind. Let it die on its own time. Mercy.

personality_effects.Mary: Watched her drink from that pool. Feels like failure.
personality_effects.James: The ox is just flesh. The question is whether you're made for this or not.

medium.journal_entry: September 10th. The alkali took the ox like a slow poison. Three oxen left now, and winter's already showing its teeth in the night air. Thomas would have known what to do. He always did. I don't. The meat is rotting or we are. There's no third choice out here.

medium.title: The Flour is Ruined
medium.description:
You discover the flour sack you pulled from the creek yesterday is growing mold—a gray-green bloom spreading across the damp cloth. Ten pounds of flour, already meager stores, now poisoned. Mary sits nearby, pale and trembling from dysentery. The oxen are thin. You have maybe three weeks of walking left to reach the valley, if luck holds.

James checks the other provisions. The hardtack is still good. The jerky is still good. But flour was meant to stretch everything, to make a kind of bread when there was nothing else.

You could try to scrape off the mold and bake it anyway—people have eaten worse and lived. You could burn it and save the ash for something. Or you could dump it and accept the loss.

choices.label: Scrape off the mold and keep the flour. It's food, mold or not.

consequences.journal_note: Used the moldy flour anyway. James has stomach cramps by evening. Mary can barely keep anything down.

choices.label: Burn the flour. Better to lose it than lose people.

consequences.journal_note: Watched ten pounds of flour burn. It felt like burning money. Mary didn't protest. She knows what spoiled food does to people already sick.

choices.label: Tell Mary the flour is fine and ration it to her. Let James and yourself eat the hardtack and jerky.

consequences.journal_note: Gave Mary the moldy flour. She ate it without question. James suspects but says nothing. The lie tastes worse than anything on this trail.

personality_effects.default: The waste of food—any food—weighs on everyone. Three of you are already in the ground.

medium.journal_entry: Creek took the flour. Now the flour takes what's left of us. Mary's fever came back in the night. She asked if we'd make it to Oregon. I told her yes. The second lie comes easier than the first.

medium.title: The Shoshone Cache
medium.description:
In the gray afternoon, Thomas spots something wedged in the hollow of a massive Douglas fir: a leather parfleche, weathered but intact. Inside, you find dried salmon, pemmican, and two pairs of moccasins—enough food for a week, maybe more. But the cache is recent. Someone left it here deliberately, and recently enough that the leather still holds its shape.

Mary kneels beside it. "This is an offering place," she says quietly. "Or a supply line."

The oxen are exhausted. The rain won't stop. You have maybe three weeks of meager rations left before the high passes. James's jaw tightens. Taking it feels like theft. Leaving it feels like suicide.

choices.label: Take the cache. You need it more than whoever left it.

consequences.journal_entry: We took the Shoshone's stores. Mary wouldn't speak to me for two hours. Thomas said we did what we had to. I kept thinking about someone returning to that tree. The rain tastes like copper.

choices.label: Leave it. Mark the spot and move on. Your conscience is worth something.

consequences.journal_entry: We left the cache untouched and moved on. Mary cried—not from sadness, but relief. Thomas called it foolish. I don't know what it was. The forest felt less hostile afterward, or maybe that's just what we tell ourselves to sleep.

choices.label: Take half. Leave half. A compromise that satisfies no one.

consequences.journal_entry: We took what we thought we needed and left the rest like an apology. It felt cowardly. A full theft or a full mercy—either one would have meant something. This means we're just hungry and guilty.

personality_effects.Mary: Her faith troubles her. She sees divine intention in the cache—either a blessing or a test.
personality_effects.Thomas: Pragmatic. Survival is moral. The dead don't judge.
personality_effects.James: Leadership means living with the choice, whatever it is.

medium.journal_entry: Rain and mud. Two weeks from the passes if we push. Sarah would have known what the cache meant. She understood this country better than we ever will.

medium.title: The Calf
medium.description: You find a calf separated from any herd, maybe three days old, wandering the buffalo grass near your evening camp. It's thin-legged and bawling. Thomas says it probably got cut off in a stampede—they happen out here without warning. Mary suggests taking it along. William points out you're already stretched thin on feed. The calf will die if you leave it. It will slow you down if you don't.

choices.label: Take the calf. Strap it to a wagon, share rations.

consequences.description: The calf settles after the first mile, mute and trusting. Mary names it Henry. William doesn't speak at dinner. The weight slows your pace; you'll make Denver three days later than planned. But Sarah watches the calf at night, and something in her face softens. The oxen don't mind the extra mouth.

choices.label: Butcher it. Fresh meat for three days, hide for patching.

consequences.description: The work is quick and efficient. You'll eat well for a stretch—real protein, not just hardtack. The hide will patch the worn canvas on wagon two. But Mary doesn't eat her portion. Thomas stares at the flames. Sarah asks, very quietly, if it had a mother. William shrugs and says that's how the frontier works. No one sings that night.

choices.label: Leave it tied to a stake with half your water and move on.

consequences.description: You tell yourself the Pawnee or a Lakota band might find it. That it bought you two extra miles today. That survival isn't about sentiment. James knows it's a lie—the calf will be dead by morning, wolves or thirst or just loneliness. You don't sleep well. In the dark, you hear Sarah crying in the other wagon.

personality_effects.Mary: Will push for mercy. Believes the group's strength is measured by what they won't abandon.
personality_effects.William: Will argue pragmatism. Already worried about reaching California before winter. Every pound matters.
personality_effects.Sarah: Quiet. Will internalize whatever you choose. But it will register.

medium.journal_entry: Found a calf today on the prairie. Felt like God testing something, though I don't know what. Every choice here costs blood or conscience, and I'm not sure which is which anymore. The graves we pass don't tell you what killed them—just that they died trying.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox and the Storm
medium.description:
One of the oxen that drank from the alkali pool this morning moves with a hitch in its gait. Its breathing is shallow. The sky above the bluffs has turned the color of a bruise. Mary says the storm will hit within the hour—she can smell it. Thomas suggests pushing hard to reach the next creek before dark. Sarah notes the ox may die if pushed. James notices the animal's eyes are filming over.

The oxen are your only way forward. Lose one here, and the wagon gets heavier for the remaining four.

choices.label: Push hard for the creek. The ox might surprise you.

consequences.journal_note: Pushed the sick ox hard. It collapsed two miles shy of water. We left it there. The storm caught us in the open.

choices.label: Make camp now and tend the animal. Lose ground, but maybe save it.

consequences.journal_note: Camped early. Used almost all the quinine on the ox. Storm passed south of us. The animal's breathing steadied by morning, though it's weak.

choices.label: Slaughter it now for meat. At least something good comes from it.

consequences.journal_note: Killed the ox in an hour. Fresh meat, but the work was ugly. Mary wouldn't look at it. We're one animal lighter and moving slower because of it.

choices.label: Leave it behind. Ride ahead and find water, come back for it later if it survives.

consequences.journal_note: Left the ox. Found good water and shelter from the storm. Sent back at first light but the animal was gone—taken by something, or it wandered off and died alone.

medium.journal_entry: The alkali took one of the oxen. We have four now. The bluffs rise like broken teeth. Thunder sounds close enough to touch. We are small here, and getting smaller.

medium.title: The Fever Dreams
medium.description: Mary wakes screaming in the predawn dark. Mountain fever has her seeing things—she swears there was a woman standing at the edge of camp, dressed in buckskin, watching. When you check, there are fresh tracks in the dust near the fire. Human. Small feet. They lead maybe twenty yards into the rocks, then stop. No scuff marks. They just... end. Mary is burning up. The medicine you have might break the fever, or might not. Thomas suggests pressing on. James, you're tired enough that you can't quite trust your own judgment anymore.

choices.label: Use the medicine now. Mary's delirium could kill her if it worsens.
Wait. Rest the party a full day. Let the fever break on its own or let it run its course.
Investigate the tracks before they're lost. Maybe there's an explanation.

medium.journal_entry: August 15th. Mary saw something. Or didn't. The tracks are real enough. We're two months into the mountains now and the air tastes like copper. The oxen are nervous. Thomas keeps his rifle closer than before. I haven't slept properly since Independence Rock. When did I stop being the one making decisions and start being the one hoping someone else knows what to do? The peaks get whiter every day. We're not halfway there.

medium.title: The Thing in the Ravine
medium.description: During the night watch, you hear something large moving through the ravine behind camp—too deliberate to be elk, too heavy for deer. The sound stops at the tree line fifty yards out. Thomas swears he sees a shape, massive and wrong, before fog swallows it. By morning, three oxen are spooked half-dead with fear. Mary's fever has worsened. The ground is churned mud where nothing was yesterday. No tracks. Just the smell—copper and rot, old and fresh at once.

choices.label: Investigate the ravine. Something killed your oxen's will to move—you need answers.

consequences.journal_note: Found scraps of something—fur, bone—in the ravine. Not elk. Not anything I have a name for. Thomas is quiet now. Won't look at me.

choices.label: Leave immediately. A sick party can't afford delays. Whatever it was, it's gone now.

consequences.journal_note: Drove the oxen hard through the rain. Thomas stares behind us. His hands shake. Mary coughs blood into her sleeve.

choices.label: Stay put. Tend Mary properly. Post watches. Whatever hunts here, it won't try a defended camp twice.

consequences.journal_note: Used the last of the medicine on Mary. Spent the night awake. Heard it circle twice. Thomas fired twice at nothing. The second shot—something screamed.

personality_effects.James: Leadership failing. Every choice now feels like surrender.
personality_effects.Thomas: Breaking. The not-knowing is worse than the thing itself.
personality_effects.Mary: Fever and fear are the same thing. She asked us to leave her behind.

medium.journal_entry: October 1st. Rain all night. Something came to the ravine. I don't know what hunts in these forests. The Pawnee spoke of things that walk on two legs in the deep timber. I thought they meant men. I was wrong about a great many things.

medium.title: The Cough
medium.description: Mary wakes with a wet, rattling cough that won't stop. She's been quiet about it for days—you see the blood on her handkerchief when she thinks you're not looking. The cold fog has settled in the forest like something alive. She insists she can ride, but her horse moves slower. Thomas had a cough like that. So did William, before the fever took him. You have one dose of medicine left.

choices.label: Give her the medicine and half your meager rations. Rest the party for a day.
Keep moving. The medicine won't help if it's lung fever anyway. Ration it for later.
Tell her to ride ahead with one ox and minimal supplies. You'll catch up with the heavier wagon.

personality_effects.Mary: Her trust in you bends like a green branch—it might snap or might hold, depending on what you choose.

medium.journal_entry: October 10th. Mary is dying. I know the signs now. Three graves behind us, and I'm running out of answers that don't end in a shovel and stones. The rain keeps coming. Everything is wet. Everything rots.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description: The ox that drank from the alkali pool this morning moves with deliberate slowness, lagging behind the others. Its eyes are dull. By noon, it's clear the animal won't keep pace without rest. You have maybe three hours before the wagon train catches up to you—if you're still here. The sky darkens to the west. Rain coming, probably hard.

choices.label: Push on. The ox will either recover or it won't. Staying put invites the Pawnee to find us idle.

consequences.notes: The ox collapses at dusk. You butcher it for meat before nightfall. Tough, stringy flesh. William won't stop staring at the bones. The others say nothing.

choices.label: Camp here. Rest the ox, wait it out. Tend it with what medicine we have.

consequences.notes: Rain comes that night—heavy, cold. The ox recovers partly. You lose two days and the security of moving with the main party. When you break camp, no one has caught up. The trail feels wider and emptier.

choices.label: Leave the ox with a note. Take what meat you can carry. Move fast—catch the wagon train before dark.

consequences.notes: You butcher hastily, leaving blood on everything. The meat draws wolves—you hear them hours later, working the bones. You catch the train just before dusk, but the meat is already spoiling in the heat. James makes the call to throw most of it away by morning.

personality_effects.James: Pragmatism. He's chosen hard paths before.
personality_effects.Mary: Duty. She won't say it, but she's relieved when others decide.
personality_effects.Thomas: Fatalism. Whatever happens was always going to happen.
personality_effects.Sarah: Mercy. She sees the ox's eyes and remembers her father's farm.
personality_effects.William: Fear. Separation from the group unmakes him.

medium.journal_entry: April 20, evening. The alkali took one of ours. Nothing poisons slower or quieter than that water. You can't see the sickness coming—it just arrives one morning, settled in like fog. We're three weeks from the Fort and already learning that the land doesn't negotiate. It takes what it wants. The question is only whether we're the ones paying or the animals. For now, they're interchangeable. By Oregon, we might not be.

medium.title: The Wet Flour
medium.description: By midday, the flour has begun to ferment. What started as a salvageable dampness has become a sour paste in the sacks. Mary finds mold threading through the grain—pale, almost delicate. The smell is sharp enough to make Sarah gag. You have roughly 60 pounds of flour left that's still edible, maybe 70 if you're not particular. The rest will spoil within days in this warmth. William suggests burning it. Mary suggests drying what you can over a slow fire. Thomas points out you're two weeks from Fort Laramie, where flour costs dearly. James, it's your call.

choices.label: Dry the flour over slow fires. Salvage what you can.

consequences.description: You camp early and organize a drying operation. Mary tends the flames with methodical patience. The work is tedious—spreading, turning, scraping—and you lose most of the afternoon's travel. Some flour does dry usable enough. Others look at the charred, brittle result and say nothing. But the effort itself matters. People see you trying.

choices.label: Burn it all and ration harder to Fort Laramie.

consequences.description: The flour goes into the fire. William nods approval—clean loss, clean decision. Sarah watches it burn and turns away without speaking. Mary's jaw tightens. You gain nothing but the removal of a rotting thing, and everyone knows it. The math of the trail is cruel: you'll need to hunt more, buy more at inflated frontier prices, and explain to people why their flour turned to ash. But you won't spend days nursing spoiled grain either.

choices.label: Give the worst flour to the oxen. Feed yourselves the rest.

consequences.description: You mix the fouled grain into the oxen feed. They eat it without complaint—animals lack the luxury of refusal. One ox develops a loose belly by evening and falls behind the herd by morning. You press on without it. Thomas says nothing but drives the cart with particular precision. Sarah helps tend the animals but her eyes are hard. The oxen were replaceable. The decision itself, less so.

personality_effects.description: Each party member has different thresholds for waste, pragmatism, and cruelty. Mary values resourcefulness. William values decisiveness. Sarah values mercy. Thomas values survival. Watch what each choice reveals about who they are becoming.

medium.journal_entry: The creek took our flour and gave us mold in return. Sixty-five pounds of grain rotting in canvas sacks. James had to choose between waste and time, between hunger now and hunger later. There's no good answer on the plains. There's only the answer you live with.

medium.title: The Pawnee Hunter
medium.description: A Pawnee hunter on horseback appears at dusk, moving parallel to your wagons at a distance. He carries a rifle and wears a trade-cloth shirt. One of your oxen went lame this morning — Thomas thinks it was the thunder — and you're moving slower than usual. The hunter doesn't approach, but he doesn't leave either. He watches. Mary thinks he's counting your animals. William thinks he's checking if you're armed. Sarah is afraid. The light is failing fast.

choices.label: Offer him the deer meat — a gesture of goodwill.

consequences.description: You send Thomas out with half the fresh kill wrapped in cloth. The Pawnee takes it without expression, nods once, and rides west into the darkening prairie. He's gone by full dark. Sarah sleeps better. But you've given away meat you'll need, and William mutters about weakness. The oxen still needs attention.

choices.label: Circle the wagons tight and ready the rifles — show strength.

consequences.description: You bark orders. Men load weapons. The wagons creak into a rough defensive ring. The Pawnee watches this for ten minutes, then turns his horse and disappears into the dusk without hurrying. You kept your meat. You kept your dignity, maybe. But Mary's jaw is tight — she sees how easily this could have gone different. All night, someone's always awake. No real rest. The lame oxen suffers from the stress.

choices.label: Ignore him. Keep moving steady, act like he isn't there.

consequences.description: You give no orders. You don't point or stare. You move the wagons forward at the same pace, like a man walking past a stray dog. The Pawnee follows for another two miles, then cuts away north. But Sarah saw him watching James — only James — the whole time, like he was reading something. The uncertainty gnaws at everyone. That night, Thomas and William argue about whether the hunter meant threat or just curiosity. No one sleeps well. The lame oxen will need real care tomorrow.

medium.journal_entry: May 10th — Pawnee country now. The land opens up and there's nothing between us and the sky but grass. Thomas's deer was a mercy, but it drew attention. A hunter came at dusk. We made our choice and lived with it. The oxen is struggling. There are nights out here where you feel the weight of every decision, every mile behind you, and you understand that going back isn't possible anymore. Only forward into what we can't see.

medium.title: The Fever Comes
medium.description: Sarah wakes in the pre-dawn gray, shaking. Her skin is hot to the touch. By afternoon, William starts complaining of chills despite the heat. Mary recognizes the symptoms — mosquito fever, though she doesn't say it aloud. The swelling in Sarah's neck worsens. William's coherence starts slipping by evening. You have four doses of quinine remaining. James wants to push on. Mary argues for a rest day. The storm that's been building all afternoon finally breaks, soaking the camp and making travel impossible anyway. The question becomes: how do you ration medicine for two people when neither may survive regardless?

choices.label: Give both of them full doses of quinine now. Exhaust your medicine reserves.

consequences.journal_note: Emptied the medicine chest for them. Their fevers broke by midnight. Whether it was the quinine or their own stubborn constitutions, we'll never know. Mary watched me do it without comment.

choices.label: Give Sarah the medicine — she's weaker and less likely to survive without it. Comfort William with willow bark and hope.

consequences.journal_note: William's fever broke this morning but something in his eyes is different. Mary won't look at me. James said nothing, which was worse than if he'd shouted. The willow bark didn't cure him — it just postponed the real reckoning.

choices.label: Ration the medicine between them — give each person a half dose, stretch it further, pray it's enough.

consequences.journal_note: Split the doses. Neither recovered fully. Both are still sick, weaker now, and we still have a thousand miles ahead. I may have killed them slowly instead of quickly. The mathematics of survival don't feel like mathematics at all.

choices.label: Save the medicine. Nature will decide. Separate them from camp to avoid spreading it further — practical thinking.
