consequences.journal_note: They're still breathing but barely. The others won't speak to me around the fire. In the night, I heard Mary say something about 'murderer's economics.' She wasn't wrong. The medicine is still in my pack. I can't bring myself to use it now.

personality_effects.note: Mary has stronger moral convictions. James is pragmatic but not cruel. Thomas is sentimental. Sarah and William are dying regardless — how you treat them defines the party's survival psychology as much as their bodies.

medium.journal_entry: The river runs red-brown and the sky is the color of a bruise. Sarah asked for water this morning and I gave it to her. That much at least I can do. There are names carved in Independence Rock behind us. I wonder if anyone will remember ours, or if we're just another set of scratches the rain will wear smooth. The fever doesn't care about intentions. It just takes what it wants.

medium.title: The Crossing
medium.description: You find a shallow ford two miles upstream, but a Pawnee hunting party—maybe eight riders—has set up a temporary camp there. They're not aggressive, just watching. Your scout Thomas says they're tracking buffalo. The river here runs fast from recent rains. Mary's fever has worsened; she needs rest more than travel. The storm building in the west will be here in two hours. The ford is passable now. The Pawnee haven't moved to stop you, but they haven't moved aside either.

choices.label: Cross at the ford. Do it now, before the storm. Ignore the Pawnee.

consequences.notes: Crossing is rough. One ox panics in the current and is lost. Sarah takes a fall into the water—soaked and shaking. The wagon tilts but stays upright. Mary barely conscious in the back. You make it across before the storm hits, but the water was colder and faster than it looked.

choices.label: Wait. Camp here tonight. Talk to the Pawnee—trade for information about the route ahead.

consequences.notes: The Pawnee are cautious but willing to trade. An elder tells you the main crossing is ten miles north—broader, slower water. He warns of Lakota hunting parties farther west. You trade 50 cents worth of tobacco and sugar. Mary spends the night in delirium. By morning the fever hasn't broken, but it hasn't killed her either.

choices.label: Take the southern route around the river. It adds days, but you avoid the Pawnee entirely.

consequences.notes: The detour is longer and drier. Mosquitoes are relentless at dawn and dusk. You burn through medicine trying to manage Mary's fever. One pair of shoes falls apart on rocky ground. You cover barely two miles a day. The storm passes south of you, leaving everything humid and stinking. Gain nothing but time wasted.

medium.journal_entry: June 15. Found the ford. The Pawnee are there. Mary's eyes are glassed over—she doesn't know where she is. We have three choices and none of them are good. The storm's coming either way. I can feel the heat breaking already, that pressure drop that means lightning. We're 500 miles from Independence and I'm wondering if Mary will see 600.

medium.title: The Scout's Fever
medium.description: Thomas returns from scouting the trail ahead, his horse lathered and stumbling. His eyes are bright with fever—too bright. He claims he rode through a ravine where the air itself seemed wrong, thick and cold despite the heat. Said he heard singing. Couldn't find the trail back the first time. When he did, he'd lost two hours he can't account for. Now his skin burns and he's asking for water he doesn't drink. The storm clouds are darkening fast. You need to decide: push on while you can, make camp and nurse him, or send someone back to find what he lost in that ravine.

choices.label: Push on to higher ground before the storm. Thomas will recover or he won't.

personality_effects.Thomas: resentment—driven hard while sick
personality_effects.Mary: doubt—questions James's judgment
personality_effects.Sarah: hardened—survival over sentiment

consequences.journal_entry: Thomas burning up but we couldn't stop. Storm came anyway. Made camp in the dark, mud to the wheel hubs. He's talking fever-talk about the ravine. I gave him the last of the laudanum. Mary won't look at me.

choices.label: Make camp now. Use the medicine to bring down his fever. The storm can pound.

personality_effects.Mary: gratitude—you chose mercy
personality_effects.Sarah: impatience—two days wasted
personality_effects.James: doubt—delay costs time we don't have

consequences.journal_entry: Made camp. Thomas's fever broke by midnight but the storm was vicious. Lightning struck an ox down the line—not ours, thank Christ. Two days lost but Thomas is speaking sense again. Or most of it. He still won't talk about the ravine.

choices.label: Send Mary back with a rifle to find what Thomas lost. You'll camp and wait, but keep the animals ready to move.

personality_effects.Mary: anger—sent alone into fever-country
personality_effects.Sarah: fear—something's wrong with that ravine
personality_effects.Thomas: guilt—knows he's unreliable

consequences.journal_entry: Mary rode back with a rifle and came back in two hours soaked through, saying she found the ravine but couldn't enter it. The mouth looked like it was breathing. Said she heard the singing too. Wouldn't go further. Thomas won't eat. None of us slept well.

personality_effects.Thomas: fevered and uncertain—did something happen in that ravine or was it just sickness?
personality_effects.Mary: observant—she's wondering if Thomas is reliable
personality_effects.Sarah: watching—she senses the group is fragmenting
personality_effects.James: leader's burden—every choice costs someone something

medium.journal_entry: June 25. Thomas came back wrong from scouting. Says he lost time. Says he heard singing in a ravine that shouldn't exist. The fever's real enough. The rest—I don't know. Storm coming hard. We're pinned here until I decide. Whatever I choose, someone suffers for it.

medium.title: The Fever
medium.description:
Sarah's been quiet since yesterday. This morning she wouldn't eat. By noon her skin is hot and dry despite the water you've made her drink. Mary thinks it's the heat. Thomas thinks it's something worse—he's seen cholera on the Mississippi, says the way she's breathing reminds him of corpses. Sarah herself says nothing, just stares at the dust ahead with eyes that don't track the wagons properly.

You've got three days until the next reliable water at Fort Laramie. The heat shows no sign of breaking.

choices.label: Push the pace hard—reach the fort faster, get her proper help.

consequences.description: The oxen labor under doubled speed. Sarah coughs dust and whispers for water you can't spare fast enough. By nightfall one ox has collapsed—heat and strain. You leave it, take the meat before it spoils. Sarah's fever hasn't broken. Thomas grows quieter. Mary's jaw sets tight as iron.

choices.label: Slow down, ration water carefully, nurse her at camp.

consequences.description: You make camp by a dried riverbed and wait. Mary tends Sarah through the afternoon, uses one of your precious medicines—willow bark and opium mixed thin. Sarah sweats through it by evening but the fever breaks a little. Thomas paces the perimeter, says you're wasting time, that Fort Laramie is three days away not five. He's not wrong. The oxen rest though. Water is rationed to a pint per person.

choices.label: Sarah rides ahead with Mary and two oxen to reach the fort alone. You follow with Thomas and the supplies.

consequences.description: Mary takes it hard—you can see it in how she ties Sarah's bedroll. They ride out before dawn with canvas, water, and two of the stronger oxen. You don't know if that was mercy or cowardice. Thomas is satisfied, even pleased. He doesn't say it but you can feel it: the deadweight is gone. You make steady time. At night you wonder if Mary will reach the fort. If Sarah will. If you'll find them there or find nothing at all.

personality_effects.Mary: If slow-down chosen: grows protective, calculates odds of survival obsessively. If split chosen: becomes distant, blames you quietly through every camp task.
personality_effects.Thomas: If fast pace chosen: respects the gamble, sees it as strength. If slow chosen: mutters about poor decisions, questions your leadership. If split chosen: becomes the alpha, starts suggesting camp arrangements uninvited.
personality_effects.James: The weight of it. Fever breaks or doesn't. Cholera or heat sickness—no way to know until the fort. You made the call.

medium.journal_entry: Sarah is burning from inside. Mary won't say it but I see her checking Sarah's fingernails for the blue tint that means death. We are 800 miles from Missouri and the fort is still three days distant if we're lucky. The dust tastes like iron. The sun doesn't set so much as it abandons us. I think about that Henderson grave. I think about how many more are out here that nobody bothered to mark. The oxen know something we don't. They can smell the water that isn't there yet.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox and the Storm Coming
medium.description: By midday, the ox that drank from the alkali pool has stopped pulling. Its eyes are rheumy. It stands with weight favoring three legs. The sky to the west is darkening—a real storm, the kind that turns gullies into rivers. You're in a narrow valley with rock walls on both sides. Mary is burning with fever in the wagon. Thomas suggests pressing on to higher ground before the rain hits. James says the sick ox needs rest, but resting here means wet camp and Mary getting worse in the damp. Either way, someone suffers.

choices.label: Push hard for higher ground. Leave the sick ox here with supplies if it falls.

consequences.description: The ox collapses a mile up the trail. You leave it with water and grain you can't spare. Mary's fever spikes in the wagon bed as the storm hits anyway—the higher ground barely saves you. The rain is biblical. By morning, her cough has a wet, dangerous sound. Thomas approves of the decision. Mary doesn't forgive it.

choices.label: Make camp in the valley. Bleed the ox, rest it. Ride out the storm.

consequences.description: You lance the ox's leg to let bad blood out—an old remedy with dubious results. It stands easier. The storm passes overhead, thunder loud enough to hurt. Your camp is mud and stone. Mary's fever holds steady. Thomas grows irritable about lost time. By morning the ox is walking, slow but willing. The delay costs you—you'll hit the high passes later in the season.

choices.label: Give the sick ox an extra ration of grain. Double-check Mary's medicine. Choose the steadiest ox to cut from the team.

consequences.description: You abandon one of the good oxen to lighten the load—a waste of what's healthy. The sick ox doesn't recover from grain alone. Mary gets her quinine and willow bark. The storm hits as you camp, but moving faster means you're mostly clear of it by dark. In the morning, the abandoned ox is gone—coyotes, likely. You saved something that couldn't be saved anyway.

medium.journal_entry: August 15th. The mountains are beautiful in a way that makes you lonely. One ox is poisoned and Mary is burning. The storm comes either way. In the West, you learn that good choices are a luxury. You get to pick which people you fail.

medium.title: The Ruined Flour
medium.description: The creek crossing yesterday was worse than you thought. The flour sack absorbed water like a wound. This morning, Mary discovered the top layer has gone gray with mold—a fist-sized bloom that stinks of rot. The rest might be salvageable if you act now, but it means stopping for half a day in this exposed country. The oxen are tired. So is everyone else. You have 25 pounds of food left and over 300 miles to the Willamette Valley.

choices.label: Spread the flour to dry in the sun. Pick through it later.

consequences.narrative: Mary works methodically, spreading the damp flour across a blanket. Her hands move like she's done this before—perhaps she has. Thomas helps without being asked. By afternoon, the sun has crusted the outer layer, and you salvage perhaps 18 pounds of usable grain. But the delay costs you miles, and the cold night that follows is harder than expected. Someone coughs. It might be nothing.

choices.label: Discard it. The mold will spread to everything else.

consequences.narrative: You scrape the ruined flour into the dust. Mary's jaw tightens but she says nothing. Thomas stares at the waste like you've burned money. You push on. The savings in time feel hollow. That flour represented a week of eating something other than salt pork and hardtack. Everyone knows it. No one speaks of it.

choices.label: Keep it. Boil the good parts into mush. People are hungry enough not to ask questions.

consequences.narrative: You boil the flour with water and salt, skimming the visible mold. The resulting paste is gray and smells wrong, but it fills bellies. Thomas eats without complaint. Mary picks at hers, then eats anyway. James—you—taste nothing but hunger and the faint chemical bitterness of something dying. Within two days, James's stomach cramps. The medicine helps, but not much. The question no one asks: what else are you willing to hide?

personality_effects.Mary: Practical. Waste offends her. She will judge harshly any choice that squanders resources or lies about them.
personality_effects.Thomas: Hungrier each day. He won't complain, but he notices. The younger ones always notice scarcity first.
personality_effects.James: Tired of making impossible choices. This one feels smaller than it is, which somehow makes it worse.

medium.journal_entry: September 1st. The flour is spoiled. We have not enough to be wasteful and not enough to be careless. The desert gives nothing and the mountains are still ahead. Mary's face when I made the choice—I should not have looked at it so carefully. Thomas is losing weight. His ribs show now. The coyotes are still out there. I hear them sometimes when I should be sleeping, and I cannot decide if the sound is real or if I have simply become the kind of man who hears things that hunt him.

medium.title: The Meat Won't Last
medium.description: Mary's dysentery has worsened overnight. She can't keep food down. The venison Thomas shot is already turning in the heat—you'll lose half of it by tomorrow if you don't act. James wants to push hard toward Fort Hall, maybe five days if the oxen hold. Mary needs rest, shade, and broth made from bones. You have enough firewood for a cook fire, but burning it means losing time and drawing attention from whoever else might be in this country.

choices.label: Make camp and boil the spoiling meat into broth. Mary needs it; the trail can wait.

personality_effects.Mary: gratitude mixed with guilt
personality_effects.James: frustration at delay

choices.label: Gut and salt the venison now. Preserve what you can and ride hard. Mary will have to manage.

personality_effects.Mary: resentment deepens
personality_effects.James: pragmatism justified

choices.label: Leave the venison. Take Mary on the wagon alone, send the others ahead with oxen and supplies. Split the party.

personality_effects.Mary: abandoned but alive
personality_effects.James: knows he abandoned someone
The weight of three graves already. He thinks like a man counting days, not people.
personality_effects.Mary: The dysentery made her delirious last night. She asked for water that wasn't there. She knows she's slowing them down.

medium.journal_entry: Sept 15. Meat spoiling. Mary burning with fever again. The desert doesn't care what you've already lost—it just takes more. Thomas would've known what to do. Thomas always knew.

medium.title: The Cough
medium.description: Thomas wakes before dawn with a wet, rattling sound in his chest. Not the usual morning cough. This is something deeper—something that doesn't stop. Mary holds a candle near his face and sees the dark spots on the cloth when he pulls it away from his mouth. James knows what blood in sputum means this time of year. The rain outside shows no sign of letting up. Thomas insists he can ride, but his hands shake when he tries to saddle his horse.

choices.label: Push onward at steady pace. Thomas rides between the wagons where the wind is less.
Make camp here. Boil water for steam. Use the last of the medicine.
Leave Thomas with minimal supplies at the next settlement. You'll move faster, and he'll find help—or die warm in a bed instead of on the road.

Mary.choice_0: Her jaw tightens. She doesn't argue. She knows.
Mary.choice_1: Relief floods her face—someone is doing something. She takes the first watch to boil water.
Mary.choice_2: She looks at James like he's already a ghost. She won't speak to him for three days.

Thomas.choice_0: He nods, grateful not to slow the group. By evening his fever climbs to 103.
Thomas.choice_1: Tears come. Not from pain. From the unfamiliar sensation of being cared for.
Thomas.choice_2: He doesn't argue. Isn't sure if relief or rage is winning in his chest. Probably both.

medium.journal_entry: October 1st. The Willamette Valley is so close I can almost taste it in the rain. But Thomas is coughing blood. The medicine is nearly gone. Mary looked at me this morning like I was the one dying—like she could see which of us wouldn't make it. I thought by now we'd buried Sarah and William and that would be the price. Seems God wasn't finished collecting.

medium.title: The Hermit's Claim
medium.description: A man emerges from the rain-soaked forest, rifle lowered but not raised. His cabin sits fifty yards back, smoke coming thin from the chimney. He's been watching your wagon. Says he owns this stretch of forest—has a claim, though you see no marker, no survey stake. He offers shelter for the night: warm fire, roof, maybe some salt meat. But he wants payment. Twenty dollars, he says. Or he wants to buy Mary's wedding ring. His eyes don't leave it. He seems neither threatening nor friendly—just transactional, like a man used to being alone and having learned to price what loneliness costs others.

choices.label: Pay the twenty dollars and stay the night.

consequences.notes: The cabin is warmer than the wagon. No vermin. The hermit says little. In the morning, your oxen are rested but one has a slight limp—nothing serious. You leave at dawn.

choices.label: Refuse to pay and camp in the rain.

consequences.notes: The hermit watches from his porch as you circle wagons downslope. Rain soaks through. James develops a wet cough by morning. Mary's spirits drop further.

choices.label: Offer the ring. It's only metal.

consequences.notes: Mary doesn't speak for hours after you hand it over. The ring was her mother's. The hermit grins—a real smile, first time. He gives you salt pork and lets you stay. But something in Mary's face has gone quieter, flatter. She stares at her naked hand.

choices.label: Tell him you'll camp here and he can leave or you'll shoot.

consequences.notes: The hermit nods slowly, unsurprised. Steps back inside. You hear him bolt the door. The shot you fired is a waste—your shot control is bad when you're scared and cold. That night, James sees a figure watching the wagon perimeter. Might have been the hermit. Might have been the forest. Either way, no one sleeps well.

medium.journal_entry: October 10th. Encountered a man in the forest who claims ownership of ground that belongs to no one and everyone. He wanted money or jewels for shelter from weather that costs nothing. The question of what a man may charge for what he has taken from the wilderness—no answer in the trail books. James thinks we were tested. I think we were simply in the way of someone else's survival. The distinction may not matter.

medium.title: The Sick Ox
medium.description: At dawn, Thomas finds one of the oxen down—fever-sick, breathing shallow. Its eyes are rheumy. The animal won't stand. You've another 800 miles to go, and oxen are currency out here. The creature might recover in a few days of rest. It might die by tomorrow. Rest means burning daylight and supplies. Moving on means abandoning it to wolves, or a mercy killing.

choices.label: Push the herd on. Leave the ox behind.

consequences.description: The ox's cries follow you into the afternoon. Thomas won't speak for hours. William says nothing but his jaw is tight. Mary nods—she understands the mathematics of survival. By evening the sounds stop. You make good distance, but something in the camp's breath has changed. There's no singing at the fire.

choices.label: Camp here three days. Nurse the ox.

consequences.description: Sarah boils water with willow bark—an old remedy her mother taught her. Thomas stays with the ox through the second night. By the third morning, it stands. Weak, but standing. The party moves slower, lets the herd pick the pace. Thomas looks less like a ghost. But James knows you're running behind schedule now, and the Rockies wait for no one. Mary watches the calendar more carefully after this.

choices.label: Put a bullet in it. Use the meat.

consequences.description: The shot echoes across the prairie. You butcher the carcass in two hours—the meat is decent, no rot. Rations improve for a week. William approves; he's practical about such things. Sarah turns away during the work. Thomas says nothing, but his eyes are a stranger's eyes now. He was the one who'd been tending the animal since Missouri. That night he stares into the fire like he's reading something in the flames. The meat tastes like necessity and regret.

personality_effects.Thomas: Bond with animals deeper than is wise on the trail
personality_effects.William: Willing to make hard choices without sentiment
personality_effects.Sarah: Healer's instinct—wants to fix what's broken
personality_effects.Mary: Timekeeper—always counting what's been lost to delay
personality_effects.James: Leader caught between mercy and miles

medium.journal_entry: The ox went down this morning. Hard to say why—could be the water here, could be bad luck following us from the start. Thomas was raw about it. Whatever we choose, something gets left behind: time, conscience, or the animal itself. The prairie doesn't care which. The grave we passed yesterday was a year old. I wonder how many days that Henderson family wasted deciding what to do.

medium.title: The Fever Dream at Dusk
medium.description: William hasn't left the wagon since dawn. His skin is hot-dry. Mary found him muttering about a woman in a white dress standing at the river's edge, beckoning. There's no woman. The sluggish ox collapsed an hour ago—heat, alkali poisoning, or something worse. Thomas wants to shoot it. James is silent, which means he's afraid. The storm is coming darker than it should be for June. Someone needs to decide: push through the night to make better time, or camp here and tend William properly.

choices.label: Camp here. Tend William. Rest the oxen. Accept the lost day.

consequences.notes: William's fever breaks by midnight. He remembers nothing. The dead ox becomes jerky. The party is fractionally steadier but Mary notices James's hands shaking—he almost let a man die to save time.

choices.label: Push on through the night. Shoot the ox. William will have to manage.

consequences.notes: William delirious by 2 a.m. Hallucinates the woman again—this time, James sees her too for a moment in the lightning. Probably fever. Probably. You reach the Platte crossing by dawn, but Mary won't look at James. Thomas is quiet in a way that means something broke.

choices.label: Split the party. Send William back to Fort Laramie with one person. The rest continue.

consequences.notes: The party fractures. Mary volunteers to take William back—80 miles against the grain. She's furious with James but won't say why. Sarah thinks it's practical. Thomas agrees. William is grateful and ashamed. You're down two people and won't see them again. The road feels longer now.

personality_effects.James: A leader watching his authority crack. The woman in white haunts him now too. He hasn't told anyone.
personality_effects.Mary: Begins to doubt whether James will choose people or distance.
personality_effects.Sarah: Survival logic. She's already calculating who's expendable.
personality_effects.Thomas: Loyal but observant. Knows something is wrong with more than just William.
personality_effects.William: Caught between gratitude and shame. Fever or curse—he isn't sure which scares him more.

medium.journal_entry: William saw a woman at the river. I saw her too in the storm, but that's not possible. We lost an ox to the alkali. The storm is purple-black, wrong for the season. Chimney Rock is behind us now. Three hundred miles to Fort Laramie if we're lucky. Mary made coffee at midnight and wouldn't meet my eyes. I think she's afraid of me. Or afraid for me. The woman is still standing at the edge of my vision.

medium.title: The Scout's Fever
medium.description: Thomas returns from scouting the pass ahead. His shirt is soaked through despite the cool mountain air. He says he found a way through—a narrow canyon, steep but passable—but his eyes won't focus right. When you touch his forehead, he's burning. He keeps talking about figures in the rocks that weren't there when he looked directly at them. Mary says his lips have gone pale. The pass he found might save you two days of climbing. Or it might be a dying man's hallucination.

choices.label: Take the canyon route Thomas found. Trust his scout work.

consequences.description: You push into the canyon by late afternoon. The passage is tight—oxen scrape stone on both sides. Thomas rides near the rear, slumped in his saddle. That night, his fever spikes to 104. You use your last bottle of quinine and pray. By dawn, he's lucid again, though weak. The canyon saved you the long way around, but Thomas paid for it. He doesn't remember the figures in the rocks.

choices.label: Ignore the canyon. Take the main pass—longer but safer.

consequences.description: You bypass the canyon. Thomas rests in the wagon, fever dropping by evening. The main pass climbs harder and takes longer, eating into your stores. But Thomas lives to see the far side. James notices Mary watching the canyon mouth as you leave, her jaw tight. She says nothing.

choices.label: Leave Thomas here with a day's rations. Send James back with him when he's fit to travel.

consequences.description: You split the party. James stays with Thomas in a sheltered draw with a small fire, medicine, and food. You press on with Mary and the oxen. That night, lying in your bedroll, you hear a sound like wind but formed into something almost like a voice. In the morning, James catches up—Thomas is better, moving slow but moving. James doesn't mention the sound. Mary does. She says she heard it too.

personality_effects.Mary: Watching. Judging your choices about the sick.
personality_effects.James: Silent. He knows fever from the cholera years—knows what it means.
personality_effects.Thomas: Grateful if helped. Ashamed if left. Either way, changed.

medium.journal_entry: Thomas came back wrong from the pass. Fever dreams or mountain sickness—the difference matters less than the choice it forced. We are thin now. Four of us started. Two are gone. The mountains don't care about our intentions. They only care about weight and days. We will remember this as the day we learned which lives cost how much.

medium.title: The Shoshone Camp
medium.description:
You crest a ridge at dusk and see smoke rising from a canyon below. Twenty or thirty lodges arranged in a loose circle. Horses grazing. A hunting party has clearly had success—you can see elk hides stretched on frames. One of the Shoshone notices your party and raises a hand. Not hostile. Not yet. Mary is feverish and breathing shallow. Thomas's boots are held together with cloth wraps. You have four oxen, fifty-five pounds of food, and three days before the next pass becomes dangerous with snow.

The Shoshone are watching. Waiting to see what you'll do.

choices.label: Approach the camp. Trade what you can for fresh meat and information about the pass ahead.

consequences.notes: The Shoshone are cautious but willing. You trade ammunition and cash for forty pounds of smoked elk and directions. An elder woman examines Mary's fever, gives you prepared willow bark—not reliable, but something. One young warrior eyes your oxen too closely. You leave feeling watched but not threatened. The meat will help.

choices.label: Circle wide around the camp. Keep moving. You don't know their intentions.

consequences.notes: You push hard into the darkening hills to avoid them. One ox goes lame on rocky ground—you'll have to shoot it tomorrow. Mary's fever spikes in the cold night. No medical help. You make distance but the cost is visible on everyone's faces. Thomas mutters about cowardice. You smell the elk meat cooking below, carried on the wind.

choices.label: Send Thomas down alone with trade goods. Less threatening. You stay with Mary and the oxen.

consequences.notes: Thomas descends as the light fails. Two hours pass. Three. You watch the fires, hearing distant voices. He returns before dark with meat and a warning: the Shoshone are moving the camp tomorrow, heading south. Winter's coming early to these mountains. The pass will be dangerous. Thomas won't say what made them decide to trade, only that an old woman studied your supply list and laughed. Thirty pounds of elk. No medicine. Mary still burning.

personality_effects.James: Responsibility without clarity. Every choice now has a shadow.
personality_effects.Mary: The fever is eating her judgment. She sleeps and wakes confused about which day it is.
personality_effects.Thomas: Anger sharpened by helplessness. He wants the mountains to end.

medium.journal_entry: August 15, 1848. The Shoshone camp below. Mary burning. Two days of food left if we're careful. The oxen won't last another week in these rocks. I keep thinking about the old woman laughing at our supplies. What does a woman who lives in these mountains see when she looks at us? That we're dying? That we've already lost?

medium.title: The Bones at Willow Creek
medium.description: You're following the canyon floor where water runs thin and cold. The oxen are thirsty but moving steady. Then Mary points ahead—bones scattered at the edge of a willow thicket. Human bones. A skull picked clean. The leather scraps nearby are old, maybe a year or more. No grave markers. No indication of how many are here or what killed them. Mary's fever is making her see meanings in everything. James, this could be Shoshone dead from the cholera that swept through last spring, or it could be emigrants. The remains are arranged odd—not a natural scatter. One femur is split lengthwise, as if for marrow. Your oxen are uneasy. You have maybe two hours of good light left.

choices.label: Keep moving. Don't stop to examine or bury them.

consequences.description: You push on without stopping. It's the practical choice—you're short on time, food, and daylight. But Mary watches those bones until they're out of sight, fever-bright and muttering. She doesn't speak to you for the next eight miles. That night she's worse: 103 degrees by feel, talking about things that aren't there. The image stays with you too, sharper than you'd like.

choices.label: Stop and gather the bones to give proper burial.

consequences.description: You stop and spend two hours collecting bones into a pile, speaking a few words that feel thin in the canyon wind. You find no personal effects, no names. Mary helps despite her fever—it seems to calm her, the ritual of it. That night her temperature drops slightly and she sleeps without nightmares. But you've only made two miles, and you're burning daylight. The medicine you used to bring her fever down leaves you with one dose remaining.

choices.label: Examine the site closely for supplies or information about what happened here.

consequences.description: You spend two hours picking through the site. The split femur disturbs you more up close—the cut is clean, deliberate. No scavenger work. In the thicket you find a powder horn and five rounds, a torn journal page (illegible, water-soaked), and a child's shoe, small and worn through at the heel. Mary vomits twice and won't explain why. That night she's worse again—muttering about what people do when the food runs out. You both understand now what you're looking at. It changes something. The miles feel longer.

personality_effects.Mary: Already fragile with fever. Whatever you choose will shape how she sees you and the trail.
personality_effects.James: The weight of leadership. There is no choice here that costs nothing.

medium.journal_entry: September 10. Found dead at Willow Creek. The bones were arranged like they were trying to tell us something, but I don't know the language. The canyon is closing in. Mary is burning up. The oxen smell something on the wind I can't place. We are 1400 miles from Independence and I think I understand now why so few people talk about what the trail really is. It's not the weather that kills you. It's what you become when the weather has already killed everything else.

medium.title: The Shoshone at the Crossing
medium.description: At dawn, you find three Shoshone hunters camped at the river crossing ahead—your planned route. They're butchering an elk. One of them sees your wagon and calls out in broken English: 'You trade?' He points to your oxen, then to dried meat laid out on hides. Behind him, his companions watch without expression. Your water is nearly gone. Mary is fevered and weak. The meat could keep her alive another week. But the crossing is the only ford for thirty miles, and these men control it.

choices.label: Trade two oxen for the dried elk meat and permission to cross.

consequences.journal_note: Gave the hunters what they wanted without haggling. Mary ate for the first time in days. James felt the weight of being down to one ox.

choices.label: Offer $40 instead of oxen. You need them more than meat.

consequences.journal_note: The hunters seemed insulted. One spat. They let you cross but took your money and made you wait until dark. Mary's fever worsened from the delay.

choices.label: Attempt to cross upriver, avoiding the hunters entirely.

consequences.journal_note: The river was deeper than it looked. One ox drowned. You nearly did. Arrived at camp drenched and hypothermic, fever-dreaming about Thomas's face in the current.

choices.label: Approach peacefully, show no weapons. Ask to share the meat—as guests, not traders.

consequences.journal_note: The lead hunter studied James's face for a long time, then nodded. They shared elk and water around a fire. No words were necessary. At dawn, they pointed the easiest crossing and sent you on. James wondered if he'd just met men as trapped as he was.

medium.journal_entry: September 15th. The river country is beautiful and pitiless. We have seen no other wagons in three days. Mary speaks in fragments—about her mother's garden, about things that haven't happened yet. I check her forehead every hour. One ox left would not be enough for the High Desert. The Shoshone knew this. They knew we were desperate before we said a word. That is their country. We are trespassers who must bargain for passage.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description: One of your oxen moves through the mud like it's pulling the whole wagon alone. Its breathing is shallow. The other two compensate, straining against their yokes. Thomas says the alkali poisoned it — says you saw it drink yesterday. Mary wants to shoot it and lighten the load. James is quiet, which means he's thinking about the money that ox represents. You have three miles until the next camp where there might be water that's safe. The rain is getting heavier.

choices.label: Push on. The ox might recover.

consequences.description: The ox collapses two miles from camp. You have to leave it behind, throat cut to spare it suffering. The wagon is lighter but the remaining oxen are pushed harder than they should be. Thomas doesn't say I told you so, but his eyes do.

choices.label: Shoot it now. Salvage what meat you can.

consequences.description: The shot echoes through the forest. Butchering takes time. The meat is dark and you're not certain it's entirely wholesome, but you salt what you can. The decision feels necessary and wrong at once. Mary's practical. Thomas looks away the whole time.

choices.label: Leave it behind and push harder with the remaining oxen.

consequences.description: The remaining oxen labor under the increased load. One stumbles in the mud. You make good time but something has shifted in Thomas — he keeps staring back at where the ox was, as if it's still following. When you stop, he barely eats. Mary is quieter than usual. The rain tastes like alkali.

personality_effects.Thomas: Weakened by the weight of abandonment. Practical cruelty troubles him more than James or Mary.
personality_effects.Mary: Strengthened by decisive action. She sees mercy in efficiency.
personality_effects.James: The ledger always matters. Money becomes harder to hold onto than it seems.

medium.journal_entry: Oct 1 — One ox drank poison yesterday. The rain won't stop. We're three days from the Willamette Valley by James's reckoning, but the trail is becoming soup. Everything smells like rot. Sarah and William are buried three weeks behind us. I wonder if the rain reaches them too.

medium.title: The Flour
medium.description: The soaked flour has begun to ferment in its sack. When Mary opens it to portion the morning rations, the smell hits—sweet, vinegary, wrong. The flour is mostly ruined, though the bottom layers are still usable if you're willing to eat it. You have 35 pounds of it. The rain continues. Your oxen are tired. James notices Mary staring at the sack for a long time without moving.

choices.label: Use it anyway. Fermented flour won't kill you, and you need the calories.
Discard the ruined portion, salvage what you can. Ration it thin.
Push hard today without breakfast. Hunt if you can find game in this weather.

personality_effects.Mary: She's the one who sees the flour spoil. If you waste it, she feels the weight of it—three people dead and now this. If you eat it anyway, there's something like relief in her face, the decision made for her.

medium.journal_entry: October 10th. The creek took the flour. Mary found it this morning, gone sick. We're 1700 miles in and the rain is relentless. The oxen smell it too, the ferment. Even animals know when something's wrong. Thomas used to say waste was a sin. He's not here to judge.

medium.title: The Cough
medium.description: Thomas has been quiet since this morning. By midday, when the wagons stop for water, you hear it—a wet, rattling cough that doubles him over. He spits into the grass. Blood, thin and bright. He straightens, says it's nothing, just the dust. But Mary pulls you aside. She's noticed the fever behind his eyes. The returning party mentioned cholera in the settlements to the south. It might be that. It might be something else. Might be nothing at all, just spring dust in the lungs. The pass snow comes in six weeks. If Thomas gets worse, you'll have to decide whether to push through or stop and wait. Neither choice feels good.

choices.label: Keep steady pace. Watch him closely. Medicine only if it worsens.

consequences.journal_entry: Thomas coughs. We move on. No remedy yet but prayer and caution.

choices.label: Stop here two days. Use medicine. Boil water. Tend him.

consequences.journal_entry: We camp. I give Thomas willow bark and whiskey. Mary sits with him through the night. The cough eases by dawn.

choices.label: Bleed him. Old remedy. It worked before in other sicknesses.

consequences.journal_entry: I bled Thomas as my father taught me. His face went gray. The cough worsened. Mary won't look at me.

medium.journal_entry: Found Thomas coughing blood into the grass this morning. The returned travelers spoke of cholera. We have not yet decided what kind of people we will be out here.

medium.title: The Burial
medium.description: Mary finds a grave marker half-buried in prairie grass—a wooden cross, weathered but readable: 'Margaret Hutchins, age 7, died of fever, June 1847.' The ground around it is disturbed. Animal work, likely. Sarah goes pale. William suggests moving camp quickly. Thomas kneels and studies the marker with an intensity that makes everyone uncomfortable. James asks what you want to do. There's no town for fifty miles. The body is long gone. But leaving it unmarked feels like a choice, and choices out here have weight.

choices.label: Repair the grave marker and say words—waste time but honor the dead
Leave it as is and move on—there's no shortage of graves on this trail
Take the wood for repairs—the oxen need better yokes and Margaret needs nothing anymore

personality_effects.Mary: Wants to honor the child. The fever worry lives in her now.
personality_effects.Thomas: Sees Margaret's mother in this. Lost his own sister to consumption—similar age.
personality_effects.Sarah: Young enough to imagine herself in that grave. Or her children there.
personality_effects.William: Pragmatist. Graves don't move wagons. The dead don't complain.

medium.journal_entry: Found a child's grave today. Margaret Hutchins. The prairie takes them young and keeps them anonymous. Made me think about what happens to our names out here—whether they matter when the weather erases them. Thunder last night. Thomas's deer is nearly gone. We're eating well but the comfort of it feels temporary, like everything else.

medium.title: The Bones and the Buffalo
medium.description: You crest a rise at dawn and find them: a scattered herd of buffalo, perhaps two hundred strong, grazing in a shallow valley. The animals are enormous, their shaggy bodies dark against the pale grass. But something else catches your eye—human bones scattered among the rocks to the north, picked clean, aged white by sun and weather. A leather pouch lies nearby, still intact. The herd hasn't noticed you yet, but the wind is shifting. Your oxen are nervous. You have perhaps ten minutes before the animals bolt or charge.

choices.label: Hunt the buffalo. Divide the party—send hunters while others guard the wagons. Fresh meat will last weeks.

personality_effects.Sarah: impressed—sees you taking risks for survival
personality_effects.William: resentful—wounded in the hunt, questions your tactics
personality_effects.Mary: distant—afraid the noise will draw worse things

consequences.journal_entry: We took two bulls and a cow. William took a horn to the ribs—not deep, but he'll feel it for days. Sarah was steady with the rifle. We left the bones where they lay. Didn't touch the pouch.

choices.label: Leave the herd alone. Investigate the bones and the pouch from a distance. Supplies are adequate.

personality_effects.Thomas: grateful—respects caution
personality_effects.William: frustrated—we could have eaten for weeks
personality_effects.Sarah: uneasy—asks repeatedly why we didn't check the pouch

consequences.journal_entry: The pouch held a journal, pages brittle and faded. A trapper named Harmon Lease, 1842. Last entry: 'The sickness took my feet. Can't walk. They left me.' We buried what we could and moved on. The herd watched us pass. Neither of us was brave.

choices.label: Circle wide around the herd. Don't hunt, don't investigate. Keep moving.

personality_effects.William: bitter—calls you cautious in a way that means coward
personality_effects.James: self-doubting—was there food and answers we should have taken?
personality_effects.Mary: relieved—fewer chances for disaster

consequences.journal_entry: William wouldn't look at me all day. We could see the herd for five miles after we passed. By evening, the bones were forgotten, but not by everyone. Some men are built to seize what the world offers. I'm not certain I am.

personality_effects.James: decision will haunt him either way—caution feels like failure, risk feels like recklessness
personality_effects.William: on edge; hunger and resentment are mixing into something harder

medium.journal_entry: May 10th. Found a dead man's grave and a living man's fortune, both in the same valley. The choice between them tore at us in ways a simple meal never could.

medium.title: The Sick Pawnee
medium.description: A Pawnee man lies near the trail, fever-bright and shaking. His horse is dead beside him, picked at by birds. He's alone. His people's summer camps are two days north, but he won't make it walking. The storm building to the west will hit hard within hours. Your party needs to keep moving—you're already slow, and William's cough is getting worse. The man looks at you with something between pride and resignation. He doesn't ask for help. That might be worse.

choices.label: Give him medicine and food, leave him to recover or not
Help him onto an ox, detour north toward his camps. Lose a day.
Give him your horse (if you have one) and move on. You don't.
Don't stop. The trail is hard enough.

personality_effects.Mary: Compassion wars with pragmatism. She's quiet afterward.
personality_effects.Thomas: Tests every mercy like it's a weakness.
personality_effects.Sarah: Sees her own helplessness in his face.
personality_effects.William: Survival is survival. Judges nothing.
personality_effects.James: The weight of every choice settles lower.

medium.journal_entry: June 1st. Found a Pawnee man dying by the trail near sunset. The fever was eating him from inside. I could see his ribs counting down. We made a choice, though I'm not certain which choice it was—the one we picked or the one we didn't. Either way, he's behind us now, and the storm came through hard in the night. In the morning, everything smelled like wet sage and iron. Thomas won't talk. Sarah cleaned the medicine kit twice.

medium.title: The Pawnee Scout
medium.description: A lone Pawnee scout emerges from the heat shimmer on the horizon, approaching your camp at dusk. He's mounted on a good horse, unarmed except for a knife. His sign language is clear: he offers to guide you to the Platte River — a three-day detour east — for tobacco, ammunition, or payment. Your water is nearly gone. The main trail ahead offers nothing for sixty miles. Mary thinks it's a trap. Thomas says you have no choice.

choices.label: Accept the scout's offer. Trade 20 rounds of ammunition and $50 for safe passage to water.

consequences.journal: The scout did not lie. The river ran muddy but cold. We refilled barrels and the oxen drank deep. He left us at sunrise without a word. Thomas felt a hand on his shoulder in the night — nothing there when he turned. He has not mentioned it.

choices.label: Refuse him. Push hard for water sources ahead. You've survived worse than hearsay.

consequences.journal: The next water hole was alkaline and foul. One ox died lamed on crusted ground. Sarah drank from it anyway and spent a night fevered, calling names of people long buried. The scout's tracks stayed visible for two days, then vanished. We did not discuss it.

choices.label: Trade the scout information instead: tell him about military movements you've heard from other travelers. Keep your supplies, negotiate passage differently.

consequences.journal: He listened to James speak of Fort Kearny, of soldier patrols and supply routes. His expression did not change. He pointed northeast before leaving. We reached a spring, brackish but drinkable. Mary said later: 'We just sold someone's scalp for water.' No one answered her.

medium.journal_entry: The heat is making philosophers of us all. William would have known what to do with a scout. Now we're just guessing, and guessing wrong costs lives or conscience — sometimes both. The trail wants a choice we won't feel clean about. It always does.

medium.title: The Ridge
medium.description: You crest a pass at dusk. Below, a valley offers shelter. But the ridge itself presents a problem: fresh rockslide has narrowed the trail to a width that will require unhitching the oxen and leading them single-file across loose shale. One misstep means a fall of maybe two hundred feet into scrub and boulders. The sluggish ox—the one from the alkali—is the weakest of the five. Behind you, clouds are darkening. The storm could be here in an hour.

choices.label: Cross tonight. Lead them across while there's still light.

consequences.notes: One ox balks halfway. James has to drag it while Mary steadies the others. Exhausting work. No falls. You make the valley before the storm hits. The risk paid.

choices.label: Camp here on the ridge. Cross at first light when you can see the footing.

consequences.notes: The storm hits hard. Rain turns to sleet at elevation. Mary gets soaked and shivers all night despite the fire. By morning, the ridge is glazed with ice. The crossing takes twice as long. One ox slips but catches itself. The sluggish one goes lame—useless for pulling now.

choices.label: Slaughter the sluggish ox now. Butcher what you can carry, cache the rest. Lighter load means faster crossing.

consequences.notes: Brutal work with darkness coming. Raw ox meat in your pack, still warm. The crossing goes smooth with four oxen. But Sarah refuses to eat it. Says it wasn't necessary. She has a point. The carcass feeds something in the dark—you hear it tearing at your cache halfway through the night.

choices.label: Leave the sluggish ox behind. Redistribute its load among the others and push on now.

consequences.notes: You shoot it. James does. Has to be done. Four oxen carrying what five should—the crossing becomes a grinding, slipping nightmare in the last light. Thomas dislocates his shoulder helping a beast up. You make it across but barely. The ox left behind watches you go. Everyone is quiet after.

medium.journal_entry: The ridge narrows to nothing. Mountains don't care how tired you are. We are 914 miles from Independence and the trail is getting meaner. The sluggish ox is a problem that won't solve itself. Every choice here costs something we can't replace. William would have known what to do.

medium.title: The Herder's Mercy
medium.description: Morning scout finds a Shoshone herder alone with thirty head of mountain sheep in a high pasture. He's old, weathered to leather, and his horse has gone lame. He watches your party with neither fear nor welcome—just the flat gaze of someone who's seen emigrant trains before. Behind him, smoke rises from a camp where women and children move. He holds up one hand, not hostile, and points to your oxen. Then to his sheep. Then to the mountain pass ahead, snow-thick and narrow. He speaks no English. But his meaning is clear: trade oxen for sheep, fresh meat to cross the high country. Or move on. He will not beg.

choices.label: Trade two oxen for twelve sheep. You'll move slower but eat well.

consequences.journal_entry: Gave up two oxen to an old Shoshone. Mary said we'd have made it through hunger. James said we'd have made it through weakness too. The sheep are butchered now, hanging on frames. We move like cripples but at least we eat.

choices.label: Refuse. Push for the pass today while you can see the trail.

consequences.journal_entry: Left the herder standing. Thomas said we owed him nothing. Mary said we owed ourselves mercy. The pass closed behind us in afternoon snow. We climbed hungry and burned our last good clothes for warmth. No one speaks anymore.

choices.label: Offer to take the herder and his women across the pass. Trade one oxen, no sheep.

consequences.journal_entry: We took eight of them across. One child had fever. Mary used our last decent medicine. The herder's sister knew trails we didn't. She showed us the pass that saved us two days of scrambling. James says we lost an oxen to save ourselves. I think she saved us, not the other way. Harder to live with than guilt.

medium.journal_entry: August 1st. The Shoshone herder did not smile. Neither did we. This is the country where help and harm wear the same face.

medium.title: The Mercy Question
medium.description: Mary's mountain fever worsens through the night. By dawn she can barely sit her horse, delirious, calling out names of people long dead. The passes ahead are narrow and steep—two days of hard climbing before you break through to easier country. Thomas suggests leaving her in a sheltered spot with supplies and pushing on. He's not being cruel about it. He's being practical. The snow is coming. The passes wait for no one. James, you have to decide.

choices.label: Push through the pass slowly. Mary travels with the party, even if it means slower progress.

consequences.notes: Mary survives the fever but barely. She's still sick when you reach the summit. Thomas resents the slow pace bitterly—he mutters about poor leadership. The weather holds, barely.

choices.label: Leave Mary in a cave with blankets, medicine, and half the remaining food. Send word when you reach California.

consequences.notes: You make the pass before the snow. Thomas stops complaining. James knows he may have just killed a woman who saved his life at the Platte River. Mary's fate unknown. The cave is empty when anyone returns.

choices.label: Stay put for three days. Give Mary time to recover or... complete her dying. No more guessing.

consequences.notes: By the third morning, Mary's fever breaks. She's weak but conscious. Or she isn't. Weather clouds gather overhead. The delay costs you—snow reaches the lower passes now. Thomas watches the sky like it's a judge.

personality_effects.James: You ordered men forward at the Little Blue. You pushed past the cholera camps. You made decisions that worked. This is different. This is Mary, who shot a buffalo when you were starving, who stanched Thomas's wound without flinching. The arithmetic is the same. The weight is not.
personality_effects.Thomas: You respect leaders who move forward. But you also remember your own fever two weeks ago—how Mary sat with you through the night. Survival isn't just arithmetic either, and you know it. You'll follow whatever James decides, but you'll judge him for it. You'll judge yourself too.

medium.journal_entry: Mary delirious. Snow on the high country. Thomas looking at me like I'm the scales and he's waiting to see which way I tip. Two men dead already. Four oxen left. 55 pounds of flour and a woman burning up in a tent. The pass is fifteen miles and a thousand feet of climbing. I can see it from here. Clear as judgment.

medium.title: The Dust and the Thirst
medium.description: By midday, the desert heat has turned the air to brass. Mary collapses on the wagon bench—not from fever, but from dehydration. Her lips are cracked and bleeding. You have two days' water left, maybe three if rationed to nothing. Thomas found deer sign an hour back, heading northeast toward what might be a spring. But the trail bends west, toward the established crossing. Going after water means losing the marked route. The oxen are flagging. James, your call.

choices.label: Follow the deer sign northeast. Fresh water is more valuable than the trail.

consequences.description: Thomas leads. You find a spring by luck or madness—a trickle from limestone, clear and cold. Mary drinks until she vomits, then drinks again. But the detour costs you a full day's travel and one ox goes lame on the rocky ground. The uncertainty of whether you made the right choice sits in your chest like a stone.

choices.label: Stay on the marked trail. Springs are marked for a reason. Mary will have to endure.

consequences.description: Mary's eyes glaze by evening. You use your last dose of laudanum to keep her conscious. She stops complaining, which is worse. The trail delivers you to water by next dawn, but Mary is hollow-eyed and won't look at you. Thomas thinks you chose the trail over her life. He's not wrong.

choices.label: Send Thomas ahead with two canteens. Push the wagons slow. He'll scout and return with water or guidance.

consequences.description: Thomas rides off into the shimmer. You don't see him again for eighteen hours. He returns with news of a Shoshone camp three miles north—they have water and will trade for ammunition. But Thomas is furious at being sent alone. He says you were ready to abandon him if the gamble failed. Maybe he's right. The trade saves Mary but costs trust you can't rebuild.

personality_effects.Mary: dependency
personality_effects.Thomas: resentment
personality_effects.James: doubt

medium.journal_entry: The desert doesn't ask permission before it breaks you. I watched Mary's lips crack like dried mud and felt the weight of four lives depending on a choice I wasn't sure about. Water is thirst made visible, and out here you learn that water is the only god that answers. Sarah and William don't have to drink anymore. Sometimes I envy them for that.

medium.title: The Scout's Find
medium.description: Mary returns from scouting ahead, her face tight. She found a abandoned wagon two miles up canyon—fresh enough that canvas still holds shape. Scattered around it: three bodies, bloated and dark. A child's doll. A journal with dates from August. No sign of Lakota raiders or disease. The oxen are gone. James, you need to decide fast—there's salvage here, but the bodies mean sickness or violence, and staying means losing daylight in canyon country where you move slow.

choices.label: Strip the wagon for supplies and bury the bodies properly.

consequences.journal_entry: Found death in the canyon. Three of them. We buried what was left and took what wouldn't come with us. Mary didn't speak the whole time. The doll we left on the grave. James felt righteous about it. Righteousness tastes like dust and rot.

choices.label: Take only what you can carry in ten minutes and move on before dark.

consequences.journal_entry: Left them there. Left the child's doll there. Mary looked at James like he'd died already. Sometimes survival means leaving ghosts behind. Sometimes the ghosts follow anyway.

choices.label: Salvage what you can but don't bury them—leave at dusk to cover ground.

consequences.journal_entry: We took what we could and left the rest to the canyon. James paid Mary 50 cents to stop looking back. By morning neither of them would remember agreeing to that. The canyon remembers though. It always remembers.

personality_effects.Mary: Her faith is fracturing. Three bodies in a box canyon—the same way the trail kills you if you stop moving. She's starting to believe the land wants them dead.
personality_effects.James: He has to choose between mercy and miles. Both cost. He's learning that leadership is picking which people to disappoint.

medium.journal_entry: September 10th. Snake River canyon. The walls squeeze tighter each day. Found a grave that wasn't ours. Wondered how long until someone finds ours.

medium.title: The Crossing
medium.description:
You find a shallow ford two miles upstream, but the riverbed is choked with volcanic rock—jagged and treacherous. Your oxen are thin. Mary is barely conscious, burning with fever. The alternative is to backtrack a full day, lose miles, and camp another night without knowing if she'll see morning.

A Shoshone man appears on the far bank. He watches you from his horse without expression. He points downriver—a gesture, nothing more—then turns and rides away.

choices.label: Cross at the ford. Risk the oxen's hooves and wheels on the rocks.

consequences.journal: Pushed the ford at sunset. Lost an ox to a gash on its foreleg. Couldn't stop the bleeding proper. Spare axle cracked in two places. Mary didn't wake when we crossed.

choices.label: Follow the Shoshone's gesture downriver. Trust the stranger.

consequences.journal: Followed the pointing hand like fools. Found a wide crossing where the river splits around an island. Cost us time and Mary's last quinine, but the oxen crossed easy. The man knew this land better than any map.

choices.label: Backtrack. Camp here tonight. Wait for Mary to stabilize before moving.

consequences.journal: Turned back. Used our last medicines on Mary's fever. She asked for water three times and didn't recognize me. The delay felt like drowning slow. Every hour we don't move is an hour closer to winter.

personality_effects.James: Deciding Mary's fate has cracked something in you. Your hands shake worse than before.

medium.journal_entry: The Shoshone knew something we didn't. Whether that was kindness or a test, I can't say. The desert doesn't care which. Mary's skin is hot enough to brand with. We're 1500 miles from Independence and I'm not sure we'll make it to California. I'm not sure I want to anymore.

medium.title: The Fever Breaks
medium.description: Mary wakes with her shirt soaked through. The fever that took her for three days has burned itself out. She's alive, barely—gaunt, weak, her voice a rasp. But she's asking for water and broth. James finds her sitting up, staring at the charred remains of last night's fire. 'I saw them,' she says quietly. 'Thomas and Sarah. Standing at the edge of the trees. They were waiting for me.' She grips James's wrist. 'They looked... not angry. Just waiting.' Then she lies back and asks if there's any of that Pawnee jerky left. The fever's broken. Whether what she saw was delirium or something else, there's no way to know now.

choices.label: Press her about what she saw. Write it in the journal as a warning.

consequences.description: Mary shuts down. Won't speak of it again. The probing reopens something she'd rather leave sealed. Her morale fractures further. You make the entry anyway—'Fever visions of the dead'—and wonder if you're recording her madness or something real. Either way, you've lost her trust.

choices.label: Say nothing about it. Give her the jerky and extra rations to rebuild her strength.

consequences.description: Mary eats slowly, deliberately. The color returns to her face by evening. She doesn't mention the apparitions again, and neither do you. Some silences are a form of mercy. She's stronger tomorrow. So is your partnership. The food stores dip, but a living Mary is worth the cost.

choices.label: Tell her it was the fever. Suggest moving camp today to break the hold this place has on her.

consequences.description: She nods, accepting the rational explanation. You move through the mud while she's still fragile, making slow headway—five miles in the rain before camping in worse conditions. It's a risk. But leaving ground might matter if winter comes hard. Mary appreciates the decisive choice, even if her body resents the travel. The doubt remains, though. You heard her certainty.

personality_effects.Mary: responding to whether you acknowledge or dismiss her experience

medium.journal_entry: Oct 10 — Mary's fever broke. She speaks of seeing the dead standing in the trees. Real or delirium, I don't know. Three gone, two of us left, and the forest watches. Winter coming hard. The oxen are getting thin. We push on.

medium.title: The Pawnee Scout
medium.description: A lone Pawnee scout emerges from the west at dusk, riding a clean horse. He approaches the fire with his hands visible. Through signs and broken English, he indicates a buffalo herd three miles north—easy hunting. He also warns of Lakota war parties moving south through the region, two days behind. He accepts tobacco and leaves before dark. The warning is credible. The opportunity is real. The timing troubles you: why does he help? Why now? Mary is still fevered and weak. Sarah's cough is getting worse.

choices.label: Hunt the buffalo. The fresh meat will strengthen the sick and provision you well.
Push north at first light away from the Lakota warning. Skip the buffalo. Trust the scout's warning more than his gift.
Stay put one more day. Rest the sick. Fortify. Gather firewood and prepare for trouble. Ignore both the herd and the warning.

personality_effects.William: Restless. Wants action, wants meat, wants to prove something.
personality_effects.Thomas: Cautious about the scout's motives. Doubts kindness from strangers.
personality_effects.Mary: Feverish. Dreams about her sister in Saint Louis. Barely aware of the decision.

medium.journal_entry: April 20th, evening. A Pawnee came out of nowhere with a gift and a warning both. The herd is real—men say they saw dust. The Lakota are also real, or he made them real by speaking them. The women are failing. Whatever we choose now, someone pays. I keep thinking about his horse: too clean, too fresh. Like he'd ridden hard from somewhere specific. What was he running from, or toward? The tobacco smoke just sits in the cold air. Doesn't rise like it should.

medium.title: The Drowned Flour
medium.description:
You discover the damage from yesterday's ford is worse than thought. The flour barrel leaked. What remains is a pasty, fermented sludge—maybe thirty pounds of it edible if you're desperate. Mary estimates you've lost sixty pounds of flour. At your current consumption, that's ten days of meals gone.

Worse: Thomas finds mold creeping through the hardtack stores. The damp got everywhere.

Sarah suggests drying what you can over fires today and tonight. William, quieter than usual, mentions a Pawnee camp he spotted two miles north at dawn. They were moving east, toward the river.

James, the choice is yours. You have three days of good weather ahead.

choices.label: Spend the day drying flour and hardtack. Lose a day's travel but salvage what you can.
Push on today. Ration what's left and hunt aggressively to compensate. Trust the rifle.
Send William and Thomas to trade with the Pawnee camp. Flour, metal goods, whatever they'll take for smoked meat or corn.

consequences.trade_risk: The Pawnee are moving; they may not wait. William dislikes being sent like a boy. Thomas fears the exchange more than hunger.

personality_effects.Mary: Steady. She'll accept drying out the supplies without complaint, but questions whether it was negligence at the ford.
personality_effects.Thomas: Anxious about being sent into the Pawnee camp. Less anxious about hunger than about strangers with bows.
personality_effects.Sarah: Her morale lifts with any action. She'll work the fires cheerfully or push the oxen without complaint.
personality_effects.William: Resents inaction. Resents being told what to do more. The camp trade galls him—feels like James is buying time with his labor.

medium.journal_entry: May 10th. The creek took more than we thought. Flour's fouled. Mary says we're ten days shorter if we don't fix this, which means cutting north to find game or cutting rations. The Pawnee are out there, moving their own way. William saw them. I didn't ask him to look. That's the thing about the trail—you learn things you didn't want to know, and then you have to decide what they're worth. The sky's clear. It won't be after Wednesday.

medium.title: The Ravine
medium.description: By midafternoon, the sky turns the color of a bruise. You're navigating a steep ravine when Thomas spots something wedged between two rocks thirty feet down—a trunk, leather-bound and partially exposed. The storm is coming fast. Mary says the detour will cost you miles and daylight. James notices one of the oxen is favoring its left rear leg badly. Thomas wants to go down. The thunder is close enough now that you feel it in your chest.

choices.label: Send Thomas down to retrieve the trunk before the storm hits.

personality_effects.Thomas: reckless_act_survives

consequences.narrative: Thomas makes it down in twelve minutes, moving like a man possessed. He hauls up a trunk of trade-goods—calico, needles, thirty pounds of salt. The rain starts just as he climbs back. His shoulder takes a bad wrench on the final pull. But the salt alone is worth $40 to a trading post, and the calico might buy goodwill somewhere ahead. Mary watched him risk his neck for cloth and spice. She hasn't looked at him the same since William died.

choices.label: Push on. The trunk is not worth lives.

personality_effects.Thomas: denied_choice

consequences.narrative: You keep moving. Behind you, lightning splits a cottonwood like a bone breaking. By evening, one ox has gone completely lame—the rocky ground did what the day's tension couldn't. You'll have to shoot it or leave it to the wolves. You choose the bullet. James makes the shot. Thomas sits apart from the fire, jaw working on jerky. He won't say what he was thinking about in that ravine, but you know: his brother William kept journals. Kept records of things. Maybe that trunk held answers. Maybe not. The storm passes north. You'll never know.

choices.label: Investigate the ox's injury first. If it's lame, you can't afford distractions.

personality_effects.James: pragmatist_vindicated

consequences.narrative: You examine the ox's leg. A stone wedged deep in the hoof, already starting to abscess. You work it free with a knife and a stick of willow, wrap it with cloth soaked in your remaining laudanum. The ox calms. By the time you're done, the storm has moved closer but not yet arrived. Thomas helps without being asked. Mary even brings water. It's the first time in days the group has moved in concert instead of separate grievances. The trunk remains in the ravine. Unexamined. Undisturbed. Like a question nobody is forced to answer.

personality_effects.Thomas: questioning_leadership
personality_effects.Mary: growing_hardness
personality_effects.James: accumulating_doubt

medium.journal_entry: August 1st. High country now. Sarah and William behind us, not ahead. Found a grave from '47—Henderson. How many more of these will we pass before we reach the valley? Saw a trunk in a ravine today. Thomas wanted it. I don't know what was in it, and I'm not sure that matters anymore. We're running on meat and salt and the memory of why we started. The mountains don't care about reasons. They just take.

medium.title: The Mule Track
medium.description: You find Thomas crouched at the edge of camp before dawn, studying the ground. He's found fresh tracks—a mule, shod, heading northwest. Not Pawnee or Shoshone. Someone else is out here. He says he recognizes the gait: it belonged to a man from Independence, William Harrow, who left the wagon train in July after a dispute over provisions. Harrow had a rifle, a mule, and enough supplies for one man to reach the valley. Thomas wants to follow the tracks. He could get to water, perhaps a cache Harrow abandoned. Or information about the terrain ahead. The mule's hoofprints are fresh—maybe two days old. But following them means leaving the trail proper, and you've lost people already. Mary thinks it's a trap—or at least a waste of time. James, you decide.

choices.label: Follow the tracks with Thomas.

consequences.description: You find a dry camp and scattered bones—elk, not human. The mule tracks veer north and fade into rock. Thomas is silent on the walk back, angry at the lost time. That night, Mary refuses to speak to you. The water you were hoping for doesn't exist here. You're down two days and more food than you could spare.

choices.label: Send Thomas alone. Continue west.

consequences.description: Thomas leaves at dawn with half a canteen and a rifle. You push west without him. That evening he returns, fevered, with a gash on his leg—he fell on rocks while scrambling down a gulch. He found nothing but a rusted horseshoe. The wound needs cleaning and binding, but your medicine is low. He'll recover, probably, but he won't forget you sent him out alone.

choices.label: Ignore the tracks. Push harder west before first frost.

consequences.description: You drive hard. One ox goes lame in the afternoon—something in its foreleg. You shoot it and press on. Thomas watches, jaw tight. He wanted that mule's water, that cache, something. The pace is brutal and the gain is real—you cover ground the frost can't steal—but Thomas won't meet your eyes at night.

personality_effects.Thomas: He trusts your judgment less now. Every choice that doesn't chase possibility makes him feel like you're just waiting to die. He'll take risks to prove it's not true.
personality_effects.Mary: She respects decisive action, even action she disagrees with. The harder you push west, the more she believes you mean to reach the valley alive.

medium.journal_entry: Found sign of Harrow. Feels like the land is full of ghosts trying to warn us or trap us—can't tell which. Thomas wants to follow everything that moves. I chose not to. Whether that was courage or cowardice depends on whether there's water out there, and we'll never know. The ox that went lame had kind eyes. Didn't want to shoot it but the alternative was leaving it to the coyotes while it still lived.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description: The ox that drank from the alkali pool this morning moves with a limp now, favoring its right rear leg. The other three are fine. By midday, it's clear the animal won't make good time. You can see the Snake River cutting through the canyon ahead—water, at least. But stopping costs you a day, maybe more. Mary suggests putting the ox down and redistributing its load. James is quiet, watching the animal breathe shallow.

choices.label: Rest here one full day. The ox might recover. You have time.

consequences.journal_note: Waited by the river. The ox did not improve. Drank good water, at least.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Butcher what meat you can carry. Press on.

consequences.journal_note: Three oxen now. The meat will spoil in three days. Needed to be done.

choices.label: Leave the ox behind. Redistribute gear to the others. Move faster.

consequences.journal_note: Left the ox at the canyon. Oxen work harder now. Mary says we're cruel. Perhaps.

medium.journal_entry: September 10th. An ox is no good to anyone dead or dying. We have 1400 miles behind us and perhaps 200 ahead. The high desert does not forgive weakness. I wonder if that's true of people too.

medium.title: The Swollen Ox
medium.description: Mary points to the lead ox. Its left foreleg is twice the size it should be, hot to touch. It limps badly. You've seen this before—a snakebite, probably from last night when it drank at the creek. The animal is useless for another day, maybe two before it goes septic and dies anyway. You have three oxen left. You need at least two to pull the wagon with Mary as sick as she is. The nearest trading post is forty miles south, but that's away from the trail. You could butcher it now—the meat would last you and Mary a week, maybe more. Or you could try to nurse it along, gambling that the swelling goes down and you don't lose the animal for nothing.

choices.label: Butcher the ox. Fresh meat is certain. The trail is not.

consequences.journal_note: Killed the lead ox. Mary watched from the wagon. We ate the liver raw. It was still warm.

choices.label: Bind the leg with cloth and medicine. Reduce rations further. Move slow.

consequences.journal_note: Used the last of the good cloth to wrap its leg. Gave it water and waited. James said the swelling might go down. It might not.

choices.label: Leave it behind. One less mouth to feed. Push the two healthy oxen harder.

consequences.journal_note: We left the ox behind. It screamed. Or maybe I imagined that part. Hard to say anymore.

Mary.note: Watching the ox suffer makes her weaker. She turns away. The dysentery is worse when she's frightened.

medium.journal_entry: September 15th. The ox is done. Two weeks ago it was strong enough to pull double weight. Now it stands on three legs and breathes like there's gravel in its lungs. Mary asked me what I would do if I were the ox. I didn't answer her. Some questions don't deserve the truth.

medium.title: The Sick Ox
medium.description: Thomas notices one of the oxen moving stiffly, favoring its rear leg. By midday, the animal refuses to pull. Its breathing is labored, eye clouded. You've seen this before—lameness that doesn't improve. The ox is useful only if it recovers, which it won't. You have three oxen. You need them all to reach the valley before snow locks the passes. The animal is suffering. It's also meat.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Process what you can carry.
Leave it behind. The strong survive.
Camp here. Give it rest and the last of your medicine.

personality_effects.James: exhaustion sharpens his jaw. he won't say it, but he's calculating weight and miles.
personality_effects.Mary: she's quiet. she once owned a horse. she doesn't watch when the choice comes.
personality_effects.Thomas: he understands necessity. he's already seen too much to pretend otherwise.

medium.journal_entry: Oct 1st. The rain won't stop. One ox is done—doesn't matter why. The valley is still forty days off if we move fast. If we don't, the snow will come down like judgment. I can feel it in the cold fog. We're past the place where sentiment costs anything but time.

medium.title: The Deer and the Stranger
medium.description: The rain hasn't stopped in two days. Your oxen are exhausted, hooves sinking deep in the mud. This morning, while Mary was butchering the last of Thomas's deer, a man emerged from the forest—gaunt, fever-bright eyes, speaking in Chinook jargon mixed with broken English. He says he's a trapper, wintering alone upriver. He's eyeing your fresh meat with an intensity that makes your hand move to your rifle. When you don't offer him food, he doesn't leave. He just stands there in the rain, watching. There's something wrong with his left arm—it hangs at an angle that no arm should hang. He says he knows a faster route through the mountains. Saves three days, he claims. But his eyes are wrong. Mary wants him gone. James wants to know if he's lying.

choices.label: Give him meat and ask about the route. Information might be worth the food.

consequences.journal_entry: Gave the trapper venison. He ate it like a dog, didn't speak. When he finally talked, his directions made no sense—kept pointing at the fog like it was a map. His arm was definitely broken. Weeks old, maybe. He left before nightfall, walking into the rain without a word. Mary won't look at me. James says we were lucky. Lucky for what, he didn't say.

choices.label: Turn him away. You can't spare the food, and his story doesn't hold water.

consequences.journal_entry: Told him to move on. He nodded slow and walked back into the trees. Three hours later, James spotted hoofprints—fresh ones, heading the same direction we came from. The oxen were restless all night. Nothing attacked. Nothing was there. But something kept the camp awake.

choices.label: Offer him shelter for one night only. A man with a broken arm in this weather won't last.

consequences.journal_entry: The trapper slept by the fire, didn't move once. In the morning, his arm was set differently—better—but he didn't do it himself. His fingernails were black. When he left, he left muddy footprints that didn't match his boots. Mary says I'm seeing things from fever. She might be right. His tracks went three directions at once before disappearing in the rain.

personality_effects.default: The encounter sits heavy. James is quieter than usual. Mary is afraid—not of the trapper, but of what he represents: how thin the line is between civilization and the forest.

medium.journal_entry: October 10th. Rain falling like judgment. The oxen are thin now, ribs showing. We've lost Thomas, Sarah, and William to sickness and the river. Only four of us left. A stranger came out of the woods today with a broken arm and no business knowing our names. We didn't tell him our names.

medium.title: The Calf
