medium.description: You find a newborn buffalo calf separated from its mother, crying in the tall grass near your trail. It's too young to survive alone — maybe two days old. The mother is nowhere visible. Thomas wants to shoot it to put it out of its misery. Mary suggests trying to nurse it back to health with some of your dwindling milk stores. William points out that a live calf might draw the mother back, which could mean trouble. James notices storm clouds building to the west.

choices.label: Shoot the calf. It's mercy, not cruelty.

consequences.description: The shot echoes across the prairie. You butcher it quickly—fresh meat is fresh meat, and you don't have the luxury of sentiment. But the look on Mary's face as you work suggests she'll remember this. That night, she speaks to you only when necessary. William seems to understand the logic, at least.

choices.label: Leave it and push on before the storm hits.

consequences.description: You ride past the crying thing. No one says anything for the next two hours. The storm hits hard at dusk—rain like nails, lightning that makes the oxen nervous. You make camp in a draw, soaked through. Later, Sarah mentions she heard the calf screaming before the wind picked up. Probably her imagination. Probably.

choices.label: Try to keep it. Milk it with your stores, see if it lives.

consequences.description: You coax the calf into the wagon. It's weak, resistant, its breath ragged. Mary takes charge—she's done this before with farm animals. Two days of precious milk, two nights of sleep broken by its bellowing. It dies anyway, at dawn on the second day. The kindness that might have saved it somewhere with shelter and time just draws out the suffering here. Mary weeps. Thomas won't look at you. The calf stinks in the back of the wagon until you bury it.

personality_effects.Thomas: Pragmatism. Efficiency. The trail breaks mercy.
personality_effects.Mary: Empathy is exhausting out here.
personality_effects.William: Watching you decide what lives and what dies.
personality_effects.Sarah: Some sounds you can't unhear.

medium.journal_entry: Found a calf today. Didn't know what to do with it, so we did what we thought we had to. The prairie doesn't care about our choices. It just waits for the next one.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description: The ox that drank from the alkali pool this morning moves with a limp now, favoring its back left leg. Its eyes are dull. The veterinary knowledge in your party is limited to what you've learned by necessity. Thomas suggests the leg might be broke. Mary thinks it's poison from the water. The animal will slow the wagon considerably if kept, but oxen are worth their weight in gold out here. You have five. You need at least four to move safely. The storm building in the west won't wait for you to decide.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Butcher what you can carry. Move on.

consequences.journal_entry: Shot the ox at noon. The smell of it in this heat is something I won't forget. We packed what fit. The others couldn't look at each other while we worked. Sarah cried, though she tried to hide it. Necessary doesn't make it easier.

personality_effects.Sarah: guilt_pragmatist
personality_effects.Mary: resentment_builds

choices.label: Leave the ox behind. Redistribute its load. Slow your pace but keep moving.

consequences.journal_entry: Left the ox behind. It won't make it—we all know that. Better taken by scavengers than watched dying in harness. The wagon is heavier now. We'll be slower. The storm is coming faster than we are.

personality_effects.Thomas: quiet_disapproval
personality_effects.Sarah: mercy_noted

choices.label: Try to nurse it. Make camp early. Use your medicine on it and rest.

consequences.journal_entry: Stopped the wagon. Gave the ox water mixed with whiskey—old remedy, probably useless. Mary cleaned the wound on its leg. The storm came through at dusk, hard rain and thunder that spooked everything. The ox stood quiet through it all, as if it knew we were trying. By morning, it was still lame, but the eye was clearer. We're one day behind. Whether that matters depends on what comes next.

personality_effects.Mary: hope_fragile
personality_effects.James: uncertainty_visible
leader_doubt
personality_effects.Mary: compassion_tested
personality_effects.Thomas: pragmatism_strained
personality_effects.Sarah: innocence_eroding

medium.title: The Fever in Mary's Eyes
medium.description: Mary wakes with a tremor in her hands. Her skin is hot—not the heat of exertion, but something deeper. She insists she's fit to travel, but her eyes have the glassiness you've learned to recognize. Malaria, perhaps. Or mountain fever. Thomas coughs into his elbow nearby—the same cough he's had for two days. The high passes still lie ahead. Snow comes early in these mountains. You have three doses of quinine left.

choices.label: Rest here two days. Ration the medicine between Mary and Thomas.
Push on. Save the medicine. The lower elevation ahead might clear the sickness.
Give Mary the full course—three doses. Thomas will have to manage without. It's arithmetic.

medium.journal_entry: Aug 1st. Mary burning up this morning. Thomas hacking blood into his handkerchief. The peaks are beautiful and terrible. Snow waiting up there like a judgment. The meat from Thomas's deer is getting gamey. Kept two oxen from bolting in the storm yesterday but the wagon bed is cracked—we'll lose supplies if we ford anything deep. Three doses of medicine left and two people dying by inches. The mountains don't care about arithmetic. Neither do I, anymore. I think I stopped caring somewhere around mile 800.

medium.title: The Fever Breaks Wrong
medium.description: Mary wakes before dawn, soaked through. Her fever has broken—but she's shaking, lucid for the first time in four days, and she's asking questions. Why did we leave Sarah's body at Willow Springs? Why did Thomas shoot our oxen instead of rationing? She's weak but clearheaded, and she's looking at you with an expression that says she remembers things. The morning is silent except for wind off the peaks. The others are still asleep. You could talk to her now, alone. Or you could let her rest and avoid what she might ask.

choices.label: Sit with her. Answer what she asks—all of it.

consequences.description: You tell her about the decisions. The rationing that ran short. The choices made in desperation. She listens, tears cutting tracks down her hollow face. When you finish, she nods slowly. 'At least you didn't lie,' she says. Thomas overhears from his bedroll and pulls away from the fire. Mary's trust in you hardens into something real—but the party fractures. Thomas won't look at anyone. The day ahead will be tense.

choices.label: Tell her she was delirious. Let her rest.

consequences.description: You smooth her hair back, tell her the fever made her confused. She's weak, needs water and broth. Her questions fade as exhaustion pulls her back under. It buys peace for now. But when she fully recovers in a day or two, the questions will return—sharper. And she'll know you dodged them. The lie costs nothing today but compounds interest.

choices.label: Give her laudanum. Let her sleep through another day.

consequences.description: You measure out the tincture into her water. She drinks without argument and drifts back into dark dreams. It gives you another twenty-four hours of silence. But you're using your last reserves of medicine on suppression, not healing. And when she wakes tomorrow, clearheaded and medicated, she'll understand what you did. There's a look people get when they realize they're being managed like a problem instead of trusted like a person.

personality_effects.Mary: Her lucidity is dangerous. She sees clearly now—the exhaustion, the shortcuts, the bodies left behind. How you respond determines whether she becomes your advocate or your witness.
personality_effects.Thomas: Already fracturing from guilt. Any honest accounting of rationing decisions will break his fragile justification for what he did.
personality_effects.James: You're running out of narrative room. The trail is collapsing the distance between action and consequence.

medium.journal_entry: August 15th. Mary's fever broke in the dark. For a moment I thought we'd won something. Then she opened her eyes and asked where Sarah was. I realized I don't have an answer that doesn't end this party. The mountains are closing in. Snow on the high peaks. We're maybe four weeks from the valley, if the snow waits. If the oxen hold. If no one else asks difficult questions. But Mary will ask. They always ask when the fever burns away the mercy of confusion.

medium.title: The Shoshone Scout
medium.description: You're rounding a canyon bend when you see him—a young Shoshone man on a ridge above, watching your oxen. He's alone, unarmed. When he sees you've noticed him, he doesn't ride away. Instead he signals downward with his palm, a gesture that might mean peace or might mean stay still. Your oxen are nervous. Mary is gripping the rifle. James, you have maybe thirty seconds before someone does something they can't take back.

choices.label: Signal back peacefully and approach slowly with trade goods

consequences.journal: Traded salt and two dollars for information about the canyon ahead. The scout—his name was something close to Taps—said water is scarce for the next forty miles and that a larger party of Shoshone camps near the north rim. He seemed more curious than hostile. Gave us dried salmon too, which Mary refused to eat.

choices.label: Keep riding, don't acknowledge him. Move fast.

consequences.journal: Pushed hard through the canyon. One ox went lame—likely from the rocky terrain we rushed through. The Shoshone didn't follow. But every shadow on the canyon wall looked like a rider for the next two hours. Mary still has the rifle loaded.

choices.label: Raise the rifle and warn him away. Make it clear the oxen are not to be taken.

consequences.journal: Fired a warning shot. The scout disappeared into the rocks immediately. James felt something crack in his chest afterward—not pain, exactly, but a weight. Mary said we did right. Nobody believes her, including Mary. Used medicine to treat the tremor in James's hands that wouldn't stop for hours.

medium.journal_entry: September 10. The canyon narrows. I can touch both walls if I stand in the wagon bed. Three buried. Two living. The oxen smell water ahead but the scout said there is none for two days more. We are thin as the shadows here. Whatever happens next, we are not the same people who left Missouri. We left pieces of ourselves at every grave.

medium.title: The Bones in the Rocks
medium.description: You find a skeletal hand jutting from the volcanic rock near your camp, fingers still clutching a tin cup. The bones are recent—months old at most. No grave marker. No name. Mary is feverish and doesn't notice. You could bury what's left, move the bones aside, or search for answers among the rocks.

choices.label: Bury the remains and say words over them

consequences.journal_note: We gave a stranger what dignity we could. James's hands blistered from digging in that hard earth. The cup was dented but clean. Someone loved it once.

choices.label: Search the rocks for more—there might be supplies cached nearby

consequences.journal_note: Found a leather pouch with coins. We told ourselves we'd earned it, searching in that sun. But the tin cup stayed in the rocks. We moved faster after, like the dead might follow.

choices.label: Take the cup, leave the bones. Move on quickly before dark

consequences.journal_note: James carried the cup for two miles before throwing it. Mary didn't speak all evening. Some decisions don't leave you, even after you leave them.

personality_effects.James: The weight of three buried party members and now a stranger. His jaw tightens when the wind picks up dust.

medium.journal_entry: The Snake River country is red rock and old death. We passed Henderson's grave a week back. Now this. Mary said nothing but her fever spiked that night. I wonder if it was fever or if she saw the hand before I did and didn't tell me. The oxen are nervous. They know something we don't, or they remember something we've forgotten.

medium.title: The Missing Scout
medium.description: Thomas went ahead at first light to find a better ford across the swollen creek—the main route is impassable. He hasn't returned. It's been six hours. The rain hasn't stopped. Mary is shivering in the wagon, her fever worse. James finds Thomas's horse tied to a deadfall, saddled but empty. Beside it: Thomas's rifle, propped against the tree. His boots are gone. The ground is too soft and wet to read tracks. William would have gone after him. William is dead.

choices.label: Wait until morning. Send a search party then.

consequences.description: Mary's fever spikes through the night. You use the last of your medicine to bring it down. The creek doesn't recede. At dawn, Thomas's horse is gone. Dragged away or taken. No sign of Thomas at all—not a scrap of cloth, not blood. Just his rifle in the mud, barrel pointed downstream. James wonders if the boy simply walked into the forest and kept walking.

choices.label: Go after him now with James and one lantern.

consequences.description: You push into the dark forest. The rain sounds like applause. Your lantern swallows nothing. After an hour, you find Thomas's hat, floating in a ravine stream. After two hours, your boots are soaked through and you can't feel your feet. After three hours, you turn back. Mary is alone with the wagons. She's as good as alone now. Thomas is either drowned or he walked. Either way, he's gone. You tell no one what you think—that he might have chosen it.

choices.label: Take the rifles and his horse. Leave a note. Move on.

consequences.description: You prop his rifle against a stone. Beneath it: a page torn from your journal, reading only 'Thomas. October 1st.' His horse is strong. The oxen are failing. You don't say this aloud to Mary or James. You simply do it. The creek can be forded two miles south if you're willing to risk a wagon tipping. You are. By nightfall, the forest is behind you. Mary stares at the fire and speaks to no one. James cleans the rifle you took. It's a good rifle. Thomas won't need it.

personality_effects.Mary: She asked Thomas to scout. She told him to be careful. Fever dreams might include his face.
personality_effects.James: Three people dead or missing under his leadership. The numbers are becoming hard to ignore.

medium.journal_entry: The forest takes what it wants. Sometimes it's quick. Sometimes it's slow. Thomas was a good boy—afraid, yes, but good. I keep his boots in my mind, wherever they went. The rain hasn't stopped since he disappeared. I wonder if it ever will.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description: The ox that drank from the alkali pool this morning moves with visible effort. Its breathing is shallow. By afternoon, it's falling behind the wagon, and the others are pulling uneven. James estimates you're losing a mile per hour. The ox's eyes have a glassy quality. Mary thinks it might recover with rest. Thomas mutters about culling it before it infects the others. Sarah hasn't spoken much since yesterday—she's been watching the animal. William keeps checking the harnesses, methodical, saying nothing.

choices.label: Push hard to the next water source. The ox either keeps pace or doesn't.

personality_effects.Thomas: hardened, pragmatic
personality_effects.Sarah: fractured, distant

choices.label: Camp here for two days. Rest the ox. Hope the alkali passes through its system.

personality_effects.Mary: patient, maternal
personality_effects.William: restless, doubting

choices.label: Shoot the ox now. Use the meat for food. Redistribute the load to the stronger animals.

personality_effects.Sarah: shaken, questioning mercy
personality_effects.William: vindicated, efficient
personality_effects.Mary: grieving, complicit

choices.label: Cut the ox loose. It can follow or not. You owe it nothing and it slows no one.

personality_effects.Sarah: haunted, resentful
personality_effects.Mary: coldness hardening in her

personality_effects.James: leader weighing the cost of every choice
personality_effects.Thomas: survival above sentiment
personality_effects.Sarah: fracture lines spreading

medium.journal_entry: April 20th, 1848. The ox won't recover. We all know it. The question isn't whether it lives—it's whether we wait for it to die slowly or make the choice ourselves. There's no clean answer. There never is.

medium.title: The Sick Ox
medium.description: Thomas finds one of the oxen lame at dawn—swollen foreleg, won't bear weight. The animal is worth maybe $40 if you sold it at the last settlement, which is four days behind. Ahead, the terrain doesn't flatten until Fort Laramie, another month's travel. You need all six to haul the wagons. Thomas thinks it's a stone bruise. Mary thinks infection. William says shoot it and lighten the load. Sarah won't stop looking at it.

choices.label: Rest the ox here for two days, apply poultice, hope it improves
Shoot the ox, butcher it for meat, redistribute cargo to remaining five
Abandon the ox and push on at a harder pace, split its cargo among the others
Fashion a makeshift splint, reduce its load by half, keep it with the train

personality_effects.Sarah: She kneels beside the animal and speaks to it in a low voice no one else can hear. When she stands, her face is dry but something in her has bent.
personality_effects.William: Pragmatism is not cruelty, he'll say. But he's the one who'll have to drive the knife or the whip.
personality_effects.Mary: She knows infection when she sees it. She's seen three children die of less.

medium.journal_entry: The ox's name was Pharaoh. No one called it that but me. Found him lame at the creek crossing. The rain came just after we decided what to do—or rather, we delayed deciding, and the rain decided for us. The meat won't cure properly in this damp. Everything out here tastes of necessity and ash.

medium.title: The Pawnee Scout
medium.description: A lone Pawnee scout approaches on horseback from the north, riding parallel to your column at a distance. He makes no hostile move, but he's studying your wagons. Your oxen grow restless. William reaches for his rifle. The scout stops a quarter-mile off, watches for ten minutes, then rides back the way he came. But he didn't leave. You find his tracks circling your camp at dusk.

choices.label: Post extra guards through the night and move out at first light. Better to travel tired than caught sleeping.

consequences.journal_entry: Didn't sleep. Every sound was a knife. The scout's message was clear enough — we're being watched. We ran. Can't say I blame the men for it.

choices.label: Leave a gift at the scout's trail: tobacco, a knife, spare cloth. Signal you're not a threat.

consequences.journal_entry: Left good things on the trail like leaving flowers on a grave. Whether it buys us passage or just delays the inevitable, we'll find out soon enough. William called it cowardice. Maybe it is.

choices.label: Send William and Thomas ahead to scout the next water. If trouble comes, you'll see it coming and have options.

consequences.journal_entry: Sent the two strongest men forward. It thins the guard here, but William likes having something to do besides worry. Thomas didn't complain. Women watched them disappear into the grass like they were riding toward a cliff.

choices.label: Do nothing different. Keep the same pace, same camp routine. Showing fear invites predation.

consequences.journal_entry: We acted like we belonged here. Like we weren't trespassers counting days until we could stop running. The scout didn't return. Maybe that was the point — to know we were seen and didn't break.

personality_effects.William: wants action and sees caution as weakness
personality_effects.Mary: fears violence but respects mercy toward other peoples
personality_effects.Sarah: trusts instinct over strategy
personality_effects.Thomas: follows James without question
personality_effects.James: carries the weight of every decision

medium.journal_entry: The plains are not empty. Even when you can't see them, they're there. We learned that today.

medium.title: The Pawnee Camp
medium.description: At dawn, you crest a rise and see them: seven Pawnee riders, maybe a mile distant, moving parallel to your trail. They're not attacking. Not yet. One rider breaks formation and approaches slowly, hands visible on his horse's neck. Behind you, William is already checking his rifle. Mary touches your arm. The rider is close enough now to see his face is scarred, one eye milky white. He speaks in broken English: 'You have meat. We have no meat. Three days no buffalo.' He gestures back at his group. 'Trade or trouble. You choose.'

choices.label: Give them 20 lbs of the fresh venison. Keep moving.

consequences.journal_entry: Gave the Pawnee some of Thomas's deer. William didn't like it. The scarred rider nodded and they rode off. Bought us safe passage, maybe. Or just delayed the problem.

choices.label: Refuse. Tell them you need every pound. Hand weapons to all party members visibly.

consequences.journal_entry: Refused them. They tested us—two shots fired before they wheeled away. William took a graze. The scarred one looked at me for a long moment before he left. We're moving again, but something about this feels unfinished.

choices.label: Invite the scarred rider to eat with you. Learn what you can about the trail ahead.

consequences.journal_entry: The Pawnee ate with us. His name was something like Ash-Ka. He told us the buffalo have moved south. Said there are settlers three days ahead digging well, said cholera took two of them. He knew things—when to avoid the river, where the Lakota are hunting. When he left, he left half his payment uneaten. William thinks we're fools. I think we might live because of it.

personality_effects.William: Distrusts any negotiation. Sees Indians as a single threat, not individuals. His caution has kept people alive before.
personality_effects.Mary: Sees suffering and wants to help, even when it costs her. This impulse has saved them and endangered them equally.
personality_effects.Thomas: Young and untested. His kill earned him pride. He's learning what that pride costs.
personality_effects.Sarah: Watches. Doesn't speak much. But she notices who acts from fear and who acts from something else.

medium.journal_entry: May 10th. The plains are wide enough that you think you're alone until you're not. We're 320 miles in. Still 1,600 to go. Every choice here carries weight—not metaphorical. It comes home in flesh, in ammunition, in how people look at each other at the fire.

medium.title: The Bones in the Pass
medium.description:
You find a skeleton wedged between two boulders, half-buried in scree. The bones are old—months, maybe a year. A leather journal lies nearby, pages brittle and water-stained. James recognizes the handwriting on the cover: it belongs to Robert Hastings, a man from your original wagon train who split off in May. The skeleton wears no shoes. There are no tracks leading away from the body.

Mary kneels down and opens the journal. The final entry is dated June 14th: "Turned back. Foolish. The northern pass is closed. No water for thirty miles. Taking the ridge route alone. If God wills it—" The rest is illegible.

There's a small leather pouch clutched in the skeletal hand. It contains $73 in coin and a small vial of laudanum, nearly full.

choices.label: Take the money and medicine. Bury the bones properly.

consequences.journal_note: The act of burial took energy none of us had. James said words over Hastings. Mary wept for William again.

choices.label: Take only the medicine. Leave the bones and journal untouched.

consequences.journal_note: James couldn't meet our eyes. Mary called him a coward. We rode on as though we hadn't seen anything. The feeling didn't pass.

choices.label: Read the full journal. Use the laudanum to trade with the next settlement for food.

consequences.journal_note: We read Hastings' whole descent. The laudanum would fetch good price, but knowing what desperation drove him to write those final words—knowing we might sell his last comfort—changed something in us. No one speaks of it directly.

choices.label: Leave everything. Mark the location and continue. Don't mention it to the others.

consequences.journal_note: James rides ahead of the group now. When Mary asked why he was distant, he said it was the altitude. The skeleton stays in his head. Some burdens are meant to be carried alone.

medium.journal_entry: August 1st. Found Robert Hastings today, or what was left of him. The mountains don't forgive hesitation. They don't forgive pride either, or bad luck, or decisions made in desperation. We are moving through a landscape of graves now—some marked, most not. Thirty miles behind us is William. How many more are scattered across these peaks? The laudanum in that pouch smells like mercy, like surrender. We didn't take it. We couldn't afford the weight of knowing we did.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description:
One of your oxen—the bay you bought in Independence—won't pull. It stands with its head low, occasionally drinking from its own urine. The other three oxen are sound enough, but pulling a full wagon with three beasts means slower pace and a higher risk of breakdown before South Pass. Thomas says the alkali water poisoned it. Mary, burning with mountain fever in the wagon bed, says nothing. You have perhaps two hours of good light left to decide.

You can shoot the ox and redistribute the load. You can try to rest it overnight and hope it recovers—losing a day. Or you can push on with three oxen and see what happens. The mountains don't care which choice you make.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Butcher what you can carry, redistribute the load.

consequences.journal_entry: Put a bullet in the bay this evening. Its meat will keep us another week if the weather holds cold. The others watched. Nobody spoke.

choices.label: Rest the ox overnight. Delay departure until morning.

consequences.journal_entry: Camped early. Gave the sick ox water and rest. Mary's fever broke slightly in the night. We're losing time but the oxen need it. Maybe the bay will recover. Maybe we're wasting a day on a dead animal.

choices.label: Push on with three oxen. Increase pace to compensate.

consequences.journal_entry: Drove hard through the afternoon with three oxen pulling weight meant for four. By dusk, the rightmost wheel developed a crack. The bay followed behind, barely keeping pace. We'll make better ground but something will break soon.

personality_effects.context: James is practical and pushes forward. Thomas is cautious and respects animals. Mary's fever makes her silent on decisions, but weakness shows mercy.

medium.journal_entry: August 15th. Mountains all around us now. The alkali pools look like water until you drink them. Sarah and William are buried behind us. Mary burns with fever. The oxen are failing in their own ways, and so are we. Another choice with no good answer.

medium.title: The Ruined Flour
medium.description: Morning light shows the damage plain. The flour sack that went under yesterday is a paste now—maybe eight pounds salvageable if you're not particular about grit and creek water. Mary sits by the dead fire, staring at it. Thomas says nothing but his jaw works. You have twenty-five pounds of food total. The desert ahead is two weeks minimum to the Humboldt valley, assuming no delays. Assuming the oxen hold. You can feel the math the way you feel cold seeping through worn boot leather.

choices.label: Ration tighter. Stretch what's left to the valley.

consequences.journal_entry: Cut the rations again. Belly's a stone in my chest. Mary won't look at me. Can't blame her.

choices.label: Hunt. Send Thomas and James out for antelope or bighorn. Risk a full day.

consequences.journal_entry: Thomas came back grim but with a bighorn. Meat is meat. We eat tomorrow at least. Mary says a day lost is a day we don't reach winter camp. She's not wrong.

choices.label: Abandon the flour entirely. Leave now, make forced march.

consequences.journal_entry: Drove them hard. One ox went lame by noon. We left it. Mary's face said what she wouldn't. James is no leader. He's just the man who makes the bad choices first.

medium.journal_entry: The creek took the flour. We camp on the bank of our own bad luck. Something in the dark last night wasn't coyotes—I heard it twice, moving parallel to the camp. Never came close enough to see. Maybe it was watching the oxen. Maybe it was watching us. Either way, we move or we starve. There's no third choice on this trail.

medium.title: The Burned Wagons
medium.description: You round a bend in the canyon and find three wagons, burnt to their iron frames. The wood is old—weeks, maybe months. Animal bones scattered nearby. No bodies. Sarah's arrowheads suddenly feel less like curiosities. The wind carries ash. Mary points to wagon bed markings: a family name, burned almost illegible. You can make out 'K-R-A-U-S-E.' One wheel is intact. The other parts are scattered.

choices.label: Take what's salvageable. You need the spare wheel and axle parts.

consequences.journal_note: Scavenged from the dead. Their wheel may save us. James didn't look at Mary while doing it.

choices.label: Search the area for survivors or information. Maybe someone escaped.

consequences.journal_note: Found nothing but blackened canvas and the smell of old smoke. Mary thinks she heard singing in the canyon at dusk. It wasn't there when you checked.

choices.label: Mark it and move on. The Krauses are gone. Your family needs distance from this place.

consequences.journal_note: Left the wagons behind. Mary said we were cowards. James said the dead don't need us anymore. Mary didn't speak for hours after.

medium.journal_entry: Found the Krauses' end today. Or what was left of it. Three burnt wagons in a canyon that echoes wrong. No way to know what happened—Pawnee, Shoshone, accident, hunger. Just ash and bone and the cold certainty that the trail doesn't care who you were before it.

medium.title: The Meat Question
medium.description: The deer meat is already turning in this heat. Mary's dysentery has worsened—she needs protein, but fresh meat could worsen her condition. You could smoke what you can salvage over a fire, cook it thoroughly tonight, or trade it to a passing freight wagon you spot on the horizon for medicine and supplies. The choice costs time you don't have. First frost could come any night now.

choices.label: Smoke the meat over a slow fire. It'll take two days but preserve it.

consequences.narrative: You build a rack from sage and juniper. The smell draws Mary from the wagon. She watches you work, feverish and hollow-eyed. Two days stationary means two days closer to winter. The meat dries unevenly. Some of it spoils anyway.

choices.label: Cook it all tonight. Fresh meat now, nothing wasted.

consequences.narrative: The meat sizzles in the cast iron. Mary eats three bites and vomits an hour later. James eats his portion without tasting it. The rest goes to the oxen. In the morning, the waste attracts coyotes. They circle the camp at dusk, watching.

choices.label: Hail the freight wagon. Trade the meat for laudanum and quinine.

consequences.narrative: The freighters are Californians heading back east—lean men with hard faces. They offer less than the meat is worth. You take the deal anyway. Mary's fever breaks by morning. She doesn't ask what you gave up for it. James says nothing, but his jaw works like he's chewing on something bitter.

choices.label: Leave it. Bury what you can't carry. Move on.

consequences.narrative: The meat stays behind. Forty pounds of protein left to rot in the desert. Mary doesn't speak for two days. When she does, her voice is different—flatter, further away. James looks at the dead oxen tracks in the dust and wonders if this was the moment something broke that can't be fixed.

personality_effects.Mary: Each choice reveals how James values her life against the group's survival. She's aware of it.

medium.journal_entry: September 15th. Killed a deer. Didn't realize how little that solves anything. The meat spoils faster than hope out here. Mary's eyes are different now—like she's watching from inside a fever dream and can't wake up. The oxen need shoes we don't have. The mountains ahead look like teeth. Three people left and sometimes I think that's three too many.

medium.title: The Oxen Won't Move
medium.description: Morning. The rain stopped but left everything slick. One of your oxen—the big red one that pulled steady for eight hundred miles—stands in the mud and won't move. Its eyes are dull. When you approach, you see the problem: its left rear hoof is swollen black, the flesh around it rotted-looking. Thomas says it might be hoof rot from all this wet. He's right. The animal is done. You have three choices: kill it now and eat what meat you can (you're down to twenty pounds of flour and hardtack), leave it and hope the other two oxen can pull the wagon alone, or spend precious medicine trying to treat it—though neither he nor Mary knows if it will work. You lose a day either way.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Butcher what you can.
Leave it. Push on with two oxen and lighter pace.
Use your medicine. James and Mary work all day treating the wound.

Thomas.note: Thomas respects the practical choice but his jaw tightens. He knew that ox.

Mary.note: Mary insists on helping. By evening her hands are raw. She doesn't complain.

medium.journal_entry: October 1st. The red ox is finished. Rain won't stop. We are 1630 miles from home and the forest here feels like it's closing in. Thomas carved Sarah's name into a tree this morning when he thought no one was watching. Two people dead and we're not even through the mountains yet. The medicine is almost gone. Mary asked me if we'll make it. I didn't answer her. Sometimes not answering is the kindest thing.

medium.title: The Burned Wagon
medium.description: A mile ahead, smoke rises from the trail. As you approach, the charred remains of a Conestoga come into view—canvas gone, wheels collapsed, oxen bones scattered. Near the wagon sits a man, maybe fifty, with a deep burn across his left side. His clothes are singed. He's alive but barely moving. There's no sign of anyone else. His supplies are scattered: a flour sack half-burned, a water barrel intact, a rifle with the stock cracked. The man's eyes track your approach but he doesn't speak. Thomas notices something else—fresh moccasin prints in the ash, leading away from the wagon toward the river. Pawnee, maybe Omaha. Or maybe he did this himself. Hard to say.

choices.label: Take him in the wagon, share your medicines and food. He's a Christian duty.
Take his intact supplies and water barrel. Leave him water and biscuits—he can walk to the next station.

personality_effects.Mary: grew distant

choices.label: Leave immediately. Don't stop. Whatever happened here isn't your concern, and stopping invites trouble.

personality_effects.Thomas: questioned your leadership
personality_effects.Mary: spoke bitterly of James

choices.label: Ask him what happened. Listen to his story before deciding. Help or don't—at least you'll know.

personality_effects.Mary: believes suffering requires mercy
personality_effects.Thomas: suspects every stranger of duplicity
personality_effects.William: pragmatic about waste and sentiment

medium.journal_entry: Found a burned wagon on the trail today with a man near dead inside it. The prints in the ash told two stories and we couldn't know which was true. That's the thing about this country—it keeps its secrets. Whatever we chose, we'll carry it.

medium.title: The Fever Dreams
medium.description: Sarah wakes in the pre-dawn cold, soaked through. She's been fevering since yesterday—nothing severe, just the kind of weakness that settles into bones after weeks of trail dust and river water. But this morning she talks about a figure that stood at the edge of camp last night. A woman in a dress too fine for the plains, asking if Sarah had seen her daughter. When Mary touched her forehead, it was cool. When James asked which direction the woman went, Sarah pointed west. Toward where you're headed. No one else saw anything. Sarah insists she wasn't dreaming.

choices.label: Push hard today—cover extra miles and get ahead of whatever that was

consequences.journal_entry: Drove the oxen hard. Sarah pale but didn't complain. William thinks we're running from ghosts. Maybe we are. The marker we passed—Henderson, 1847—means there are graves older than the graves we know about.

choices.label: Rest the day, dose Sarah with quinine, observe her condition

consequences.journal_entry: Stopped. Gave Sarah quinine—her fever broke by afternoon. She remembers nothing of the woman now. James is anxious about schedule. Thomas paced the perimeter like we were under siege. One day lost feels like ten when you're this far from anywhere.

choices.label: Ask Sarah to describe the woman in detail; plot it in the journal with coordinates

consequences.journal_entry: Sarah described a face. A name: Catherine. She described the dress in colors that seem too vivid. Mary thinks we're encouraging delusion. William thinks it's a sign we should turn back. I wrote it all down anyway. The coordinates sit on the page like evidence of something I don't believe in but can't ignore.

personality_effects.Sarah: Feverish and uncertain whether her own mind is trustworthy
personality_effects.Mary: Practical. Skeptical of supernatural talk but watchful
personality_effects.William: Superstitious. The graves and the story are feeding his fear
personality_effects.Thomas: Analytical. Wants to explain it away but uneasy
personality_effects.James: Focused on miles. Irritated by delays but aware morale is fragile

medium.journal_entry: April 28th. One of our own saw something that wasn't there, or was. The fever may have broken but her mind hasn't settled. We're 150 miles from Independence and every mile west feels like we're drawing closer to something, not away from it. The prairies don't give back what they take.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description: By midday, the ox that drank from the alkali pool won't keep pace. It lags behind the wagon, breathing shallow. Thomas says it's poisoned. Mary thinks rest and water will fix it. William, feverish himself since yesterday, suggests putting a bullet in it and moving on. The storm is coming faster now—dark bruise-colored clouds to the west. You have maybe four hours of good light left.

choices.label: Stop here. Rest the ox and the party. Weather the storm.

consequences.notes: The ox recovers enough to walk by morning. Rain is hard but the tent holds. William's fever worsens in the wet cold. Mary tends him carefully.

personality_effects.Mary: respect
personality_effects.William: resentment
personality_effects.Thomas: neutral

choices.label: Kill the ox. Butcher what you can carry. Push hard before the storm hits.

consequences.notes: You make good time but the meat spoils faster than expected in the heat. Sarah steps on a cactus spine during the butchering. The storm catches you in open grassland.

personality_effects.Mary: anger
personality_effects.William: gratitude
personality_effects.Sarah: doubt

choices.label: Abandon the ox. Leave it with water and move on. It might survive.

consequences.notes: You don't look back. That night, William has a fever-dream of the animal following the wagon. No one sleeps well.

personality_effects.Mary: coldness
personality_effects.William: unease
personality_effects.Thomas: approval

personality_effects.James: weight_of_command
personality_effects.Mary: compassion_tested
personality_effects.Thomas: pragmatism_questioned
personality_effects.Sarah: vulnerability_exposed
personality_effects.William: fever_loosens_reason

medium.journal_entry: June 1st. The Platte runs brown and the grass is green but something in the water will kill you slow. One of the oxen is dying. We are five people and six animals and not enough of either. The sky looks like a bruise. Every choice here has teeth.

medium.title: The Fever Breaks—Or Something Does
medium.description:
Mary's been delirious for two days with the cholera, burning up despite the heat. This morning she's ice-cold, barely breathing, and asking for her mother who died in Cincinnati eight years ago. You've seen this before on the trail. People don't come back from it.

But then, around noon, her eyes open. Clear. She asks for water and broth. The fever's gone.

Thomas swears he saw a woman in a gray dress standing over Mary's bedroll last night. Tall. No face he could make out. He thought it was Sarah fetching water. Sarah was asleep twenty yards away.

Mary won't talk about what she remembers. Just stares at her hands like they belong to someone else.

choices.label: Push hard today—Mary's recovery is a sign we should make time while we can.
Rest the day. Mary needs it. Thomas needs to stop talking about what he saw.
Question Mary directly about the fever, what she remembers, who was there.

personality_effects.Mary: Whatever she remembers from the fever, she's keeping it. Won't meet your eyes now.
personality_effects.Thomas: Convinced something unnatural happened. Every shadow means something to him now.
personality_effects.Sarah: Quietly relieved Mary lived. Doesn't care about the rest of it.
personality_effects.James: You're the leader. They'll follow what you decide to believe.

medium.journal_entry: June 15th. Mary opened her eyes this morning and asked for water. Two days ago I was digging a hole. Thomas claims he saw something. The flour is still damp from the creek. Everything smells like sickness and river mud and the coming storm. The oxen are restless. I don't know what I believe anymore.

medium.title: The Smell
medium.description:
Mary finds you at dawn, before the others wake. She's holding a cloth to her nose. One of the oxen is down—the red one, the strongest of the five. Its breathing is shallow, labored. The smell coming from its hindquarters is wrong. Flesh-rot, though the animal still lives. She says she noticed it favoring that leg yesterday but didn't want to say anything with morale where it is.

You check the animal. The hind leg is swollen twice its size, the skin hot and weeping. Infection, deep in the muscle. You've seen this before. Sometimes rest and bleeding help. Sometimes the animal dies in harness three days later, and you're stranded with one less ox when the terrain gets worse.

Mary waits for your decision. The others will wake soon. Thomas will want to shoot it and salvage the meat—there's utility in that. Sarah will cry. James will defer to you because that's what he does.

choices.label: Bleed the leg and rest the ox here for two days. Reduce pace, hope infection drains.
Shoot the ox now. Butcher it for meat. Keep moving at steady pace.
Hitch the sick ox to an empty wagon as makeshift travois. Push hard—faster pace. Trust it survives.

medium.journal_entry: The red ox is sick. Rot in the leg. We're four days from Fort Laramie if water holds and nothing else breaks. Mary found it. She has good eyes for animals—better than mine some days. The dust is so thick now you taste it in your teeth at night.

medium.title: The Surveyor's Map
medium.description: In a rocky draw, you find a man's skeleton slumped against a cairn of stones. His leather satchel is still intact—weathered but dry. Inside: a detailed hand-drawn map of the mountain passes ahead, dated 1846, with notations in fading ink. 'Water here—poisoned July.' 'Pass impassable after snow.' 'Shoshone camp 8 miles east—trade possible.' A small journal documents his final weeks. The map shows a route that cuts 40 miles off your planned course, but it requires trusting the observations of a dead man in a landscape that's changed since he drew it. You also find a tin of laudanum and a half-full bottle of whiskey. The skeleton's clothing is rotted beyond use.

choices.label: Take the shortcut using the map. Save time and supplies.

consequences.uncertainty: The map is old. Rockfall could have closed passes. A storm at altitude kills faster than starvation.

choices.label: Follow the established trail. Ignore the map—it's too risky.

consequences.note: Mary mutters about the waste. Thomas approves of caution. The days stretch thin.

choices.label: Send James and Thomas ahead with the map to scout the shortcut. Rest the party here.

consequences.risk: Split party in mountains. If scouts don't return in 4 days, you move without them. Mary will not forgive this.

choices.label: Take the laudanum and whiskey. Leave the map.

consequences.note: That night, someone drinks more than is wise. In the morning, no one admits to it. The map stays behind, unfolded in the wind.

personality_effects.Mary: She stares at the skeleton's hands. Long time stopping here.
personality_effects.Thomas: Practical. 'His mistakes are a gift. Don't waste them.'
personality_effects.James: Leader's burden. Every choice here kills someone, maybe just differently.

medium.journal_entry: Found a dead man who knew this country better than we do. His knowledge costs nothing except the pride of following our own way. Odd how the dead can still lead you astray.

medium.title: The Shoshone Camp
medium.description: You crest a ridge at dusk and see smoke rising from a valley below. Through the scope, a small Shoshone encampment—maybe eight lodges. Two figures stand watching the trail. One raises a hand, not hostile, but not a welcome either. Mary shivers against the wagon seat, the mountain fever burning through her in waves. The highest pass is still three days ahead. These people likely have medicine—pemmican too, if they'd trade. But your ammo is low, your trade goods lower, and the Shoshone have been known to take horses from smaller parties passing through their territory. Your oxen are worth more than money out here.

choices.label: Ride down to the camp. Negotiate for medicine and supplies.

consequences.narrative: The Shoshone woman who greets you—grey streaking her braids—examines Mary with the clarity of someone who's seen mountain fever before. She offers a bundle of willow bark, actually useful, and trades six pounds of elk jerky for fifty cents and a button from your spare coat. Her grandson stares at your oxen the whole time. She doesn't ask. You know what you're leaving behind.

choices.label: Circle wide around the camp after dark. Keep moving.

consequences.narrative: You push on through the high pass in darkness, Mary delirious, crying out names of people back in Missouri. The trail narrows to a ledge where loose stone sounds like breaking glass under hooves. You don't sleep. By morning, Mary's fever has peaked to something worse—she's stopped sweating. Thomas says the word pneumonia. You have three doses of medicine left.

choices.label: Send Thomas down alone with trade goods. Less threatening.

consequences.narrative: Thomas comes back with willow bark and jerky, but the Shoshone asked for ammunition by sign language. He gave them ten rounds—he was frightened, he says. The old woman watched him the entire time. She said something in her language, and the others laughed. You don't know what it meant. Mary improves slightly. Thomas drinks more whiskey than usual that night.

personality_effects.Mary: Fever strips away politeness. She asked, in a clear moment, whether you'd leave her behind if she gets worse. You didn't answer.
personality_effects.James: The weight of the choice—medicine or safety, charity or survival—sits heavier than hunger.
personality_effects.Thomas: His hands still shake from the encounter. He's afraid of these mountains. He's afraid of being afraid.

medium.journal_entry: August 15th, 1848. Saw smoke at dusk. Shoshone people, not hostile but not friendly either. Mary is burning up. The mountains don't care whether you live or die—they're indifferent that way, which is worse than malice. Passed a grave marker today, weathered past reading. Wondered if anyone will remember the name. Oxen are lean. The pass waits.

medium.title: The Shoshone Scout
medium.description: A Shoshone man on a grey horse appears from the dust ahead, moving parallel to your trail. He's watching. Not hostile—not yet—but his hand rests on a bow slung across his chest. He wears a U.S. Army blanket over his shoulders, which is either a good sign or a very bad one. Your oxen are restless. Thomas reaches for the rifle. The scout raises his free hand, palm out, and calls something in his language. It sounds almost like a question.

choices.label: Halt the wagon and approach him alone with trade goods.

consequences.description: You leave your rifle behind and walk toward him with sugar, coffee, and a tin of tobacco. He watches you the whole way. Close up, you see he's maybe forty, with a scar that splits his left cheekbone. His horse is better fed than your oxen. He takes the goods, examines them, grunts. Points west and says something sharp. You don't understand the words, but the meaning is clear: don't camp near the river tonight. He rides off, disappearing into the dust within minutes. Your party relaxes slightly, but Thomas is furious—says you've encouraged him to demand more next time. Maybe he's right. Maybe you just bought three days of safety.

choices.label: Move the wagon faster and ignore him.

consequences.description: You snap the reins and drive hard. The scout makes no move to follow, but he doesn't leave either. He sits his horse and watches until you're far enough away to lose him in the haze. One of your oxen founders—its left hind leg goes lame. You'll have to shoot it or leave it behind. Mary watches it limp and says nothing. That ox carried extra weight you no longer have. Tonight, the rations feel thinner.

choices.label: Have Thomas shoot him from 80 yards while you drive hard.

consequences.description: Thomas is a decent shot. He hits the scout's horse, which screams and thrashes. The scout is thrown but gets up running—you see him disappear into a gulch. The horse won't move. You drive hard for two days straight, switching oxen every few hours, sleeping in shifts. On the second night, you see distant fires on the ridge above the trail. Just fires. No sounds. No attacks. But you know what you've done. Mary won't look at you. Thomas keeps his rifle close. The question isn't whether they're following—it's whether they want revenge badly enough to catch four exhausted people with a dead horse rotting behind them. The Shoshone know this country. You do not.

medium.journal_entry: A scout came out of the dust today. Army blanket and a bow. We made a choice about what kind of people we are, or maybe we just made a choice about what kind of people we need to be to reach Oregon. The land doesn't care which it was.

medium.title: The Herd at Dawn
medium.description: You wake to Mary coughing. The sound carries across the cold canyon. When you step outside to gather wood, you see them on the ridge—a dozen elk, maybe more, silhouetted against the pale sky. They're close. Close enough that James could make the shot. The oxen are restless. You have 89 rounds and 15 pounds of food. Mary needs protein. Your animals need rest. The elk don't move. They watch.

choices.label: Take the shot. One elk could feed everyone for two weeks.

consequences.notes: The kill is clean. Butchering takes most of the day. Mary's fever breaks after eating fresh meat. But the sound of the rifle echoes wrong in the canyon—longer than it should. The oxen spook and two wander toward the ravine before you corral them.

choices.label: Let them go. Save the ammunition. Push on to better hunting grounds.

consequences.notes: The herd disappears. Mary watches them go, says nothing. By evening her fever returns worse. You ration the meager stores further. She doesn't eat much anyway.

choices.label: Send James to stalk them on foot. A quiet kill. One animal only.

consequences.notes: James is gone most of the day. When he returns, he has meat but one of the oxen has gone lame—stepped wrong in the canyon while you weren't watching it. The animal can still pull but won't last much longer. James doesn't explain what took so long. Says the elk led him deeper into the canyons than he expected. Says he could have sworn he heard something following him. Doesn't say what.

personality_effects.James: pragmatic, capable, but something in the canyons has him unsettled
personality_effects.Mary: weakening, watching, waiting to see if you'll sacrifice for her

medium.journal_entry: Sept 10. The canyon funnels everything—sound, cold, doubt. Mary's fever is a separate thing now, like it belongs to someone else's body. We are so few. The meat we carry tastes like something has already died once before reaching the mouth. First frost is coming. The oxen know it. I know it. Even the dead know it, though they sleep in the ground five hundred miles back.

medium.title: The Coyote at the Perimeter
medium.description: Near dusk, while James tends the oxen, a coyote circles the camp—gaunt, deliberate, unafraid. It's been there twenty minutes. Mary watches from the fire, feverish, while the animal fixes on their meager food stores. It doesn't flee when James shouts. It waits. The thing moves like it knows they're dying slower than it is.

choices.label: Shoot it. One less predator, and the hide might trade for something.

consequences.note: The shot echoes across empty country. You hit it clean. Mary flinches—the sound seems louder than anything in weeks. You field-dress the carcass in the dark. The hide is worth nothing. The meat is tough and rank. You boil it anyway.

choices.label: Let it stay near camp. It might warn you of larger predators.

consequences.note: James agrees to leave it be. The coyote settles thirty yards out, watching. During the night, it eats two pounds of jerky it tears through the canvas bag. Mary can't sleep—the sound of tearing, chewing. By morning she's worse. The animal is gone, and you're hungrier, but no larger threats came.

choices.label: Poison the water you know it drinks from downwind. Slow work, but sure.

consequences.note: You use laudanum from the medicine kit—most of it. The coyote drinks at dawn three miles downwind. You never see it die. But the act costs something. Mary asks what the medicine was for. You don't answer. Neither of you sleeps well after. The silence feels thinner, somehow.

personality_effects.James: Each choice hardens him differently. Shooting affirms control. Restraint erodes it. Poisoning introduces a knowledge he'll carry to Oregon.
personality_effects.Mary: Watches how James handles scarcity and fear. Her faith in his choices determines if she recovers or worsens. Poisoning frightens her.

medium.journal_entry: September 15th. A coyote came at dusk. Thinner than us. We decided its fate like we decide everything now—which deaths matter and which don't. William's grave is three days behind. The mountains are somewhere ahead. Mary's fever breaks and returns with the cold. I don't know if we'll see Oregon. I know we'll see more choices like this one.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description: One of your oxen drank from that alkali pool two days back. Now it moves like it's pulling the world behind it—head down, breath labored. The animal's ribs show through its hide. Rain comes sideways, and you're losing light. The forest closes in, mud thigh-deep. You have maybe five miles left before dark makes the trail invisible. The ox won't make it far at this pace. Mary suggests leaving it behind with extra fodder. Thomas wants to kill it now and eat the meat—you're on meager rations and it would be waste otherwise. James says nothing, just watches the thing suffer.

choices.label: Kill the ox. Butcher it for meat. You'll gain weight but lose a draft animal.
Leave the ox behind with supplies. Lighten the load, move faster. The ox might recover or not.
Push through. Make camp early. Tend the ox with medicine and rest. Hope it recovers by morning.

personality_effects.note: Thomas values pragmatism and survival over sentiment. Mary holds onto hope and reserves. James watches, measures, judges slowly.

medium.journal_entry: October 1st. The alkali animal is dying by inches. We are dying by inches. The rain won't stop. Sarah and William are already gone—buried in unmarked ground that will be grown over by spring. The living owe nothing to the dying, but the living owe something to themselves. That's what I tell myself. The mud smells like iron and rot.

medium.title: The Soaked Flour
medium.description: Mary discovers the flour sack has split open inside the wagon bed. Water pooled underneath during the creek crossing. Maybe twenty pounds of it is paste now, growing gray-green at the edges. The rest—perhaps fifteen pounds—is still salvageable if you act today. The rain keeps coming. She stands there holding the ruined sack, not saying anything, which is worse than if she screamed.

choices.label: Spread the wet flour on canvas to dry it by the fire. It'll take all day and night.

consequences.description: You lose a day to the rain. The flour never fully dries—it cakes, it scorches on the bottom. You salvage maybe ten pounds of something that tastes like wet ash and char. Mary works beside you in the smoke without complaint. That matters more than the flour.

choices.label: Accept the loss. Discard the ruined flour and ration harder.

consequences.description: You throw the paste into the forest. Move on. Mary watches you do it. Later, when the hunger hits hard in two weeks, she'll remember this moment. The oxen press forward through deeper mud. By nightfall your shoulders ache from pushing the wagon out of ruts.

choices.label: Mix the wet flour with what grease you have left and make hard biscuits. It's not ideal but it's food.

consequences.description: You work until dark. The biscuits are dense, slightly sour, gritty. You use your last measure of laudanum to boil the water clean first. Mary eats one without comment and says the radio—then stops herself. Her eyes unfocus for a moment. The biscuits sit heavy in your stomach all night. Neither of you sleep well.

personality_effects.Mary: Her sanity is fraying. She said 'radio.' She meant 'ration' but the word that came out was wrong. James notices but doesn't correct her. In the rain-dark, the distinction seems important.

medium.journal_entry: Oct 10 — The creek took our flour. Mary found it this morning. Nothing to do but choose which way to lose. The coyotes sounded almost like laughter last night. We're 1700 miles from Missouri and maybe 500 from the valley. The oxen are thin. I can count their ribs. Mine too, I expect. The rain keeps coming. It doesn't stop anymore. It just comes.

medium.title: The Carcass
medium.description:
A buffalo lies half-buried in a draw where the wagon ruts lead. Maybe three days dead. Bloated and dark. The smell hits you before you see it — sweet rot and something else, something chemical that makes the back of your throat close.

James kneels by the skull. A rifle wound through the left side, clean shot. Fresh enough that flies still swarm. Someone killed it and didn't field-dress it, just left it. Waste like that doesn't sit right in this country.

Mary points to the hoofprints leading northeast — unshod horses, recent. Maybe a hunting party. Maybe something else.

William coughs. He's been weak since Independence. The smell isn't helping.

There's still meat on it. Weeks of rot won't touch all of it yet.

choices.label: Butcher what you can. Smoke the meat.

consequences.journal_entry: Spent the day with our hands in spoiled meat. Got maybe 45 pounds worth. The stomach lining broke open halfway through. Mary gagged twice. William wouldn't come near it. We built a smoke frame and got what we could on it, but the taste will haunt everything we cook for days. Necessary. Unpleasant. No one thanked anyone.

choices.label: Leave it. There are animals that need it more than you do.

consequences.journal_entry: James said we're not scavengers. Left the carcass to the coyotes and whatever else finds it. Mary looked relieved. There's something in a man that doesn't want to bend too far toward survival. Or maybe we're just not hungry enough yet. The thought troubles me more than it should.

choices.label: Follow the hoofprints. Find who made that shot.

consequences.journal_entry: Rode northeast for hours. Found nothing but more grass. The horses' prints faded into hardpan. Wasted ammunition on a warning shot. Thomas thinks we're fools chasing ghosts. Maybe he's right. James wanted answers. Got only silence and the sense of being watched from somewhere we couldn't see. Came back to the wagons at dusk with nothing but questions and less bullets.

medium.journal_entry: The buffalo's been dead three days in this heat. Someone shot it for sport or practice and didn't care about the meat. Saw Mary's face when she realized — that look of waste, of rules broken that nobody enforces out here. Rules we have to enforce on ourselves or we're nothing but wolves.

medium.title: The Burial
medium.description: You find a grave on the trail—recent, maybe a week old. The wooden cross is crude but deliberate. No name carved into it. Beside it, a child's doll made from cloth scraps and a button, left as an offering or abandoned in haste. The oxen sense something and grow restless. Thomas wants to keep moving. Mary suggests stopping to say words. William mentions the doll might fetch a few cents if cleaned up. Sarah looks pale.

choices.label: Stop and mark the grave properly—add stones, say a prayer.

consequences.description: You lose a day but the act settles something in the group. Mary leads a quiet prayer. Even Thomas seems less burdened afterward. But the delay weighs on William—he mutters about falling behind schedule. Sarah touches the doll one last time before you leave it undisturbed.

choices.label: Take the doll—might trade it for supplies at the next post, or sell it.

consequences.description: William's practical. The doll is worth something to someone. You wrap it carefully and pack it. Mary doesn't speak to you for two hours. Sarah's jaw stays tight. That night, Sarah wakes screaming—fever dream or something else, she won't say. You sell the doll at the next trading post for 45 cents. The buyer doesn't ask questions. Neither do you.

choices.label: Leave it as is and move on—respect the grave without slowing down.

consequences.description: You ride past without stopping. Practical. The doll stays where it was left. No one speaks much that afternoon. Thomas and William seem relieved—momentum matters on the trail. But Mary watches the grave disappear behind you, her expression unreadable. Sarah hums something low and tuneless for the next five miles.

personality_effects.description: The group splits on how to treat the dead. Mary leans toward ritual and remembrance. William toward utility. Thomas toward forward motion. Sarah absorbs whatever choice is made like a sponge absorbs water.

medium.journal_entry: Found a grave today. No name. A child's doll left beside it. The weight of unknown dead follows you west. We debated what to do as if we had any real choice at all. The doll still sits in that grass, or it doesn't. Either way, it marks something true about this journey—we are moving through a landscape of other people's endings, and we will leave our own marks whether we intend to or not.

medium.title: The Fever Dream at Dusk
medium.description: William collapsed during the afternoon heat, burning up and muttering in a language none of you speak. By evening his fever broke, but he remembers nothing—only says he was standing in a field of white stones while someone sang. Mary found welts on his arm, fresh as if made this morning, though no insect bite looks quite like that. The storm is coming hard now. You need to decide whether to push on through the weather or make camp here by the river, where the mosquitoes are worst but shelter is better. William is conscious but weakened. The oxen are restless.

choices.label: Push through the storm. The river valley narrows ahead—high ground is half a mile on.

consequences.notes: One ox founders in the mud. William's fever spikes again during the crossing—his delirium seems clearer this time, more articulate. He speaks of a pale woman at the riverbank. By dawn he's lucid but insists someone was following the wagon all night. No one else saw anything.

choices.label: Camp here. Set a watch, keep fires bright, dose William with whiskey and quinine.

consequences.notes: The mosquitoes are merciless. Thomas develops welts on his neck by morning—not the fever kind, just the bites. William sleeps without dreams, and wakes asking why no one told him about the singing. When asked to explain, he can't. Sarah notices fresh water in the river runs red-brown, though there's no blood she can find. Likely iron from upstream.

choices.label: Send James and Thomas ahead to scout high ground. Keep the wagon moving slow with Mary in charge.

consequences.notes: James and Thomas return drenched, reporting good camp two miles ahead. But they're spooked—both say they heard voices from the river while scouting, calling names. Not English names. Mary held the line with the wagon, kept the oxen steady. William improved slightly with purpose restored. When you reach the camp site, you find fresh tracks in the mud. Pawnee, maybe a day old. The fire you build that night burns hot and wrong—the wood won't catch properly.

personality_effects.William: His fever-dream has shaken him. He believes he was touched by something. Every shadow now carries weight.
personality_effects.Mary: She dosing William, keeping practical. Respects crisis but shows no patience for superstition.
personality_effects.Thomas: The scouting expedition unsettled him more than he admits. Jumps at sound.

medium.journal_entry: June 1st. William took ill this afternoon with a burning that broke as quick as it came. He won't say what he remembers, only what he doesn't. The welts on his arm look fresh. The river here is thick and slow, choked with mosquitoes and something else—a smell like copper under rot. The storm built like anger. No one sleeps easy tonight, whatever choice we make. Two thousand miles to Oregon, and the land is starting to show its teeth.

medium.title: The Sick Woman and the Stranger's Offer
medium.description: Mary's fever peaks at midday. Her skin is gray and slick. You've stopped the wagons near a stand of cottonwoods where a creek runs brown from recent rain. Thomas suggests moving on—the mosquitoes here are worse, and she needs to be away from the water. But as you're tending her, a Pawnee man rides down from the north bluffs. He's alone, maybe sixty, with fresh deer meat wrapped in hide. He speaks broken English and says his grandmother was Pawnee-French from the trading days. He looks at Mary and says her spirit is leaving through her mouth. He has medicine—root and bark from plants you don't recognize. He wants one of your oxen. He doesn't seem like he's bargaining. He seems like he's telling you the price of her staying alive.

choices.label: Accept the trade. Give him an ox for the medicine.

consequences.journal_entry: We gave Spotted Elk an ox. Mary took the bitter tea. Whether it was the medicine or her own stubbornness, the fever broke by morning. One less animal to feed, but we needed her alive more.

choices.label: Refuse. Your medicine is sufficient. Push on to higher ground.

consequences.journal_entry: We moved the wagon uphill as Thomas wanted. Mary deteriorated through the night. By dawn she couldn't hold water. Sarah wouldn't speak to me. I wonder if Spotted Elk is still watching from the bluffs.

choices.label: Negotiate. Offer him food and ammunition instead of an ox.

consequences.journal_entry: Spotted Elk took 15 pounds of jerky and ammunition. He left a pouch of dried willow bark. Whether it helped or Mary's own body broke the fever, we'll never know. We kept the ox. We still lost ground.

choices.label: Tell him no. Mary is going to die—why waste an ox?

consequences.journal_entry: I said the words out loud. Spotted Elk just nodded and rode back north. Mary heard. She stopped fighting the fever. She was gone by the evening. The oxen didn't care. We buried her near the cottonwoods and said nothing to each other for two days.

personality_effects.James: Leader bears weight of the choice.
personality_effects.Mary: Survival depends on what you decide.
personality_effects.Thomas: Practical. Survival favors the ruthless.
personality_effects.Sarah: Compassion is her compass.

medium.journal_entry: The river valley smells like wet earth and sickness. A stranger came at the worst moment, speaking in a language that sounded like truth.

medium.title: The Bluff's Gift
medium.description: You're moving through high grassland when Thomas spots something glinting in the ravine below—metal, definitely metal. The bluff rises sheer on either side. Down there, wedged between rocks near a dried creek bed, is what looks like a wagon axle. Good iron. Behind it, half-buried in silt, are the remains of a canvas-topped frame. No bodies visible. The storm is maybe an hour away, and the ravine will flood when it hits. Mary's fever hasn't broken. The detour down and back up will cost you four miles and most of the daylight.

choices.label: Climb down. Get the axle and salvage what you can.

consequences.journal_note: Found axle and scattered rounds. Sarah twisted her ankle on the descent. We made camp in the ravine before the storm hit—wise or foolish, hard to say. The wagon we found had been here months, maybe longer. No names carved anywhere. No way to know if they made it out.

choices.label: Keep moving. You've got enough parts. The storm's coming.

consequences.journal_note: Passed a wreck in the ravine. Could have salvaged something, but the sky was turning yellow-gray. Made twelve miles before the storm pinned us down under oilcloth. Thomas hasn't stopped talking about what we left behind. Mary's fever spiked in the wet cold.

choices.label: Send Thomas and James down. Mary and Sarah stay with the wagons.

consequences.journal_note: Split the party. Bad idea, maybe, but necessary. The men got down and back up before the worst of it, but we were separated for three hours. Mary watched the sky darken from the wagon seat. Sarah kept saying she heard voices from the ravine. Both are jumpy now, and there's a coldness between Mary and James that wasn't there before.

medium.journal_entry: June 25th. The bluffs showed us a grave that wasn't ours. Yet. The storm came on fast, turning the sky the color of old meat. We made a choice—every choice leaves something behind. That's the arithmetic of this trail. You trade distance for salvage, caution for preparation, unity for survival. I don't know which equation we got right.

medium.title: The Pawnee Scout
medium.description: A lone Pawnee scout on a gray mare appears from the heat shimmer, moving parallel to your train at a distance. He's studying your oxen. Your water situation is critical—the remaining barrel won't reach the next reliable source, maybe 40 miles east. The scout doesn't approach, doesn't threaten. Just watches. James remembers the trade post manager saying the Pawnee this season are hungry, their buffalo hunt poor. The scout's horse is ribs and angles.

choices.label: Offer trade: flour and salt pork for information about water ahead

consequences.message: The scout accepts with a nod. He draws water locations in the dust—two sources within 25 miles, both small but reliable if you know where to dig. He eats standing, watching your oxen the whole time. Rides west without another word. You feel lighter and more nervous at once.

choices.label: Say nothing. Drive on. Don't acknowledge him

consequences.message: The scout matches your pace for another hour. Mary keeps glancing back. Thomas fidgets with his rifle. The anxiety eats at everyone. By evening he's gone, but so is the name of that water. You camp uncertain, rationing what you have. That night, Thomas talks about ambush.

choices.label: Approach slowly with hands visible. Attempt conversation

consequences.message: You walk out alone. The scout's hand goes to a knife. You stop 30 paces off, palms open. He doesn't move. You ask about water. Long silence. He speaks Pawnee. Neither of you understands the other. Thomas, unsettled, fires a warning shot. The scout spins his mare and vanishes into the heat shimmer. No one speaks the rest of the day. You gained nothing but James's reputation as reckless, and Thomas's as trigger-happy.

choices.label: Let him take one ox in exchange for his knowledge and escort past Pawnee hunting grounds

consequences.message: The scout considers. Nods. He stays with you two days, pointing out seeps and salt-licks, pushing your pace. He's efficient. Professional. On the third morning he takes the weakest ox—the one with the limp—and disappears north. You're down to four, and faster because of it. Mary thinks you bought survival. James thinks you sold the future.

personality_effects.base: The scout's presence splits the group. Mary and Sarah see trade and negotiation. James and Thomas see threat.

medium.journal_entry: July 10th — A Pawnee scout appeared today in the heat. We are dying of thirst and he knew it. Everyone looked to me. I chose to look back. What does a man's hunger say about his intentions? What does ours say about ours? William would have shot. I'm grateful he's not here to judge.

medium.title: The Fever Dream
medium.description: Mary wakes you at dawn, delirious. She speaks of her sister—dead three years in Ohio—standing at the edge of camp, beckoning her toward the peaks. The fever has her bad. When you check her, she's burning at 104 degrees. Thomas finds fresh blood on her lips when she coughs. The mountain air is thin up here. Thin enough that the mind plays tricks. Or thin enough that other things slip through. She keeps saying her sister's name. Keep moving and she might not make it to the next pass. Stop and the snow will come down hard—you felt it in last night's wind. The oxen are already spooked, bunching together. James, your call to make.

choices.label: Push hard for the pass. Keep her moving—rest stops only for water. Mountain fever breaks or it doesn't. Staying here guarantees nothing.

personality_effects.Mary: resentment_and_fever
personality_effects.Thomas: doubts_your_mercy

choices.label: Make camp. Use the remaining medicine and let her rest. Tend her yourself through the night. It costs time but she might live to see Oregon.

personality_effects.Mary: knows_you_chose_her
personality_effects.Thomas: sees_you_as_weak

choices.label: Give her the laudanum. All of it. Let her sleep hard and dreamless. Move steady. If she wakes up worse, you'll know what needed doing.

personality_effects.Mary: you_drugged_her_into_compliance
personality_effects.Thomas: understands_but_hates_it
personality_effects.James: knows_what_you_did

personality_effects.Mary: fever_delirium_worsening
personality_effects.Thomas: watching_how_you_choose
personality_effects.James: the_weight_of_command

medium.journal_entry: August 15th. Mary called out names that weren't hers. The peaks above us hold snow. Thomas won't meet my eyes. I've learned that mountains don't kill you with cold or hunger—they kill you with the time it takes to decide who lives and who doesn't. Her sister isn't here. I'm almost certain of it. Almost.

medium.title: The Sickness Spreads
medium.description: Mary wakes before dawn with a fever. Not the usual trail fatigue—her skin is hot, her breath shallow. Thomas has similar signs: chills, a persistent cough. The fresh deer meat was good, but three days of eating it has left the others uncertain. James suspects dysentery. Mary insists it's just the dust. The medicine chest holds two doses of quinine. The next settlement is forty miles away through high desert. The oxen are already thin from the pace.

choices.label: Dose both Mary and Thomas with quinine. Push hard for the settlement.

personality_effects.Mary: sees James as decisive, not reckless
personality_effects.Thomas: grateful but weakened

consequences.note: The quinine steadies them enough to travel. But the pace breaks Thomas further. One ox collapses on the second day. Mary's fever breaks, but she's hollow after.

choices.label: Rest here two days. Ration the meat carefully. Save the medicine in case it worsens.

personality_effects.Mary: resents the delay; fever gets worse before it gets better
personality_effects.Thomas: coughs through the night, can't sleep

consequences.note: By the end of the second day, Mary's fever spikes to 104. Thomas is delirious. James realizes waiting was a mistake. They move on anyway.

choices.label: Send James and Mary ahead on two oxen to reach the settlement doctor. Thomas and Mary will follow with the wagons.

personality_effects.Mary: terrified of being left behind with a sick man
personality_effects.Thomas: furious; feels abandoned
personality_effects.James: carrying guilt that won't settle

consequences.note: James and Mary reach the settlement in three days. The doctor gives them laudanum and sends them back with supplies. They find Thomas and Mary camped twelve miles back. Thomas hasn't spoken to James in two days. The rift doesn't heal.

personality_effects.James: The weight of sick people is different from the weight of corpses. At least with corpses you know what you've lost.
personality_effects.Mary: Fever dreams. Her mother was there. The river ran backward.
personality_effects.Thomas: Watched the meat spoil yesterday. Watched Mary shake. Watched James calculate odds.

medium.journal_entry: Sept 1st. Mary sick. Thomas too. The deer tasted good when we killed it. Tastes different now. Quinine is gone or we save it. Either way someone pays. The desert doesn't care which choice we make. It only waits. Frost coming. Sarah would have known what to do with a fever. Sarah's in a grave near Fort Hall with rocks piled heavy so the wolves wouldn't dig. The living are harder to save than the dead are to bury. — J.

medium.title: The Oxen Won't Drink
medium.description: At the Snake River crossing, your four oxen refuse the water. They circle the bank, bellowing, refusing to approach. The river runs clear enough—you've drunk from worse—but the animals won't touch it. Mary suggests fever sickness in the herd. James notices vultures circling upstream. The canyon walls cut off any other water source for miles. You need these oxen. Without them, you walk to Oregon.

choices.label: Force them downstream to drink from a narrower bend. They'll go where they're driven.

consequences.journal_entry: Forced the lead ox into the water by hand. It went in thrashing and came up coughing black bile. Lost it in the current before we could rope it back. Mary didn't speak the rest of the day. Three oxen now. We're thinner rations or bust.

choices.label: Climb the canyon rim. Find another water source higher up, even if it costs a day.

consequences.journal_entry: Spent the morning hunting in the rocks above the river. Found a spring running cold from limestone. The oxen drank it like their lives depended on it.
