Maybe they knew something about the river we didn't. Mary's hands are bleeding from the climb. We're a day behind but alive. There's things animals understand we don't.

choices.label: Slaughter the sickest-looking ox and use its meat. Conserve food. Press on with three.

personality_effects.Mary: hardened

consequences.journal_entry: Butchered the ox at dusk. Its meat was gray inside. Not sick—something else. We cooked it anyway. It tasted like copper and dust. Mary held the knife steady while I made the cuts. Didn't look away once. I wonder what that does to a person. Three oxen left. The meat will last ten days if we're careful. If we're not careful, it'll be gone in five and we'll be hungry again.

choices.label: Wait. Make camp and observe the herd. Sometimes animals know things before we do.

consequences.journal_entry: On the third morning, the oxen walked to the river and drank without hesitation. Whatever had been wrong had passed. We lost three days of travel and six pounds of flour. Mary asked what would have happened if we'd pushed them. I told her I didn't know. The truth is worse—I know exactly what would have happened. Three oxen and forty miles left to walk.

personality_effects.James: doubt
personality_effects.Mary: caution

medium.journal_entry: The canyon closes in. The animals know something about this country we don't. We're learning it slow, and some of us are learning it dead.

medium.title: The Cough
medium.description: Thomas has been coughing since yesterday. This morning he coughed blood into his handkerchief. The others notice. Mary thinks it's just the cold fog. James knows better. Thomas insists he can walk, but his breathing is labored and wet. The rain keeps coming. You have one dose of medicine left. You have no doctor within fifty miles. The forest is thick here—slow going even without sickness.

choices.label: Give Thomas the medicine and reduce rations further to stretch food
Tell Thomas to stay behind at the next trading post. He'll have shelter and a chance
Push harder. Increase pace to reach the Willamette Valley faster where there's real help

personality_effects.Thomas: The blood in his handkerchief was real. So is the fear behind his eyes.
personality_effects.Mary: She's seen this before. Her sister in Cincinnati.
personality_effects.James: The mathematics of survival don't include sentiment.

medium.journal_entry: October 1st. Thomas coughed blood this morning. The rain tastes like rust. We are 1,630 miles from Independence and roughly 200 from the valley. The medicine bottle sits in my pack like a choice I haven't made yet. Every choice leaves someone behind.

medium.title: The Ferryman's Price
medium.description: A swollen creek blocks the trail ahead—rain has made it impassable. A Shoshone man with a flatboat offers to pole your wagon across for five dollars or twenty pounds of food. He's alone, methodical, watching your dwindling oxen. The crossing will take two hours. If you attempt to ford it yourself, the current is fast and the mud banks are unstable. Your party is thin. One accident ends everything.

choices.label: Pay the ferryman. You need those oxen alive.

consequences.description: The Shoshone man poles with silent efficiency. James watches the water churn against the wheels. The oxen are steady. On the far bank, Mary remarks that the money was worthless out here anyway. James doesn't answer. They press on into fog.

choices.label: Offer food instead. Preserve the cash.

consequences.description: The ferryman nods and accepts. As he loads the jerky into a pack, James notices the man's ribs through his shirt. The crossing is smooth. But Mary watches James watch the hungry man, and something between them cools. They've both seen what hunger does.

choices.label: Attempt to ford. You've crossed worse.

consequences.description: The first ox makes it. The second gets swept sideways—James wades in, rope around his waist, hauling. Cold. The animal bellows. They get it across but downstream, hours spent retrieving supplies scattered on rocks. James's shoulder throbs. That night, Mary tends his wound in silence. The ferryman is long gone. So is James's certainty that he can outthink the land.

choices.label: Ask the ferryman for work. Trade labor instead.

consequences.description: The Shoshone man studies James for a long moment, then nods. They spend the day clearing deadfall from his boat's approach, James and the ferryman working in rain without speaking. At dusk, the man shares elk soup. It's the best thing James has tasted in weeks. When the wagon is across, the ferryman presses six pounds of dried meat into James's hands. 'You come back this way,' he says. James doesn't think either of them believes he will.

medium.journal_entry: Oct 10 — Creek swollen black as a bruise. Pawnee meat holding. Oxen down to three. The ferryman watched us like he already knew what we'd choose. Maybe he did. Thomas and Sarah and William are ash now, or trees, or something else. Mary won't meet my eye anymore. I wonder if that's mercy or judgment. The fog tastes like copper. Two thousand miles and we're still hungry.

medium.title: The Pawnee Scout
medium.description: A lone Pawnee scout rides out from the western bluff at dusk—not hostile, not friendly. He circles your camp at a distance, studying the oxen. Your people tense. The scout carries a rifle and wears a US Army jacket, weathered but recognizable. He doesn't approach. He doesn't leave. Thomas wants to shoot. Mary thinks he wants to trade. William—the living one—is already checking his powder horn.

choices.label: Offer trade goods. Coffee, tobacco, a knife. Show peaceful intent.

consequences.outcome: The scout takes the goods without speaking. He points northeast—away from your planned route—and rides off. Whether warning or direction, you'll never know. But no shot was fired. No blood on the grass.

choices.label: Stay silent and still. Don't provoke. Let him decide.

consequences.outcome: The scout watches for nearly an hour. The tension cracks something in Sarah—she begins trembling, won't stop. Eventually he rides away. But she's worse now. Quieter. The waiting was its own kind of wound.

choices.label: Send Thomas out with a white flag. Talk, if talking is possible.

consequences.outcome: Thomas rides out. The scout waits. When Thomas returns, his jaw is clenched. He won't say what was said. His hands shake. He gives you a bearing—a river two days north where 'trouble is coming from the west.' Whether Thomas learned this or imagined it in the fear, he can't or won't clarify. The scout is gone. The warning remains, and with it, doubt.

choices.label: Harness the oxen. Move camp now, tonight. Don't wait for dark.

consequences.outcome: The pace is brutal—one ox goes lame in the dark, and you have to shoot it. Mercy is harder than leaving something to die slow. The scout is gone by then anyway. Whether you ran from a threat or from nothing, the fear is just as real. And now you're six oxen instead of seven on the longest stretch ahead.

personality_effects.James: Watching his people watch him. Waiting to see if he chooses right.
personality_effects.Mary: Sees the scout as a person. Sees choosing wrong as a door that doesn't close.
personality_effects.Thomas: His hand wants the gun. His mind knows better. One has to win.
personality_effects.Sarah: Already thinking about the grave behind them. Wondering how many more.
personality_effects.William: Alive when another William isn't. That math doesn't balance.

medium.journal_entry: April 28th. A scout came at dusk wearing Army cloth and an expression I couldn't read. We made a choice—one of four ways to be afraid. The prairie keeps its secrets. So do we, now.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description: The ox that drank from the alkali pool yesterday moves slower than the others. Its flanks are sunken. Thomas says it'll be dead by tomorrow. Mary argues you should rest the animal and dose it with what medicine you have. William suggests butchering it now—the meat won't last long in this heat, but it's better than watching it suffer and waste away. James hasn't spoken. The other oxen are restless, as if they sense something wrong in the herd. You have perhaps two hours of good light left to decide.

choices.label: Rest the ox here for two days. Use medicine to try to save it.

consequences.outcome: The ox survives but never fully recovers. It limps along at half-speed for the rest of the journey. Thomas mutters about wasted time. William stops offering suggestions.

choices.label: Butcher the ox now. Smoke what meat you can carry.

consequences.outcome: The butchering is efficient and grim. The meat spoils faster than you hoped—you'll lose half of it in a week. Mary won't look at you. The remaining oxen pull harder as if they understand they've been lightened by one of their own.

choices.label: Leave it behind. Travel through the night.

consequences.outcome: You abandon the ox to whatever end comes. Nighttime travel exhausts everyone. Someone swears they heard the animal calling behind you for hours, though the distance makes that impossible. Sarah develops a cough that won't stop.

personality_effects.James: watches the ox without judgment, waiting for your decision
personality_effects.Mary: believes suffering can be prevented
personality_effects.Thomas: accepts necessity without sentiment
personality_effects.Sarah: is bothered by waste
personality_effects.William: sees problems as equations to solve

medium.journal_entry: May 1st. Chimney Rock behind us like a gravestone. The alkali pool was a test we didn't know we were taking. One of the oxen failed it. Now we stand in the late light arguing about mercy and practicality like they're different things. They're not. Everything west of the Missouri is just choosing which loss you can live with. The thunder on the horizon doesn't scare me half as much as the look on Mary's face when William mentioned the knife.

medium.title: The Wet Flour Question
medium.description: By midmorning, the flour sacks have begun to mold. Not all of it—maybe thirty pounds are salvageable if you act now. Mary argues for drying what you can over a hot fire. Thomas suggests discarding the lot and hunting to make up the calories. William, ever practical, notes that moldy flour won't kill anyone if you cook it hot enough. Sarah sits quiet, watching the oxen. The smell is already turning your stomach.

choices.label: Spend the day drying flour over fires. Gamble on salvaging most of it.

consequences.narrative: You build drying racks and tend the fires through the heat of the day. The flies come in clouds. Recover roughly twenty-two pounds of usable flour, though it has a peculiar taste. Thomas mutters about wasted time. Mary approves of the resourcefulness, but your scouts report a storm building to the northwest. You've lost a day's travel.

choices.label: Dump the flour. Hunt and push hard for the next settlement.

consequences.narrative: You leave the sacks behind and head into prairie grass where antelope scatter. Thomas and William spend six hours hunting, bringing down one good buck. It's enough, but the loss of flour sits heavy on Mary. She calculates rations in quiet fury. You cover eighteen miles before dark, but you're running leaner now, and the herd is tired.

choices.label: Cook the moldy flour into hardtack. Use it within three days before it worsens.

consequences.narrative: You boil the flour with salt and fat, baking it hard on griddles. The hardtack is dense and tastes faintly of rot, but it keeps. You move camp by afternoon, covering eight miles before stopping. That night, Sarah complains of stomach cramps. William seems unsurprised—he's eaten worse. By morning, two others report the same. Nothing serious yet, but you've essentially traded miles for sickness.

personality_effects.James: Leadership is choosing between three wrongs. You feel it now.
personality_effects.Mary: Waste offends her more than hunger.
personality_effects.Thomas: Ambition and practicality war in him. Inaction galls him.
personality_effects.Sarah: Her optimism is cracking. She watches the decisions and says nothing.
personality_effects.William: He knows survival math. He knows what people won't admit.

medium.journal_entry: The creek took our flour. We took the rest. Three choices, none good. The prairie stretches flat and indifferent. Nobody says it, but we're all calculating how long we can move on thin rations. The coyotes last night weren't hunting us. They were laughing.

medium.title: The Scout's Fever
medium.description: Thomas staggers into camp at dusk, delirious. His skin is hot enough to blister. He was scouting ahead for water when the fever took him—he barely made it back. The thermometer reads 104 degrees. Mary thinks it's cholera. Sarah thinks it's sun-stroke. James knows they can't afford to lose another person, and they can't afford to stay put in this heat. Water is critical. The next known water source is twelve miles north, but it's disputed Pawnee territory, and the Pawnee have been aggressive toward emigrant trains this season.

choices.label: Push on tonight with Thomas in the wagon. Reach water by dawn.

consequences.note: Oxen collapse in night heat. Thomas's condition worsens in wagon jolting. You reach water at dawn, but one ox dies. Thomas is semiconscious. If he pulls through, it will take days.

choices.label: Make camp here. Ration water strictly. Treat Thomas with quinine and rest.

consequences.note: Thomas stabilizes slightly. But you're immobilized in exposed terrain with water running low. Mary grows anxious. Dust storm passes through at midnight—abrasive, choking, no visibility.

choices.label: Send James and Mary north NOW to scout water. Leave Sarah to tend Thomas. Risk is acceptable.

personality_effects.Sarah: abandonment_resentment
personality_effects.Thomas: fever_dreams

consequences.note: You find water. Good water. But Pawnee scouts shadow you the last two miles—they don't attack, just watch. When you return, Sarah's eyes are hard. Thomas has been hallucinating. He called for his dead mother. Sarah didn't stop him.

personality_effects.James: doubt
personality_effects.Mary: medical_helplessness
personality_effects.Sarah: duty_weighing
personality_effects.Thomas: fever_distance

medium.journal_entry: July 10th. The heat has teeth. Thomas burns from the inside. We are five days from Fort Laramie if Thomas can survive it. If he cannot, we are still five days from Fort Laramie, and one person shorter. I do not know which outcome is worse. The oxen are failing. The dust here tastes like iron and bones. Last night I dreamed William was alive, asking for water. When I woke, I was asking for it too.

medium.title: The Sickness in Mary's Eyes
medium.description: Mary keeps pace this morning but her movements are wrong—too deliberate, like she's thinking through each step. At noon, you notice her staring at the treeline for minutes without blinking. When you ask if she's well, she says the air tastes like copper and that she can hear singing from the rocks. Thomas whispers he saw her drinking from a snowmelt stream yesterday, upstream from where the oxen watered. The afternoon storm is building. You have maybe four hours before it hits hard.

choices.label: Push harder to reach lower elevation before dark. Mary rides; others walk.

consequences.note: The strain helps Mary stay conscious but exhaustion hits the rest of you. Storm catches you at 8,000 feet. You find a rock shelter, cramped and cold. Mary's fever breaks by morning but she remembers nothing clearly.

choices.label: Make camp now in the lee of boulders. Boil all water. Ration medicine to manage her fever.

consequences.note: You catch the sickness early. Mary recovers within two days. The delay costs miles but saves her mind. Thomas respects the choice—he was terrified she was dying.

choices.label: Leave her at this camp with two days' rations and the strongest oxen. Return in five days with help from Fort Laramie.

consequences.note: She will likely live or die in that camp alone. If she survives, she will not forgive you. If she doesn't, you'll carry it all the way to Oregon. The party fractures. James's leadership is questioned.

personality_effects.default: Mary's illness forces a reckoning. James must choose between pragmatism and loyalty. There are no good answers.

medium.journal_entry: August 1st. Mary sick with something caught from bad water. The mountains don't forgive carelessness. Thomas looks at me like I'm supposed to know what to do. I don't. The storm is coming and we're high enough to die from it. Her eyes are wrong. Everything about this is wrong.

medium.title: The Thermometer Reading
medium.description: Mary's fever broke in the night, but she won't wake properly. Her skin is cool now—too cool. You find the thermometer in the medical kit. It reads 96 degrees. She shivers under two blankets in the August morning while the others eat cold biscuits and wait for your word on whether to move or stay. Thomas keeps glancing at the peaks above, where clouds are gathering dark and low. The oxen are nervous. You have maybe six hours of decent light before the storm hits.

choices.label: Push on toward the pass. Mountain storms pass quick. Mary needs to get lower, warmer.

consequences.notes: The storm caught you on the ridge. One ox went lame; you left it behind. Mary convulsed twice during the afternoon. The cold made it worse before it got better. Thomas nearly turned back.

choices.label: Stay put. Build up the fire. Keep Mary warm and still. Ride out the weather.

consequences.notes: The storm was brutal. You burned wood meant for trade at Fort Hall. Mary stabilized by evening. The delay tightens your margin for snow in the high passes.

choices.label: Send Thomas down to the valley settlement you passed yesterday. Get a doctor's opinion. You keep moving with Mary.

personality_effects.Thomas: splitting_party

consequences.notes: Thomas left before dawn. You won't know if he found help or just kept going. Mary got worse, then better. The not-knowing is its own kind of fever.

personality_effects.James: fatigue_mounting

medium.journal_entry: Mary's skin was cold. That was worse than the fever. Thomas wanted to know if we should stop. I didn't answer him right away. The mountains don't care what you decide—they'll take what they want either way. The thermometer read 96 degrees and I thought about how she was fine yesterday and how the women always get sick out here and how nobody talks about why. I didn't write that down at the time.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description: The ox that drank from the alkali pool three days back is failing. Its eyes are dull, breathing labored. The other three oxen are sound, but you need four to manage the wagon through this canyon terrain. Nights are dropping below freezing. The animal will not last another week—maybe not another three days. You have perhaps six pounds of decent feed remaining. One of your party suggests mercy. Another suggests pushing hard for the next settlement, twenty miles distant, hoping the ox holds. A third option: leave the wagon, cache the supplies, and walk out with what you can carry on the remaining three oxen.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Use the meat to stretch your food supplies.
Push for the next settlement hard. The ox might survive if you make good time.
Cache the wagon and non-essential supplies. Travel light with three oxen.

personality_effects.James: Leader bears the choice. All weight settles on him. Mary watches his hands when he decides.
personality_effects.Mary: Already hollow-eyed from losses. Whatever James chooses, she will execute without argument or mercy.

medium.journal_entry: The ox stands with its head down. I can hear it breathing—a sound like wind through a cracked door. Three oxen left. Three oxen and a woman who has watched her husband, two children, and a brother die on this trail. She is waiting for me to decide what we are now.

medium.title: The Mercy Question
medium.description: Mary finds one of the oxen has gone lame—swollen foreleg, can barely walk. The rain hasn't helped. She comes to you at dusk, mud on her skirts, and says straight: We can shoot it now, butcher what we can carry. Or we can try to nurse it through another day, see if the swelling goes down. But that means slower going, and we're already burning daylight. James, we're three people and three oxen left. We lose one, we lose a wagon. You know that.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Butcher it while there's light.

consequences.note: Meat is heavy but keeps. The sound of the shot spooked the remaining oxen. You'll move slower tomorrow anyway, breaking them.

choices.label: Rest it one day. Hope the swelling breaks.

consequences.note: You poultice the leg with what medicine you have left. Rain turns to sleet by midnight. By morning the ox is worse. You'll have to shoot it anyway, but now you've burned a day's food and medicine for nothing. Mary won't say it, but you can see it in her face.

choices.label: Leave it behind. Strip what gear you can and press on with two oxen.

consequences.note: The ox follows for a mile, then stops. You don't look back. Mary cries quietly that night—not from weakness, but from rage at the waste. James loses something too, watching her. The remaining oxen strain under redistributed weight. One starts favoring its hip.

personality_effects.Mary: She's the one who caught the problem. Whatever you choose, she'll execute it without hesitation. But her morale reflects whether she agrees it was necessary.

medium.journal_entry: October 10th. Rain all day. Found the ox lame this evening—God's cruelty or bad luck, makes no difference now. We are three oxen, three people, one wagon short of what we need. The forest smells like rot and wood smoke. Thomas would have known what to do without asking. Mary knows too. That's almost worse.

medium.title: The Pawnee Scout
medium.description: A lone Pawnee scout approaches your camp at dusk, riding hard from the west. He's bleeding from a gash above his eye. He speaks broken English and gestures urgently toward the horizon — says there are Lakota war parties moving north, three days behind him, angry about settlers killing buffalo near the Platte. He wants tobacco and asks if you have whiskey. James recognizes the genuine panic in his face. The scout won't stay long. He needs to ride on.

choices.label: Give him tobacco, whiskey, and dried meat. Get information about the route ahead and Lakota movements.

consequences.description: The scout drinks fast, eats faster. His hands shake. He tells you the Lakota are moving toward the Republican River crossing — three, maybe four days north. You have the advantage if you push east toward the Chimney Rock route instead. Says the war parties are looking for revenge, not settlers specifically. He rode for two days straight to warn whoever he could. When he leaves, Sarah watches him disappear into the dark. She says nothing, but her jaw is tight. James feels the weight of knowing something bad is coming.

choices.label: Turn him away. You cannot afford to help everyone. Keep moving and hope the war parties stay south.

consequences.description: The scout stares at James for a long moment — not angry, just disappointed in a way that cuts deeper. He rides west without another word. That night, nobody sleeps well. Mary says quietly that they turned away a man who was trying to save lives, and now they don't even know if they're riding toward danger. Thomas admits he's seen Lakota warriors before, years back — says they move like wolves. Sarah won't speak to James at all. The wind sounds wrong all night.

choices.label: Give him supplies, but ask him to stay the night and scout ahead for you at first light.

consequences.description: The scout hesitates. You can see him weighing survival against obligation. He agrees, but wakes before dawn and is gone before anyone can stop him. He took the food, the ammo, and left a knife stuck in the earth pointing northeast. William says that means something — good luck, maybe, or a direction. James realizes the scout was never going to stay. He just needed the supplies to survive what comes next. Nobody knows if this helps or if they've been played for fools. The uncertainty tastes like metal.

medium.journal_entry: A Pawnee rode in bloody and afraid. Said the Lakota are hunting. The trail just became a choice between three different kinds of regret. The mosquitoes are still here. Independence Rock is already behind us. Nothing stays still on this road.

medium.title: The Pawnee Camp
medium.description: You crest a rise and see smoke to the north—a Pawnee encampment perhaps a mile off the trail. Maybe thirty lodges. A hunting party, by the look of the horses. They haven't approached, but they've seen your wagons. Thomas says they're peaceful this time of year. Mary says the Pawnee trade fairly. William is checking his rifle. Sarah's hands are shaking. You have perhaps an hour before they decide whether to come to you.

choices.label: Ride out with gifts—tobacco, cloth—and trade for fresh meat and information about the trail ahead.
Circle the wagons, post watch, and press on. Show strength—don't stop.
Send Sarah with a white flag to negotiate alone. A woman seems less threatening.

William.note: His hand rests on his rifle. He's lost kin to the Lakota in Missouri. The Pawnee aren't Lakota, but fear doesn't parse tribe from tribe.

Sarah.note: Still recovering from dysentery two weeks back. The sight of the lodges makes her pale—she's heard stories. Most of them exaggerated.

medium.journal_entry: May 1st. The plains roll on without mercy or favor. We saw our first peoples since Fort Kearny. They didn't attack. They didn't run. They watched us the way we watch buffalo—assessing whether we're worth the trouble. The moment passed. It will come again.

medium.title: The Pawnee Scout
medium.description: A single Pawnee scout on a lean pony approaches your camp at dusk. He's unarmed, hands visible, but his horse is lathered and he rides with urgency. He speaks broken English mixed with hand signs. Behind him, no war party—just the empty plain. He points north, then west, then makes a throat-cutting gesture. When James steps forward, the scout says: 'Cholera. Two days ahead. Crossing place. Many wagons. Many dead.' He pauses. 'Alternate trail. North fork. Longer. No water marked. Pawnee know it.' He doesn't ask for payment. He's waiting.

choices.label: Trust him. Take the northern fork trail he describes.

consequences.reasoning: The detour costs time and supplies. Mary's fever may worsen without reliable water. But you avoid cholera—a disease that kills whole wagons in hours. The scout's warning feels genuine; his urgency wasn't performative.

choices.label: Follow the main crossing. Cholera kills fast but the route is known.

consequences.reasoning: Speed matters when disease is ahead. You have four doses of medicine left. You might reach the crossing before the worst of it, or pass through quickly enough to avoid infection. Mary's fever puts the group at higher risk. William is already weakened. This gamble assumes discipline at the crossing—no shared water, no lingering.

choices.label: Give the scout food and supplies. Ask him to guide you north.

consequences.reasoning: The scout becomes your guide. He knows water sources you won't find. He knows the land. But you're depleting food fast—120 pounds is already lean—and you're paying in ammunition and cash for insurance. The party sees James trusting an outsider. Some approve. Some will resent the loss when hunger comes later.

choices.label: Turn him away. Tell him you're armed and prepared.

consequences.reasoning: The scout nods, turns his pony, and rides back into the dusk without another word. The party feels the weight of what you refused—a gift, a warning, a choice. Sarah thinks of her children. William watches him go and doesn't speak for hours. You proceed forward with your knowledge, which is incomplete. The scout's silence afterward feels heavier than his words.

personality_effects.note: Effects applied based on chosen option. See consequences above.

medium.journal_entry: June 1st. A Pawnee came to us with a warning about cholera ahead. His horse was worn hard. He didn't want anything from us at first—just wanted to talk. I wonder if he has family on the main trail. I wonder if he's tired of burying white people. The mosquitoes got worse after he left, or maybe I just noticed them more. Mary slept fitful. The coffee was bad. Tomorrow we choose which way the trail goes.

medium.title: The Fever Breaks Wrong
medium.description:
Mary's cholera has burned through her for six days. This morning she woke lucid for the first time—her skin cool, her eyes clear. She asked for water and broth. Everyone felt the relief like a physical thing.

But by noon, she was burning again. Hotter. She's speaking in fragments now, calling out names of people James knows she's never met. Her lips have gone white at the edges.

Thomas found you alone checking the oxen. "The fever's breaking the other direction," he said quietly. "You've seen it before."

You have. Once, in Independence. A woman who seemed to recover, then went rigid and didn't stop screaming until she didn't anymore.

You have four doses of quinine left. Mary's taken two already. You could give her another—might buy time, might do nothing. You could save the medicine for the rest of the party. Or you could let her rest and see if the fever turns again on its own.

The storm clouds are building dark and mean in the west. You'll need to move soon or camp here another night in wet ground. Either way, Mary will have to ride.

choices.label: Give her the third dose of quinine. The fever might break. It might not, but you don't stop fighting.
Don't give the quinine. Keep moving today, let her rest in the wagon. The medicine stays for those still whole.
Tell Mary the truth about what you've seen. Let her choose whether to take it.
Leave her here with two days' food and the spare canvas. Send Thomas back with a horse after you reach the fort. Better one person's grief now than five people's death on the trail.

personality_effects.James: Each choice reveals what you believe you owe the dying. None of it will feel like enough.
personality_effects.Mary: Fever dreams don't distinguish between mercy and abandonment.
personality_effects.Thomas: He'll know what you decided, and remember it when his own turn comes.

medium.journal_entry:
June 15, 1848 — River Valley, Great Plains

Mary's fever came back meaner than it left. She spoke names we didn't know. The quinine sits in its bottle like a question with no good answer. We have four miles before the river forks and better ground. We have a woman who might live or might not, and a storm that won't wait for either outcome.

Thomas said: 'You've seen it before.' He meant: you know how this ends.

I told him I hadn't decided yet. That was a lie. The decision was made the moment she stopped screaming.

medium.title: The Meat Question
medium.description: That night, as you butcher Thomas's deer by firelight, Mary notices the meat is going gray at the edges—spoiling faster than it should in this thin mountain air. You have perhaps two days before it's inedible. Thomas wants to smoke it over a slow fire, which means staying put for three days. James (you) knows staying costs time and fuel. The oxen are already worn. Mary suggests salting what you can and abandoning the rest. William would have pushed on and wasted nothing, but William's in a shallow grave two weeks back.

choices.label: Stay three days. Smoke the meat properly. Rest the oxen.
Salt what keeps. Push on at dawn with 20 lbs. Waste the rest.
Smoke half. Pack the other half raw in cloth, wrapped in snow from the peaks. A gamble.

personality_effects.Thomas: Trusts your judgment. Wants the meat kept. Spent an hour stalking that deer.
personality_effects.Mary: Pragmatic. Wants to move. Every day in the mountains is a day closer to winter.

medium.journal_entry: Got meat. Got spoilage. Got the kind of choice that kills parties—not quickly, but slow. Thomas shot clean. Mary's right about the time. I'm the one who has to live with what rots, whether it's the venison or something else.

medium.title: The Ravine
medium.description:
You're descending a narrow pass when Thomas spots it—a ravine maybe thirty feet down, choked with white water from last night's storm. The trail switchbacks around it, but a shortcut cuts straight across on a natural stone bridge, maybe eight feet wide. No railings. No margin.

Mary's fever has her delirious. She's been riding the last two hours barely conscious. The longer route adds four miles and another day in these mountains. The weather's turning. You can smell it.

Thomas dismounts and tests the bridge with a stick. The stone looks solid, but water spray has made it slick. One ox might slip. One person might fall.

choices.label: Take the bridge. Move fast, careful. Save the day.
Go the long way. Four more miles. Mary gets another day to recover.
Leave Mary here with supplies. She'll slow you down. You'll move faster without her.

personality_effects.Mary: abandoned

personality_effects.Thomas: witnesses_leadership

medium.journal_entry: August 15. A stone bridge over a gulf. Mary burning with fever, seeing things that aren't there. Time is a knife edge. Snow coming down from the peaks like it's August in hell. We could move faster. We could save her. We probably can't do both.

medium.title: The Bones in the Canyon
medium.description: You find a skeleton arranged deliberately beside the trail—bones stacked with care, a faded cloth tied around the skull. No grave markers. No names. Mary thinks it's a warning. James notices the skeleton's arms are positioned pointing west, down the canyon. The dust here smells wrong: copper and something older. That night, both you and James wake simultaneously at 3 AM, neither able to say why. Mary claims she heard singing—not voices, but something without a mouth making music.

choices.label: Leave the bones undisturbed. Push on at dawn without investigation.

consequences.narrative: Mary won't stop looking back. She mutters that the dead were telling you something and you refused to listen. By midday her pace slows. The singing, she says, followed you for an hour after you left. James says nothing. The canyon narrows ahead.

choices.label: Dig the bones deeper, bury them properly with what words you know.

consequences.narrative: The digging takes longer than expected—the earth is hard, and neither of you wants to fracture the bones. Mary speaks a prayer her grandmother taught her. When you cover the grave, the wind dies completely. It stays dead calm for six hours. No sound but the oxen breathing. Mary sleeps without waking that night.

choices.label: Strip the cloth and any metal from the bones. Use them for your own need.

consequences.narrative: The cloth tears to dust in your hands. There's no metal—nothing of value. Mary refuses to eat that evening. She sits away from the fire, staring at you. James avoids your eyes. The singing comes every night after. Mary swears it's closer. The canyon walls seem to lean inward when you sleep.

personality_effects.Mary: She is watching you. Three dead in your party, and the living grow thin on their own doubt.
personality_effects.James: The weight of leadership has become a visible thing. His jaw works constantly, like he's chewing words he won't speak.

medium.journal_entry: Sept 10. Found a dead man arranged like a compass. The arrangement was too clean for accident. Mary heard music nobody else could hear. I heard it too, though I haven't told her. Or maybe I heard her hearing it and invented the rest. The difference has stopped mattering. Thomas, Sarah, William are behind us now. The living have become the real ghosts—we're just the ones who haven't stopped moving yet. The canyon smells like a grave.

medium.title: The Drowned Oxen
medium.description: A creek swollen from three days of rain blocks your path. The ford looks passable, but the current runs hard and the water is the color of old iron. Your lead ox—the strongest—stumbles on a submerged rock halfway across. It goes down. The animal thrashes, bellowing. Mary shouts from the bank. James, you're closest. The ox's head slips under. Its ribs heave once, twice. Then stillness. The carcass wedges against a deadfall downstream. You have two oxen left. The rain doesn't stop.

choices.label: Wade in and salvage what meat you can. The animal is fresh.

consequences.journal_note: Stripped the ox in the rain. Meat is good. But we are fewer now. Mary wouldn't look at me while I worked.

choices.label: Leave it. Find another ford upstream—lose the day but keep moving.

consequences.journal_note: Walked the creek for two hours in cold. Found a shallow crossing. The ox is gone. So is Mary's faith that we'll make it.

choices.label: Butcher the remaining two oxen now. Convert them to food. Walk the last 200 miles on foot.

consequences.journal_note: We killed them ourselves rather than let the creek have another. Two oxen into smoke and salt. James's hands shook. Mary said nothing. We walk now.

James.note: Another creature dead because you couldn't keep it alive. The weight of this accumulates.

Mary.note: She is watching you fail. She is calculating whether you can get her to Oregon. The answer is unclear.

medium.journal_entry: October 10th. The oxen are dying in the rain. So far we have buried three people and lost two beasts to this country's indifference. The creek took the fourth ox while we stood and watched it happen. Mary's face was a closed door. I don't know what we're walking toward anymore. I only know what we're walking away from, and it's getting farther back every day.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox and the Pawnee Riders
medium.description: The ox that drank from the alkali pool this morning is moving slower, favoring its rear leg. By midday, you see dust on the horizon—three Pawnee riders approaching from the northwest. They're not moving to intercept, just... watching. The sluggish ox will need rest or doctoring. The riders will reach you in an hour.

choices.label: Push the pace hard now, leave the damaged ox behind if needed. Outrun the riders before they reach you.

consequences.journal_note: Rode hard like cowards. Lost an ox. Made good miles but Sarah's cough got worse from the dust.

choices.label: Halt and tend the ox with your medicine. Stand your ground. Show the riders you're not afraid.

consequences.journal_note: Used laudanum on the ox's leg. Pawnee riders passed by without stopping. One nodded. William thinks we should've run.

choices.label: Send Thomas ahead with a white cloth to parley. Find out what they want before deciding.

consequences.journal_note: Thomas traded 50 cents worth of salt pork for information: a Lakota war party is two days behind us, moving faster. The Pawnee don't want trouble. Thomas looked shaken when he returned.

personality_effects.James: Cautious leadership tested. Will respect bold stands or ruthless efficiency.
personality_effects.Mary: Compassionate—prefers not abandoning animals or people.
personality_effects.Thomas: Brave but learning caution. Parley appeals to his dignity.
personality_effects.Sarah: Already frail. Hard pace will worsen her condition.
personality_effects.William: Nervous. Wants speed and distance. Sees riders as threat first.

medium.journal_entry: April 20, 1848. The alkali poison is in the ox's blood. We are 50 miles from Independence, and riders watch us from the grassland. The land here is big in a way that makes your choices feel very small. Sarah asked if the riders meant us harm. I didn't have an answer that wasn't a lie.

medium.title: The Burial on the Flats
medium.description: You find a grave marker—fresh, maybe two weeks old. A wooden cross, no name. Beside it, a child's shoe, worn through at the heel. The earth is torn as if something dug here recently. Coyote, probably. Or worse. Mary kneels and says a prayer. Thomas notices the grave is oriented east-west, not Christian. William suggests moving camp downwind. Sarah sits on a rock, staring at nothing.

choices.label: Rebury the remains properly, Christian fashion. It'll take an hour.
Leave it. Not your grave, not your problem. Push hard for 15 miles before dark.
Take the shoe. Maybe someone ahead is looking for it. It's evidence they came this way.

personality_effects.Mary: Believes in Christian burial rites. Sees unmarked graves as tragedy.
personality_effects.Thomas: Practical but superstitious. Respects the dead out of habit and fear.
personality_effects.Sarah: Already worn thin. The shoe disturbs her more than it should.
personality_effects.William: Skeptical of sentiment. Wants to keep moving.
personality_effects.James: Leader's burden. No good choice here.

medium.journal_entry: Found a grave today with a child's shoe still in it. The coyotes had been at the body. No way to know who—Pawnee, settler, or someone caught between. The cross wasn't Christian, and that detail meant something different to each of us. We argued without using voices, the way families do when they're too tired for actual words. The shoe is still in my hand as I write this. It's small enough to fit in a child's pocket. I don't know why I kept it.

medium.title: The Sick Child
medium.description: Sarah wakes fevered and delirious. Her skin is hot to the touch, her eyes unfocused. Mary believes it's the mosquito sickness—the malaria or dengue that kills slow. Thomas suggests we've seen this before; it passes in a few days if the body can bear it. William, always practical, notes we're three weeks from Fort Laramie. James watches Sarah's lips crack and bleed. The afternoon heat is building. Someone will need to stay behind with her while the wagon moves on, or we slow the whole party and risk running short of supplies before the mountains. Sarah keeps asking for water. There's enough for now.

choices.label: Push on at steady pace. Sarah rides in the wagon, minimal exertion. Ration her extra water and use two of the four medicines.
Make camp here for two days. Let Sarah rest. Use all four medicines aggressively and see if the fever breaks. Delay everything.
Leave Thomas with Sarah and supplies. He drives the wagon at half pace. Rest of us ride ahead with the oxen to make Fort Laramie faster. Reunite in one week.
Sarah is slowing us down. Leave her in the care of a passing trapper's camp two miles upriver. Mary stays with her. You and the others move on.

personality_effects.base: William grows quiet after Sarah's sickness. Mary insists on staying close. Thomas says nothing but his jaw tightens.

medium.journal_entry: June 1st, river valley near Independence Rock. The mosquitoes left us gifts. Sarah is burning up. The fever came fast—too fast. Thomas has seen people walk away from it. Mary has seen people die from it. There is no good choice here, only the choice that lets you sleep at night, and I am no longer certain any of them do. The heat is relentless. The river runs brown and slow. We are 450 miles from Independence and still have the mountains ahead. Sarah keeps asking for water. There is always water.

medium.title: The Scout's Find
medium.description:
Thomas returns from scouting ahead, moving faster than usual. He's found fresh horse tracks—unshod, heading northwest into the high passes. More concerning: a canvas bundle wedged between rocks, partially buried. Inside, a woman's dress. A child's shoe. A ledger with names in fading ink, dated 1846. No bodies. The dress smells of sage and old blood.

Thomas says the tracks are recent—maybe a week. Mary thinks it's a grave robbery or worse. James notices the shoe is the right size for a child of four or five. The ledger lists 23 names. Only five are crossed through.

The high passes ahead will cut two weeks off the journey. The lower valley route adds fifteen days but skirts settlements. You have enough provisions for either—barely.

choices.label: Follow the tracks. Find out who left the bundle and why.

consequences.notes: You lose the main trail for four days. Find a Shoshone camp abandoned in haste—evidence of recent sickness, possibly smallpox. The shoe belonged to a child who didn't survive. You learn nothing useful. Return to the trail with the knowledge that death was here, recently, and you were hours behind it. James gains grim certainty; Mary's faith cracks further.

choices.label: Bury the bundle properly. Take the high passes and move on.

consequences.notes: You spend half a day giving the dead proper Christian burial—all of them, as best you can with the names from the ledger. It feels like the right thing. The high passes have early snow. One ox goes lame. You push through anyway. The morale boost fades when Mary realizes you spent money you needed on a funeral for strangers.

choices.label: Take the ledger and the shoe. Leave the rest. Take the valley route.

consequences.notes: Practical. Cowardly. Thomas wanted to follow the tracks; you're choosing safety over answers. The valley route is slower but gentler on the oxen. You keep the ledger. At night, James reads the crossed-out names to himself. The shoe sits in the wagon like an accusation. You'll never know who left it or why. Some of the party will resent that.

personality_effects.James: Leader's burden deepens. Death follows this trail like a wolf pack. He begins to see the wagon less as a conveyance and more as a hearse.
personality_effects.Mary: Her prayers feel thinner each day. She wants to believe there's providence in survival. The bundle makes that harder.
personality_effects.Thomas: Scout's instinct says follow. Every choice that ignores his instinct plants a seed of resentment.

medium.journal_entry: August 1st, 1848. Found sign of the dead today. Not our dead—someone else's. The mountains don't bury their secrets well. The shoe was small enough for a child. I keep wondering if we passed them on the trail, if we could have helped. Probably not. Probably they were already gone by the time we started this journey. Still. The not knowing sits in the chest like a stone.

medium.title: The Fouled Water
medium.description:
By midday, Thomas complains of stomach cramps. By evening, he's vomiting. Mary finds the source: the flour you salvaged from the creek crossing three days ago has begun to ferment and smell of something organic and wrong. The bag is half-consumed. Thomas drank water used to make biscuits from it yesterday. James finds Mary standing over the remaining flour, her jaw tight.

"We can boil it," she says quietly. "Kill whatever's growing in it. The heat might make it safe."

But boiling uses fuel and water—both scarce here. And Thomas is already sick. The question isn't whether the flour is bad. The question is whether you feed the others with it anyway.

choices.label: Burn the flour. Bury what's left. Tighten rations further.
Boil the flour in batches. Use fuel and water to make it edible. Let Thomas rest.
Feed the flour only to the oxen. Tell the party it's gone. Eat what game you can trap.

personality_effects.Mary: watching you. waiting to see what kind of leader you are when no one's watching.
personality_effects.Thomas: fever-weak and afraid. whatever you choose, he'll remember it.
personality_effects.James: himself. your own thoughts are the loudest voice in your head.

medium.journal_entry: The flour is poison now. Three days since the creek and it's turned against us like everything else on this trail. Thomas heaves into the dust. Mary doesn't say I told you so but I hear it anyway. We've lost two already. I won't lose a third to flour and my own negligence. But charity out here is a luxury we may not afford. Every pound of food is a day of life. Every day is a mile closer or further from the end. There's no right choice. Only the choice you can live with.

medium.title: The Skeleton at the Water
medium.description:
At a bend in the Snake River canyon, you find a corpse half-submerged in the shallows. Male, maybe forty. His clothes are rotted through—could be weeks old, could be months. A Colt revolver lies in the dust nearby, rusted but intact. The skull shows no obvious wound, but the position suggests he crawled here to drink and didn't leave. Mary points to his pack, caught between rocks. Inside: tobacco, a journal with entries dating to July, and what looks like a map marked with small crosses—locations or graves, you can't tell. No food. No water container. The journal entries describe fever and the loss of companions. His last words: "No one comes this way anymore."

James, you need to decide whether this man dies alone, or whether he gets buried. The digging would cost time and energy. The oxen are already thin. But leaving him here—for the birds, for the coyotes—that's another calculation. And that map. The crosses might mean danger. They might mean something else.

choices.label: Bury him properly. Say words. Take nothing from the pack.

consequences.journal_note: We gave a stranger the dignity we might hope for ourselves. The oxen are weaker for it. I wonder if that was courage or indulgence.

choices.label: Take the gun, the tobacco, the map. Leave the body. You've lost too much already.

consequences.journal_note: We took what we could carry and left him to the earth. Mary won't speak to me. The map shows seven crosses between here and the Willamette Valley. I don't know what they mean and I wish I didn't.

choices.label: Take only the map and the gun. Roll the body into the river—let it go to the Pacific, not rot here.

consequences.journal_note: A compromise that satisfied no one. James was too tired to argue. The gun is good weight in my hands. The map feels like a curse. Tonight the wind carried a sound like a man calling for water. Just the canyon. Has to be.

personality_effects.James: Pragmatism fraying at the edges. Every choice leaves a mark.
personality_effects.Mary: Watching her husband's choices more than the trail now. Trust is a supply that doesn't replenish.

medium.journal_entry: September 10th. Found what the trail leaves behind when it's finished with you. The passes are close. Snow coming. We are three people and four oxen and I have begun to forget what my sister's voice sounded like. The man in the canyon knew the answer to a question I haven't asked yet. The map won't tell me what it is.

medium.title: The Burned Camp
medium.description:
You find the remains of a camp in a small clearing—blackened fire rings, scattered bones, and the charred frame of a wagon. The bones are human. Small bones too. No bodies remain, just ash and the smell of old smoke. The fire was weeks old, maybe longer. There are no signs of struggle, no weapons, no indication of who did this or why. Mary thinks it was Indians. James, you know better—you've seen what starvation and fever do to a group.

Then William spots something: a leather journal, soaked but legible. The last entry, dated six weeks prior, reads only: "Gone to find water. Do not follow." The handwriting deteriorates midway through the sentence.

You have three oxen left. The forest is dense. Winter is coming hard.

choices.label: Read the full journal. Make camp here and study it for clues about what happened.
Bury the bones. Say words. Move on before dark. Don't read it.

personality_effects.Mary: mor: +2
personality_effects.James: mor: -1

choices.label: Take the journal but don't read it. Strip the camp of anything useful—nails, metal, cloth scraps.

personality_effects.Mary: mor: -3
Increasingly convinced the Shoshone killed this group. Wants armed watches.
personality_effects.James: Recognizes the signature of starvation and desperation. Wonders if the journal explains the 'gone to find water' line.

medium.journal_entry: Found a burned camp today. Small bones in the ash. Mary says Indians. I think we found a family that gave up. The forest here feels like a grave. We're 200 miles from the Willamette Valley. The oxen are thin. My rations calculation shows 18 days of meager food left if we don't hunt. The journal sits in my pack. I haven't opened it. Sometimes ignorance keeps a man moving forward. Sometimes it's cowardice. I can't tell the difference anymore.

medium.title: The Sick Ox
medium.description: William spots one of the oxen limping badly—its left rear leg swollen and hot to the touch. The animal can barely keep pace. Mary examines it with what knowledge she has from farm work. She says the leg is infected, probably from a cut during the river crossing three days back. The infection is spreading. Without treatment, the ox will be dead in a week. With treatment—poultices, rest, careful doctoring—it might recover in five days. You have five oxen pulling weight now. Lose one, and the remaining animals work harder, faster, angrier. Push through without stopping, and you lose time you can't get back. The choice sits with you.

choices.label: Stop for five days. Mary tends the ox with medicine and poultices. The herd rests.

consequences.note: The ox recovers fully. Oxen health restored. But five days of rations consumed, and the trail ahead grows longer with each mile not traveled.

choices.label: Keep moving. Dose the ox with laudanum each night to dull the pain. Push harder.

consequences.note: The ox dies on the fourth day, its hooves bloody, its breath labored. You press on with five oxen doing the work of six. The remaining animals grow lean and sharp-tempered.

choices.label: Shoot the ox now. Butcher it for meat. One less mouth to feed, one more problem solved.

consequences.note: The meat is good. The weight of the act is heavier. Sarah won't look at you. Mary speaks only when necessary. The Pawnee tracks you saw yesterday seem closer somehow.

personality_effects.Mary: Practical. Wants to save the animal, but understands hard choices.
personality_effects.Sarah: Sentimental. Sees the ox as a living thing, not a tool.
personality_effects.William: Weary. Doesn't care much either way, but grateful for rest.
personality_effects.Thomas: Pragmatic. Follows James's lead without question.
personality_effects.James: Tasked with deciding. Whatever choice he makes, someone will resent him for it.

medium.journal_entry: April 20. The rains came and went. Found the berries Sarah pointed out—sweet, small, gone in an hour. But oxen don't eat berries. The sick one stands in the corral like it's thinking about something far away. Mary says infection. I say choice. Either way, something dies on this prairie. Either the ox, or a piece of us. Maybe both.

medium.title: The Cough in the Dark
medium.description: Three days past the Henderson grave, William wakes the camp at midnight, hacking. Not the ordinary trail cough—wet, sustained, the kind that doesn't stop. By dawn his shirt is flecked with blood. He claims it's nothing. Mary thinks otherwise. The sky is darkening in the west. You have perhaps six hours before weather hits, and William's condition will worsen either way.

choices.label: Push hard for the next settlement. Twenty miles today if the weather holds.
Make camp early. Rest, boil water, use your medicine to manage the cough.
William says he can ride. Leave him in the wagon bed and move at half pace. The rest of you walk.

personality_effects.Mary: If you rest: her confidence in leadership grows slightly. If you push or compromise: she begins keeping her own counsel about supplies and health.
personality_effects.Thomas: The blood troubles him more than William's cough. He'll work harder in camp if you stop, but resentment builds if you keep moving.
personality_effects.Sarah: Her feet are already failing. Pushing exhausts her further. Resting gives her respite, but she worries aloud about falling behind.

medium.journal_entry: William's blood on white cloth this morning. The Pawnee said the air turns foul before the big rains. I believe them now. Every choice costs something we don't have.

medium.title: The Buffalo Stand
medium.description: At dawn, a dust cloud rises from the west—a herd of buffalo, thousands strong, moving northeast across your line of travel. The animals are maybe two miles out, but the earth trembles with their passage. William spots them first and wakes the camp. Your scouts report the herd will cross your path in roughly four hours. There's no going around; the prairie is flat for fifty miles in every direction. You could wait for them to pass, push hard now to cross ahead, try to hunt some for fresh meat, or—as one of the scouts quietly suggests—attempt to drive them north with fire and noise to clear a path. Mary is still fevered and weak. The oxen are restless.

choices.label: Wait it out. Make a strong camp and let the herd pass. It's safer.
Push hard now. Race ahead before the herd arrives. Rough pace, no rest.
Hunt them. Send William and Thomas out with rifles. Fresh meat for weeks.

consequences.health_risk: William or Thomas could be trampled. Buffalo are unpredictable.

choices.label: Light fires downwind. Drive the herd north. Risky, but it clears the path.

consequences.health_risk: Fire could spread. Smoke could choke Mary's fever lungs. The herd could turn south instead.

personality_effects.note: Mary's fever makes her vulnerable to exertion and smoke. William's restlessness about danger might push him toward hunting. Thomas is steady but tired. Sarah is increasingly cautious. James must decide who pays the cost.

medium.journal_entry: May 1st. The earth itself seems to move beneath us. Mary sweats through her blankets even in the cool morning. The scouts say the buffalo come like God's judgment, and they're right to be afraid. I can feel the choice settling in my chest like a stone. Every path forward takes something. Every wait costs time we don't have. The fever doesn't care which direction we choose.

medium.title: The Pawnee Camp
medium.description: Scout reports a Pawnee encampment a half-mile north — maybe thirty people, horses, smoke. They've seen your dust. A rider approaches slowly, hand visible, unarmed. Behind him, women and children pause their work. Behind you, William's grave is three days back. Your oxen are lean. Your water barrel still leaks.

choices.label: Invite the rider to parley. Trade flour and tobacco for information about the trail ahead.

consequences.description: The rider's name is Hard Elk. He tells you the Platte narrows badly fifty miles north — alkaline water, no grass. He also tells you two wagon trains passed through five days ago, pushing hard. One lost a child to cholera before they left his village. Hard Elk seems neither hostile nor friendly, only observant. Mary sits rigid during the exchange. Thomas asks questions about river fords. The flour and tobacco disappear into the encampment. You camp separately that night, and no one sleeps well.

choices.label: Circle the wagons and prepare for defense. Do not engage.

consequences.description: You order the oxen herded close. Everyone on weapons. The rider waits at two hundred yards, then turns back without speaking. The encampment breaks camp within an hour — you see them move northwest, away from the trail. By dark, they're gone. William was a good shot. The reminder sits heavy. That night, Sarah asks quietly whether you were right to refuse trade. No one answers her. The next morning, someone has left three dried fish on a stone fifty paces from camp. No one admits to walking out.

choices.label: Send Thomas to trade alone. You stay with the wagons.

consequences.description: Thomas goes forward with flour and salt pork. He's gone two hours. When he returns, he has six rounds of trade ammunition, information about a shortcut (two days saved but rough country), and a wound on his shoulder — shallow, from a knife hilt, not a blade. He won't explain it clearly. Hard Elk apparently wanted to know if Thomas was worth trading with, or worth testing. Thomas seems uncertain which. The ammunition is good. The shortcut information he shares freely, but his shoulder tells a different story about what happened in that camp.

medium.journal_entry: May 10th. Saw our first people since the grave. Not settlers. Not lost. Just people watching the land they know while we tear through it. There's a calculus happening that I don't understand. William used to say you can read a man by what he doesn't say. The Pawnee rider said nothing. That's what frightens me. We're three hundred miles in. Halfway to something. The water barrel still drips. Sarah keeps asking if we made the right choice at every fork. I don't know anymore what right looks like. Thunderstorms coming.

medium.title: The Sick Woman's Fever
medium.description:
Mary's cholera has worsened. Her skin is gray as old cloth, and she's burning up despite the heat. She keeps asking for water—more water than any person should want. Thomas suggests we stop here by the river for a day, let her rest. But the afternoon sky is darkening fast, and the mosquitoes are worse than you've ever seen them. James, you notice the flour you salvaged from the creek crossing is showing gray mold spots. There's maybe 40 pounds of it left worth eating. Sarah is quiet, watching Mary with the kind of look that means she's thinking about William.

You have maybe two hours of good light before the storm hits.

choices.label: Make camp here. Rest Mary, boil water constantly, use 2 medicine doses to bring fever down.

consequences.journal_note: Mosquitoes came with the storm. Mary still burning. The medicine didn't take.

choices.label: Push on 8 more miles before dark. Mary rides, no walking. Save the medicine—she may need it worse later.

consequences.journal_note: Pushed her too hard. She stopped speaking around mile 504. The others know it.

choices.label: Discard the moldy flour (saves 20 pounds carrying weight). Set up proper camp, use 1 medicine dose, and try to trap some fish or hunt before dark.

consequences.journal_note: Caught three catfish. Mary kept down some broth. Thomas hasn't looked at us like we're fools in weeks.

personality_effects.Mary: fears being left behind; cholera makes her paranoid
personality_effects.Thomas: practical; losing patience with mercy; questions James's leadership
personality_effects.Sarah: haunted by William's death; sees Mary and sees him; withdrawn
personality_effects.James: leader bearing weight of two deaths already; every choice feels like it could be the last

medium.journal_entry: June 15th. The river valley smells like rot and standing water. Mary asked me this morning if I thought William suffered much. I told her no. I was lying. The sky's a bruise now, and we're still five hundred miles from anything that looks like promised land. The oxen are thin. The flour is ruined. And I'm beginning to understand that good decisions might not exist anymore—only the choice of which people we disappoint.

medium.title: The Crossing at Devil's Gate
medium.description:
You find a natural stone bridge spanning a canyon—ancient, narrow, slick with mineral deposits from thermal springs below. The regular ford lies three days north through worse terrain. Your oxen are spooked. Thomas swears he saw movement in the rocks above—a figure that vanished. Mary says it was a bighorn sheep. James knows the animals are right to be afraid of something.

The bridge will shave two days off your journey. It will also kill at least one ox if anything goes wrong. The afternoon light is already failing. A storm is building in the west.

choices.label: Use the bridge. Move fast before dark.

consequences.flavor: The first ox slips halfway across. Its screams echo off the canyon walls for a long time after it falls. The rest of you make it. No one speaks for the rest of the day.

choices.label: Camp here tonight. Attempt the bridge at first light.

consequences.flavor: The storm hits at midnight. You're sheltered enough, but the oxen bolt at lightning strike. You recover three of them by dawn, shaking. Thomas claims he heard singing in the rocks. No one believes him. No one disbelieves him either.

choices.label: Go around. Take the long way north.

consequences.flavor: Safer ground, but the detour burns through your food stores and wastes late summer. Mary suggests you're afraid. She's right, and that makes it worse somehow.

personality_effects.James: Leader's caution is being tested. Every shortcut is a small gamble that adds up.
personality_effects.Mary: Wants to press ahead. Impatient with fear. Getting harder to tell if it's courage or desperation.
personality_effects.Thomas: His senses are sharper than they should be. Or his mind is making things real that aren't there. Increasingly difficult to know which.

medium.journal_entry: August 1st. Found the bridge. The water below is too far down to see clearly through the mist. Thomas says he saw a man watching us from the rocks, dressed like nothing from this time. When I asked him to point, he couldn't remember where. The oxen know something we don't. They always do. In the end, the only real choice is whether you trust your animals or trust your arithmetic. Both have failed us before.

medium.title: The Abandoned Camp
medium.description:
You find a half-buried wagon frame near a cut-bank, canvas rotted to wisps. Three bleached oxen bones arranged in a circle—deliberate, not scattered. A tin cup sits upright on the wagon tongue, untouched by scavengers. Inside the cup: a child's silver button and a note, ink faded nearly illegible. You can make out: '—fever took her—could not—forgive us.' The handwriting degenerates into scratches.

Thomas kneels and touches the button. Mary looks away. James, your remaining ox are uneasy, shifting weight. The camp is maybe two weeks old, maybe three. Whatever happened here, it happened alone.

choices.label: Bury the button and note properly. Say words over it.

consequences.journal_note: We gave her a grave. That's all we could do.

choices.label: Take the button. Leave the note. Keep moving—it's getting late.

consequences.journal_note: The button is small. Maybe we'll find who it belonged to. Probably not.

choices.label: Search the wagon frame for anything useful—medicine, food, anything.

consequences.journal_note: We found hardtack and a bottle of laudanum. We told ourselves we needed it. We didn't think too hard about that.

personality_effects.Thomas: Quiet. Stares at the bones.
personality_effects.Mary: Her hands shake. She asks if the child suffered.
personality_effects.James: Already calculating miles to the next water.

medium.journal_entry: The desert keeps its dead close. We are 1,300 miles from Independence and God is not here—or he stopped looking some time ago. We have food for maybe eight more days if we stretch it. The Pawnee were kind to us. The desert is not.

medium.title: The Oxen Won't Drink
medium.description: At dawn, you find all four oxen bunched at the spring's edge, heads up, refusing the water. The spring smells wrong—sulfurous, mineral-thick. Mary's fever broke in the night, but she's weak and disoriented. The canyon walls press close here. You have maybe ten miles of viable terrain before the rock becomes impassable. Ahead, smoke rises from a ridge-top—cooking fire, or something burning natural gas from the earth. Behind, nothing but the way you came.

choices.label: Force the oxen to drink. You need them moving regardless.

consequences.description: One ox collapses an hour into the march. Its eyes roll back white. You shoot it to spare it further suffering, butcher what meat won't spoil in the meager cold. The others pull harder now, ribs showing. Mary watches you work the knife and says nothing.

choices.label: Investigate the ridge-top smoke. There might be a settlement, or at least cleaner water.

consequences.description: The smoke comes from a Shoshone seasonal camp—three lodges, maybe fifteen people. An old man with burn scars across one arm watches you approach. He trades you five gallons of clean water from a hidden spring higher up for ammunition and two pounds of your salt pork. Mary manages to stand while you negotiate. The Shoshone have seen two other emigrant parties this month. One turned back. The other lost five people to something they won't name. The old man says the canyon ahead is passable but hard. He doesn't say hard how.

choices.label: Circle around the canyon. Head north toward the higher passes—longer but safer.

consequences.description: The passes are steeper than the maps suggested. One ox goes lame crossing scree. Mary deteriorates in the thin air—her fever returns, worse. She hallucinates for stretches, calls out names of people you've never heard her mention. By the time you rejoin the main canyon trail, you've lost nearly two weeks to the detour. The first real snow will come soon.

personality_effects.James: Exhaustion is settling into his bones. He makes decisions faster now, with less doubt. This is sometimes good. Sometimes it isn't.
personality_effects.Mary: The fever changed something in her. She's quieter. She watches James differently—not with trust, but with the calculus of survival. She knows their lives depend on his judgment, and his judgment has been untested in real crisis.

medium.journal_entry: September 10th. The oxen knew something we didn't. They always do. Thomas would have known what to do here—he had a way with animals. Mary's fever broke but left her hollowed out. I keep thinking about the smoke on the ridge and what it means that we can't identify it. In this country, every mystery can kill you. Every answer costs something. We are 1,400 miles from Independence and the math is getting harder.

medium.title: The Shoshone
medium.description: Three riders materialize from the dust at dusk—Shoshone, moving unhurried toward your camp. Their horses are lean but sound. One carries a dead antelope across his saddle. They do not seem hostile, but they do not seem friendly either. The lead rider points at your oxen, then at the antelope. His companions watch Mary, who is too weak to stand. James, you have 54 rounds and three oxen that are worth less with each mile closer to winter.

choices.label: Trade two oxen for the antelope and whatever information they'll give.

consequences.journal_note: They told us water lies northwest, two days' travel. Whether they were truthful or sending us toward something worse, we'll know soon enough.

choices.label: Refuse the trade. Offer tobacco and coffee instead.

consequences.journal_note: They took what we offered without gratitude. Watched us for an hour before leaving. I don't know if we made an enemy or just confirmed we were nothing worth killing.

choices.label: Trade the antelope for the information, but not the oxen. Signal you're armed.

consequences.journal_note: One of them smiled when I chambered a round. He may have been amused or may have been deciding whether to come back tonight. The antelope is old meat, stringy. Mary can eat it. Whether that matters depends on what his smile meant.

medium.journal_entry: The desert gives nothing and takes slowly. Three oxen remain. Mary's eyes are hollow. The dust hasn't settled from the storm, and now strange riders bring meat we didn't ask for. In this country, generosity is its own kind of weapon. I don't know which one they were using.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description: The ox that drank from the alkali pool three days back is moving slower each morning. Its eyes are dull. This morning it refused to pull, and the wagon lurched sideways in the mud. Mary says it's poison working through its blood. Thomas, sitting hollow-eyed on the bench, says we should butcher it now and smoke the meat. James watches the animal labor and says nothing. You have three oxen. You need at least two to reach the valley before winter locks the passes. The rain falls heavier.

choices.label: Push the sick ox hard for two more days—see if it recovers or dies pulling.

consequences.narrative: The ox collapses in mud two miles from camp. It takes an hour to unhitch it. You leave it behind. Thomas won't stop looking back.

choices.label: Butcher it now. Smoke the meat. Lose a day's travel but gain protein.

consequences.narrative: The work is grim. Mary uses the last of your carbolic to keep the meat from spoiling in the damp. The ox yielded less than expected—ribs, haunches, nothing but bone beneath. You camp in its blood-stained clearing.

choices.label: Trade or sell the sick ox to a passing emigrant wagon. Keep moving.

consequences.narrative: A Missouri family with four children stops at noon. They're desperate. You sell them the ox for three dollars. The father thanks you. His youngest daughter has a cough like Sarah's was. You don't tell him that.

personality_effects.James: Leader's burden: any choice fails someone. The weight of it shows in how he grips the reins.
personality_effects.Mary: Practical. She's already thinking about smoking times and salt ratios. Her mind moves to solutions.
personality_effects.Thomas: Desperation in his voice. Two dead. One sick ox. He wants to act—any action feels better than watching.

medium.journal_entry: October 1st. The rain won't stop. Oxen failing. The Willamette Valley is still two hundred miles distant and the passes close in six weeks if the snows come early. We saw Chimney Rock yesterday like a monument to how far we've come and how far we still have to go. Mary says mountains don't care about our schedule. She's right about that.

medium.title: The Flour is Ruined
medium.description:
The creek water soaked through the flour sacks during the ford. What looked like damp this morning is now a gray paste at the bottom of the barrel—thirty pounds of it, maybe more. Mary found it when breaking camp. The rain hasn't stopped. Your oxen are thin. Winter is coming hard through these mountains, and you're down to three people and meager rations.

James, you need to decide what stays and what goes. The flour was supposed to last another month.

choices.label: Scrape what you can into a separate container. Use it first, waste nothing.

consequences.journal_entry: Spent the morning on our hands and knees picking gray paste out of a barrel. Mary didn't complain. Neither did I. We'll choke it down if we have to.

choices.label: Dump the whole barrel. The mold will sicken you worse than starvation.

consequences.journal_entry: Watched thirty pounds of flour hit the mud. Mary looked at me like I'd murdered something. Maybe I did. We'll be on half-rations now, assuming we can hunt. The coyotes won't go hungry tonight.

choices.label: Push on to the next settlement. Sell the oxen if you have to, buy dried meat or hardtack.

consequences.journal_entry: Drove hard through the rain to reach a trading post. The man there looked at our oxen like a wolf eyes a lamb. We got enough jerky to last two weeks, but we're down to two yoke now. The math isn't good.

choices.label: Tell Mary the flour is fine. Ration it carefully and say nothing.

consequences.journal_entry: I lied to her face. She'll know within a week when the sickness starts. She might forgive me. She might not. Out here, the difference between a lie and mercy is hard to measure.

medium.journal_entry: The creek didn't just wet our flour. It rotted our margin for error. Three of us left and three thousand miles behind. The rain tastes like ash.

medium.title: The Fever Comes for Sarah
medium.description:
Sarah wakes burning. Not the flush of exertion—something deeper. Her skin is hot and papery. She claims she's fine, but her hands shake as she tries to help break camp. By midday, she's delirious, muttering about her sister who died of scarlet fever in Cincinnati. The other women exchange looks. You have maybe six hours before the fever either breaks or takes a turn nobody comes back from.

Thomas suggests stopping here—proper rest, water, time. Mary says you're only 150 miles out; turn back now and Sarah reaches a real doctor in Independence. William wants to keep moving at half-pace; staying still invites worse sickness. James looks to you.

choices.label: Make camp here. Nurse Sarah through it.
Turn the wagons back to Independence. Sarah needs a physician.
Half-pace ahead. Sarah rides in the wagon. If she worsens, you stop.
Give her laudanum and whiskey. Push on at steady pace.

personality_effects.Mary: believes in rest and proper care—her mother was a midwife
personality_effects.William: has seen fever camps where delay killed more than the sickness
personality_effects.Thomas: hunted deer; knows how to wait for wounded things

medium.journal_entry: Sarah's fever came in the night like a thief. She doesn't remember the morning. The thunder may have weakened her, or it was always coming. Either way, we're stopped now—truly stopped—and the prairie around us is very quiet.

medium.title: The Shoshone at the Water
medium.description: At dawn, you reach a spring in the rocks where the trail forks. Three Shoshone men are there with horses, filling waterskins. They see you, watch you. One has a rifle. The water here is the next reliable source for thirty miles. Mary whispers that they could be scouts. Thomas's hand drifts toward his rifle. The deer meat hangs in the wagon—fresh enough to trade, but you're down to twenty-five pounds of flour and hardtack. No one moves. The Shoshone don't move either. Just wait.

choices.label: Approach with the deer meat. Trade. Make a deal.

consequences.journal_note: The Shoshone took half the venison and gave us dried roots and information. Said the canyon ahead floods in cold. Mary thinks we paid too much. Thomas says we bought our scalps.

choices.label: Water the oxen quietly. Leave. Don't engage.

consequences.journal_note: Took a longer route around the spring. The oxen are thirsty. Everyone's nerves are worn thin from the choice we didn't make.

choices.label: Challenge them. Show teeth. Let them know you're armed and will defend your people.

consequences.journal_note: Shot in the air. They rode off. One of their arrows hit the wagon—James took a graze to the shoulder. The oxen bolted. Lost an animal in the chaos. No one speaks except Thomas, who speaks too much.

personality_effects.James: Realizes now that fear and choice are the same thing. Neither Mary nor Thomas will forget what he decides.

medium.journal_entry: September 1st. The spring was real water and the men were real men with real choices of their own. Thirty miles to the next water. The deer meat will spoil if we don't move. Everything spoils out here—meat, hope, the idea that we're in control of anything.

medium.title: The Ox Won't Rise
medium.description: Dawn breaks cold and gray. One of the oxen—the spotted one that limped yesterday—won't get up. It's breathing shallow, ribs heaving. The other three mill restlessly, sensing something. Mary says it's anthrax. James thinks it's just exhaustion. Either way, it won't pull a wagon today. You have maybe three hours before you need to decide: shoot it, leave it, or waste a day waiting to see if it recovers. The canyon walls rise close on both sides. Snow is coming down from the mountains. You can feel it.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Strip what meat you can carry and move on.

consequences.journal_note: The report echoed wrong in the canyon. Mary wouldn't look at me while I butchered it. We're down to three oxen now. The meat will keep us maybe four days if we're careful. If we're not, less.

choices.label: Leave it. Maybe it'll recover and follow, or maybe another wagon train will use it.
