consequences.journal_note: We left it standing there. James kept looking back. I told him oxen die on the trail all the time, and he said yes, they do, but we don't usually watch it happen. We're making poor time with only three. The canyon is narrower ahead.

choices.label: Camp an extra day. Drain an abscess on its leg—might be infection, not sickness.

consequences.journal_note: Mary was right. Thick pus came out like a foul spring. The ox stood quiet while she worked. It got up by evening. But we lost a full day and the temperature dropped ten degrees. First snow on the peaks now. We might have just wasted our margin.

personality_effects.Mary: She knows ox sickness. She's kept notes on what kills them—scours, bloat, anthrax—written in the margins of her Bible. She's watching James now, waiting to see if he'll think before he acts.
personality_effects.James: Tired. The weight of decisions shows in how he holds his shoulders. Three dead in the party. One dead ox is just another accounting. But his hands shake when he doesn't know anyone's looking.

medium.journal_entry: Day 126 on the trail. We've come far enough to see the mountains we were aiming for, and they look like they're not going to let us through easy. The canyon squeezes tighter every mile. One ox down before noon. Mary and I don't argue anymore about hard choices—we just make them and live with the taste. The dust tastes like copper and old blood.

medium.title: The Crossing
medium.description: You find a ford at dawn, rocky and shallow enough. But the current is faster than it looks. As James tests the first oxen, Mary collapses against the wagon bed—fever's worse. She's burning up. The river crossing will take hours in this cold. Wait for her to stabilize, and you lose the good light and risk an evening ford in darkness. Push through now with her half-conscious in the wagon, and the jolting might worsen the dysentery. Or turn back upriver to find easier water, losing two days and eating into food stores that are already thin.

choices.label: Ford now while light holds. Mary rides in the wagon, unconscious or not.

personality_effects.Mary: resentment

choices.description: The wagon lurches through the rocks. Mary vomits into the straw. One oxen goes lame crossing a submerged boulder. You make it across before dark but barely. She's still burning when you camp. You use the last of the calomel.

choices.label: Wait. Let Mary rest and cool. Ford tomorrow when she's steadier.

personality_effects.James: patience_tested

choices.description: She sleeps through the afternoon. Her fever breaks slightly by evening. But the delay costs food—two days burning daylight. When you ford the next morning, the current has risen. The crossing is harder, slower. You lose hours making sure Mary doesn't fall from the wagon.

choices.label: Leave Mary here with one day's food and half the medicine. Scout upriver for an easier crossing alone.

personality_effects.Mary: betrayal

choices.description: You leave her the rifle and tell her to stay in the wagon. The upriver route adds miles and time—by the time you find better water and return, she's been alone nearly two days. You find her sitting by the wagon, not delirious, just hollow-eyed. She doesn't speak when you tell her you found a crossing. She never really will again.

personality_effects.James: leadership_crisis

medium.journal_entry: Found the Snake River at a ford. Mary's dysentery is turning serious. The choice is between pushing through her illness or losing time we don't have. The oxen are thin. The food won't last past October if we dawdle. Every day is a negotiation with the land that doesn't care about one sick woman or three dead ones behind us. I think about turning back. I think about it more than I should.

medium.title: The Ox in the Mud
medium.description: One of the oxen has sunk to its belly in a mud pocket off the trail. The animal bellows and thrashes, sinking deeper. Rain continues. It's maybe forty minutes until dark. The ox is valuable but the mud is hungry and getting hungrier. Thomas wants to shoot it and move on. Mary thinks you can still pull it free if you sacrifice some rope and time. James looks at you for the decision.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Butcher what you can carry. Move before dark.
choices.narrative: The shot echoes flat in the wet air. The ox stops struggling. You and Thomas work the carcass in failing light, cutting what meat won't spoil, leaving the rest for the ravens and coyotes. Forty pounds of decent beef. The rain hisses on the bones. Mary watches from the wagon without speaking. The remaining oxen pull easier without the extra weight, but something in the air between you all has gone colder than the weather.

choices.label: Spend two hours trying to extract it. Use rope and the other oxen.
choices.narrative: You tie rope to the stuck ox and chain the other two to pull. The mud makes sounds like it's alive. The stuck animal panics and bellows. After ninety minutes of pulling and resetting and cursing, the ox lurches free, bleeding from a gash on its flank. It limps but walks. You lose light and camp in the rain without a proper fire. The meager rations taste like mud and vindication. Mary looks less hollowed out than she has in weeks.

choices.label: Leave it. The ox chose the mud. You didn't.
choices.narrative: You order the wagon forward. The ox's bellows follow you into the fog until they don't. No one speaks as you make camp. James won't meet your eyes. Thomas nods like he understands the mathematics of survival. Mary sits apart and doesn't eat. In the dark, you wonder if leaving things to their fate is mercy or cowardice or just the only arithmetic that works anymore.

medium.journal_entry: Oct 1st. Rain hasn't stopped. One ox in the mud. The forest tastes like rot. We're close to the Willamette now but close is a liar's word out here. Close means another month. Sarah used to sing in the rain. Thomas wants the gun. Mary wants mercy. I want to wake up in a place that isn't wet.

medium.title: The Shelter
medium.description: An old trapper's cabin appears through the rain-soaked pines, maybe a half-mile off the trail. Smoke rises from the chimney. Your oxen are exhausted, hooves slipping in the mud. Mary's cough has worsened since morning. The cabin could mean dry bedding, a fire, maybe trade goods. But you don't know who lives there, or if they'll welcome you. The rain is getting worse. Staying on the trail means a cold night in the wagon, maybe pushing to the next settlement by tomorrow evening.

choices.label: Leave the trail. Approach the cabin and ask for shelter.

consequences.notes: You lose distance but gain rest. Stranger's hospitality is unpredictable. Roll outcome separately.

choices.label: Push through the rain. Make camp in the forest tonight.

consequences.notes: Cold seeps into everything. Mary's cough worsens. James's confidence that you'll reach the valley soon keeps spirits barely alive.

choices.label: Burn one of the spare wagon parts for a hot fire. Stay on the trail but rest here two hours.

consequences.notes: Fixes nothing permanently. But warmth matters. You'll reach the cabin area again in two days if you're going the same direction. One less repair if the wagon breaks.

personality_effects.James: He wants the cabin. Says three is too small a group to survive alone. His voice when he says it sounds like he's trying to convince himself.
personality_effects.Mary: She leans against the wagon rail. Doesn't argue about anything anymore. That's worse than arguing.

medium.journal_entry: October 10th. Rain all day. Saw a cabin with smoke. Thomas would have made a joke about the timing of it. Mary didn't laugh at anything today. The jerky from the Pawnee is holding us. Counted it twice. We're thinner than the oxen now. James keeps looking at me like I know what comes next. I don't.

medium.title: The Pawnee Encampment
medium.description: You crest a rise at dawn and see them: three dozen Pawnee, maybe more, moving south along a creek bed two miles east. They haven't seen you yet. Your scout reports fresh horse tracks—they're hunting buffalo. One of your oxen is lame and slow. Thomas says they'll smell weakness. Mary suggests pushing hard north, off the trail. William—the one you're still thinking about, the one you buried—would have wanted to avoid trouble altogether. Sarah's checking the powder in her rifle.

choices.label: Push the herd hard north, abandon the trail for high ground. Avoid contact.

personality_effects.Thomas: distrusts your caution
personality_effects.William: pushes harder than his body allows

choices.label: Hold position. Send James forward under a white cloth to trade. Offer coffee, salt, ammunition.

personality_effects.James: gains respect for pragmatism
personality_effects.Mary: fears the risk
personality_effects.William: remembers his mother's stories about trading posts

choices.label: Cull the lame ox now. Slaughter it before they catch the scent of blood. Smoke the meat fast, move at night.

personality_effects.Mary: haunted by the practical butchery
personality_effects.Thomas: approves of necessity

personality_effects.James: Whatever you choose, you feel the weight of five people depending on a decision made in seconds.
personality_effects.Mary: She keeps one hand on her skirts like she's ready to run or pray or both.
personality_effects.Thomas: His jaw is set. He's thinking about ratios—how many guns, how many horses, whether numbers favor you.
personality_effects.Sarah: Her rifle is loaded. She's watching you, waiting to see if you're the kind of leader who hesitates.
personality_effects.William: He's pale. His cough is worse. You wonder if he has the strength for any of this.

medium.journal_entry: April 28th. Saw Pawnee at first light. Three dozen or more. Some said trading, some said running. We held our breath like the prairie was holding its breath with us. The lame ox knew something was wrong. Everything on this trail knows before you do. You just have to decide if you're paying attention or already dead.

medium.title: The Sick Oxen
medium.description:
William notices one of the oxen—the spotted one you've relied on since Fort Laramie—moving stiffly. By noon, it won't pull. You stop to examine it. The animal's eyes are clouded. Its breathing comes shallow and fast. Thomas recognizes the signs: murrain, maybe anthrax. It could spread. The beast is suffering. You have six oxen; you can spare one if you're careful with the remaining five. But careful means slower. The snow warnings echo from that eastbound party.

Sarah suggests shooting it and pressing on. William, who's nursed this animal through three months, won't look at you.

choices.label: Shoot the ox. Butcher what you can carry. Move on.

personality_effects.William: William is quiet at night now. Doesn't argue anymore, which is worse.
personality_effects.Sarah: Sarah nods. She understands the math of survival.

consequences.journal_entry: Shot the spotted ox this afternoon. Its meat will buy us three days' rations if we're miserly. William hasn't spoken since. Thomas said nothing either, just dug the grave. The remaining five oxen are leaner than I'd like them to be.

choices.label: Rest here two days. Hope the ox recovers. Give it medicine and water.

personality_effects.William: William sits with the ox through the night. In the morning, it's dead anyway. He blames himself.
personality_effects.James: James knows every day lost is a day closer to snow. He feels it in his shoulders.

consequences.journal_entry: Stayed with the ox. It died at dawn regardless. Two days squandered and the animal gone all the same. Snow in the passes. William wept where he thought no one could see. I pretended not to notice.

choices.label: Leave it behind. Cut it loose near water and move on. Maybe a Pawnee or Cheyenne band finds it.

personality_effects.William: William considers abandoning the company that night. Only Mary talking him down keeps him with the wagon.
personality_effects.James: James feels the weight of being a coward. He knows it, and so does William.

consequences.journal_entry: Left the oxen to die slow. Cowardice dressed as pragmatism. William looked at me for the first time all day and I couldn't meet his eyes. We'll make better time but something broke in the wagon tonight that supplies won't fix.

personality_effects.William: This trial will define him—either he becomes harder or he fractures.
personality_effects.Sarah: She watches James to see which man he is.

medium.journal_entry: The spotted ox moved wrong this morning. Thomas called it murrain. Now we wait, and waiting is a luxury we may not afford.

medium.title: The Pawnee Camp
medium.description: By midday, you spot smoke rising from a ravine ahead. As you crest the ridge, you see a Pawnee encampment—maybe thirty people, mostly women and children. Their horses are thin. One man approaches on foot, unarmed, holding up his palms. He speaks broken English, learned from traders. He says his band is moving south because the buffalo herds have vanished. The heat has scattered them. He asks if you have meat to trade. His eyes are on your oxen. Behind him, you see a child with a suppurating wound on her leg. No one has touched it. Your party is thirsty and you have no fresh water for another eight miles.

choices.label: Trade one ox for fresh meat and information about water sources ahead.

consequences.note: The Pawnee will tell you where the next reliable water is. One of your oxen is worth more than 35 pounds of dried meat, but you gain trust and a genuine advantage. The water knowledge may save you days of suffering.

choices.label: Refuse to trade. Push hard for the water source they mentioned from a distance.

consequences.note: You drive forward fast. Three hours later, Thomas and Sarah show signs of heat sickness. The water source they pointed to—if they were truthful—is not where they indicated. You find a stagnant seep instead. Everyone drinks. Two hours later, Thomas vomits.

choices.label: Offer medicine and help with the child's wound. Ask for water in return.

consequences.note: You spend the afternoon treating the infected wound. It's bad. You use your last proper bandage and laudanum. The Pawnee are grateful but wary. They give you water and a cache of pemmican hidden in the ravine—50 pounds. A Pawnee hunter stays with your camp that night. He leaves at dawn without saying goodbye. That evening, James dreams of the child's face. He wakes up several times.

personality_effects.James: Distrusts the Pawnee but will follow the majority. Tired of moral weight.
personality_effects.Mary: Sees the child's wound as a test of character.
personality_effects.Thomas: Wants to keep moving. Fears delay more than thirst.
personality_effects.Sarah: Her son died of infection two years ago. She cannot look away from the child.

medium.journal_entry: The plains are a furnace now. We saw Pawnee today and the question hung in the air like dust: who survives, and by what permission. There is no good answer out here. Only choices that feel like theft from someone. William would have known what to do. William knew the price of things in ways I never will.

medium.title: The Fever Dream
medium.description: Mary wakes screaming at dawn. Not from pain—from certainty. She swears she saw a woman in a white dress standing at the edge of camp, motionless, watching. When you check, there's nothing. Fresh snow fell overnight. No tracks but yours and the oxen's. Mary's fever has spiked. She's lucid enough to know how this looks. Thomas finds a piece of cloth—white, wool, old—caught on a rock thirty yards from the fire. It's not from anyone's supplies. James, you have three doses of medicine left, and Mary's cough is getting worse.

choices.label: Give Mary two doses of medicine now. The fever needs breaking, dream or not.

consequences.notes: The fever breaks by evening. Mary sleeps without nightmares. But you're down to one dose for the rest of the journey.

choices.label: Ration one dose, see if her body fights it. The medicine might be needed worse later.

consequences.notes: Mary's fever lingers into a second day. She drifts in and out of clarity. She keeps asking who the woman was. You find no answer.

choices.label: Tell Mary the cloth was from a Shoshone scout, nothing worse. Rest and broth will fix her faster than worry.

consequences.notes: Mary doesn't believe you. She knows you're lying, and she knows you know she knows. Trust erodes. But you save the medicine for now.

choices.label: Pack up early. Move camp. The mountain air here is wrong, and that cloth changes things.

consequences.notes: One ox goes lame on the rocky descent. Mary's fever worsens from the hard ride. But you leave the place behind. Whether that matters, you don't know.

personality_effects.James: leadership_tested
personality_effects.Mary: uncertainty_deepens
personality_effects.Thomas: pragmatism_wavers

medium.journal_entry: Aug 15. Mary saw something in the dark that may not have been there. Found cloth that shouldn't exist. Fever dreams or mountain sickness playing tricks, but the women's fear is real enough. The Rockies don't feel like other places. The air tastes of copper and old snow. We're 1150 miles from home with 2000 still ahead. Medicine runs thin. I'm running thin. The oxen sense it too.

medium.title: The Shoshone Camp
medium.description: You crest a ridge at dawn and spot smoke below—a Shoshone encampment maybe half a mile south, near the only water for twenty miles. Five or six lodges. Horses grazing. No immediate sign they've seen you, but Thomas spots movement. Mary suggests waiting until dark to water the oxen and slip past. James says you need to know if they're hostile or willing to trade. Thomas is trembling. Your oxen are flagging. One animal won't make it another day without water.

choices.label: Ride down under a white cloth. Attempt trade for water and fresh meat.

consequences.outcome: The Shoshone are wary but not hostile. An older woman examines your goods. They trade twelve pounds of pemmican and let you water at the creek. One man studies your remaining medicine bottle for a long time but doesn't ask. As you leave, Thomas notices the same man watching Mary. Nothing happens, but the weight of that watching stays with you all afternoon.

choices.label: Circle wide around the camp at night. Water at the creek under darkness.

consequences.outcome: You move slow and quiet. The oxen are spooked by the unfamiliar ground. One stumbles badly in the dark—a foreleg swelling by morning, likely a deep bruise. You get water, but barely. As you're breaking camp at first light, you see three Shoshone riders on the ridge behind you, just watching. They don't follow. They don't need to. You're one ox closer to walking.

choices.label: Push on. There's water ahead—there has to be. Ration what's left.

consequences.outcome: Stubborn arithmetic. You find a seep ten miles on, brackish and shallow, but it's water. By then Thomas is hallucinating. Mary's lips crack and bleed. You lose two oxen in the push—one simply stops moving, one you shoot to spare it further suffering. The second mercy killing sits different than the first. You camp at the seep and nobody speaks.

personality_effects.James: Leadership questioned—each option reveals a different gamble.
personality_effects.Mary: Physically deteriorating. Suggests caution but recognizes desperation.
personality_effects.Thomas: Fear of the Shoshone is visible. Rationing plan appeals to his instinct to flee, but the cost is written on his face.

medium.journal_entry: Found the Shoshone. Found water. Found that some choices don't divide into good and bad—they divide into which people you're willing to harm. William's grave is thirty miles behind us now. The dust covers everything equally. At night I can't remember his face clearly anymore. That troubles me more than the hunger.

medium.title: The Woman at the Salt Flat
medium.description: You crest a ridge at dawn and see her a quarter mile off—a woman in a torn dress, walking barefoot across the salt flat toward you. She moves with purpose, not desperation. As you close the distance, you realize she's alone. No wagon. No supplies visible. Her skin is burnt brown and her hair matted, but her eyes track your approach with an alertness that troubles you. Mary whispers that she's a ghost. James notices fresh boot prints in the salt crust—hers, maybe a day old, leading back toward the southwest. The woman doesn't call out. She just stops and waits.

choices.label: Approach and offer her water and food

consequences.narrative: She drinks three cupfuls without stopping, then asks if you're headed to the valley. When you say yes, she says nothing else—just climbs onto the wagon bed and sits facing backward. By evening, Mary's dysentery worsens. The woman gives you a hard look and says Mary needs willow bark and rest, not dust and jogging. She knew things. Things she shouldn't.

choices.label: Ask where her party is and move on without delay

consequences.narrative: She doesn't answer. Just watches you for a long moment, then sits down in the salt crust. You push the oxen forward. Three miles on, you look back once. She's still sitting there. Mary doesn't speak to you for two days. James knows what you did—knew what you chose—and it sits between you like a corpse.

choices.label: Give her a horse and directions to Fort Hall, then leave

consequences.narrative: You have three oxen. You're using two to pull and one as spare. You give her the third, bridled but bare-back. She doesn't thank you. Doesn't ask questions. Just mounts and rides northeast toward the fort, not looking back. By nightfall, you find your pace quickening without the weight. The rest feels earned, even if you can't explain why.

medium.journal_entry: Sept 15th. Found a woman in the salt like she was waiting for us. No explanation. No story. Some things out here don't add up—maybe fever from the water, maybe something else. Mary says the nights are getting colder. Thomas and Sarah and William are gone. The woman knew things. The oxen move slower now, or maybe we're just tired. Fort Hall is still three weeks off if the snow holds. If it holds.

medium.title: The Meat Won't Last
medium.description: The deer meat is already going dark at the edges. Rain seeps through the canvas wagon cover. Mary notices the smell this morning — not quite rotten, but wrong. The cold keeps it slower, but not slow enough. You have maybe two days before it's inedible. The oxen are gaunt. Winter's coming hard through these Oregon pines, and you're still a month from the valley.

choices.label: Smoke and salt what you can. Use precious cloth scraps for wrapping.

consequences.journal_note: Preserved 8 pounds. Lost a good petticoat to wrapping. Mary didn't complain, but her jaw was set.

choices.label: Cook it all today and tomorrow. Eat until it's gone. Push hard on steady rations after.

consequences.journal_note: Two days of full bellies. The oxen moved better with less weight and stronger men. But we'll pay for it soon.

choices.label: Leave it. Bury what you can't carry. The oxen don't have the strength to haul dead weight.

consequences.journal_note: Buried forty pounds of meat in the mud. Mary watched without speaking. There's a smell to waste that follows you.

medium.journal_entry: October 10th. Rain won't stop. The meat from Thomas's deer is spoiling. We've lost three people and the mercy is they don't need feeding anymore. The oxen are ribs and sinew. Mary says nothing when she thinks I'm not watching, but I see her counting on her fingers — miles left, days left, how long the oxen will last. She's doing the math I don't want to do. The fog this morning smelled like graves.

medium.title: The Drowning
medium.description: Thomas waded into a swollen creek this morning to water the oxen. The spring runoff had turned it into something meaner than it looked. He's downstream now, caught against a deadfall, conscious but pinned waist-deep. The current is strong. Getting him out will take time, rope, and bodies in that water. Mary is already moving toward him. William stands frozen. The oxen are thirsty. You have maybe ten minutes before the cold does what the creek won't.

choices.label: Wade in with rope. Get Thomas out, whatever it takes.

consequences.narrative: The water hits like a fist. You and William go in, rope around your waist. Thomas is heavy with water and shock. It takes both of you, and the current nearly takes William twice. You get him out. His lips are the color of old meat. The rope burns your palms to red. Mary wraps him in a blanket, talking low. Thomas coughs up creek water and something darker. By evening he's fevered and won't stop shaking.

choices.label: Send only William and Mary in. Conserve your strength for what's ahead.

consequences.narrative: You order them in. Mary doesn't hesitate. William hesitates, then goes. The rescue is sloppy, desperate. William goes under once. Mary pulls him up, then Thomas. They drag Thomas onto the bank like a corpse. William vomits water and rage. He won't look at you. Thomas lives, but something broke in that creek, and not just his fever. The camp is quiet that night in a way that makes the rain sound louder.

choices.label: Cut the rope. The creek takes what it wants. Move on.

consequences.narrative: You don't say it out loud. You don't have to. The silence spreads like blood in water. Mary tries to go in anyway. James restrains her. Thomas drowns screaming or quiet—the rain makes it hard to hear. The oxen drink from the creek that killed him. No one speaks for three days. At night, you hear Mary crying. By day, she stares ahead like the trail might swallow what happened if she doesn't look at it. William mutters about justice. Sarah cleans her rifle for no reason. The money you save means nothing. You've already spent it.

personality_effects.Mary: her capacity for mercy is being tested
personality_effects.William: proving himself or proving he's a coward, depending on the choice
personality_effects.Thomas: what happens to him shapes his trust in the leader
personality_effects.Sarah: watching to see who you are when the choice is hard
personality_effects.James: the leader's choice is the only truth that matters now

medium.journal_entry: The creek was running brown. We should have known. Should have waited. Thomas is the one who has to die because we didn't, or because we did. The oxen don't care which. They drink the same water.

medium.title: The Pawnee Camp
medium.description: At dusk, you crest a rise and see smoke. Below, perhaps a mile off, a Pawnee encampment spreads along a creek—maybe thirty lodges. Your scouts report they've seen your wagons. A rider approaches on a spotted pony, alone, hand raised. He's young. Behind him, more Pawnee gather but don't advance. The rider stops fifty yards out and calls something in his language. Thomas recognizes one word: trade. Your stores are good. His people look lean. Spring hunting has been poor.

choices.label: Approach and trade. Offer surplus food for fresh game and information about the trail ahead.

consequences.journal_note: They gave us pemmican and dried elk. The young rider's name was Petalesharo. He warned us the Platte narrows west of here—water fights over grass. He didn't lie.

choices.label: Circle the wagons and refuse. Arm everyone. Trade invites trouble.

consequences.journal_note: We locked down the camp. The Pawnee watched us for two hours, then left. William said we were cowards. Mary said we were alive. Both true.

choices.label: Send Thomas alone with minimal trade goods. Gather intelligence without risking the party.

consequences.journal_note: Thomas returned with good information but shaken. He said the Pawnee spoke of fever moving through the tribes. He wouldn't say what else he saw. Sarah thinks he's hiding something.

choices.label: Wait until dark, then quietly move the wagons north. Avoid the camp entirely.

consequences.journal_note: We drove hard through the night without lights. Lost an ox to a prairie-dog hole. Saw fires behind us at dawn but nothing followed. Cost us peace of mind and a good animal.

medium.journal_entry: Met the Pawnee on the high plain. They were hungry or curious or both. We had seconds to decide who we were—the kind of people who trade, or the kind who wall themselves in. No good choice exists out here, only the one you can live with tomorrow.

medium.title: The Pawnee Scout
medium.description: A young Pawnee scout on a lean pony appears at the edge of camp at dusk. He's unarmed, hands visible. He doesn't approach closer than fifty yards. Through hand signs and broken English, he indicates there's a Lakota war party two days ahead, moving south. He seems to want something—points at the oxen, then at his horse, which is badly lamed. He could be scouting for raiders himself. He could be genuine. The light is failing. The decision is yours.

choices.label: Give him an ox and supplies. Send him on his way.

consequences.journal_entry: We gave the scout an ox and dried meat. Mary said it was Christian charity. William said we were fools. The Pawnee rode north at first light without looking back. Whether he warned us true or led the Lakota straight to us, we may never know. Some choices have no good answer.

choices.label: Trade him information about Lakota movements ahead for information about water and grazing ahead.

consequences.journal_entry: The scout spoke true enough. He knew the creeks and the grass. We made good time the next day and found water where he said it would be. A clean trade—knowledge for knowledge. Thomas said it was the only honest dealing he'd seen on this trail. Still, I wonder what the Lakota will do when they find an ox with our brands on it.

choices.label: Send him away. Post extra watch. Keep moving at dusk.

consequences.journal_entry: We sent him away. He sat on that ruined horse for a long time, watching us. Then he rode into the dark. Whether we made a mistake or dodged a blade, there's no way to know. The whole camp slept poorly. Every sound was hoofbeats.

choices.label: Invite him to the fire. Feed him. Learn what you can.

consequences.journal_entry: The scout ate with us. He was young—couldn't have been more than sixteen. He showed us a wound in his leg, fresh and infected. Sarah tried to clean it with whiskey. He told us the Lakota took his sister in a raid last year. Said he was heading north to find her. Said he would have warned us anyway. After he left, Mary wouldn't look at James. Nobody slept. By morning, the infection in the young man's leg was in all our heads.

medium.journal_entry: May 1st. A scout came to the edge of camp. He brought news and questions. We had to choose what kind of people we were—or seemed to be. Out here, seems and are aren't often the same thing.

medium.title: The Cough
medium.description: Sarah's persistent cough has worsened overnight. This morning she vomited blood into the canvas water bucket. The others pretend not to notice, but James sees it. She's burning hot when he touches her forehead, though she shivers and pulls her blanket tighter. Mary thinks it's pneumonia. William thinks it's consumption and won't sit downwind of her anymore. Sarah insists she can walk, but her breathing sounds like wind through a broken bellows. The storm that's been on the horizon is moving faster than expected—dark clouds rolling in from the northwest. You have perhaps six hours before it hits.

choices.label: Push hard for the next settlement—Fort Kearny is maybe forty miles on. Run from the storm and get her medical attention.

consequences.journal_note: Pushed the oxen hard. Sarah could barely stay in the wagon. The storm caught us anyway, three miles short of the fort.

choices.label: Make camp now and prepare for the storm. Let Sarah rest. Hope it passes in a day or two.

consequences.journal_note: Storm came on us with hail the size of acorns. Sarah's fever broke on the second day, but something has changed in her eyes. She stares at nothing now.

choices.label: Sarah can't continue. Leave her at the last homestead you passed—two miles back. The family there seemed decent. You'll move faster without her.

personality_effects.Mary: hostile
personality_effects.James: guilt

consequences.journal_note: Paid the homesteader fifty dollars to take her in. Mary won't speak to me. Thomas says we did what we had to do. But the oxen know we're lighter now, and they seem relieved.

choices.label: Bleed her. The fever is caused by imbalance of the humors. Draw out the sickness.

consequences.journal_note: Sarah fainted after the bleeding. The blood in the pan looked black. Mary says I might have killed her. Maybe I did.

personality_effects.Mary: concerned
personality_effects.Thomas: pragmatic
personality_effects.William: fearful

medium.journal_entry: May 10th. 320 miles from Independence. Sarah is dying and there's nothing honest I can do about it. The storm is coming. God help us if we're still on this prairie when it arrives.

medium.title: The Trader's Offer
medium.description: A man emerges from the cottonwoods at dusk—half-breed, weathered, leading a mule loaded with furs and trade goods. He speaks Pawnee to the scouts, then English to you. Says he's wintering upriver, knows the next fifty miles better than his own scars. Offers to guide you through the bad water crossings ahead. Wants payment: thirty dollars, or a rifle and fifty rounds. He's watching Mary cough in the tent. 'Woman needs quinine,' he says. 'I got some. Not for trade. Gift.' But nothing's free on the trail, and his eyes don't match his smile.

choices.label: Pay him thirty dollars and hire him as guide.

consequences.journal_note: A guide cuts two days off the worst stretch. He knew where the Pawnee hunt. He knew where the water runs clear. He didn't steal from camp, but we watched him anyway.

choices.label: Refuse him but accept the quinine for Mary. Ask nothing.

consequences.journal_note: He left the quinine by the fire and rode out into darkness. Mary's fever broke by morning. We never learned his name, or why he helped. The debt of it sits heavier than any coin.

choices.label: Offer him a rifle and twenty rounds—less than he asked.

consequences.journal_note: He took the rifle and smiled that smile again. Said we'd cheated him, but he'd guide us anyway. By the third crossing, we understood: he was right, and we were wrong to doubt the cost of our own survival.

choices.label: Turn him away. Trust your own navigation.

consequences.journal_note: The river rose that night. We lost a full day at the second crossing, oxen up to their necks, Mary delirious with fever. The quinine sat in his saddlebag, riding away. Pride costs more than thirty dollars.

personality_effects.James: Questions whether trust is a luxury only the living can afford.
personality_effects.Mary: Fevered, lucid only in flashes. Sees the stranger's face in the dark.
personality_effects.Thomas: Practical. Knows guides have saved caravans. Also knows they've slit throats.
personality_effects.Sarah: Sees only that he offered help. Sees only that we refused.
personality_effects.William: Says nothing. Watches the man's hands.

medium.journal_entry: June 1st. The river runs brown and fast. A man came out of nowhere, offering knowledge we don't have. Offering medicine we need. The trail teaches you quick that every choice is a trade—not just goods for goods, but safety for suspicion, pride for survival. Mary's cough echoes through the tent. By tomorrow, one version of us will know if we made the right decision. The other version will never know it was wrong.

medium.title: The Sick Oxen
medium.description: Dawn breaks gray and still. One of the oxen won't rise. Its eyes roll back, breathing shallow. The second ox shows the same glassy look—fever. Mary lies in the tent behind you, burning with cholera. Thomas kneels by the animals, checking their mouths. 'Anthrax, maybe,' he says quietly. 'Or the water. Same water we drank.' The storm yesterday drove you to camp near a slaughterhouse site—bones scattered in the grass, carcasses half-buried. You didn't think about it then. The herd needs four oxen minimum to move. Lose two more and you're trading for animals at the next trading post, if you can get there before the sick oxen spread it to the rest. Or you kill them now, use what meat you can salvage, and make hard choices about what stays in the wagons.

choices.label: Kill the two sick oxen. Salvage the meat. Push on.
Wait one day. See if they recover. Dose them with whiskey and vinegar.
Move the sick oxen away from the herd. Leave them. Reduce to four oxen and lighter loads.
Kill them and burn the carcasses. Bury the ash. Don't tell the others what you saw at that slaughterhouse.

personality_effects.Thomas: Notices everything. Will know if you lie about the animals.
personality_effects.Sarah: Desperate. Might respect a clean, quick decision.
personality_effects.Mary: Fever is getting worse. Won't remember much of this.

medium.journal_entry: Buried William five days back. Now the oxen are dying of the same sickness that took the water. The valley smells like rot and wet earth. We haven't seen another wagon in two weeks. The river is still there, patient, moving toward something. We are not.

medium.title: The Starving Pawnee
medium.description:
You come upon a Pawnee man collapsed beside the trail, his horse dead three days back by the smell of it. He's conscious but barely. Your guide says the Pawnee have been pushed out of their hunting grounds by the Lakota moving south. This man is alone. He won't last the night without help.

Mary thinks you should help. Thomas points out you're on meager rations with two passes still ahead. Sarah says nothing. James—you—stand there knowing that every pound of food is the difference between reaching California before the snow comes or not.

choices.label: Give him food and medicine. Leave him with supplies to reach the next settlement.

consequences.description: The man nods when you approach. Doesn't speak English. You give him jerky, biscuit, salt pork. Thomas watches like you're throwing his life away. The Pawnee eats slowly, carefully, as if the food might vanish. Before you leave, he presses something into your hand—a small carved bone whistle, cracked but deliberate. Mary's eyes are wet. Thomas won't speak to you for two days.

choices.label: Leave him. You can't afford to lose provisions. Your family comes first.

consequences.description: You ride past. No one speaks. That night, Sarah stares into the fire like she's seeing something else. Mary won't eat her ration. James, you lie awake listening to the wind and thinking about the bone whistle you didn't receive. On the third day, you crest a ridge and see him below, still moving east, slower now. Or maybe it's someone else. The distance makes it impossible to know.

choices.label: Take him with you to the next pass. He can help with the oxen, lighten the load.

consequences.description: The man is weak but he understands work. He doesn't talk, but he moves with purpose—clearing rocks from the path, checking the oxen's hooves, starting fires. Thomas seems less angry when someone else is sharing the labor. But the man slows you a day through the pass. At the summit, he stops and looks back east for a long time. In the morning, he's gone. The oxen are fine. Your provisions are lighter, somehow, than they should be.

personality_effects.Mary: Her faith means something here. Every choice matters to her faith.
personality_effects.Thomas: Survival is mathematics. Charity is the luxury of the safe.
personality_effects.Sarah: Silent, but watching. She sees what you choose and remembers it.

medium.journal_entry: July 20th. Found a Pawnee dying on the trail. His nation is being hunted out of existence. We are here because of choices made long before we were born, choices that pushed people like him into the margins. I made a choice today too. I will live with it. The mountains don't care what we choose. They only care that we keep moving.

medium.title: The Fever Takes Root
medium.description: That night, Mary complains of chills despite the warm blankets. By morning, her skin burns hot. The deer meat Thomas shot yesterday sits half-butchered in the cache—you wrapped it poorly in the rain. She ate more of it than anyone. Now her eyes won't focus right, and she's asking for Sarah, who's been in the ground for two weeks. Thomas thinks it's bad meat. James thinks it's mountain fever. Nobody knows which is worse.

choices.label: Give her the medicine. All three doses. Stop at the next creek for a week.

personality_effects.Mary: grateful_fragile
personality_effects.James: helpless
personality_effects.Thomas: guilty

choices.label: Ration one dose. Keep moving. If she dies, she dies. The snow won't wait.

personality_effects.Mary: resentful_dying
personality_effects.James: haunted
personality_effects.Thomas: contemptuous

choices.label: Make her ride in the back wagon. Give her water and the deer broth. Pray it's not the meat.

personality_effects.Mary: uncertain
personality_effects.James: doubtful
personality_effects.Thomas: anxious

personality_effects.James: The choice is yours. Don't pretend it isn't.
personality_effects.Mary: Please. I can taste something wrong in the meat.
personality_effects.Thomas: I should've cleaned it better. I know that.

medium.journal_entry: Mary took ill this morning. The mountains don't care about illness. They've killed better people than us. The deer was good meat yesterday. Today it might be poison. There's no way to know until the body tells you—and by then it's too late. We have three days of good weather before the pass closes. We have three doses of calomel and laudanum. We have each other and nothing else that matters. I have to choose what Mary's life is worth against the lives of the people depending on us reaching Fort Hall. That's the choice nobody tells you about when they talk about manifest destiny.

medium.title: The Burned Wagon
medium.description: You crest a ridge and find a wagon half-buried in ash, its canvas burned away. Scattered nearby: a child's doll, a ledger book warped by heat, animal bones picked clean. The fire is recent—maybe three days old. Fresh hoofprints circle the site, too many to be coincidence. In the distance, smoke rises from a canyon cut. Your oxen are restless.

choices.label: Search the wagon thoroughly. There might be supplies or information about who did this.

consequences.journal_note: Found a journal entry naming the settlers as the Hartley party. Date was five days prior. James's hands shook reading it. We are not alone on this trail.

choices.label: Leave immediately. You have three days' food. Stopping costs time you don't have.

consequences.journal_note: Rode past without stopping. Mary wouldn't look at the wagon. James said nothing until camp. 'We should have buried them,' he said to the darkness.

choices.label: Take the doll. Leave markers. Move on.

consequences.journal_note: Used a shirt to mark a grave-site. The doll sits wrapped in cloth at the bottom of our supplies. James says it's a debt we're paying forward. Mary calls it a curse we're carrying.

choices.label: The smoke in the canyon. You could investigate before dark.

consequences.journal_note: Found Pawnee hunters butchering elk. The misunderstanding lasted seconds. One arrow missed James by a hand's width. They let us ride on—we were too few to bother killing. James's jaw doesn't move when he talks anymore.

medium.journal_entry: The trail marks the dead now as much as the living. Every burned thing we pass is a question we can't answer and a choice we have to make anyway.

medium.title: The Pawnee Scout's Warning
medium.description: A lone Pawnee scout approaches your camp at dusk, riding hard from the west. His horse is lathered. He speaks broken English and gestures urgently at the sky—clouds gathering over the mountains, moving fast. He points south, then shakes his head. North, he nods. He won't stay to eat. Before leaving, he draws a symbol in the dust: three wavy lines crossed by a slash. You've seen Pawnee sign before. This one means sickness ahead, or flood, or both. Mary coughs from the wagon. The scout rides off without waiting for thanks.

choices.label: Trust the warning. Divert north, add 40 miles and 4 days to reach higher ground before the storm hits.
Push straight on. The scout could've been warning anyone. You lose time you can't afford, and Mary needs the fastest route to Sacramento.
Send James ahead with one ox and half the remaining food to scout the direct route. Mary and remaining supplies follow at slower pace.

James.option_1: Doubt gnaws. You read too much into signs.
James.option_2: Harder now. Three people dead and Mary wasting. No more favors from strangers.
James.option_3: The loneliness of command. You ride alone into nothing.

Mary.option_1: Relief. Someone tried to help. But the detour exhausts you further.
Mary.option_2: Panic. The direct route feels like giving up. Like choosing faster dying.
Mary.option_3: Resentment. You stay behind. Always left behind.

medium.journal_entry: Sept 15 — A Pawnee came at dusk with a warning drawn in dust. Three lines and a cross. J. says it means sickness or flood. The sky does look wrong to the west. Mary's fever is back. I can hear her breathing from here. The scout didn't ask for anything and didn't stay for supper. That alone made me believe him. But believing and acting are different animals. We are so few now. Three oxen. Ten pounds of food. Two miles from the grave of J. Henderson who didn't make it past last year. I wonder what choices he faced in this same country.

medium.title: The Thing in the Fog
medium.description: Morning breaks gray and featureless. The fog sits so thick you can't see past the oxen's heads. Mary's fever broke in the night, but she's weak and won't eat. Thomas finds something near the tree line while gathering deadfall for the fire—a deer carcass, mostly intact, maybe two days old. The kill looks wrong. The ribs are splayed outward like something burst from inside. There's no blood trail leading to it. No visible wounds except that impossible rupture. Thomas wants to butcher it anyway. Meat is meat. James, you need to decide whether the risk of spoiled game or something stranger is worth the calories you don't have.

choices.label: Butcher the deer. Starvation kills slower than anything else.

personality_effects.Thomas: trust_gained
personality_effects.Mary: trust_lost

consequences.journal_entry: Fed the party on carrion. The meat tasted wrong—sweet and dark. Mary wouldn't touch it. Thomas ate two portions and said it was the best thing he'd had in weeks. I watched him eat and wondered what kind of animal leaves itself open like that.

choices.label: Leave it. Burn the carcass and move on before the fog clears.

personality_effects.Thomas: trust_lost
personality_effects.Mary: trust_gained

consequences.journal_entry: Thomas called me a coward. We burned the thing while he watched. Used the last of the medicine to quiet Mary's stomach. The oxen wouldn't walk past the fire. One of them screamed like I've never heard an animal scream. We made six miles.

choices.label: Bury it deep and don't tell the others what you found.

consequences.journal_entry: Dug in the mud until my hands bled through my gloves. The soil was warm. Too warm. Thomas found me and asked what I was burying. I lied. He knew I lied. In a group this small, a lie is a choice to be alone.

personality_effects.James: lead_weight_grows
personality_effects.Mary: fever_lingers_in_sanity
personality_effects.Thomas: patience_fraying

medium.journal_entry: The forest doesn't want us here. Every mile tastes like rust and old rain. Sarah and William are with whatever made that deer. Sometimes I think I can hear them laughing in the fog.

medium.title: The Cabin
medium.description: Through the rain-soaked forest, you find a half-collapsed cabin beside the trail. Smoke rises from a hole in the roof. The door hangs open. Your oxen are favoring their front legs—mud and constant wet have worn them thin. Mary says the cabin might have supplies. James says you should push on before dark. The cabin's interior is too dark to read from outside. You hear nothing. No movement.

choices.label: Investigate the cabin thoroughly

consequences.description: Inside: a dead man in a rotted blanket, three months gone. Beside him, a burlap sack with eight pounds of hardtack wrapped in oilcloth. A glass bottle of laudanum. A wool coat, barely worn. Someone was waiting for help that never came. Mary is shaken but grateful for the food. You camp here the night despite the smell. The oxen do not rest well. Next morning, one is limping badly. By afternoon, it will have to be shot.

choices.label: Strip what you can see from outside, move on

consequences.description: You pull the door wider and grab a saddlebag hanging on a nail—three pounds of dried meat inside, leather still supple. Nothing else is worth the time. You leave the body where it lies. Mary doesn't look at you as you ride past. She's right not to. By evening the rain becomes a downpour. You camp in a stand of fir with no shelter. The fire barely holds. Cold seeps into everything.

choices.label: Shelter in the cabin for the night, deal with consequences later

consequences.description: You drag the corpse outside and cover it with branches. The cabin is shelter—cracked wood and holes in the roof, but walls. A fire holds heat. You and Mary eat half your meager rations trying to replace what warmth the dead man stole from the place. In the night, you hear something moving outside. Possibly scavengers. Possibly something else. It circles the cabin twice and leaves. Mary sleeps fitfully. You don't sleep at all. Your sanity is worn thin as the cabin walls. In the morning, the smell is worse, and the oxen refuse to approach the building.

medium.journal_entry: Found a cabin. Found a man in it, long dead. The rain doesn't care what we do with the bodies. It falls on the living and the corpses the same. We are running out of choices and running out of people to make them. Oregon is still two hundred miles. Feels like two thousand.

medium.title: The Sluggish Ox
medium.description: One of the oxen that drank from the alkali pool this morning has slowed to a halt. Its eyes are glassy. The animal stands with legs splayed, breathing shallow. You've lost maybe two hours already. The rest of the herd shifts nervously. Thomas says it'll die by nightfall. Mary suggests pushing on—the animal's nearly spent anyway. William won't meet anyone's eyes. The prairie stretches flat and indifferent. Chimney Rock still pulls at the horizon.

choices.label: Shoot the ox and salvage what meat you can. Move on.
Stop here for the day. Make a poultice with what medicine you have. Wait and see.
Leave the ox behind. Redistribute its load to the other five. Push harder to make up time.

personality_effects.description: William has oxen sense—his father kept a small herd in Ohio. He knows alkali poison when he sees it. Mary's practical about losses; she'll respect the decision to cut loose. Thomas drives hard. Sarah's softer. She'll feel the weight of whatever dies today, whether the ox or the journey's humanity.

medium.journal_entry: The ox drank poison without knowing. It didn't ask for the mistake. We did. Now we choose what it costs us—time, meat, ammunition, or the slow arithmetic of suffering. There's no choice here that doesn't leave a mark. The prairie doesn't care which one we pick.

medium.title: The Ruined Flour
medium.description: By midday, the water damage from yesterday's creek crossing has become impossible to ignore. The flour barrel shows white bloom across the top—mold already working. Thomas estimates you've lost forty pounds to rot, maybe more if you don't act now. The smell is faintly sweet, wrong. Mary suggests drying what's salvageable in the sun. William wants to throw it all out and stretch the remaining stores. Sarah is quiet, which is worse than complaint. James, you need to decide what happens to your flour and what happens next.

choices.label: Dry the flour in the sun. Salvage what you can, waste nothing.

consequences.description: You spend the afternoon spreading flour on canvas. Some of it might be good. The work feels purposeful until late afternoon when clouds roll in—dark and fast. Half the flour gets rained on again. By evening, you've recovered maybe twenty pounds of usable grain. Mary appreciates the effort. William won't meet your eye.

choices.label: Discard it. A full barrel of moldy flour will sicken people. Better to be lean than poisoned.

consequences.description: You order the barrel emptied into a ravine. The loss is brutal—nearly a third of your flour. Thomas watches it spill like you're burying money. William nods, seeing no waste in caution. By nightfall, the wagon feels lighter and the silences longer. Sarah keeps her hand on a sack of cornmeal as if it might vanish next.

choices.label: Mix the suspect flour with the cornmeal. Dilute the rot, stretch both.

consequences.description: You and Thomas work through the afternoon, mixing damaged flour with good cornmeal in roughly equal parts. It's clever. It's also a gamble. Some of the party will eat this mixture. In three days, you'll know if the mold was dormant or active. William mutters about poisoning people by degrees. Sarah's stomach aches by evening—possibly imagination, possibly not. The mathematics are sound. The ethics are another thing.

medium.journal_entry: The creek took more than we thought. Food is precious out here, but so are teeth and guts. I made a choice about flour. I'll make a hundred more like it before Oregon. The trail doesn't care if you sleep easy about any of them.

medium.title: The Pawnee Hunter
medium.description: A lone Pawnee hunter on horseback appears at the edge of camp as you're breaking down tents. He's young, maybe twenty, with a rifle across his lap. He doesn't approach closer than fifty yards. He watches your oxen with obvious interest. After several minutes, he calls out in broken English: 'You go west. Bad water ahead. Two days. I show you good water. I want one ox.' He doesn't seem hostile, but he doesn't seem friendly either. Just transactional. Thomas mutters that you shouldn't trust him. Mary points out you're low on water containers anyway.

choices.label: Trade one ox for the water route information

consequences.journal_note: Traded an ox to the Pawnee. He guided us to a freshwater spring two days north of the main trail. Thomas still thinks we were cheated. We're lighter now, but the oxen are grateful for the reprieve.

choices.label: Refuse and push on — you have water enough

consequences.journal_note: Refused the Pawnee's offer. Found the water hole he meant — fouled with animal carcass. Two oxen went lame. Sarah's fever returned.

choices.label: Negotiate — offer ammunition instead of an ox

consequences.journal_note: The hunter took 40 rounds. He seemed to consider us carefully after, then pointed northwest. Whether he actually knows good water or just needed bullets, we'll find out in two days. William thinks we negotiated well.

choices.label: Offer him food instead — preserve the oxen

consequences.journal_note: He took the dried meat without comment and rode south. No information. William said we looked weak. Thomas looked away. Sarah asked if we just fed the man who might have scouted us for trouble.

personality_effects.Mary: pragmatic — values survival over doctrine
personality_effects.Thomas: cautious — distrusts outsiders, especially after the turnback stories
personality_effects.William: anxious — sees trade as relief from unknowns
personality_effects.Sarah: intuitive — senses the hunter wasn't lying, but worries about the cost

medium.journal_entry: 1848-05-01. The plains stretch like a held breath. Met a Pawnee hunter who knew things we don't. He wanted oxen. We have six left. How many do we need to reach Oregon? How many can we lose and still arrive breathing? Every trade is a small death.

medium.title: The Crossing
medium.description: You find a shallow ford two miles upstream, but the Pawnee have been here recently—fresh horse droppings, a discarded leather bridle. The water runs fast from last night's storm. Mary shivers despite the heat, her cholera worsening. Thomas suggests waiting for the river to drop. Sarah points out the storm clouds gathering again to the west. You have maybe three hours of good light.

choices.label: Cross now while you can see the bottom. Risk high water, but you move today.

consequences.description: Sarah's horse panics mid-ford. She nearly drowns before James pulls her out. One ox swept downstream, lost. Everyone is soaked, shaking with cold despite the heat. Mary's fever spikes from the shock of the water. You make good distance but at a cost.

choices.label: Wait here for the river to drop. Risk another storm, risk the Pawnee return, but safer crossing tomorrow.

consequences.description: You camp on the riverbank. The storm hits hard at dusk—lightning strikes near the stock. Mary delirious through the night, crying out. You use precious medicine to keep her breathing steady. No sign of Pawnee, but you burn two days. The delay gnaws at everyone.

choices.label: Send Thomas and Sarah ahead on horseback to scout a better crossing. Wait here with Mary and James.

consequences.description: Thomas and Sarah return in three hours with news: a crossing a mile north, easier but swampy on the far bank. But splitting the party unsettles everyone. Mary worsens in James's care alone—he's no doctor. When you finally cross, the swamp sucks an ox to the knee. You extract it, but the animal limps now, useless for hard pulling.

Mary.note: Each choice carries risk of cascading fever. Her presence constrains your options.

Thomas.note: Pragmatic. Favors caution. Will resent rushed decisions that endanger others, but will follow orders.

Sarah.note: Young, eager to move. Chafes at delay. A dangerous combination on a river crossing.

James.note: Leader. Each choice reflects on his judgment. Party watches for weakness.

medium.journal_entry: June 15th. The river runs brown and fast. Mary lies in the wagon, burning up. The sky is gathering itself for another blow. We found the Pawnee were here—not hostile signs, just the proof they move through this land like water through water, and we are stones. I feel the weight of five people's lives in my spine. One is already gone. I will not lose another to caution, but I will not throw them away on haste either. The next hour will decide that.

medium.title: The Sick Ox
medium.description: One of your oxen—the red one, the one that pulled hardest through the Platte crossing—has gone lame. Its rear left leg is swollen thick as a tree trunk, hot to the touch. The animal is breathing heavy and won't take water. The leg may be infected, or the tendon may be ruined. Either way, you have five oxen and need at least four to reach Fort Laramie. You could butcher it now while the meat is good. You could rest it a day, perhaps two, and hope. Or you could trade it to the Pawnee band camped two miles north—runners spotted smoke that direction this morning. They might give you fresh horses in exchange, or dried meat, or information. Resting costs time. Trading costs the oxen you know. Butchering costs the feed value and wastes the hide.

choices.label: Butcher the ox. Smoke the meat. It's protein.
Rest camp two days. Keep the ox alive if you can.
Ride north to the Pawnee camp. Trade the ox for whatever they offer.
Kill the ox quietly and leave it. Travel light and fast—no time for smoke.

medium.journal_entry: June 25th. Red ox is done for. The swelling won't go down. I've seen this before—gangrene sets in fast in this heat. Every choice tastes like poison. Mary wants to rest and pray over it. Thomas says we're behind schedule. Sarah just stares at the animal and won't speak. I remember when we bought this ox in Independence for forty dollars. It felt like we were building something then. Now I'm deciding how to dispose of it, and the smell of the mosquitoes is making me sick.

medium.title: The Poisoned Well
medium.description: By late afternoon, the scouts find water—a deep well with a hand pump, impossible out here. The wood is new. Near the lip, half-buried in dust, is a tin cup and a child's doll, cloth rotting. Thomas drinks first without waiting. The water tastes metallic. Within minutes his stomach cramps. Mary notices the doll's dress matches fabric from a wagon team. The well could be poisoned deliberately. It could be coincidence. You have maybe six hours before dark, and the next confirmed water is thirty miles north.

choices.label: Dose Thomas with medicine and push through the night to the next water source.
choices.journal_entry: Used our last good medicine on Thomas. The night was black and the oxen faltered twice. Sarah wept from thirst. Mary said nothing but her eyes held a question I couldn't answer.

choices.label: Stay at the well. Rest and ration water carefully. Boil what you take.
choices.journal_entry: Stayed. Boiled the water but Thomas worsened through the night. His fever came hot. By dawn he could barely sit. We lost two days and gained only certainty: we should have left sooner.

choices.label: Take the doll and the cup. Bury them properly a quarter-mile east. Leave a warning carved into the well frame.
choices.journal_entry: We buried her small and proper. Mary wept. Sarah whispered a prayer. It was foolish, stopping for a child we never knew. It felt necessary anyway. Thomas watched us from the wagon, fever-eyed and angry.

choices.label: Take the doll. Leave it in the well as a marker for others. Move on immediately without stopping.
choices.journal_entry: Left the doll floating in the dark water. Thomas vomited twice before sunset. No one spoke to me the rest of the day. I can still see Mary's face when I told her we were leaving it behind.

medium.journal_entry: The plains give nothing without asking for something worse in return. A well appears where it shouldn't. A doll marks a grave no one remembers. Thomas drank first and the metal taste is still in our mouths.

medium.title: The Shoshone Courier
medium.description: A lone Shoshone rider appears at dawn, leading a lathered horse. He speaks broken English and offers to trade: fresh meat and directions through the high passes in exchange for two of your oxen. He says a storm is coming—bad one. The passes close for days when it hits. He leaves at noon with or without a deal. Mary watches from the tent, still feverish. Your oxen are thin. The meat he's offering would go far.

choices.label: Trade the two oxen. You need the meat and the route knowledge.
Refuse the trade. Push for the passes yourself before the storm hits.
Trade one ox and ask him to guide you personally. Pay extra from your money if needed.
Ask him to wait one day while Mary recovers. Offer medicine as payment.

medium.journal_entry: The Shoshone knew the mountains like scars on his own hands. He didn't seem to care much whether we lived or died, just whether we could pay. The storm was still hours away, maybe a day. But fever doesn't wait for weather. Neither do mountains.

medium.title: The Ravine
medium.description: You crest a ridge at midday. Below: a ravine you didn't know was here. The mapped trail should descend into it, but the walls are unstable—loose shale, recent rockfall. Mary points to a ledge path skirting the north face. It looks passable but narrow. Thomas is studying the ravine floor. 'Water down there,' he says. 'Fresh.' Your oxen are thirsty. The direct route would save two miles, but the ledge terrifies Mary. Going around adds four miles and a full day. The afternoon storm clouds are building fast.

choices.label: Take the ravine floor directly. It's the route. Trust it.

consequences.description: You descend into the ravine. The water is sweet. The oxen drink deep. You make good time until the second ox clips a loose stone crossing a streambed. It goes lame. By camp, it's clear the animal won't walk tomorrow. You'll have to shoot it or abandon it. Either way, you're down to three oxen for the remaining 600 miles. The meat spoils too fast to preserve in this heat.

choices.label: Take the ledge path. It's slower but safer.

consequences.description: The ledge is narrower than it looked. James and Mary make it across without incident, but Thomas freezes halfway. He's afraid of heights—always has been. James has to coax him inch by inch while the oxen wait, growing restless. The afternoon storm hits before Thomas reaches the far side. Lightning cracks close. No one is struck, but the fear is real and leaves its mark. You make camp in poor shelter, wet and shaken. You cover only six miles.

choices.label: Go around. Add a day, keep everyone whole.

consequences.description: You backtrack and swing north, adding hours to the route. The detour is grinding, boring—worse somehow than danger. Mary complains quietly. Thomas stares at the oxen. James says nothing. You find a wider pass as the storm rolls in. You're in good shelter when the rain comes hard. It fills every container you have. In the morning, you've gained eight miles of good trail and fresh water. But the delay costs you.

personality_effects.James: Fatigue is showing. Two burials in four weeks. He makes decisions faster now—less thinking, more doing.
personality_effects.Mary: She's becoming acute to danger. Sees risks others miss. Trust her fear or overrule it?
personality_effects.Thomas: Quieter than before. William's death hit him hard. He follows James without question, but you sense something breaking.

medium.journal_entry: Aug 1 — The ravine proved we don't know this country the way the maps claim. One storm, one loose stone, and the whole arrangement changes. William would have made a joke about it. Thomas didn't today. Mary is right to be afraid. Fear keeps you alive out here. Stupid courage gets you buried beside the trail.

medium.title: The Injured Pawnee
medium.description: You crest a ridge at dusk and find a Pawnee man, maybe forty, with a lance wound in his thigh wrapped in cattail down soaked dark. He's alone, sitting against a boulder, his horse dead three yards away with an arrow in its neck. When he sees you, his hand moves to a knife—not threatening, just checking it's there. Mary shivers beside you. The wound smells like rot starting. He speaks no English. Thomas mutters that Pawnee raided a settlement two months back. The sun will be down in twenty minutes.

choices.label: Approach carefully with medicine and water. Risk or mercy—you choose.

consequences.narrative_note: He takes the water but not the bandages. When you leave at first light, he's gone—hoofprints heading northwest suggest he had a second horse hidden. Mary's fever breaks that night, which may mean nothing.

choices.label: Circle wide around him. Keep moving before dark makes the trail treacherous.

consequences.narrative_note: You hear a sound like a man choking or laughing when you're a quarter mile on. No one speaks about it.

choices.label: Thomas wants to take his horse. The man's half-dead anyway.

consequences.narrative_note: The Pawnee doesn't fight. He watches you take the horse with no expression. That night, James hears hoofbeats circling the camp—close enough that the oxen panic. Nothing is attacked. Nothing is stolen. By morning, the sound is gone. Thomas insists he saw riders. James knows he didn't.

personality_effects.Mary: sick:mountain_fever persists
personality_effects.Thomas: fracture in party trust widening

medium.journal_entry: August 15th. Saw a man the mountains had already begun to take back. The choice felt less like mercy or cruelty and more like deciding which burden to carry. We are not good people out here. We are just people, smaller than we thought.

medium.title: The Flour is Ruined
medium.description:
You find Thomas kneeling by the supply wagon at dawn, holding handfuls of paste. The flour sack split during yesterday's creek crossing. Twenty pounds of it has turned to a gray slurry that smells of river mud and rot. Thomas's hands are shaking. He says he should have secured it better. He won't look at you.

Mary appears with a cup of coffee—actual coffee, the last of it. The oxen are restless. The desert stretches ahead, empty and vast. You have maybe three weeks of walking before the Cascades. The meager rations were already thin.

choices.label: Tell Thomas it's done, move on. Ration the rest harder.

consequences.description: Thomas's jaw tightens. He nods and doesn't speak for the rest of the morning. By afternoon, he's lagging behind the wagon. Mary pulls him forward by the arm, says something you can't hear. The cold cuts deeper when no one speaks.

choices.label: Split the ruined flour into a stretcher soup. Boil it with salt and whatever else stretches it.

consequences.description: It tastes like wet ash and boiled leather. Thomas eats three bowls. Mary refuses hers and gives it to him. That night you trade James's ammunition to a passing Shoshone hunter for six pounds of dried deer. He doesn't ask questions. You don't ask why he's alone out here. The next morning, Thomas's morale lifts slightly. Your own stomach feels hollow in a new way.

choices.label: Leave the wagon here, redistribute supplies to the oxen, and push faster.

consequences.description: The wagon takes two hours to dismantle. Thomas cuts his palm on rusted iron. You leave it standing like a monument to waste. The oxen move faster without the weight, but so do your feet. By evening, your legs are singing. Mary asks once if you made the right call. You don't answer. That night, no one speaks. The cold feels like punishment.

medium.journal_entry: Sept 1st. Lost twenty pounds of flour to the creek. Thomas blames himself. I blame the river. We have maybe two hundred miles left, maybe three weeks if the weather holds and we don't have to dig in for snow. The oxen are thin. I can see their ribs now. Mary's cough came back this morning. She tries to hide it behind her hand. We saw smoke to the south yesterday—could be a settlement or could be nothing. Could be hostile. I don't have the heart to ask Thomas about it. He's already carrying enough weight.

medium.title: The Burned Wagons
medium.description: You come upon the charred remains of two wagons in a narrow bend of the canyon. The skeletons of oxen lie nearby, still harnessed. No bodies—or what's left of them has been scattered by animals. The metal frame of a trunk sits half-buried in ash. Mary finds a child's doll, its cloth burned away, leaving only the wooden core. There are no fresh tracks. This happened weeks ago, maybe longer. James examines wagon marks in the dust leading north, away from the trail. Whoever survived went into the mountains. The dry wood of the burned wagons would burn hot and fast—accidental fire, or deliberate? The silence suggests the latter.

choices.label: Search the trunk and nearby wreckage for supplies or information.

consequences.description: You spend hours sifting through ash. The trunk contains a ledger—names, dates, a final entry dated three weeks prior: 'Sickness. Decided to turn back.' Someone made a choice to leave the trail. You find no food worth taking, but your hands and lungs pay the price. The burnt smell clings. Mary stares at the doll longer than necessary.

choices.label: Mark the location and press on without stopping—don't invite speculation.

consequences.description: You say nothing to the party about what this means. Mary wants to bury the doll. You don't stop. The canyon bends, and the wagons disappear behind you. No one speaks for hours. James notices Mary gripping the rifle tighter than usual. She may not forgive the choice to leave them unnamed.

choices.label: Follow the tracks northward into the mountains—they may have found shelter, or supplies.

consequences.description: The tracks fade into rocky terrain. You find an abandoned camp two miles up—a lean-to, scattered belongings, no people. A child's shoe. Fresh blood on a stone. The oxen goes lame descending. Mary insists you did the right thing. James knows you wasted time and resources chasing ghosts. The mountains offer nothing but confirmation that leaving the trail is dying—just slower.

medium.journal_entry: Found wagons burned in the canyon bend. Sarah would have wanted to bury them proper. We didn't. The doll Mary found has no face now. Everything in this country is being unmade—by weather, by sickness, by fire, or by choice. I can't tell which is worse anymore. Three months from the pass. Snow is coming.
